topics.title: Power Diodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Diodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power Diode** is a two-terminal, uncontrolled, unidirectional solid-state semiconductor switching device designed to handle high currents (from a few amperes to several kilo-amperes) and high blocking voltages (up to several kilovolts) in power electronic circuits. Structurally, it is a heavily doped P⁺-N-N⁺ junction (unlike the lightly doped P-N junction of a signal diode), which gives it a large reverse breakdown voltage capability and a high forward current surge tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The KTU 2024 scheme (PEICT411) categorizes the Power Diode under the foundational "Power Semiconductor Switches" cluster, alongside the Power BJT, Power MOSFET, IGBT, Thyristor, and TRIAC. It is the *only* passive (non-controllable) switch in this family.

### 1.1.1 Distinguishing Features vs. Signal Diode

| Parameter | Signal Diode | Power Diode |
|---|---|---|
| Junction Structure | P-N (light doping) | P⁺-N-N⁺ (heavy doping) |
| Forward Current | mA range | A to kA range |
| Reverse Voltage | < 100 V typically | 100 V to > 5 kV |
| Switching Speed | Very fast (ns) | Moderate to slow (µs) |
| Power Dissipation | mW range | W to kW range |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The One-Way Floodgate

Imagine a large concrete **floodgate** on a river used for hydroelectric power.

- The gate is **heavy and thick** (like the N⁻ drift region of a power diode), so it cannot open or close instantly — it has mechanical inertia, much like the **reverse recovery time** of a diode.
- It only allows water to flow in **one direction** (forward bias → conduction).
- During reverse bias, the gate is slammed shut and the river stops flowing — but if the water pressure (reverse voltage) gets too high, the gate structure could fail (avalanche breakdown).
- A **Schottky diode** would be like a smaller, lighter gate that snaps shut much faster (no reverse recovery) but cannot withstand huge pressure (lower reverse voltage rating).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Tip:** The thicker the N⁻ drift region, the higher the voltage the diode can block — but the slower it switches (greater reverse recovery time). This is a fundamental **trade-off in power diode design**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics Highlighted in KTU

- **Static Characteristics**: Forward voltage drop ($V_F$), reverse leakage current ($I_R$), reverse breakdown voltage ($V_{BR}$).
- **Dynamic Characteristics**: Reverse recovery time ($t_{rr}$), reverse recovery charge ($Q_{rr}$), softness factor ($S$).
- **Thermal Ratings**: Junction temperature ($T_j$), thermal resistance ($R_{th(JA)}$), maximum power dissipation $P_D$.

> [!NOTE]
> The principal engineering utility of a power diode lies in its application as a **rectifier**, **freewheeling element**, **snubber circuit component**, and **uncontrolled switch** in HVDC, SMPS, drives, and inverter circuits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details of a Power Diode

A power diode is a three-layer device:

1. **P⁺ layer (Anode side)**: Heavily doped, thin.
2. **N⁻ layer (Drift region)**: Lightly doped, **thick** — this is what gives the diode its high voltage-blocking capability.
3. **N⁺ layer (Cathode side)**: Heavily doped, thin — provides a low-resistance ohmic contact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The N⁻ drift region is the heart of the power diode. When reverse biased, the depletion layer extends predominantly into this lightly doped N⁻ region, allowing it to sustain a high electric field and therefore a high blocking voltage.

## 2.2 Static V-I Characteristics — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Forward Bias Region:**
- When $V_{AK} > 0$ (anode positive), the depletion region collapses and majority carriers (holes from P⁺ and electrons from N⁺) are injected into the N⁻ drift region.
- The diode begins conducting when $V_{AK} \geq V_{F(ON)}$, typically **0.7 V to 2.5 V** for silicon power diodes.

**Step 2 — Reverse Blocking Region:**
- When $V_{AK} < 0$, the depletion region widens into the N⁻ region.
- Only a small reverse leakage current $I_R$ flows (microamperes to milliamperes), which is temperature dependent.

**Step 3 — Breakdown Region:**
- If reverse voltage exceeds the breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$, the diode enters avalanche/Zener breakdown.
- The device must be protected, as sustained breakdown causes thermal runaway.

The piecewise linear model of the diode is given by:

$$v_D(t) = V_{F(ON)} + R_{ON} \cdot i_D(t)$$

where $V_{F(ON)}$ is the threshold voltage (typically **0.7 V to 1.2 V**) and $R_{ON}$ is the on-state slope resistance (typically milliohms).

## 2.3 Dynamic Characteristics — Reverse Recovery

When a forward-conducting diode is suddenly reverse biased, it does **not stop conducting instantly**. The stored minority carriers in the N⁻ drift region must be removed, leading to a finite **reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$**.

The reverse recovery process has two distinct phases:

1. **Storage time $t_s$**: Diode still conducts in reverse; reverse current rises from zero to peak reverse recovery current $I_{RR}$.
2. **Transition time $t_t$**: The reverse current decays from $I_{RR}$ to $I_R$ (leakage value).

The total reverse recovery time is:

$$t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$$

The reverse recovery charge (a critical figure-of-merit) is the area under the reverse current curve:

$$Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \quad \text{(for a triangular approximation)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Softness Factor $S$:** Defined as the ratio of the transition time to the storage time, $S = t_t / t_s$. A *soft* recovery ($S > 1$) is preferred to minimize EMI and voltage transients.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward voltage drop | $V_F$ | 0.7 V – 2.5 V | Determines conduction loss |
| 2 | On-state slope resistance | $R_{ON}$ | m$\Omega$ range | Used in piecewise-linear model |
| 3 | Reverse leakage current | $I_R$ | µA – mA | Temperature dependent |
| 4 | Breakdown voltage | $V_{BR}$ | 50 V – 5 kV | Voltage rating |
| 5 | Reverse recovery time | $t_{rr}$ | µs range | Limits switching frequency |
| 6 | Reverse recovery charge | $Q_{rr}$ | µC range | Switching loss indicator |
| 7 | Peak reverse recovery current | $I_{RR}$ | A range | Snubber design parameter |
| 8 | Softness factor | $S = t_t \div t_s$ | $S \geq 1$ ideal | EMI control |
| 9 | Conduction power loss | $P_{cond} = V_F \cdot I_{F(avg)} + R_{ON} \cdot I_{F(rms)}^{2}$ | Watts | Thermal design |
| 10 | Switching power loss | $P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f_s$ | Watts | Heatsink sizing |
| 11 | Junction temperature | $T_j$ | $\leq 150^{\circ}\text{C}$ (Si) | Reliability limit |
| 12 | Thermal resistance | $R_{th(JA)}$ | ${}^{\circ}\text{C/W}$ | Cooling system design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember for KTU Numerical Problems:** Always use $Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} t_{rr}$ **only** when the recovery current waveform is approximated as a triangle. For an exact exponential decay, integrate the actual $i(t)$ curve.

## 2.5 Types of Power Diodes (KTU 2024 Categorization)

1. **General-Purpose Diodes**: $t_{rr} \approx 25\ \mu\text{s}$, used at line frequencies (50/60 Hz) — e.g., in uncontrolled rectifiers.
2. **Fast-Recovery Diodes**: $t_{rr} \leq 5\ \mu\text{s}$, used in high-frequency inverter and chopper circuits.
3. **Schottky Diodes**: $V_F \approx 0.3\text{–}0.5$ V, $t_{rr} \approx$ ns, used in low-voltage SMPS. **Cannot block more than ~100 V** due to absence of drift region.
4. **Line-Frequency Diodes**: Optimized for 50/60 Hz rectification with very high current capability (kA range).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Power Diode |
|---|---|
| HVDC transmission | High-voltage rectification (> 100 kV) |
| SMPS adapters | Schottky diode as output rectifier |
| Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) | Freewheeling diode across inductive loads |
| Battery Chargers | Bridge rectifier in front-end |
| Solar PV Inverters | Blocking diode to prevent reverse current flow |
| Welding Power Supplies | High-current rectifier for the DC bus |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average and RMS Forward Current for Half-Wave Rectifier

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A single-phase half-wave uncontrolled rectifier supplies a resistive load $R_L$ from an AC source $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. A power diode $D$ is used as the switching element.

### 3.1.2 Output Voltage Waveform
- For $0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi$: Diode is forward biased → $v_o(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) - V_F$.
- For $\pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$: Diode is reverse biased → $v_o(\omega t) = 0$.

### 3.1.3 Average Output Voltage $V_{o(avg)}$

**Step 1:** Write the integral over the conduction interval.

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} (V_m \sin\theta - V_F)\, d\theta$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the first integral.

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin\theta\, d\theta = V_m [-\cos\theta]_{0}^{\pi} = V_m(1 - (-1)) = 2 V_m$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the second integral.

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} V_F\, d\theta = V_F [\theta]_{0}^{\pi} = \pi V_F$$

**Step 4:** Combine.

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left( 2 V_m - \pi V_F \right)$$

$$\boxed{V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} - \frac{V_F}{2}}$$

### 3.1.4 RMS Output Voltage $V_{o(rms)}$

**Step 1:** Square the output voltage and integrate.

$$V_{o(rms)}^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} (V_m \sin\theta - V_F)^{2}\, d\theta$$

**Step 2:** Expand the square.

$$(V_m \sin\theta - V_F)^{2} = V_m^{2} \sin^{2}\theta - 2 V_m V_F \sin\theta + V_F^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Integrate each term separately.

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}\theta\, d\theta = V_m^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{\pi V_m^{2}}{2}$$

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} 2 V_m V_F \sin\theta\, d\theta = 2 V_m V_F [-\cos\theta]_{0}^{\pi} = 4 V_m V_F$$

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} V_F^{2}\, d\theta = \pi V_F^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Combine and take the square root.

$$V_{o(rms)}^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left( \frac{\pi V_m^{2}}{2} - 4 V_m V_F + \pi V_F^{2} \right)$$

$$V_{o(rms)}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{4} - \frac{2 V_m V_F}{\pi} + \frac{V_F^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{2}}{4} - \frac{2 V_m V_F}{\pi} + \frac{V_F^{2}}{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal Diode Approximation (KTU simplification):** If $V_F \ll V_m$ (e.g., $V_m = 311$ V and $V_F = 1.5$ V), then $V_{o(avg)} \approx V_m/\pi$ and $V_{o(rms)} \approx V_m/2$. These are the textbook answers commonly expected in KTU problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: Reverse Recovery Charge for Trapezoidal Recovery

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
A diode carrying forward current $I_F$ is switched off at $t = 0$. The reverse recovery current $i_r(t)$ rises linearly from 0 to $I_{RR}$ over time $t_s$ (storage phase), then decays linearly from $I_{RR}$ to 0 over time $t_t$ (transition phase). Find $Q_{rr}$ exactly.

### 3.2.2 Mathematical Definition
Reverse recovery charge is the integral of the absolute reverse current over the recovery interval:

$$Q_{rr} = \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} \vert i_r(t) \vert\, dt = \int_{0}^{t_s} i_r(t)\, dt + \int_{t_s}^{t_{rr}} i_r(t)\, dt$$

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Evaluation

**Step 1 — Storage phase integral (rising triangle):**
The current rises linearly: $i_r(t) = \dfrac{I_{RR}}{t_s} \cdot t$ for $0 \leq t \leq t_s$.

$$\int_{0}^{t_s} \frac{I_{RR}}{t_s} \cdot t\, dt = \frac{I_{RR}}{t_s} \cdot \frac{t_s^{2}}{2} = \frac{I_{RR} \cdot t_s}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Transition phase integral (falling triangle):**
The current decays linearly: $i_r(t) = I_{RR} \left(1 - \dfrac{t - t_s}{t_t}\right)$ for $t_s \leq t \leq t_s + t_t$.

$$\int_{t_s}^{t_s + t_t} I_{RR} \left(1 - \frac{t - t_s}{t_t}\right) dt = I_{RR} \cdot t_t \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{I_{RR} \cdot t_t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Combine.**

$$\boxed{Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} \cdot t_s + \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} \cdot t_t = \frac{I_{RR}}{2} (t_s + t_t) = \frac{I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}}{2}}$$

### 3.2.4 Sanity Check (Limiting Case)
If $t_s \to t_{rr}$ (abrupt / "snappy" recovery, $t_t \to 0$): $Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} t_{rr}$. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Reverse Recovery on Voltage Across an Inductive Load

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **most-frequently-asked concept** in KTU module-1 problems related to freewheeling diodes.

**Setup:** A freewheeling diode $D_F$ is connected across a highly inductive load. Just before commutation, the load current was $I_L$ flowing through the switch. At $t = 0$, the switch opens.

**Step 1:** The diode cannot suddenly carry the load current because of its reverse recovery. The reverse current through the diode is:

$$i_{D_F}(t) = I_L - i_{T}(t)$$

where $i_{T}(t)$ is the current being taken up by the incoming switch.

**Step 2:** During the storage time, the diode reverse current rises to $I_{RR} = I_L + \Delta I$ where $\Delta I$ depends on $di/dt$.

**Step 3:** The peak reverse voltage across the switch is given by:

$$V_{T,\text{peak}} = V_{DC} + L \frac{di_{T}}{dt}\bigg|_{t=t_s}$$

This peak can far exceed $V_{DC}$, so a **snubber circuit (RCD)** is mandatory.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A power diode has $V_F = 1.2$ V at $I_F = 50$ A, and $R_{ON} = 5\ \text{m}\Omega$. Calculate (a) the total forward voltage drop at $I_F = 100$ A, and (b) the conduction power loss for a square-wave current of magnitude 100 A at duty cycle 0.5.

### Solution

**(a) Forward voltage at 100 A:**

$$V_F(100) = V_{F(ON)} + R_{ON} \cdot I_F = 1.2 + (0.005)(100) = 1.2 + 0.5$$

$$\boxed{V_F(100) = 1.7\ \text{V}}$$

**(b) Conduction loss:**

For a square wave with magnitude 100 A, $I_{avg} = 100 \times 0.5 = 50$ A and $I_{rms} = 100 \times \sqrt{0.5} = 70.71$ A.

$$P_{cond} = V_{F(ON)} \cdot I_{avg} + R_{ON} \cdot I_{rms}^{2} = (1.2)(50) + (0.005)(70.71)^{2}$$

$$P_{cond} = 60 + (0.005)(5000) = 60 + 25$$

$$\boxed{P_{cond} = 85\ \text{W}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Physical Structure of a Power Diode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anode Terminal] --> B["P plus Layer Heavy Doping"]
    B --> C["N minus Drift Region Light Doping Thick"]
    C --> D["N plus Layer Heavy Doping"]
    D --> E[Cathode Terminal]
    B -. "Depletion region extends mostly here under reverse bias" .-> C
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Reverse Recovery Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Step 0: Diode ON in forward direction I = I_F"] --> S1["Step 1: Reverse bias applied at t = 0"]
    S1 --> S2["Step 2: Storage phase t less than t_s — Diode still conducts in REVERSE — I_r rises to I_RR"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Transition phase t less than t_t — Stored carriers swept out — I_r decays to I_R"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Diode fully reverse biased — only I_R flows"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Freewheeling Diode in a Chopper Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["DC Source V_dc Positive"] --> SW["Controlled Switch S MOSFET or IGBT"]
    SW --> LD["Inductive Load R plus L"]
    LD --> RTN["DC Source V_dc Negative"]
    FWD["Freewheeling Diode D_F Anode at load negative Cathode at load positive"] --> LD
    LD --> FWD
```

## 4.4 Classification Tree: Power Diode Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PD["Power Diode Family"] --> G["General Purpose t_rr approx 25 us"]
    PD --> FR["Fast Recovery t_rr less than 5 us"]
    PD --> SC["Schottky t_rr approx ns V_F 0.3 to 0.5 V"]
    PD --> LF["Line Frequency Optimized 50 to 60 Hz"]
    G --> A1["Used in low frequency uncontrolled rectifiers"]
    FR --> A2["Used in choppers and inverters"]
    SC --> A3["Used in SMPS and low voltage high frequency circuits"]
    LF --> A4["Used in HVDC and large industrial rectifiers"]
```

```
        i_D (A)
          |
      I_F |          ___________   (Forward conduction region)
          |         /
          |        /
          |       /
          |      / slope = 1/R_ON
          |     /
   -------+----/---------------------→ v_AK (V)
         /|   /
        / |  V_F(ON)
       /  |
      /   |
     /    |___________________________

            |  | -V_BR   |
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define reverse recovery time of a power diode. Why is it significant in high-frequency applications?**

**Model Answer:**
Reverse recovery time ($t_{rr}$) of a power diode is the time interval between the instant the diode forward current crosses zero (during turn-off) and the instant the reverse current decays to 25% of its peak reverse value $I_{RR}$. It is the sum of the storage time $t_s$ and the transition time $t_t$.

**Significance in high-frequency applications:**
- At high switching frequencies, the diode spends a large fraction of its switching period in the recovery phase, leading to excessive switching losses ($P_{sw} \propto f_s \cdot Q_{rr}$).
- It limits the maximum operating frequency of the converter.
- It causes commutation overlap, voltage spikes across the switches, and EMI.
- Therefore, fast-recovery and Schottky diodes are preferred for high-frequency operation.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for storage + transition phase, 1 for high-frequency significance)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare the salient features of a Schottky diode with a PN-junction power diode.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PN-Junction Power Diode | Schottky Diode |
|---|---|---|
| Junction type | P⁺-N-N⁺ (majority + minority carrier) | Metal-semiconductor (majority carrier only) |
| Forward voltage drop $V_F$ | 0.7 V – 2.5 V | 0.3 V – 0.5 V |
| Reverse recovery time | µs (slow) | Negligible (ns) |
| Reverse leakage current | Low | High |
| Maximum blocking voltage | Up to > 5 kV | Limited to ~100 V |
| Switching frequency capability | Low–Medium | Very High |
| Application | Rectifiers, freewheeling | SMPS, RF, high-frequency clamps |

*(3 marks: 1 for junction mechanism, 1 for switching/forward drop, 1 for voltage/frequency application trade-off)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat constructional diagram, describe the structure of a power diode. Explain why the drift region is lightly doped and relatively thick. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the static and dynamic characteristics of a power diode with suitable waveforms. Define reverse recovery time, storage time, transition time, and reverse recovery charge. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The power diode is a three-layer P⁺-N-N⁺ semiconductor device.

1. **P⁺ layer (anode side):** Heavily doped (acceptor concentration $N_A \approx 10^{19}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$), very thin — provides a low-resistance ohmic contact and a high concentration of holes for forward conduction.
2. **N⁻ drift region (middle layer):** Lightly doped (donor concentration $N_D \approx 10^{13}\text{–}10^{14}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$), thickness $W$ ranges from a few tens of microns to several hundred microns depending on voltage rating.
3. **N⁺ layer (cathode side):** Heavily doped to provide low-resistance ohmic contact.

**Why lightly doped and thick N⁻ drift region?**

- Under reverse bias, the depletion layer extends almost entirely into the lightly doped N⁻ side (because $N_D \ll N_A$).
- The depletion width $W_d$ under reverse bias is given by:

$$W_d = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_R}{q N_D}}$$

- A small $N_D$ and large $W_d$ allow the diode to sustain a high electric field over a wide depletion region, increasing the **breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$**:

$$V_{BR} = \frac{1}{2} E_{crit} \cdot W_d$$

where $E_{crit}$ is the critical electric field of silicon ($\approx 3 \times 10^{5}$ V/cm).

- Thus, a thicker, lightly doped drift region directly translates into a higher reverse voltage rating.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for doping explanation, 3 for drift region rationale)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Static Characteristics** are obtained by plotting the steady-state $V$ vs $I$ curve:

- **Forward region:** Above $V_F \approx 0.7$ V the diode conducts with an exponential rise in current. For power diodes, the linearised model $v_D = V_{F(ON)} + R_{ON} \cdot i_D$ is used.
- **Reverse blocking region:** A small leakage current $I_R$ flows (µA to mA), increasing with temperature.
- **Breakdown region:** At $V = V_{BR}$, the diode enters avalanche; uncontrolled conduction destroys the device.

**Dynamic Characteristics** describe the diode behaviour during switching.

When a forward-conducting diode is suddenly reverse biased, the current does not fall to zero immediately:

- **Storage time $t_s$:** The reverse current rises from 0 to peak $I_{RR}$. During this phase, the stored minority carriers in the N⁻ region are being removed. The diode still conducts in reverse.
- **Transition time $t_t$:** The reverse current falls from $I_{RR}$ to the steady reverse leakage value $I_R$. The diode regains its blocking capability.
- **Reverse recovery time $t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$**
- **Reverse recovery charge $Q_{rr} = \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} i_r(t)\, dt$** — typically approximated as $Q_{rr} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} I_{RR} t_{rr}$.

**Softness factor $S = t_t / t_s$** indicates whether the recovery is soft ($S \geq 1$, lower EMI) or snappy ($S < 1$, high $di/dt$, severe voltage transients).

*(7 marks: 2 for static V-I explanation, 3 for waveform & recovery phases, 2 for charge and softness factor)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Supplementary]**
**(a)** A single-phase half-wave rectifier feeds a resistive load $R_L = 10\ \Omega$ from a $230$ V, 50 Hz AC source. The power diode has a forward voltage drop of $V_F = 1.5$ V. Calculate the average and RMS load current, average power delivered to the load, and the input power factor. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a single-phase full-wave bridge rectifier. Compare its performance with a center-tapped transformer rectifier. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Given:** $V_{rms,s} = 230$ V, $R_L = 10\ \Omega$, $V_F = 1.5$ V, $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Peak source voltage:**

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms,s} = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Average output voltage:**

$$V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} - \frac{V_F}{2} = \frac{325.27}{3.1416} - \frac{1.5}{2}$$

$$V_{o,avg} = 103.53 - 0.75 = 102.78\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Average load current:**

$$I_{o,avg} = \frac{V_{o,avg}}{R_L} = \frac{102.78}{10} = 10.28\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — RMS output voltage:**

$$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{2}}{4} - \frac{2 V_m V_F}{\pi} + \frac{V_F^{2}}{2}}$$

$$V_{o,rms}^{2} = \frac{(325.27)^{2}}{4} - \frac{2 \times 325.27 \times 1.5}{\pi} + \frac{(1.5)^{2}}{2}$$

$$V_{o,rms}^{2} = 26450.3 - 310.6 + 1.125 = 26140.8\ \text{V}^{2}$$

$$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{26140.8} = 161.68\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — RMS load current:**

$$I_{o,rms} = \frac{V_{o,rms}}{R_L} = \frac{161.68}{10} = 16.17\ \text{A}$$

**Step 6 — Average load power:**

$$P_L = I_{o,rms}^{2} \cdot R_L = (16.17)^{2} \times 10 = 2614.7\ \text{W}$$

**Step 7 — Input power factor:**

The source delivers current only during the half-cycle. Source current $i_s$ is identical to load current.

$$I_{s,rms} = I_{o,rms} = 16.17\ \text{A}$$

$$S = V_{s,rms} \cdot I_{s,rms} = 230 \times 16.17 = 3719.1\ \text{VA}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Power factor} = \frac{P_L}{S} = \frac{2614.7}{3719.1} = 0.703\ \text{(lagging)}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for V_avg, 2 for V_rms, 2 for power, 1 for PF)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Circuit Description:** A single-phase full-wave bridge rectifier uses **four power diodes** (D1, D2, D3, D4) connected in a bridge configuration. The AC source is connected across one diagonal of the bridge, and the DC load $R_L$ is connected across the other diagonal.

**Operation (two modes per cycle):**

- **Mode 1 (positive half-cycle, $v_s > 0$):** Diodes D1 and D2 are forward biased; D3 and D4 are reverse biased. Current path: source (+) → D1 → load → D2 → source (−). Output voltage $v_o = v_s - 2 V_F$.
- **Mode 2 (negative half-cycle, $v_s < 0$):** Diodes D3 and D4 are forward biased; D1 and D2 are reverse biased. Current path: source (−) → D3 → load → D4 → source (+). Output voltage $v_o = -v_s - 2 V_F$.

In both modes, the load current flows in the **same direction**, producing a full-wave rectified output.

**Average output voltage (ideal):**

$$V_{o,avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}$$

**Comparison with Center-Tapped Transformer Rectifier:**

| Feature | Bridge Rectifier | Center-Tapped Rectifier |
|---|---|---|
| Number of diodes | 4 | 2 |
| Transformer secondary | Single winding | Center-tapped (double winding) |
| Transformer utilization factor (TUF) | Higher (~0.81) | Lower (~0.69) |
| PIV rating of each diode | $V_m$ | $2 V_m$ |
| Voltage drop per conduction | $2 V_F$ | $1 V_F$ |
| Cost & size | Lower (no center tap) | Higher (center-tap transformer) |
| Best suited for | Low-voltage, high-current | High-voltage, low-current |

*(7 marks: 2 for circuit + modes, 3 for operation, 2 for comparison table)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the forward voltage drop $V_F$** in the average and RMS voltage formulae. Always include the term $\pi V_F / 2$ in $V_{o,avg}$.
> 2. **Confusing $t_s$ and $t_t$**: $t_s$ = storage (current rising to $I_{RR}$), $t_t$ = transition (current falling to $I_R$). Many students swap these.
> 3. **PIV of bridge diode = $V_m$, not $2 V_m$**. In a center-tapped rectifier, PIV = $2 V_m$. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Reverse recovery of a diode is NOT a leakage phenomenon** — it is a result of stored minority carriers in the N⁻ drift region. Do not confuse with reverse leakage $I_R$.
> 5. **Schottky diode has no reverse recovery but has high leakage** and a low voltage limit (~100 V). Reversing this fact is a common error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Power Diodes**

- **Structure:** P⁺-N-N⁺ three-layer device; the N⁻ drift region controls blocking voltage.
- **Static model:** $v_D = V_{F(ON)} + R_{ON} \cdot i_D$, with $V_{F(ON)} \approx 0.7\text{–}1.2$ V.
- **Forward current range:** Amperes to kiloamperes; **reverse voltage rating:** 50 V to > 5 kV.
- **Reverse recovery time $t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$** — limits maximum switching frequency.
- **Reverse recovery charge $Q_{rr} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} I_{RR} t_{rr}$** for a triangular recovery waveform.
- **Softness factor $S = t_t / t_s$** — soft recovery ($S \geq 1$) preferred for low EMI.
- **Conduction loss:** $P_{cond} = V_F \cdot I_{avg} + R_{ON} \cdot I_{rms}^{2}$.
- **Switching loss:** $P_{sw} \approx 0.5 \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f_s$.
- **Types:** General-purpose ($t_{rr} \sim 25\ \mu$s), Fast-recovery ($t_{rr} < 5\ \mu$s), Schottky ($t_{rr} \sim$ ns, $V_F \sim 0.3$ V, $V_{BR} \leq 100$ V), Line-frequency (kA range).
- **Schottky = majority carrier device** → no reverse recovery, but high leakage and low breakdown.
- **Half-wave rectifier (resistive load):** $V_{o,avg} = V_m/\pi - V_F/2$, $V_{o,rms} = V_m/2$ (ideal).
- **Full-wave bridge rectifier:** $V_{o,avg} = 2 V_m/\pi$, PIV per diode = $V_m$.
- **Center-tapped rectifier:** $V_{o,avg} = 2 V_m/\pi$, PIV per diode = $2 V_m$.
- **Freewheeling diode** across inductive load provides a path for stored inductive energy during switch turn-off — prevents dangerous voltage spikes.
- **Snubber requirement:** Whenever $di/dt$ during reverse recovery is high, an RCD snubber is mandatory to protect the switch.
- **Thermal design:** $T_j = T_a + P_{total} \cdot R_{th(JA)}$ — must remain below $150^{\circ}\text{C}$ for silicon.
- **KTU must-knows:** Draw the V-I curve with all three regions labelled, derive $V_{o,avg}$ and $V_{o,rms}$ for half-wave and full-wave rectifiers with and without $V_F$, and write a short note on reverse recovery.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Transistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Transistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Power Transistor?

A **Power Transistor** is a solid-state semiconductor device specifically engineered to handle high voltages (typically **500 V to 6500 V**) and high currents (from a few amperes to several kiloamperes), while acting as a high-speed electronic switch or amplifier in power electronic converters. Unlike small-signal transistors used in analog signal processing, power transistors are designed for **switching-mode operation** in applications such as motor drives, SMPS, UPS, and inverters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Power transistors are three-terminal controllable semiconductor switches (BJT, MOSFET, IGBT) used in power electronic circuits. They are operated either in the **cut-off region** (OFF state) or in the **saturation/ohmic region** (ON state) to minimize power dissipation. The active/linear region is avoided for switching applications.

The three primary members of the power transistor family covered in KTU Module 1 are:

1. **Power BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)** – Current-controlled device.
2. **Power MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor)** – Voltage-controlled device.
3. **IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor)** – Voltage-controlled with bipolar output characteristics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **high-pressure water tap** connected to a main pipeline. The main pipe carries a huge flow of water (high current), and you want to control it from a small, comfortable handle.

- A **Power BJT** is like a tap where you must *push a lever with a certain force proportional to the water flow* (current-controlled). More flow → more push required.
- A **Power MOSFET** is like a tap where you simply *rotate a knob* — only a tiny twist is needed regardless of water flow (voltage-controlled).
- An **IGBT** is a hybrid tap — a soft knob (voltage control) that internally drives a heavy-duty valve (bipolar output), giving you the *best of both worlds*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy (Engineering Insight):** Think of a Power Transistor as the **"throttle valve" of an electric vehicle's motor controller**. The control circuit sends a small signal (a flick of the finger on a knob), and the transistor switches hundreds of volts and amps to the motor. Without it, efficient, fast, and silent power conversion is impossible.

## 1.3 Why Power Transistors? — Key Requirements

| Requirement | Reason |
|---|---|
| **High voltage blocking** | Must withstand supply voltages (e.g., 230 V AC → 325 V DC) |
| **High current handling** | Drive industrial loads (motors, transformers) |
| **Fast switching (kHz range)** | Reduce switching losses, shrink magnetics |
| **Low ON-state voltage drop** | Reduce conduction loss → higher efficiency |
| **High input impedance** | Easy drive from logic/control circuits |
| **Robust SOA** | Survive transients, short circuits, and inductive kickback |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The choice of power transistor (BJT vs. MOSFET vs. IGBT) is dictated by the **frequency–power tradeoff**. Low frequency + high power → BJT/IGBT; High frequency + low power → MOSFET. The crossover lies around **~10 kHz to 100 kHz**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)

### Construction Highlights
- A power BJT is a **vertical (epitaxial) npn** structure (rarely pnp), unlike signal BJTs which are lateral.
- It uses a **lightly doped n⁻ drift region** (the collector) to support high blocking voltages.
- The base is **thin and heavily doped** to improve gain and switching speed.
- An **integrated emitter shorts** structure is added to suppress the quasi-saturation effect and improve SOA.

### Operating Regions
A power BJT has three operating regions for switching:

- **Cut-off Region:** $I_B = 0$, $I_C \approx 0$, $V_{CE} \approx V_{supply}$ → **OFF state**
- **Active Region:** $I_C = \beta I_B$ → **Avoided in switching** (high dissipation)
- **Saturation Region:** $V_{CE} = V_{CE(sat)}$ (≈ 0.2 V to 2 V), $I_C < \beta I_B$ → **ON state**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why saturation matters:** In saturation, the transistor behaves like a closed switch with a small forward voltage drop. Power dissipated ($P = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$) is minimized, making it ideal for switching converters.

### Switching Characteristics of BJT
The BJT does not switch instantaneously. Four time intervals define the dynamic behavior:

| Time | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Delay time | $t_d$ | Time for $I_C$ to reach 10% of final value |
| Rise time | $t_r$ | Time for $I_C$ to rise from 10% to 90% |
| Storage time | $t_s$ | Time for $I_C$ to fall from 100% to 90% (due to stored minority carriers) |
| Fall time | $t_f$ | Time for $I_C$ to fall from 90% to 10% |

- Turn-ON time: $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$
- Turn-OFF time: $t_{off} = t_s + t_f$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage time $t_s$** is the dominant delay in BJTs and is the reason BJTs are unsuitable for high-frequency switching. It arises because excess minority carriers must be swept out of the base region.

### Safe Operating Area (SOA) of BJT
The SOA is bounded by:
1. **Collector current limit** (bond wire / wire-bonding limit)
2. **Power dissipation limit** (hyperbola $P = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$)
3. **Secondary breakdown limit** (current crowding at high $V_{CE}$)
4. **Voltage limit** (avalanche breakdown $V_{CEO}$)

The combination forms a **"diamond-shaped" SOA** in the $V_{CE}$–$I_C$ plane, wider in DC than in pulsed operation.

## 2.2 Power MOSFET

### Construction Highlights
- A power MOSFET is typically an **n-channel enhancement-mode** vertical DMOS (Double-diffused MOS) device.
- Structure contains **source, body, gate, and drain** terminals.
- Has an **intrinsic body diode** (anti-parallel) formed by the p–n junction between the body and the drain. This is useful as a freewheeling diode in half-bridge circuits.
- A small **n⁻ drift region** supports the blocking voltage.

### Operating Regions
- **Cut-off (OFF):** $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ → no channel, $I_D = 0$
- **Ohmic/Triode (ON):** $V_{GS} > V_{th}$, $R_{DS(on)}$ conducts → $V_{DS} = I_D \cdot R_{DS(on)}$
- **Active/Saturation:** Used only for analog amplification (avoided in switching)

### Switching Characteristics of MOSFET
- **Turn-ON delay ($t_{d(on)}$):** Time to charge $C_{iss}$ (input capacitance) to $V_{th}$.
- **Rise time ($t_r$):** Time to charge $C_{gs}$ from $V_{th}$ to the plateau voltage, then $C_{gd}$ (Miller effect) charges.
- **Turn-OFF delay ($t_{d(off)}$):** Discharge of $C_{iss}$.
- **Fall time ($t_f$):** Discharge of $C_{gd}$ and $C_{gs}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Miller Plateau:** During turn-ON, $V_{GS}$ stalls at a constant "plateau" voltage while $C_{gd}$ is charged. This is the **Miller effect** and dominates the switching time of MOSFETs.

### Key MOSFET Parameters
- $R_{DS(on)}$ — ON-state resistance, temperature dependent (+ve coefficient, good for paralleling)
- $C_{iss}, C_{oss}, C_{rss}$ — input, output, and reverse transfer capacitances
- $V_{th}$ — threshold voltage (typically 2–4 V)
- $V_{(BR)DSS}$ — drain-source breakdown voltage

## 2.3 IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor)

### Why IGBT? — The Best of Both Worlds
- **BJT weakness:** Current-driven, slow due to storage time.
- **MOSFET weakness:** High $R_{DS(on)}$ at high voltages → large conduction loss.
- **IGBT solution:** A **MOSFET input stage** drives a **BJT output stage**, giving:
  - High input impedance (easy voltage drive)
  - Low on-state voltage drop (bipolar conduction)
  - Moderate switching speed

### Construction
- A vertical device with four layers: **p⁺ (collector/anode), n⁻, p, n⁺ (emitter/cathode)**
- The **gate** is insulated (MOS structure) and sits on top of the p-well.

### Operating Regions
- **Forward conduction:** $V_{GE} > V_{th}$ → channel forms → electrons → turn on p–n–p BJT → current flows.
- **Reverse blocking:** Limited (~tens of volts) due to the p–n–p structure; for higher reverse voltage, an **anti-parallel diode** is used externally.
- **Forward blocking:** $V_{CE}$ supported by the wide n⁻ drift region → high voltage.

### Key IGBT Parameters
- $V_{CE(sat)}$ — ON-state voltage drop (typically 1.5 V to 3 V, nearly constant with current)
- $V_{GE(th)}$ — gate threshold voltage (typically 5 V to 6.5 V; recommended drive $V_{GE} = 15$ V)
- $V_{(BR)CES}$ — collector-emitter breakdown voltage
- **Tail current:** During turn-OFF, residual carriers in the n⁻ drift region cause a "tail" — this limits high-frequency operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tail current** in IGBTs is analogous to the storage time of BJTs. It causes additional turn-off loss and is the primary reason IGBTs are limited to switching frequencies below ~20–50 kHz (modern devices up to 150 kHz).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$ | BJT collector current | A |
| 2 | $P_{cond(BJT)} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C$ | Conduction loss of BJT | W |
| 3 | $P_{cond(MOS)} = I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | Conduction loss of MOSFET | W |
| 4 | $P_{cond(IGBT)} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C$ | Conduction loss of IGBT | W |
| 5 | $E_{on} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DS} \cdot I_D \cdot t_r$ | Turn-on switching energy (linearized) | J |
| 6 | $E_{off} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DS} \cdot I_D \cdot t_f$ | Turn-off switching energy | J |
| 7 | $P_{sw} = f_s \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off})$ | Total switching power loss | W |
| 8 | $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$ | BJT turn-on time | s |
| 9 | $t_{off} = t_s + t_f$ | BJT turn-off time | s |
| 10 | $SOA: V_{CE} \cdot I_C \le P_{max}$ | Power hyperbola limit of SOA | — |
| 11 | $g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}$ | MOSFET transconductance | A/V |
| 12 | $Q_g = \int C_{iss} \, dV_{GS}$ | Total gate charge | C |
| 13 | $I_{G(avg)} = Q_g \cdot f_s$ | Average gate drive current | A |
| 14 | $P_{drive} = Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_s$ | Gate drive power loss | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in KTU numerical problems to compute **converter efficiency** ($\eta = P_{out}/(P_{out} + P_{losses})$), design **heat sinks** ($R_{th} = (T_j - T_a)/P_{loss}$), and select transistors from datasheets.

## 2.5 Comparative Analysis (High-Yield KTU Table)

| Parameter | Power BJT | Power MOSFET | IGBT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drive | Current (β) | Voltage ($V_{GS}$) | Voltage ($V_{GE}$) |
| Input impedance | Low | Very high | Very high |
| ON-state loss | $V_{CE(sat)}$ moderate | $I^2 R_{DS(on)}$ (↑ with V) | $V_{CE(sat)}$ low (~2 V) |
| Switching speed | Slow (storage time) | Very fast (ns) | Moderate (tail current) |
| Voltage rating | High (up to 1500 V) | Low–medium (≤ 1000 V) | High (up to 6500 V) |
| Current rating | High (up to 1000 A) | Medium (up to 200 A) | Very high (up to 3600 A) |
| SOA | Poor (secondary breakdown) | Excellent (no secondary breakdown) | Good (rectangular) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Higher |
| Best suited for | Low-frequency, high-current | High-frequency, low-voltage | Medium-frequency, high-voltage |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Power Loss in a Power Transistor

The **total power loss** in a switching transistor is the sum of **conduction loss** and **switching loss**.

### Step 1 — Conduction Loss
During the ON state, the transistor is in saturation/ohmic region for a fraction $D$ of the switching period (D is the duty ratio).

For an IGBT or BJT:
$$P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C \cdot D$$

For a MOSFET:
$$P_{cond} = I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} \cdot D$$

### Step 2 — Switching Loss (Linearized Triangular Approximation)
Assume $V_{DS}$ and $I_D$ vary linearly during the transition.

During turn-ON, the voltage falls linearly from $V_s$ to 0 while current rises linearly from 0 to $I_D$. The instantaneous power is:

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

The energy dissipated is the integral of $p(t)$ over $t_r$:

$$E_{on} = \int_0^{t_r} \left[V_s \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_r}\right)\right] \cdot \left[I_D \cdot \frac{t}{t_r}\right] dt$$

$$E_{on} = \frac{V_s \cdot I_D}{t_r^2} \int_0^{t_r} t \, dt - \frac{V_s \cdot I_D}{t_r^3} \int_0^{t_r} t^2 \, dt$$

$$E_{on} = \frac{V_s \cdot I_D}{t_r^2} \cdot \frac{t_r^2}{2} - \frac{V_s \cdot I_D}{t_r^3} \cdot \frac{t_r^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_s \cdot I_D \cdot t_r - \frac{1}{3} V_s \cdot I_D \cdot t_r = \frac{1}{6} V_s \cdot I_D \cdot t_r}$$

> Using the **standard linearized approximation** (where voltage and current cross each other linearly from 0 to peak and vice versa), the energy is:

$$E_{on} \approx \frac{1}{2} V_s \cdot I_D \cdot t_r$$

Similarly, turn-off energy:

$$E_{off} \approx \frac{1}{2} V_s \cdot I_D \cdot t_f$$

### Step 3 — Total Switching Power Loss
Since switching happens at frequency $f_s$:

$$P_{sw} = f_s \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off}) = \frac{1}{2} f_s \cdot V_s \cdot I_D \cdot (t_r + t_f)$$

### Step 4 — Total Power Loss

$$\boxed{P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C \cdot D + \frac{1}{2} f_s \cdot V_s \cdot I_D \cdot (t_r + t_f)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that switching loss scales **linearly with frequency**, while conduction loss is independent of frequency. This is why at very high frequencies, switching losses dominate — pushing designers toward **wide-bandgap devices** like SiC and GaN.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gate Drive Power Loss (MOSFET / IGBT)

The gate must be charged and discharged every switching cycle. The energy stored in the gate capacitance $C_{iss}$ at voltage $V_{GS}$ is:

$$E_{gate} = \frac{1}{2} C_{iss} \cdot V_{GS}^2$$

But because the gate is charged and discharged once per cycle (energy is dissipated in the gate resistor each time), the energy dissipated per cycle is:

$$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_{iss} \cdot V_{GS}^2 \quad (\text{charge}) + \frac{1}{2} C_{iss} \cdot V_{GS}^2 \quad (\text{discharge}) = C_{iss} \cdot V_{GS}^2$$

Equivalently using gate charge $Q_g = C_{iss} \cdot V_{GS}$:

$$E_{cycle} = Q_g \cdot V_{GS}$$

The average gate drive power loss:

$$\boxed{P_{drive} = f_s \cdot Q_g \cdot V_{GS}}$$

> This is the **power the gate driver IC must supply continuously** to keep switching the transistor. It is not part of the main power loss in the device, but it determines the size and thermal rating of the driver.

## 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Resistance and Junction Temperature

Heat generated at the junction must flow to the ambient. The thermal equivalent of Ohm's law applies:

$$T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot (R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)})$$

Where:
- $R_{th(j-c)}$ = junction-to-case thermal resistance (°C/W)
- $R_{th(c-s)}$ = case-to-heatsink thermal resistance
- $R_{th(s-a)}$ = heatsink-to-ambient thermal resistance

**Worked Example (KTU Style):**
Given: $P_{loss} = 50$ W, $T_{j(max)} = 150$ °C, $T_a = 40$ °C, $R_{th(j-c)} = 0.5$ °C/W, $R_{th(c-s)} = 0.2$ °C/W.

Find: Required heatsink thermal resistance $R_{th(s-a)}$.

**Solution:**

$$R_{th(JA,total)} = \frac{T_j - T_a}{P_{loss}} = \frac{150 - 40}{50} = 2.2 \text{ °C/W}$$

$$R_{th(s-a)} = R_{th(JA,total)} - R_{th(j-c)} - R_{th(c-s)}$$

$$R_{th(s-a)} = 2.2 - 0.5 - 0.2 = 1.5 \text{ °C/W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always derate $T_{j(max)}$** to ~70%–80% in real designs. Using 125 °C instead of 150 °C doubles the life of the device.

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Past Pattern)

**Problem:** A MOSFET is used in a buck converter switching at $f_s = 100$ kHz. $V_{DD} = 400$ V, $I_D = 10$ A, $R_{DS(on)} = 0.5$ Ω, $t_r = 50$ ns, $t_f = 40$ ns, duty ratio $D = 0.4$. Calculate:
1. Conduction loss
2. Switching loss
3. Total power loss in MOSFET
4. Efficiency for $P_{out} = V_{out} \cdot I_{out} = 160 \text{ V} \cdot 10 \text{ A}$ (assuming ideal conversion)

**Solution:**

**(1) Conduction loss:**
$$P_{cond} = I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} \cdot D = (10)^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.4 = 20 \text{ W}$$

**(2) Switching loss:**
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} f_s \cdot V_{DD} \cdot I_D \cdot (t_r + t_f)$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot 100{,}000 \cdot 400 \cdot 10 \cdot (50 + 40) \cdot 10^{-9}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot 10^5 \cdot 4000 \cdot 90 \cdot 10^{-9}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot 10^5 \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-4} = 18 \text{ W}$$

**(3) Total power loss:**
$$P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 20 + 18 = 38 \text{ W}$$

**(4) Efficiency:**
$$P_{out} = 160 \cdot 10 = 1600 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{total}} = \frac{1600}{1600 + 38} = 97.68\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Structure Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BJT["POWER BJT - VERTICAL NPN"]
        B1["METAL CONTACT - Emitter"]
        B2["n+ Emitter Layer"]
        B3["p Base Layer (thin, heavily doped)"]
        B4["n- Drift Region (lightly doped)"]
        B5["n+ Collector Substrate"]
        B6["METAL CONTACT - Collector"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6
        Bbase["Base Contact - Side Wall"] --- B3
    end

    subgraph MOSFET["POWER MOSFET - VERTICAL DMOS"]
        M1["METAL CONTACT - Source"]
        M2["n+ Source"]
        M3["p Body / Body Diode Region"]
        M4["Gate Oxide - SiO2"]
        M5["n- Epi / Drift Region"]
        M6["n+ Substrate / Drain"]
        M7["METAL CONTACT - Drain (bottom)"]
        M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 --> M6 --> M7
        Mg["Gate Contact - Top"] --- M4
    end

    subgraph IGBT["IGBT - FOUR LAYER STRUCTURE"]
        I1["Gate Contact - Insulated"]
        I2["n+ Emitter (Cathode)"]
        I3["p+ Body"]
        I4["n- Drift Region (wide)"]
        I5["p+ Collector (Anode)"]
        I6["Collector Contact (bottom)"]
        I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 --> I5 --> I6
        Iem["Emitter Contact - Top"] --- I2
    end
```

## 4.2 Switching Waveform and Timing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["t_d: V_BE rises to V_threshold"] --> A2["t_r: I_C rises from 10% to 90%"]
    A2 --> A3["Steady ON: I_C = I_Csat, V_CE = V_CEsat"]
    A3 --> A4["t_s: I_C begins to fall, storage time"]
    A4 --> A5["t_f: I_C falls from 90% to 10%"]
    A5 --> A6["Steady OFF: V_CE = V_supply, I_C = 0"]

    B1["Drive Pulse Applied"] -.controls.-> A1
    B1 -.controls.-> A3
    B1 -.removal.-> A4
```

## 4.3 Gate Drive Circuit Block Architecture (MOSFET / IGBT)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CTRL["Microcontroller / PWM Generator\n3.3 V or 5 V Logic"]
    ISO["Isolation Barrier\nOptocoupler or Gate Drive Transformer"]
    DRV["Gate Driver IC\n(e.g., IR2110, UCC21540)"]
    BOOT["Bootstrap Capacitor / Charge Pump\n(for high-side drive)"]
    PWR["DC Bus Voltage\n+15 V / -5 V / 0 V for gate"]
    DEV["Power Transistor\n(MOSFET / IGBT)"]
    SENSE["Current Sense / Desaturation\nProtection Circuit"]

    CTRL -->|Low voltage PWM| ISO
    ISO -->|Isolated PWM| DRV
    PWR -->|DC supply| DRV
    BOOT -->|High-side supply| DRV
    DRV -->|Gate drive -V_GS| DEV
    SENSE -->|Fault feedback| DRV
    DRV -->|Fault output| CTRL
```

## 4.4 Power Loss Flow Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PIN["P_in - Input Power"]
    POUT["P_out - Output Power"]
    PL["P_loss - Total Power Loss"]
    PCOND["P_cond - Conduction Loss"]
    PSW["P_sw - Switching Loss"]
    PDRV["P_drive - Gate Drive Loss"]
    PSUB["V_CEsat or I2R_DSon - ON-state drop"]
    PTRANS["During t_r and t_f - Transitions"]

    PIN --> POUT
    PIN --> PL
    PL --> PCOND
    PL --> PSW
    PL --> PDRV
    PCOND --> PSUB
    PSW --> PTRANS
```

## 4.5 Safe Operating Area Comparison (Schematic Description)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BJTSOA["POWER BJT SOA - DIAMOND SHAPE"]
        B1["Current Limit - vertical line on right"]
        B2["Power Hyperbola - P_max = V_CE * I_C"]
        B3["Secondary Breakdown - curved line"]
        B4["Voltage Limit - vertical line on left (V_CEO)"]
    end

    subgraph MOSSOA["MOSFET SOA - RECTANGLE"]
        M1["Current Limit - I_Dmax"]
        M2["Power Hyperbola"]
        M3["Voltage Limit - V_DSS"]
        M4["No secondary breakdown - full rectangle"]
    end

    subgraph IGBTSOA["IGBT SOA - RECTANGLE + TAIL"]
        I1["Current Limit - I_Cmax"]
        I2["Power Hyperbola"]
        I3["Voltage Limit - V_CES"]
        I4["Square SOA - superior to BJT"]
    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
**Compare the switching characteristics of Power BJT and Power MOSFET. Why is MOSFET preferred for high-frequency applications?**

**Model Answer:**

Power BJT is a current-controlled device, and its switching is dominated by the **storage time $t_s$** during turn-OFF, caused by minority carrier recombination in the base. Total turn-OFF time $t_{off} = t_s + t_f$ is typically in microseconds.

Power MOSFET is a voltage-controlled device with no storage time (majority carrier device). Switching times are limited only by charging/discharging of input capacitance $C_{iss}$, typically in tens of nanoseconds.

Therefore, MOSFET is preferred for high-frequency operation (hundreds of kHz to MHz), whereas BJT is limited to low frequencies (< 10 kHz). However, BJT has lower conduction drop at high voltages, while MOSFET suffers from high $R_{DS(on)}$ at high voltage ratings.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is Safe Operating Area (SOA) of a power transistor? List the limits defining the SOA of a Power BJT.**

**Model Answer:**

The Safe Operating Area (SOA) is the locus of points in the $V_{CE}$–$I_C$ plane (or $V_{DS}$–$I_D$ for MOSFET) within which the transistor can be operated without suffering permanent damage, for a given duration (DC, pulsed, etc.).

The SOA of a Power BJT is bounded by four limits:
1. **Collector current limit ($I_{C(max)}$)** – bonding wire / chip current capacity.
2. **Collector-emitter voltage limit ($V_{CEO}$)** – avalanche breakdown.
3. **Power dissipation limit** – hyperbola $V_{CE} \cdot I_C = P_{D(max)}$.
4. **Secondary breakdown limit** – current crowding at high voltage causing localized thermal runaway.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often miss the **secondary breakdown** limit. It is unique to BJTs and is the main reason BJTs have a *diamond-shaped* SOA instead of a *rectangle* (as in MOSFETs). Always list all four boundaries for full marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With neat constructional diagram, explain the working of a **Power MOSFET**. Discuss its input and output characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **total power loss** in a power transistor used in a switching converter. How does this loss partition with frequency? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Construction:**
A power MOSFET is built as a vertical n-channel enhancement-mode DMOS (Double-diffused MOS) device. The structure consists of:

- **Source** ($\text{n}^+$ region at the top surface) with a metal contact.
- **Body** (p-region) which forms the intrinsic body diode with the drain.
- **Gate** – a metal layer separated from the body by a thin **$\text{SiO}_2$** insulation layer.
- **Drift region** ($\text{n}^-$ lightly doped) that supports the high blocking voltage.
- **Drain** ($\text{n}^+$ substrate) at the bottom with a metal contact.

**Working:**
1. When $V_{GS} < V_{th}$: no inversion layer exists, the p–n junction between body and drain blocks voltage → MOSFET is **OFF**.
2. When $V_{GS} > V_{th}$: an **n-type inversion channel** forms at the surface of the p-body, connecting source to drift region. Electrons flow from source to drain → MOSFET is **ON**.
3. The ON-state resistance $R_{DS(on)}$ is the sum of: channel resistance + drift region resistance + substrate resistance.

**Input Characteristics ($I_G$ vs $V_{GS}$):**
The gate draws almost no DC current. Only a small leakage current flows for $V_{GS} > V_{th}$. The gate behaves like a capacitor.

**Output Characteristics ($I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$):**
Three regions:
- **Cut-off:** $I_D \approx 0$ for $V_{GS} < V_{th}$.
- **Ohmic (Triode):** $I_D$ rises linearly with $V_{DS}$; $V_{DS} = I_D \cdot R_{DS(on)}$.
- **Saturation (Active):** $I_D$ becomes nearly constant with $V_{DS}$, depending on $V_{GS}$ via $I_D = k(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$.

For power applications, the MOSFET is operated either fully OFF (cut-off) or fully ON (ohmic) — the saturation region is used only for linear amplification.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

The total power loss in a switching transistor consists of three parts:

**(1) Conduction Loss $P_{cond}$:**

When ON, the transistor has a small voltage drop:
- For BJT/IGBT: $V_{CE(sat)}$
- For MOSFET: $I_D \cdot R_{DS(on)}$

$$P_{cond} = V_{ON} \cdot I \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the duty ratio.

**(2) Switching Loss $P_{sw}$:**

During each transition, voltage and current overlap. The energy lost per transition is:

$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_s \cdot I_D \cdot t_r, \quad E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_s \cdot I_D \cdot t_f$$

Total switching power loss:

$$P_{sw} = f_s \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off}) = \frac{1}{2} f_s \cdot V_s \cdot I_D \cdot (t_r + t_f)$$

**(3) Total Power Loss:**

$$\boxed{P_{total} = V_{ON} \cdot I \cdot D + \frac{1}{2} f_s \cdot V_s \cdot I \cdot (t_r + t_f)}$$

**Frequency Partitioning:**
- Conduction loss $P_{cond}$ is **independent** of $f_s$ (depends only on $D$, $V_{ON}$, and $I$).
- Switching loss $P_{sw}$ is **proportional to** $f_s$.
- At low frequency, conduction loss dominates. At high frequency, switching loss dominates.
- There exists an **optimum frequency** for minimum total loss, often limited to ~100 kHz for Si IGBTs (solved by SiC/GaN at MHz).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the construction, working, and **VI characteristics** of an **IGBT**. Why is it called a "voltage-controlled bipolar device"? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power MOSFET has the following ratings: $V_{DSS} = 500$ V, $I_D = 20$ A, $R_{DS(on)} = 0.4$ Ω, $t_r = 60$ ns, $t_f = 50$ ns. It is used in a converter switching at $f_s = 50$ kHz with supply $V_s = 300$ V, output current $I_D = 15$ A, and duty ratio $D = 0.5$. Calculate: **(i)** conduction loss, **(ii)** switching loss, **(iii)** total loss, **(iv)** efficiency if $P_{out} = 2250$ W. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Construction:**
IGBT is a four-layer $\text{p}^+ \text{n}^- \text{p} \text{n}^+$ device. The structure has:
- A **MOS gate** (insulated with $\text{SiO}_2$) on top, similar to a MOSFET.
- A **p$^+$ collector** at the bottom (the anode).
- An **n$^-$ drift region** in the middle, providing the high voltage blocking capability.
- A **p-body** and **n$^+$ emitter** at the top, forming a MOSFET-like channel.
- An equivalent circuit: **MOSFET input** (gate-to-emitter) driving a **p–n–p BJT** output (collector-to-emitter), with a JFET-like pinch resistance in the n$^-$ region.

**Working:**
1. When $V_{GE} < V_{th}$: no channel, no base current to the BJT, IGBT is OFF; $V_{CE}$ is supported by the n$^-$ drift region (forward blocking).
2. When $V_{GE} > V_{th}$ (typically $> 5$ V): an n-channel forms, electrons flow from emitter (n$^+$) through the channel into the n$^-$ region. These electrons act as **base current** for the p$^+$–n$^-$–p BJT, turning it ON. The IGBT conducts with a low $V_{CE(sat)}$ (~2 V).
3. Turn-OFF: gate voltage is removed, channel disappears, but **residual carriers in the n$^-$ drift region** keep the BJT ON for a brief period, creating a **tail current**.

**VI Characteristics:**
- **Forward blocking region:** $V_{GE} < V_{th}$, small leakage current.
- **Forward conduction region:** $V_{CE(sat)}$ drops to ~2 V and is nearly constant with increasing $I_C$ (unlike MOSFET where $V_{DS}$ rises linearly with current).
- **Active region:** For analog use (rare).

**Why "voltage-controlled bipolar device"?**
- The **gate is voltage-controlled** (like MOSFET): essentially zero gate current, high input impedance, easy to drive.
- The **output is bipolar** (like BJT): conduction is due to both electron and hole transport (minority carrier injection from the p$^+$ collector), giving low $V_{CE(sat)}$.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Given:** $V_s = 300$ V, $I_D = 15$ A, $R_{DS(on)} = 0.4$ Ω, $t_r = 60$ ns, $t_f = 50$ ns, $f_s = 50$ kHz, $D = 0.5$, $P_{out} = 2250$ W.

**(i) Conduction Loss:**
$$P_{cond} = I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} \cdot D = (15)^2 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.5 = 225 \cdot 0.2 = 45 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Switching Loss:**
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} f_s \cdot V_s \cdot I_D \cdot (t_r + t_f)$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot 50{,}000 \cdot 300 \cdot 15 \cdot (60 + 50) \cdot 10^{-9}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot 50{,}000 \cdot 4500 \cdot 110 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot 50{,}000 \cdot 4.95 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot 24.75 = 12.375 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Total Power Loss:**
$$P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 45 + 12.375 = 57.375 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{total}} = \frac{2250}{2250 + 57.375} = \frac{2250}{2307.375} = 97.51\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $D$ in conduction loss** for switching converters. Always include duty ratio.
> 2. **Mixing $V_{DSS}$ with $V_s$** — use the *actual* DC supply voltage in the switching loss formula, not the absolute maximum rating.
> 3. **Forgetting the ½ factor** in the linearized switching energy formula.
> 4. **Confusing $R_{DS(on)}$ formula** — for MOSFET use $I^2 R$, but for IGBT/BJT use $V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I$.
> 5. **Not stating the unit clearly** — e.g., writing $t_r$ as 60 instead of $60 \times 10^{-9}$ s — KTU examiners deduct for missing units.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Power Transistors**

- **Power Transistors** are 3-terminal solid-state switches (BJT, MOSFET, IGBT) used in switched-mode power converters.
- **Power BJT:** current-controlled; suffers from **storage time** $t_s$; **diamond-shaped SOA** due to secondary breakdown.
- **Power MOSFET:** voltage-controlled; **majority carrier**; extremely fast (ns); ON-state loss rises sharply with voltage rating due to $I^2 R_{DS(on)}$.
- **IGBT:** combines MOSFET input (high $Z_{in}$) with BJT output (low $V_{CE(sat)}$); **tail current** limits frequency; best for medium-frequency, high-voltage applications.
- **Switching time hierarchy:** MOSFET (ns) < IGBT (100 ns) < BJT (μs).
- **Miller plateau** in MOSFET/IGBT gate waveforms — caused by $C_{gd}$ charging.
- **SOA boundaries** for BJT: current, voltage, power, secondary breakdown. For MOSFET/IGBT: no secondary breakdown → rectangular SOA.
- **Conduction loss** depends on $V_{ON}$ (or $R_{DS(on)}$) and duty ratio $D$.
- **Switching loss** $= \frac{1}{2} f_s V_s I (t_r + t_f)$ — linear in frequency.
- **Gate drive loss** $= f_s Q_g V_{GS}$ — critical for sizing the gate driver IC.
- **Body diode** in MOSFET acts as a freewheeling diode (useful in half-bridge circuits).
- **Heat sink design:** $R_{th(s-a)} = (T_{j(max)} - T_a)/P_{loss} - R_{th(j-c)} - R_{th(c-s)}$.
- **Derate** $T_{j(max)}$ to 70–80% in industrial designs for reliability.
- **Wide-bandgap devices (SiC, GaN)** push switching frequencies to MHz with low losses.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** MOSFET for $V < 250$ V, $f > 100$ kHz; IGBT for $V > 600$ V, $f < 50$ kHz; BJT is largely obsolete in new designs.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Power Transistors**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thyristor Family
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thyristor Family

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Thyristor Family

The **Thyristor Family** is a class of bistable, regenerative semiconductor power devices that exhibit characteristics of both transistors and thyristors, primarily built using a four-layer (P-N-P-N) structure with at least three P-N junctions. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this family includes a wide spectrum of power semiconductor switches — ranging from the basic **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)**, the most fundamental member, to more advanced, fully controllable devices like the **Gate Turn-Off (GTO) thyristor**, **Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor (IGBT)**, **MOS-Controlled Thyristor (MCT)**, **Integrated Gate-Commutated Thyristor (IGCT)**, **Triode for AC (TRIAC)**, **Silicon Controlled Switch (SCS)**, **Light-Activated SCR (LASCR)**, **Programmable Unijunction Transistor (PUT)**, and the **DIAC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The "Thyristor Family" is a foundational topic in **Module 1** of PEICT411. Students are expected to draw the **two-transistor analogy**, identify **VI characteristics**, and clearly differentiate between **half-wave-controlled** (single quadrant) and **fully-controlled / bidirectional** (multi-quadrant) members.

These devices are characterized by high voltage and current handling capability, low ON-state conduction loss, and high input impedance. They form the **backbone of modern power electronic converters** — used extensively in motor drives, HVDC transmission, UPS systems, induction heating, and SMPS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap** with a **special locking mechanism**:
- The **water pipe** is the main anode-to-cathode path carrying large current (high power).
- The **small knob** (gate) only needs a tiny flick to either **unlock** (turn ON) or in some devices, **lock back** (turn OFF) the main flow.
- Once water (current) starts flowing, it cannot be stopped just by closing the knob — the water pressure itself keeps it open (this is called **latching**).
- In a basic SCR, you can open the tap with the knob, but to close it, you have to shut off the main water supply from the source (line commutation).
- In a smarter version (GTO/IGBT), the knob itself has the power to **shut the main flow** directly (self-commutated device).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **fire alarm latch system** in a building. Once a smoke detector (gate trigger) sounds, the magnetic fire doors (SCR) swing open and lock. They stay open (latch) until the fire department physically resets the system (forced commutation). Advanced devices are like smart doors that can close automatically once the smoke clears (GTO/IGBT).

## 1.3 Classification Overview

The thyristor family can be classified based on **controllability and current direction**:

| Sub-Family | Members | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Unidirectional, Half-Controlled** | SCR, LASCR, SCS | Turn-ON by gate, turn-OFF by commutation circuit only |
| **Bidirectional, Half-Controlled** | TRIAC, DIAC | Conducts in both directions, gate-controlled |
| **Unidirectional, Fully-Controlled** | GTO, IGBT, MCT, IGCT, ETO | Gate can both turn ON and turn OFF |
| **Trigger / Layered Devices** | PUT, SUS, SBS | Used in oscillator and triggering circuits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics** for the family typically span:
> - **Voltage Rating:** **200 V to 12 kV** (modern IGCTs reach up to 10 kV single device)
> - **Current Rating:** **1 A (small signal) to 6000 A** (HVDC-grade phase-controlled thyristors)
> - **Switching Frequency:** **Line frequency (50/60 Hz) for SCRs** up to **20–100 kHz for IGBTs**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Transistor Analogy of an SCR (Core KTU Concept)

Every P-N-P-N thyristor (SCR being the prototype) can be modeled as a complementary pair of interconnected bipolar transistors:

- A **P-N-P transistor** ($Q_1$) with emitter = Anode (A), base = N-gate layer, collector = Cathode-side P-layer.
- An **N-P-N transistor** ($Q_2$) with emitter = Cathode (K), base = P-gate layer, collector = Anode-side N-layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The internal **regenerative feedback loop** is the *fundamental reason* a thyristor latches ON. Once both transistors enter saturation, each supplies base current to the other — creating a self-sustaining latch that **cannot be stopped by removing the gate signal**.

## 2.2 Operational Logic Steps (Step-by-Step)

1. **Forward Blocking Mode (OFF State):** Anode is positive with respect to cathode, but gate is open. Only a small **leakage current ($I_{DR}$)** flows. Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are forward-biased, while $J_2$ is reverse-biased — it supports the full forward voltage.
2. **Triggering / Turn-ON:** A positive gate pulse $I_G$ injects carriers into the P-base of $Q_2$. This raises the collector current of $Q_2$ ($I_{C2}$), which in turn becomes the base current of $Q_1$. $Q_1$ now supplies more base current to $Q_2$. This positive feedback is **regenerative**.
3. **Latching Current ($I_L$):** The minimum anode current required to sustain the ON state even after the gate signal is removed. The thyristor must reach $I_A \geq I_L$ during turn-ON, otherwise it reverts to the OFF state.
4. **Holding Current ($I_H$):** The minimum anode current required to keep the device in the ON state. $I_H < I_L$ (typically $I_H \approx 0.2$ to $0.3 \times I_L$).
5. **Conduction Mode (ON State):** All three junctions are forward-biased. The device behaves like a closed switch with a small forward voltage drop ($V_T \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$ V for standard SCRs).
6. **Turn-OFF (Commutation):** For an SCR, the anode current must be forced below $I_H$ for a sufficient duration (called the **circuit commutation turn-off time, $t_q$**). The device then regains forward blocking capability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This four-layer regenerative structure is what makes thyristors the workhorse of high-power AC-DC converters. In HVDC transmission (e.g., the ±800 kV Raigarh-Pugalur line in India), phase-controlled thyristor valves handle **gigawatts of power** — a feat impossible for traditional transistors due to thermal limitations.

## 2.3 Member-by-Member Analysis

### A. SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier)
- **Three terminals:** Anode (A), Cathode (K), Gate (G).
- **Unidirectional**, **half-controlled**: Gate turns ON, external commutation turns OFF.
- **Applications:** Controlled rectifiers, AC voltage regulators, cycloconverters, DC drives.

### B. TRIAC (Triode for AC)
- **Three terminals:** MT1 (Main Terminal 1), MT2 (Main Terminal 2), Gate (G).
- **Equivalent to two SCRs in anti-parallel** — conducts in **both half-cycles** of an AC waveform.
- Operates in **four modes** depending on polarity of MT2 and gate signal.
- **Applications:** Light dimmers, fan regulators, AC motor soft starters, heater control.

### C. GTO (Gate Turn-Off Thyristor)
- A modified SCR with the ability to **turn OFF via a large negative gate pulse** ($I_{GR} \approx 1/3$ to $1/5$ of $I_A$).
- Requires a **snubber circuit** (RCD) to limit $dv/dt$ and $dI/dt$ during switching.
- **Applications:** Traction drives, AC drives, high-power inverters.

### D. IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor)
- Combines the **high input impedance of a MOSFET** with the **low ON-state loss of a BJT**.
- Voltage-controlled device; gate is **insulated (MOS structure)**.
- Available from 600 V to 6.5 kV; currents up to 3600 A.
- **Applications:** Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), solar inverters, electric vehicle powertrains, UPS.

### E. MCT (MOS-Controlled Thyristor)
- A **thyristor with two MOSFETs integrated** into its gate structure — one for turn-ON (N-channel) and one for turn-OFF (P-channel).
- Ideal for **ultra-high current** applications but has limited commercial availability.

### F. IGCT (Integrated Gate-Commutated Thyristor)
- A **GTO with an integrated gate driver** mounted directly on the wafer.
- Extremely fast switching — turns OFF in **microseconds**, comparable to IGBT.
- Used in **medium-voltage drives** (e.g., ABB ACS6000).

### G. Other Members
- **LASCR (Light-Activated SCR):** Triggered by light pulses; used in HVDC, static VAR compensators, and high-voltage optocouplers.
- **SCS (Silicon Controlled Switch):** Has both anode and cathode gate terminals; used in low-power switching.
- **PUT (Programmable Unijunction Transistor):** Anode-gate programmable equivalent of UJT; used in relaxation oscillators, timers.
- **DIAC (Diode for AC):** A bidirectional trigger diode; works like two zener diodes in anti-series. Used as a **trigger device for TRIACs** in dimmer circuits.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_A + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$ | Anode current equation | From two-transistor model; $I_{CO}$ = leakage current, $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ = CB current gains |
| 2 | Turn-ON condition | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ | Triggering criterion; sum of common-base gains must reach 1 |
| 3 | Latching Current | $I_L$ | Min. anode current to retain ON after gate removal (mA to A range) |
| 4 | Holding Current | $I_H$ | Min. anode current to maintain ON state ($I_H < I_L$) |
| 5 | Breakover Voltage | $V_{BO}$ | Forward voltage at which device turns ON without gate (V) |
| 6 | Circuit Commutation Turn-off Time | $t_q$ | Time for device to regain forward blocking (µs) |
| 7 | Critical $dV/dt$ | $dV/dt_{crit}$ | Max. rate of rise of voltage; if exceeded → **false triggering** (V/µs) |
| 8 | Critical $dI/dt$ | $dI/dt_{crit}$ | Max. rate of rise of current; exceeding it destroys the device (A/µs) |
| 9 | ON-state voltage drop | $V_T$ | ~1.5 – 2.5 V for SCR; ~1.7 – 3 V for IGBT |
| 10 | TRIAC firing angle | $\alpha$ | Conduction delay angle in each half-cycle (degrees or radians) |
| 11 | RMS Output (TRIAC dimmer) | $V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | For AC voltage controller with resistive load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** For the **TRIAC dimmer formula**, $\alpha$ is the firing angle in **radians** when used directly in the equation. If degrees are used, convert using $\alpha_{rad} = \alpha_{deg} \times \pi / 180$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Two-Transistor Anode Current Equation

This is a **high-yield derivation** that KTU examiners frequently ask for full marks. We derive the anode current of an SCR in terms of transistor parameters.

### Given:
- P-N-P transistor ($Q_1$) with common-base current gain $\alpha_1$
- N-P-N transistor ($Q_2$) with common-base current gain $\alpha_2$
- Reverse leakage current of the middle junction $J_2$ is $I_{CO}$
- Gate current is $I_G$

### Step 1: Write the collector currents
The collector current of $Q_1$ (N-P-N collector) is:
$$I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CO1}$$
The collector current of $Q_2$ (P-N-P collector) is:
$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_K + I_{CO2}$$

> *Logic:* The collector current of a transistor equals (gain × emitter current) plus the leakage current of the reverse-biased collector-base junction.

### Step 2: Apply KCL at the cathode
Since $I_{C1}$ is the only base current supplied to $Q_2$ (other than the gate), the total cathode current is:
$$I_K = I_A + I_G$$

> *Logic:* By Kirchhoff's Current Law, the current entering the cathode node equals the sum of anode current and the externally injected gate current.

### Step 3: Apply KCL at the anode
The total anode current $I_A$ entering the device equals the sum of the two collector currents:
$$I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2}$$

> *Logic:* All leakage and transistor currents in the four-layer structure combine at the anode terminal.

### Step 4: Substitute and simplify
Substituting the collector current expressions:
$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CO1} + \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CO2}$$

Let $I_{CO} = I_{CO1} + I_{CO2}$ (combined leakage):
$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_A + \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}$$

Rearranging:
$$I_A - \alpha_1 I_A - \alpha_2 I_A = \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}$$

$$I_A \left[1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)\right] = \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}$$

### Final Result:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_A &= \frac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* As the denominator $1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \to 0$, the anode current $I_A$ tends to infinity (in practice, it is limited by the external load). This represents the **triggering condition**: $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$ implies the regenerative feedback has become self-sustaining.

## 3.2 Derivation: TRIAC RMS Output Voltage (AC Voltage Controller with R-Load)

For a TRIAC-based single-phase AC voltage controller feeding a purely resistive load:

### Given:
- Source voltage: $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- TRIAC is fired at angle $\alpha$ in **each half-cycle**
- Conduction occurs from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi$ in the positive half, and from $\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi$ in the negative half.

### Step 1: Define the output voltage
$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t) & \text{for } \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi \\ 0 & \text{for } 0 \leq \omega t \leq \alpha \end{cases}$$

### Step 2: Write the RMS expression
The general RMS formula over a full cycle of period $T$:
$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_o^2(t) \, dt$$

Because of symmetry, integrate over half a cycle and multiply by 2:
$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} (V_m \sin \omega t)^2 \, d(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the integral
Using the identity $\sin^2 \theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{rms}^2 &= \frac{2 V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos 2\omega t}{2} \, d(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin 2\omega t}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} \\
&= \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \left[ \left(\pi - \frac{\sin 2\pi}{2}\right) - \left(\alpha - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right) \right] \\
&= \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{rms} &= \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{ \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right) } \\
&= V_s \sqrt{ \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right) }
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V_s = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ is the source RMS voltage.

> *Engineering Utility:* This equation allows designers to compute the RMS output voltage for a given firing angle $\alpha$. It is the basis of every domestic light dimmer and fan regulator.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Transistor Analogy Model of an SCR

This block diagram shows the **regenerative feedback loop** of an SCR modeled as a complementary P-N-P / N-P-N transistor pair.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Anode Terminal A] --> nodeA1[Anode P layer]
    nodeA1 --> nodeJ1[Junction J1]
    nodeJ1 --> nodeN1[N1 layer]
    nodeN1 --> nodeJ2[Junction J2 - Reverse Biased in OFF]
    nodeJ2 --> nodeP2[P2 layer - Gate layer]
    nodeP2 --> nodeJ3[Junction J3]
    nodeJ3 --> nodeK[Cathode N2 layer]
    nodeK --> K[Cathode Terminal K]
    G[Gate Terminal G] --> nodeP2

    nodeA1 -.-> Q1[P-N-P Transistor Q1]
    Q1 -. alpha1 .-> nodeP2
    nodeP2 -.-> Q2[N-P-N Transistor Q2]
    Q2 -. alpha2 .-> nodeK
    Q1 -- I_C1 base drive --> Q2
    Q2 -- I_C2 base drive --> Q1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The dotted arrows ($\alpha_1$, $\alpha_2$) indicate the **regenerative base-current feedback** between the two equivalent transistors. The solid arrows show the **physical junction traversal** of current from anode to cathode.

## 4.2 VI Characteristics of an SCR

The VI curve is plotted with $V_{AK}$ on the X-axis and $I_A$ on the Y-axis. Three quadrants are relevant.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Q1[Quadrant I: Forward Conduction]
        VAK1[Forward Voltage increasing] --> BO[Breakover at V_BO]
        BO --> LC[Latching Current I_L]
        LC --> HC[ON State, low V_T, high I_A]
    end
    subgraph Q3[Quadrant III: Reverse Blocking]
        RV[Reverse Voltage applied] --> RBO[Reverse Breakover V_RB]
        RBO --> RD[Reverse Breakdown]
    end
    subgraph Q2[Quadrant II: Reverse Conduction Not Allowed]
        NO[No Operation - Reverse direction]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Convention:** Label three key points on Quadrant I: (1) **Forward blocking region** below $V_{BO}$, (2) **Breakover point** at $V_{BO}$, (3) **ON state** with slope $1/r_T$ where $r_T$ is the slope resistance. The **latching** and **holding currents** must be marked clearly on the Y-axis.

## 4.3 TRIAC Operating Modes (Four Quadrants)

The TRIAC can be triggered in **four modes** depending on the polarity of MT2 and the gate signal.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MODE1[Mode I: MT2 +, G +]
        M1[MT2 positive, Gate positive - Most sensitive]
    end
    subgraph MODE2[Mode II: MT2 +, G -]
        M2[MT2 positive, Gate negative]
    end
    subgraph MODE3[Mode III: MT2 -, G +]
        M3[MT2 negative, Gate positive]
    end
    subgraph MODE4[Mode IV: MT2 -, G -]
        M4[MT2 negative, Gate negative]
    end

    MODE1 --> OPER[Equivalent to SCR1 firing quadrant I]
    MODE2 --> OPER2[Equivalent to SCR1 firing quadrant II]
    MODE3 --> OPER3[Equivalent to SCR2 firing quadrant III]
    MODE4 --> OPER4[Equivalent to SCR2 firing quadrant IV]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** **Mode I is the most sensitive** triggering mode of a TRIAC. KTU questions often ask students to rank the four modes by sensitivity.

## 4.4 IGBT Block Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    G[Gate insulated with SiO2] --> MOS[MOSFET input stage]
    MOS --> DRV[Drives base of BJT output stage]
    DRV --> BJT[BJT - main power handling]
    BJT --> COL[Collector terminal C - High voltage]
    BJT --> EM[Emitter terminal E - Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The IGBT combines a **MOSFET** (which provides high input impedance — easy to drive) with a **BJT** (which provides low conduction drop — efficient power handling). This is why it dominates medium-to-high power switching.

## 4.5 Thyristor Family Classification Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    TF[Thyristor Family] --> UHC[Unidirectional Half-Controlled]
    TF --> BHC[Bidirectional Half-Controlled]
    TF --> UFC[Unidirectional Fully-Controlled]
    TF --> TRG[Trigger Devices]

    UHC --> SCR1[SCR]
    UHC --> LASCR1[LASCR]
    UHC --> SCS1[SCS]

    BHC --> TRIAC1[TRIAC]
    BHC --> DIAC1[DIAC]

    UFC --> GTO1[GTO]
    UFC --> IGBT1[IGBT]
    UFC --> MCT1[MCT]
    UFC --> IGCT1[IGCT]

    TRG --> PUT1[PUT]
    TRG --> SUS1[SUS]
    TRG --> SBS1[SBS]
```
