<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> **Define the term "latching current" of an SCR. How is it different from the holding current?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Latching Current ($I_L$):** It is the **minimum anode current** that must flow through the SCR during turn-ON so that the device continues to remain in the ON state even **after the gate trigger signal is removed**. If the anode current is less than $I_L$ at the moment the gate pulse is withdrawn, the SCR will revert to the OFF state. (1.5 marks)
- **Holding Current ($I_H$):** It is the **minimum anode current** required to **maintain** the SCR in the ON state once it is already conducting. If the anode current falls below $I_H$, the SCR turns OFF. (1 mark)
- **Key Difference:** $I_L > I_H$. For a typical SCR, $I_H$ is approximately **20% to 30% of $I_L$**. (0.5 marks)

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
> **Why is an IGBT preferred over a BJT and a power MOSFET in medium-power applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **vs. BJT:** BJT is current-controlled and requires continuous base current, leading to higher drive losses. IGBT is **voltage-controlled** (MOS gate), so the gate drive power is negligible. (1 mark)
- **vs. Power MOSFET:** MOSFET has excellent switching speed but suffers from high ON-state resistance $R_{DS(on)}$ at high voltages (conduction loss is very high above 200 V). IGBT has a **BJT output stage** that provides very low saturation voltage $V_{CE(sat)}$, giving better efficiency at high voltages. (1 mark)
- **Net Result:** IGBT combines the **easy drive of a MOSFET** with the **low conduction loss of a BJT** — making it the workhorse of **motor drives, inverters, and SMPS** in the 600 V – 6.5 kV range. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat two-transistor equivalent model, explain the **working of an SCR** in the forward blocking, forward conduction, and reverse blocking modes. Derive the anode current equation and state the **turn-ON condition**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the **SCR, TRIAC, and GTO** in terms of triggering, commutation, current/voltage ratings, and applications. Draw the VI characteristics of a TRIAC. (7 marks)

### Model Solution (A)

#### (a) SCR Working + Derivation — 7 marks

**1. Two-Transistor Model (1.5 marks):**
- The four-layer P-N-P-N structure of an SCR is split into two interconnected transistors: **P-N-P ($Q_1$)** and **N-P-N ($Q_2$)**.
- The middle N-layer is the base of $Q_1$ and part of the collector of $Q_2$.
- The middle P-layer is the base of $Q_2$ and part of the collector of $Q_1$.
- The **collector of $Q_1$ is tied to the base of $Q_2$**, and the **collector of $Q_2$ is tied to the base of $Q_1$** — forming a **regenerative feedback loop**.

**2. Forward Blocking Mode (1.5 marks):**
- Anode is positive, cathode is negative, gate is open ($I_G = 0$).
- Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are forward-biased; $J_2$ is reverse-biased.
- A small leakage current $I_{DR}$ flows (≈ mA). The device supports the full forward voltage.
- The condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 < 1$ holds.

**3. Forward Conduction Mode (1.5 marks):**
- A positive gate pulse $I_G$ is applied.
- This increases the emitter current of $Q_2$ → raises $I_{C2}$ → this becomes base current of $Q_1$ → increases $I_{C1}$ → becomes base current of $Q_2$.
- **Regenerative feedback** — both transistors rapidly enter saturation.
- Condition: $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$. All junctions are forward-biased, and the device conducts with a small drop of 1.5 – 2.5 V.

**4. Anode Current Equation (2 marks):**
- As derived in **Section 3.1**:
$$I_A = \frac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$
- **Turn-ON condition:** When the denominator $\to 0$, i.e., $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$, the anode current becomes very large → the device switches ON. This is the **triggering criterion**.

**5. Reverse Blocking Mode (0.5 mark):**
- Anode is negative, cathode is positive. Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are reverse-biased.
- Only a small reverse leakage $I_{RR}$ flows until the **reverse breakover voltage $V_{RBO}$** is reached, after which the device breaks down (avalanche).

#### (b) Comparison Table + TRIAC VI — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | SCR | TRIAC | GTO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Unidirectional | Bidirectional | Unidirectional |
| **Triggering** | Positive gate pulse | Gate in 4 quadrants | Positive gate pulse (ON); negative gate pulse (OFF) |
| **Commutation** | External (line/forced) | External | Gate-controlled (self-commutated) |
| **Turn-OFF method** | Anode current < $I_H$ | Anode current < $I_H$ in each half-cycle | Large negative gate pulse |
| **Voltage Rating** | Up to 12 kV | Up to 1.6 kV | Up to 6.5 kV |
| **Current Rating** | Up to 6000 A | Up to 200 A | Up to 6000 A |
| **Switching Freq.** | 50/60 Hz (line) | 50/60 Hz typical | 1 – 2 kHz |
| **Applications** | HVDC, rectifiers, DC drives | AC dimmers, fan regulators, heater control | Traction drives, AC VFDs |

**VI Characteristics of TRIAC (2 marks):**
- Plotted in **Quadrants I and III** (since it is bidirectional).
- **Quadrant I:** MT2 positive → conducts in the forward direction. Firing modes are I (MT2+, G+) and II (MT2+, G−).
- **Quadrant III:** MT2 negative → conducts in the reverse direction. Firing modes are III (MT2−, G+) and IV (MT2−, G−).
- **Key features:** Breakover voltage $V_{BO1}$ in Q1, $V_{BO3}$ in Q3; on-state voltage $V_T$ (typically ±1.5 V); holding current $I_H$ in both directions.
- **Mode I is the most sensitive** (requires lowest gate current).

**Use of DIAC in TRIAC circuits (2 marks):**
- The DIAC is a **bidirectional trigger diode** with a **breakover voltage of ~30 V**.
- It is connected in series with the gate of the TRIAC in light-dimmer circuits to provide **symmetrical triggering** in both half-cycles.
- Without the DIAC, the TRIAC exhibits **hysteresis** and fires at different angles in positive and negative half-cycles, causing DC offset and DC motor "cogging".
- The DIAC's **negative resistance region** provides a **sharp, fast-rising gate pulse** — improving the $dV/dt$ immunity and ensuring **symmetric, jitter-free triggering**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the **VI characteristics of an IGBT** and explain its **turn-ON and turn-OFF mechanisms** with a circuit diagram. Mention its advantages over power BJT and MOSFET. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram and waveform, explain the operation of a **single-phase full-wave AC voltage controller using a TRIAC** feeding an R-load. Derive the expression for RMS output voltage. (7 marks)

### Model Solution (B)

#### (a) IGBT Characteristics + Turn-ON/OFF — 7 marks

**1. IGBT VI Characteristics (2 marks):**
- The output $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ characteristics resemble those of a **BJT** (with active and saturation regions).
- The transfer characteristic ($I_C$ vs $V_{GE}$) is similar to a **MOSFET** — it is a **voltage-controlled** device.
- **Active region:** $I_C = g_m \cdot (V_{GE} - V_{GE(th)})$.
- **Saturation region:** The IGBT drops $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.7$ to $3$ V.

**2. Turn-ON Mechanism (1.5 marks):**
- A positive gate-emitter voltage $V_{GE}$ above the **threshold voltage $V_{GE(th)}$** (typically 4 – 6 V) creates an **inversion layer (N-channel)** in the P-body region.
- This channel allows electrons to flow from the N+ emitter to the N− drift region.
- The accumulation of electrons in the N− drift region causes **hole injection from the P+ substrate (collector)**, which is the **BJT action**.
- The device is now ON with low saturation voltage.

**3. Turn-OFF Mechanism (1.5 marks):**
- Removing $V_{GE}$ removes the inversion layer → electron flow stops.
- The excess carriers in the N− drift region recombine over the **tail current time** $t_{off}$ (typically 0.1 – 1 µs).
- This **tail current** is the only drawback of IGBTs — it causes switching losses at high frequencies.

**4. Advantages (2 marks):**
- **Over BJT:** Voltage drive (no gate current), no second breakdown, easier paralleling.
- **Over MOSFET:** Lower conduction loss at high voltages; smaller chip size for same rating.
- **Combined:** Easy drive + low loss → best of both worlds in the **600 V – 1700 V** range.

#### (b) TRIAC AC Voltage Controller — 7 marks

**1. Circuit Diagram (1 mark):**
The single-phase AC voltage controller using a TRIAC has:
- **Source** $v_s = V_m \sin \omega t$ connected between MT1 and MT2 of the TRIAC through the R-load.
- **Gate trigger circuit** — typically a **DIAC + RC timing network** connected between MT1 and gate.
- When $v_s$ charges the capacitor $C$ through $R$, the DIAC fires at a preset firing angle $\alpha$ and delivers a gate pulse to the TRIAC.

**2. Operation (3 marks):**
- During the **positive half-cycle** (MT2 positive with respect to MT1), the capacitor charges towards the peak. When its voltage reaches the DIAC breakover voltage (~30 V), the DIAC fires, dumping current into the TRIAC gate — TRIAC turns ON.
- The TRIAC then conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi$.
- During the **negative half-cycle** (MT2 negative), the capacitor charges with **reversed polarity**. At $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$, the DIAC again fires (this time in the opposite direction) — TRIAC turns ON in the reverse direction.
- Conduction: $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi$.
- Thus the load sees an **AC chopped waveform** — symmetrical in both half-cycles.

**3. Waveforms (1 mark):**
- Draw $v_s(t)$, gate pulses at $\alpha$ and $\pi + \alpha$, load voltage $v_o(t)$ (chopped sinusoid), load current $i_o(t)$ (in phase with $v_o$ since R-load).
- Show that conduction starts at $\alpha$ in each half-cycle and ends at $\pi$ and $2\pi$ respectively.

**4. RMS Voltage Derivation (2 marks):**
- As derived in **Section 3.2**:
$$V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$$
- At $\alpha = 0$: $V_{rms} = V_s$ (full conduction).
- At $\alpha = \pi$: $V_{rms} = 0$ (no conduction).
- The expression is used to design the firing circuit for a desired RMS output.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mark $I_L$ and $I_H$** on the VI characteristic — KTU deducts **1 mark** if these are missing.
> 2. **Confusing the order:** $I_L$ is **always greater** than $I_H$. Reversing this loses a mark.
> 3. **Two-transistor model:** Students often forget to label the **gate current direction**. The gate pulse for turn-ON must be **positive (with respect to cathode)**.
> 4. **TRIAC modes:** Students frequently claim all four modes are equally sensitive — **Mode I is the most sensitive**; failure to state this costs marks.
> 5. **IGBT:** Many students wrongly say "IGBT is current-controlled" — it is a **voltage-controlled device** with a **MOS gate**.
> 6. **RMS formula:** $\alpha$ must be in **radians** if inserted directly into the formula. Otherwise, convert first.
> 7. **DIAC confusion:** DIAC is a **trigger device**, not a power controller. It never carries the main load current.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- 🔹 **SCR = Silicon Controlled Rectifier** = 4-layer P-N-P-N, **3 terminals** (A, K, G), **unidirectional**, **half-controlled** (gate turns ON, commutation turns OFF).
- 🔹 **Two-transistor model:** $Q_1$ = P-N-P, $Q_2$ = N-P-N. **Regenerative feedback** is the key to latching.
- 🔹 **Anode current equation:** $I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$.
- 🔹 **Turn-ON condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$.
- 🔹 **$I_L$ (latching) > $I_H$ (holding)** — must be marked on the VI curve.
- 🔹 **Breakover voltage $V_{BO}$** is the forward voltage at which the SCR turns ON **without gate signal** (unwanted, must be avoided).
- 🔹 **Critical $dV/dt$** causes **false triggering**; a **snubber RC** is used to limit it.
- 🔹 **Critical $dI/dt$** causes **local hot-spot damage**; mitigated by an **inductive snubber** or a **series reactor**.
- 🔹 **TRIAC = 2 SCRs in anti-parallel** — **3 terminals** (MT1, MT2, G) — **bidirectional**.
- 🔹 **TRIAC operating modes:** **Mode I (MT2+, G+) is the most sensitive** triggering mode.
- 🔹 **DIAC** is a **bidirectional trigger diode** used in TRIAC dimmer circuits to give **symmetric, sharp gate pulses**.
- 🔹 **GTO** is a **fully-controlled** thyristor — turn-ON by positive gate pulse, turn-OFF by **large negative gate pulse** ($I_{GR} \approx 1/3 I_A$).
- 🔹 **IGBT = MOSFET input + BJT output** — voltage-controlled, low conduction loss, **600 V – 6.5 kV**, dominant in motor drives.
- 🔹 **IGBT tail current** is its main drawback — limits switching frequency.
- 🔹 **IGCT** = GTO with **integrated gate driver** on the wafer — switches in microseconds.
- 🔹 **MCT** = thyristor with two integrated MOSFETs (one for ON, one for OFF).
- 🔹 **LASCR** triggered by **light pulses** — used in HVDC valves and high-voltage optocouplers.
- 🔹 **SCS (Silicon Controlled Switch)** has **both anode and cathode gates** — used in low-power digital/analog switching.
- 🔹 **PUT (Programmable UJT)** has externally programmable $\eta$ and $R_{BB}$ — used in relaxation oscillators, timer circuits, SCR trigger circuits.
- 🔹 **TRIAC dimmer RMS output:** $V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$.
- 🔹 **Standard ratings:** SCRs reach 12 kV / 6000 A; IGBTs reach 6.5 kV / 3600 A; GTOs reach 6.5 kV / 6000 A.
- 🔹 **Applications snapshot:** SCRs → HVDC, controlled rectifiers, cycloconverters. TRIAC → AC dimmers, fan regulators. IGBT → VFDs, solar inverters, EV powertrains. GTO/IGCT → Traction, MV drives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCRs
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** SCRs

# SCRs — Silicon Controlled Rectifiers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)** is a four-layer (PNPN), three-junction (J1, J2, J3), three-terminal (Anode **A**, Cathode **K**, Gate **G**) bipolar semiconductor power device belonging to the *thyristor* family. It is a *latching*, bistable switch that conducts current in only one direction (anode to cathode) when triggered by a small gate pulse, and continues to conduct as long as the forward anode current remains above a critical minimum level called the **holding current ($I_H$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The SCR is the *mother device* of the thyristor family. Mastery of its construction, two-transistor model, static/dynamic V-I characteristics, triggering and commutation methods is **mandatory** for the ESE.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **water tap with a mechanical latch**:

- The **main valve** is closed (SCR OFF) — no water (current) flows, no matter how much pressure (anode voltage) is applied.
- You press a small **trigger button** (gate pulse) — the latch engages, and the main valve **snaps open** permanently (SCR ON).
- The valve will **not close** even if you release the button, **as long as water keeps flowing** (current $\geq I_H$).
- Only when the water flow is reduced to a **trickle** (current $< I_H$) does the latch release and the valve close.

This "press once, it latches, it stays on until flow is interrupted" behavior is the essence of an SCR.

### 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Device Symbol:** Conventional SCR has anode (A), cathode (K), and gate (G). The arrow points from anode to cathode.
- **Standard Ratings Range (commercial SCRs):**
  - Voltage: **$V_{DRM} / V_{RRM}$ = 600 V to 6500 V**
  - Current: **$I_{T(AV)}$ = 1 A to 5000 A**
  - Gate trigger voltage: **$V_{GT} \approx$ 0.8 V to 3 V**
  - Gate trigger current: **$I_{GT} \approx$ 1 mA to 200 mA**

> [!NOTE]  
> **Cross-Reference to Power Electronics:** SCR is used in controlled rectifiers (single-phase/three-phase), AC voltage controllers, cycloconverters, choppers, and inverters. It is the workhorse of HVDC and DC motor drives.

### 1.4 Layered Structure (Recap for KTU)

The SCR has four doped layers, conventionally labelled $P_1 - N_1 - P_2 - N_2$, with the following electrode connections:

| Layer | Function | Terminal |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $P_1$ | Anode region | Anode (A) |
| $N_1$ | Drift region (thick, lightly doped) | — |
| $P_2$ | Gate region | Gate (G) |
| $N_2$ | Cathode region | Cathode (K) |

The three junctions are:
- **$J_1$** — between $P_1$ and $N_1$
- **$J_2$** — between $N_1$ and $P_2$ (this is the *gate* junction)
- **$J_3$** — between $P_2$ and $N_2$

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modes of Operation (4 Quadrants)

The SCR exhibits three principal operating modes, depending on the biasing of the three junctions:

| Mode | Bias of $J_1$ | Bias of $J_2$ | Bias of $J_3$ | Condition |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Reverse Blocking** | Reverse | Forward | Reverse | $V_{AK} < 0$ |
| **Forward Blocking** | Forward | Reverse | Forward | $V_{AK} > 0$, $I_G = 0$, $V < V_{BO}$ |
| **Forward Conduction (ON)** | Forward | Forward | Forward | $V_{AK} > 0$ + adequate $I_G$ OR $V > V_{BO}$ |

> [!NOTE]  
> In **forward blocking**, the middle junction $J_2$ is reverse-biased. The depletion region at $J_2$ widens and blocks conduction. A small leakage current ($I_{DR}$ or $I_{RR}$) flows.

### 2.2 Two-Transistor Analogy of the SCR

This is a **KTU favourite question** and a critical analytical model.

The four-layer $P_1 N_1 P_2 N_2$ structure can be visualized as **two interconnected complementary transistors**:
- **Transistor $T_1$:** $P_1 N_1 P_2$ → **PNP**
- **Transistor $T_2$:** $N_1 P_2 N_2$ → **NPN**

The connection points are:
- Anode **A** = Emitter of $T_1$ = Collector of $T_2$
- Cathode **K** = Emitter of $T_2$ = Collector of $T_1$
- Gate **G** = Base of $T_2$ = Collector of $T_1$ (approximately)
- The $N_1$ layer serves as the base of $T_1$ and is **internally connected** to the collector of $T_2$

**Why this works:** Each transistor is biased by the leakage/collector current of the other, creating a **positive feedback (regenerative) loop** once conduction begins.

### 2.3 Operating Principle — The Regenerative Loop

When a positive gate current $I_G$ is applied:
1. $T_2$ turns ON → Collector current of $T_2$ (= $I_{C2}$) flows through the base of $T_1$.
2. $T_1$ turns ON → Collector current of $T_1$ (= $I_{C1}$) flows through the base of $T_2$, supplementing $I_G$.
3. The combined base drive for $T_2$ becomes **$I_G + I_{C1}$**, which is greater than $I_G$.
4. $T_2$'s collector current rises → drives $T_1$ harder → $T_1$'s collector current rises → drives $T_2$ harder.
5. This **avalanche-like (regenerative) growth** saturates both transistors, latching the SCR ON.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **latching condition** for this regenerative feedback to be self-sustaining is: $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$. Once this condition is met, the gate pulse can be removed and the SCR remains ON.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Anode current (two-transistor model) | $I_A = \dfrac{I_{C1} + \alpha_2 I_{C2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$ | $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ are CB current gains of $T_1, T_2$ |
| 2 | Latching condition (turn-ON criterion) | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ | At breakover or after gate firing |
| 3 | Latching current | $I_L$ | Min anode current to *establish* latching after gate pulse |
| 4 | Holding current | $I_H$ | Min anode current to *maintain* conduction; $I_H < I_L$ |
| 5 | Breakover voltage | $V_{BO}$ | Forward anode voltage at which $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ without gate signal |
| 6 | Forward leakage current | $I_{DR}$ | Small current in forward blocking |
| 7 | Reverse leakage current | $I_{RR}$ | Small current in reverse blocking |
| 8 | Forward voltage drop (ON state) | $V_T \approx$ **1.5 V to 2.5 V** | Depends on current and device rating |
| 9 | Power dissipation (ON state) | $P = V_T \cdot I_T$ | Watts |
| 10 | Thermal resistance | $R_{th(j-c)} = \dfrac{T_j - T_c}{P}$ | °C/W |
| 11 | Junction temperature | $T_j = T_a + P \cdot (R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)})$ | °C |
| 12 | di/dt limit | $\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ related via $C_{j2}$ | Avoid local hot-spot heating |
| 13 | Turn-ON time | $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$ | Delay + rise time |
| 14 | Turn-OFF time | $t_{off} = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$ | Reverse recovery + gate recovery |

> [!NOTE]  
> In the formulas above, $\vert x \vert$ notation has been written out to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### 2.5 Why the SCR Behaves as a Latch — The Physics

At very small currents (forward blocking), $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ are **small** (e.g., 0.1 each), so $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \ll 1$ → leakage current $I_{DR}$ flows but no regeneration.

As the anode voltage rises (approaching $V_{BO}$) or as $I_G$ is applied:
- $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ **increase sharply** (because the space-charge region at $J_2$ narrows and the effective base width decreases for carriers).
- When the sum reaches unity, the denominator of the $I_A$ expression tends to zero, and $I_A$ rises abruptly — this is the **breakover** or **firing**.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Phase-controlled rectifiers** — convert AC to variable DC for motor drives.
- **AC voltage regulators** — for lighting and heater control.
- **Static VAR compensators (SVCs)** and **HVDC transmission** — use thyristor valves.
- **Crowbar protection** in SMPS — short-circuits the supply on over-voltage to protect downstream electronics.
- **Battery chargers, UPS, induction heating** — all rely on SCR variants.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Anode Current Equation (Two-Transistor Model)

**Starting Point — Apply KCL at the Anode and Gate Nodes:**

The anode current $I_A$ is the sum of all currents entering the anode terminal:
$$I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2} + I_{CO}$$

where $I_{CO}$ is the *thermal reverse saturation current* of the common-base transistors when their collectors are open.

**Step 1 — Express the collector currents using CB current gain ($\alpha$):**

By definition of common-base current gain:
$$I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_{A} + I_{CO1}$$
$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_{K} + I_{CO2}$$

Here:
- $\alpha_1$ = CB current gain of PNP transistor $T_1$
- $\alpha_2$ = CB current gain of NPN transistor $T_2$
- $I_K$ = cathode current

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the cathode node:**

The cathode current is the sum of the two emitter currents of $T_1$ and $T_2$:
$$I_K = I_A + I_G$$

(where $I_G$ is the external gate current).

**Step 3 — Substitute $I_K$ into $I_{C2}$:**

$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CO2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I_{C1}$ and $I_{C2}$ into the KCL equation for $I_A$:**

$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CO1} + \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CO2}$$

**Step 5 — Collect the $I_A$ terms on the left side:**

$$I_A - \alpha_1 I_A - \alpha_2 I_A = I_{CO1} + I_{CO2} + \alpha_2 I_G$$

**Step 6 — Factor and simplify:**

$$I_A \left[1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)\right] = I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G$$

where $I_{CO} = I_{CO1} + I_{CO2}$ is the *total* thermal leakage current of the device.

**Step 7 — Solve for $I_A$:**

$$\boxed{I_A = \frac{I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}}$$

This is the **canonical two-transistor-model equation** for the SCR anode current, universally tested in KTU examinations.

**Step 8 — Verify the latching condition:**

As $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, the denominator tends to zero, and $I_A \to \infty$ (limited in practice by the external load and source impedance). The device has *latched* ON. If $I_A$ is sustained above $I_L$ (latching current), the gate pulse can be removed.

After latching, the gate loses control. The device turns OFF **only** when $I_A < I_H$ (holding current) for a sufficient time $t_q$ (turn-off time).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Latching and Holding Current Relationship

**Observation:** $I_L > I_H$ — Why?

**Step 1 — Define $I_L$ (latching current):** It is the minimum anode current required to *establish* the latched state such that the gate can be removed and conduction continues.

**Step 2 — Define $I_H$ (holding current):** It is the minimum anode current required to *maintain* the device in conduction.

**Step 3 — Justify the inequality:**

The $\alpha$'s of the two-transistor model are **functions of** the anode (emitter) current $I_A$. As $I_A$ increases:
- The effective base width of the transistors *decreases* (base-width modulation / Early effect).
- The transport factor and emitter efficiency both *increase*.
- Therefore $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ *increases* monotonically with $I_A$.

**Step 4 — At the onset of conduction (just after gate firing), $I_A$ is small, so $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ is just barely $\geq 1$.** The gate signal is still essential to keep the loop alive. We need $I_A \geq I_L$ to enter the self-sustaining regime.

**Step 5 — Once the device is deeply ON, $I_A$ is large and $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ is comfortably $\geq 1$.** Even if $I_A$ drops a little, the alphas stay high. Only when $I_A$ drops below a *lower* threshold $I_H$ does $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ fall back below 1, and the device *de-latches*.

$$\therefore \quad I_L > I_H \quad \text{typically} \quad I_L \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 3 \times I_H$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Triggering vs. Commutation

| Property | Turn-ON (Triggering) | Turn-OFF (Commutation) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Required action | Apply $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ | Reduce $I_A < I_H$ for $t \geq t_q$ |
| Physical mechanism | Regenerative $\alpha_1+\alpha_2 \to 1$ | Carrier recombination at $J_2$ |
| Time scale | $t_{on} \approx 1$–$5\ \mu s$ | $t_{off} \approx 10$–$100\ \mu s$ |
| Methods | (a) Gate firing, (b) Forward voltage $>V_{BO}$, (c) High $dV/dt$, (d) Light, (e) Temperature | (a) Natural/line commutation, (b) Class A,B,C,D,E forced commutation, (c) Resonant commutation |
| Gate control? | **Yes** — gate is the *primary* trigger | **No** — gate cannot turn OFF an SCR |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** What is the difference between a *holding current* and a *latching current* of an SCR?

**Model Answer:**
- The **latching current ($I_L$)** is the minimum anode current that must flow through the SCR *during the turn-ON process* so that the device remains in conduction even *after the gate signal is removed*.
- The **holding current ($I_H$)** is the minimum anode current required to *keep the SCR in the ON state* once it has already latched.
- Numerically, $I_L > I_H$ (typically $I_L \approx 1.5$ to $3 \times I_H$).
- (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Sketch the static V-I characteristics of an SCR and label the breakover voltage $V_{BO}$, holding current $I_H$, and latching current $I_L$.

**Model Answer:**
The static V-I characteristics of an SCR have three main regions:
1. **Reverse blocking region** (Quadrant III) — for $V_{AK} < 0$, a small reverse leakage $I_{RR}$ flows until $V_{RRM}$ is reached.
2. **Forward blocking region** (Quadrant I, segment OA) — for $V_{AK} > 0$ with $I_G = 0$, only a small forward leakage $I_{DR}$ flows until the voltage reaches $V_{BO}$.
3. **Forward conduction region** (Quadrant I, after firing) — at $V = V_{BO}$ (or earlier if $I_G$ is applied), the SCR fires; the voltage drops to $\sim 1.5$–$2.5$ V and the current is limited by the load.
- $I_L$ is the anode current at which the device just latches; $I_H$ is the lowest current on the ON-state curve; $V_{BO}$ is the breakover voltage on the OA segment.
- (3 marks)

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern: A or B)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**
> **Q3 (a).** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)**. Draw its static V-I characteristics and mark all critical parameters. **(7 marks)**
> **Q3 (b).** Describe the **two-transistor model of an SCR** and derive the condition for latching. Show that $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ is essential for turn-ON. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question 3 (a) — Model Solution

**Construction:**
The SCR is a four-layer $P_1 - N_1 - P_2 - N_2$ semiconductor device with three junctions $J_1, J_2, J_3$. The anode is connected to the outer $P_1$ layer, the cathode to the outer $N_2$ layer, and the gate to the inner $P_2$ layer. The $N_1$ drift region is thick and lightly doped to provide high voltage blocking capability.

**Working Principle:**
- With $V_{AK} > 0$ and no gate signal, junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are forward-biased while $J_2$ is reverse-biased. Only a small leakage current $I_{DR}$ flows — the SCR is in **forward blocking mode**.
- When $V_{AK}$ reaches $V_{BO}$ (or when a positive gate current $I_G$ is applied), the regenerative action of the internal transistor pair causes the device to switch to the **ON state**, with $V_T \approx 1.5$–$2.5$ V across it.
- The SCR remains ON as long as $I_A \geq I_H$. It can only be turned OFF by reducing the anode current below $I_H$ (or by reversing the anode-cathode voltage) for a duration exceeding the turn-off time $t_q$.

**Static V-I Characteristics:** (See diagram in Section 4.3)
- Reverse blocking (small $I_{RR}$) → up to $V_{RRM}$ → avalanche breakdown.
- Forward blocking (small $I_{DR}$) → up to $V_{BO}$ → fires into ON state.
- Forward conduction: low voltage drop, high current, limited by load.

**Marking Allocation:** Construction 2 marks, Working 3 marks, V-I diagram 2 marks.

---

#### Question 3 (b) — Model Solution

**Two-Transistor Model:**
The four-layer PNPN structure can be split into two complementary bipolar transistors:
- $T_1$: **PNP** formed by $P_1 - N_1 - P_2$
- $T_2$: **NPN** formed by $N_1 - P_2 - N_2$

The internal feedback connection is: base of $T_1$ is tied to collector of $T_2$, and base of $T_2$ is tied to collector of $T_1$. The gate terminal is connected to the base of $T_2$.

**Derivation of Anode Current:**
Using KCL at the anode node:
$$I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2} + I_{CO}$$

Substituting the CB current relations and applying $I_K = I_A + I_G$, we get (full derivation in Section 3.1):
$$I_A = \frac{I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Latching Condition:**
For the device to turn ON without any external gate current ($I_G = 0$):
$$I_A = \frac{I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

As long as $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 < 1$, $I_A$ is finite and small. As $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, the denominator tends to zero and $I_A \to \infty$ (latching). For self-sustaining conduction:
$$\boxed{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The collector current of each transistor provides the base drive of the other. When the loop gain is unity, the feedback is regenerative and the device latches ON.

**Marking Allocation:** Diagram 2 marks, Anode current derivation 3 marks, Latching condition 2 marks.

---

#### Alternative Question 3 — Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q4 (a).** Explain the **different methods of triggering (turn-ON) of an SCR**. Compare them in terms of controllability and reliability. **(7 marks)**
> **Q4 (b).** What is **commutation**? Explain the **Class A (Resonant) and Class B (Complementary) forced commutation** methods with suitable circuits and waveforms. **(7 marks)**

**Q4 (a) Model Answer (Synopsis):**
1. **Gate triggering** — most common; $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ fires the SCR. Reliable and controllable.
2. **Forward voltage triggering** — at $V = V_{BO}$. Uncontrolled; rarely used.
3. **$dV/dt$ triggering** — junction $J_2$ capacitance allows current $i = C \cdot dV/dt$ that fires the device. Uncontrolled; mitigated by snubber.
4. **Light triggering (LTT)** — photo-carriers generated in $J_2$ fire the SCR. Used in HVDC valves.
5. **Temperature triggering** — leakage increases with $T$, eventually firing. Uncontrolled; undesirable.

**Q4 (b) Model Answer (Synopsis):**
Commutation is the process of turning OFF a conducting thyristor. Since the gate cannot turn it off, an external circuit must force $I_A < I_H$ for $t \geq t_q$.

- **Class A (Resonant load commutation):** A resonant $LC$ tank is in series with the SCR. The current becomes naturally zero at a half-cycle of resonance → SCR turns OFF.
- **Class B (Complementary / Class B):** An auxiliary SCR is used to commutate the main SCR via a charged capacitor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — SCRs Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_H$** — they are NOT equal. $I_L > I_H$. Writing "$I_L = I_H$" costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in formulas — always state Amperes for currents, Volts for voltages, °C/W for thermal resistance.
> 3. **Two-transistor model — wrong sign convention**: In the standard model, $I_A = (I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G)/[1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)]$. Writing it as $1 - \alpha_1 \alpha_2$ in the denominator is a common slip — beware.
> 4. **V-I characteristics** — students often forget the *reverse blocking* quadrant; mark the $V_{RRM}$ and $I_{RR}$ clearly.
> 5. **Commutation ≠ triggering** — write clearly that commutation is for *turn-OFF* and triggering is for *turn-ON*. Don't use the words interchangeably.
> 6. **Snubber design formula** $R_s$ and $C_s$ should be computed, not just stated. KTU expects the formula $C_s \geq \frac{V_s \cdot I_T}{V_s^2} \cdot t_{off}$ form.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- SCR = 4-layer (PNPN) 3-terminal **latching** power switch. Belongs to the thyristor family.
- **Latching current $I_L$** = minimum anode current to *establish* conduction. **Holding current $I_H$** = minimum anode current to *maintain* conduction. Always $I_L > I_H$.
- **Two-transistor model:** PNP ($T_1$) + NPN ($T_2$) with cross-coupled bases/collectors. Anode current equation:
$$I_A = \frac{I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$
- **Latching condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$. Without this, no turn-ON.
- **Static V-I curve** has three regions: reverse blocking (Quadrant III), forward blocking (Quadrant I, OA), forward conduction (after firing). Key points: $V_{BO}$, $V_{RRM}$, $V_{DRM}$, $I_H$, $I_L$.
- **Turn-ON methods:** gate pulse (preferred), high $V_{AK} > V_{BO}$, $dV/dt$, light, temperature.
- **Turn-OFF methods:** natural (line) commutation, forced (Class A resonant, Class B complementary, Class C, Class D, Class E).
- **Dynamic characteristics:** $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$ (typically 1–5 µs), $t_{off} = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$ (typically 10–100 µs).
- **Gate has no turn-OFF control** — this is the SCR's biggest limitation vs. GTO/MOSFET/IGBT.
- **Protection circuits:** over-voltage (selenium / metal-oxide varistor), over-current (fuse, MCB), $dV/dt$ (RC snubber), $di/dt$ (series inductor).
- **Ratings to remember:** $V_{DRM}$, $V_{RRM}$, $I_{T(AV)}$, $I_{TSM}$ (surge), $I^2t$ (fusing), $T_j$ (junction temp).
- **Standard forward drop:** $V_T \approx 1.5$ to 2.5 V (use this as a quick check in numerical problems).
- **Thermal resistance model:** $T_j = T_a + P \cdot (R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)})$.
- **Major applications:** phase-controlled rectifiers, AC voltage controllers, HVDC, cycloconverters, choppers, inverters, crowbar protection, induction heating.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Last-Minute KTU Quick-Fire Recall (10 Points):**
> 1. SCR is a *unidirectional*, *latching*, *current-controlled* device.
> 2. The gate can **only turn it ON**, not OFF.
> 3. Two-transistor model is **PNP + NPN** (not NPN + NPN).
> 4. Breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ is the voltage at which $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ without a gate signal.
> 5. Holding current is always **less** than latching current.
> 6. The forward blocking mode is governed by the **reverse-biased $J_2$** junction.
> 7. Commutation classes: **A, B, C, D, E** — know at least A and B.
> 8. Snubber = $R_s C_s$ in parallel with the SCR — protects against $dV/dt$ and reverse-voltage transients.
> 9. Heat sink selection is based on $R_{th(j-a)}$ budget from $T_j(max)$ and $P_{loss}$.
> 10. In KTU problems, always quote the **latching condition** explicitly — it is a high-weight keyword.

# SCRs — Silicon Controlled Rectifiers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)** is a four-layer (PNPN), three-junction (J1, J2, J3), three-terminal (Anode **A**, Cathode **K**, Gate **G**) bipolar semiconductor power device belonging to the *thyristor* family. It is a *latching*, bistable switch that conducts current in only one direction (anode to cathode) when triggered by a small gate pulse, and continues to conduct as long as the forward anode current remains above a critical minimum level called the **holding current ($I_H$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The SCR is the *mother device* of the thyristor family. Mastery of its construction, two-transistor model, static/dynamic V-I characteristics, triggering and commutation methods is **mandatory** for the ESE.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **water tap with a mechanical latch**:

- The **main valve** is closed (SCR OFF) — no water (current) flows, no matter how much pressure (anode voltage) is applied.
- You press a small **trigger button** (gate pulse) — the latch engages, and the main valve **snaps open** permanently (SCR ON).
- The valve will **not close** even if you release the button, **as long as water keeps flowing** (current $\geq I_H$).
- Only when the water flow is reduced to a **trickle** (current $< I_H$) does the latch release and the valve close.

This "press once, it latches, it stays on until flow is interrupted" behavior is the essence of an SCR.

### 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Device Symbol:** Conventional SCR has anode (A), cathode (K), and gate (G). The arrow points from anode to cathode.
- **Standard Ratings Range (commercial SCRs):**
  - Voltage: **$V_{DRM} / V_{RRM}$ = 600 V to 6500 V**
  - Current: **$I_{T(AV)}$ = 1 A to 5000 A**
  - Gate trigger voltage: **$V_{GT} \approx$ 0.8 V to 3 V**
  - Gate trigger current: **$I_{GT} \approx$ 1 mA to 200 mA**

> [!NOTE]  
> **Cross-Reference to Power Electronics:** SCR is used in controlled rectifiers (single-phase/three-phase), AC voltage controllers, cycloconverters, choppers, and inverters. It is the workhorse of HVDC and DC motor drives.

### 1.4 Layered Structure (Recap for KTU)

The SCR has four doped layers, conventionally labelled $P_1 - N_1 - P_2 - N_2$, with the following electrode connections:

| Layer | Function | Terminal |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $P_1$ | Anode region | Anode (A) |
| $N_1$ | Drift region (thick, lightly doped) | — |
| $P_2$ | Gate region | Gate (G) |
| $N_2$ | Cathode region | Cathode (K) |

The three junctions are:
- **$J_1$** — between $P_1$ and $N_1$
- **$J_2$** — between $N_1$ and $P_2$ (this is the *gate* junction)
- **$J_3$** — between $P_2$ and $N_2$

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modes of Operation (4 Quadrants)

The SCR exhibits three principal operating modes, depending on the biasing of the three junctions:

| Mode | Bias of $J_1$ | Bias of $J_2$ | Bias of $J_3$ | Condition |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Reverse Blocking** | Reverse | Forward | Reverse | $V_{AK} < 0$ |
| **Forward Blocking** | Forward | Reverse | Forward | $V_{AK} > 0$, $I_G = 0$, $V < V_{BO}$ |
| **Forward Conduction (ON)** | Forward | Forward | Forward | $V_{AK} > 0$ + adequate $I_G$ OR $V > V_{BO}$ |

> [!NOTE]  
> In **forward blocking**, the middle junction $J_2$ is reverse-biased. The depletion region at $J_2$ widens and blocks conduction. A small leakage current ($I_{DR}$ or $I_{RR}$) flows.

### 2.2 Two-Transistor Analogy of the SCR

This is a **KTU favourite question** and a critical analytical model.

The four-layer $P_1 N_1 P_2 N_2$ structure can be visualized as **two interconnected complementary transistors**:
- **Transistor $T_1$:** $P_1 N_1 P_2$ → **PNP**
- **Transistor $T_2$:** $N_1 P_2 N_2$ → **NPN**

The connection points are:
- Anode **A** = Emitter of $T_1$ = Collector of $T_2$
- Cathode **K** = Emitter of $T_2$ = Collector of $T_1$
- Gate **G** = Base of $T_2$ = Collector of $T_1$ (approximately)
- The $N_1$ layer serves as the base of $T_1$ and is **internally connected** to the collector of $T_2$

**Why this works:** Each transistor is biased by the leakage/collector current of the other, creating a **positive feedback (regenerative) loop** once conduction begins.

### 2.3 Operating Principle — The Regenerative Loop

When a positive gate current $I_G$ is applied:
1. $T_2$ turns ON → Collector current of $T_2$ (= $I_{C2}$) flows through the base of $T_1$.
2. $T_1$ turns ON → Collector current of $T_1$ (= $I_{C1}$) flows through the base of $T_2$, supplementing $I_G$.
3. The combined base drive for $T_2$ becomes **$I_G + I_{C1}$**, which is greater than $I_G$.
4. $T_2$'s collector current rises → drives $T_1$ harder → $T_1$'s collector current rises → drives $T_2$ harder.
5. This **avalanche-like (regenerative) growth** saturates both transistors, latching the SCR ON.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **latching condition** for this regenerative feedback to be self-sustaining is: $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$. Once this condition is met, the gate pulse can be removed and the SCR remains ON.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Anode current (two-transistor model) | $I_A = \dfrac{I_{C1} + \alpha_2 I_{C2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$ | $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ are CB current gains of $T_1, T_2$ |
| 2 | Latching condition (turn-ON criterion) | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ | At breakover or after gate firing |
| 3 | Latching current | $I_L$ | Min anode current to *establish* latching after gate pulse |
| 4 | Holding current | $I_H$ | Min anode current to *maintain* conduction; $I_H < I_L$ |
| 5 | Breakover voltage | $V_{BO}$ | Forward anode voltage at which $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ without gate signal |
| 6 | Forward leakage current | $I_{DR}$ | Small current in forward blocking |
| 7 | Reverse leakage current | $I_{RR}$ | Small current in reverse blocking |
| 8 | Forward voltage drop (ON state) | $V_T \approx$ **1.5 V to 2.5 V** | Depends on current and device rating |
| 9 | Power dissipation (ON state) | $P = V_T \cdot I_T$ | Watts |
| 10 | Thermal resistance | $R_{th(j-c)} = \dfrac{T_j - T_c}{P}$ | °C/W |
| 11 | Junction temperature | $T_j = T_a + P \cdot (R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)})$ | °C |
| 12 | di/dt limit | $\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ related via $C_{j2}$ | Avoid local hot-spot heating |
| 13 | Turn-ON time | $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$ | Delay + rise time |
| 14 | Turn-OFF time | $t_{off} = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$ | Reverse recovery + gate recovery |

> [!NOTE]  
> In the formulas above, $\vert x \vert$ notation has been written out to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### 2.5 Why the SCR Behaves as a Latch — The Physics

At very small currents (forward blocking), $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ are **small** (e.g., 0.1 each), so $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \ll 1$ → leakage current $I_{DR}$ flows but no regeneration.

As the anode voltage rises (approaching $V_{BO}$) or as $I_G$ is applied:
- $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ **increase sharply** (because the space-charge region at $J_2$ narrows and the effective base width decreases for carriers).
- When the sum reaches unity, the denominator of the $I_A$ expression tends to zero, and $I_A$ rises abruptly — this is the **breakover** or **firing**.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Phase-controlled rectifiers** — convert AC to variable DC for motor drives.
- **AC voltage regulators** — for lighting and heater control.
- **Static VAR compensators (SVCs)** and **HVDC transmission** — use thyristor valves.
- **Crowbar protection** in SMPS — short-circuits the supply on over-voltage to protect downstream electronics.
- **Battery chargers, UPS, induction heating** — all rely on SCR variants.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Anode Current Equation (Two-Transistor Model)

**Starting Point — Apply KCL at the Anode and Gate Nodes:**

The anode current $I_A$ is the sum of all currents entering the anode terminal:
$$I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2} + I_{CO}$$

where $I_{CO}$ is the *thermal reverse saturation current* of the common-base transistors when their collectors are open.

**Step 1 — Express the collector currents using CB current gain ($\alpha$):**

By definition of common-base current gain:
$$I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_{A} + I_{CO1}$$
$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_{K} + I_{CO2}$$

Here:
- $\alpha_1$ = CB current gain of PNP transistor $T_1$
- $\alpha_2$ = CB current gain of NPN transistor $T_2$
- $I_K$ = cathode current

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the cathode node:**

The cathode current is the sum of the two emitter currents of $T_1$ and $T_2$:
$$I_K = I_A + I_G$$

(where $I_G$ is the external gate current).

**Step 3 — Substitute $I_K$ into $I_{C2}$:**

$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CO2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I_{C1}$ and $I_{C2}$ into the KCL equation for $I_A$:**

$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CO1} + \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CO2}$$

**Step 5 — Collect the $I_A$ terms on the left side:**

$$I_A - \alpha_1 I_A - \alpha_2 I_A = I_{CO1} + I_{CO2} + \alpha_2 I_G$$

**Step 6 — Factor and simplify:**

$$I_A \left[1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)\right] = I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G$$

where $I_{CO} = I_{CO1} + I_{CO2}$ is the *total* thermal leakage current of the device.

**Step 7 — Solve for $I_A$:**

$$\boxed{I_A = \frac{I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}}$$

This is the **canonical two-transistor-model equation** for the SCR anode current, universally tested in KTU examinations.

**Step 8 — Verify the latching condition:**

As $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, the denominator tends to zero, and $I_A \to \infty$ (limited in practice by the external load and source impedance). The device has *latched* ON. If $I_A$ is sustained above $I_L$ (latching current), the gate pulse can be removed.

After latching, the gate loses control. The device turns OFF **only** when $I_A < I_H$ (holding current) for a sufficient time $t_q$ (turn-off time).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Latching and Holding Current Relationship

**Observation:** $I_L > I_H$ — Why?

**Step 1 — Define $I_L$ (latching current):** It is the minimum anode current required to *establish* the latched state such that the gate can be removed and conduction continues.

**Step 2 — Define $I_H$ (holding current):** It is the minimum anode current required to *maintain* the device in conduction.

**Step 3 — Justify the inequality:**

The $\alpha$'s of the two-transistor model are **functions of** the anode (emitter) current $I_A$. As $I_A$ increases:
- The effective base width of the transistors *decreases* (base-width modulation / Early effect).
- The transport factor and emitter efficiency both *increase*.
- Therefore $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ *increases* monotonically with $I_A$.

**Step 4 — At the onset of conduction (just after gate firing), $I_A$ is small, so $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ is just barely $\geq 1$.** The gate signal is still essential to keep the loop alive. We need $I_A \geq I_L$ to enter the self-sustaining regime.

**Step 5 — Once the device is deeply ON, $I_A$ is large and $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ is comfortably $\geq 1$.** Even if $I_A$ drops a little, the alphas stay high. Only when $I_A$ drops below a *lower* threshold $I_H$ does $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ fall back below 1, and the device *de-latches*.

$$\therefore \quad I_L > I_H \quad \text{typically} \quad I_L \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 3 \times I_H$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Triggering vs. Commutation

| Property | Turn-ON (Triggering) | Turn-OFF (Commutation) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Required action | Apply $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ | Reduce $I_A < I_H$ for $t \geq t_q$ |
| Physical mechanism | Regenerative $\alpha_1+\alpha_2 \to 1$ | Carrier recombination at $J_2$ |
| Time scale | $t_{on} \approx 1$–$5\ \mu s$ | $t_{off} \approx 10$–$100\ \mu s$ |
| Methods | (a) Gate firing, (b) Forward voltage $>V_{BO}$, (c) High $dV/dt$, (d) Light, (e) Temperature | (a) Natural/line commutation, (b) Class A,B,C,D,E forced commutation, (c) Resonant commutation |
| Gate control? | **Yes** — gate is the *primary* trigger | **No** — gate cannot turn OFF an SCR |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** What is the difference between a *holding current* and a *latching current* of an SCR?

**Model Answer:**
- The **latching current ($I_L$)** is the minimum anode current that must flow through the SCR *during the turn-ON process* so that the device remains in conduction even *after the gate signal is removed*.
- The **holding current ($I_H$)** is the minimum anode current required to *keep the SCR in the ON state* once it has already latched.
- Numerically, $I_L > I_H$ (typically $I_L \approx 1.5$ to $3 \times I_H$).
- (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Sketch the static V-I characteristics of an SCR and label the breakover voltage $V_{BO}$, holding current $I_H$, and latching current $I_L$.

**Model Answer:**
The static V-I characteristics of an SCR have three main regions:
1. **Reverse blocking region** (Quadrant III) — for $V_{AK} < 0$, a small reverse leakage $I_{RR}$ flows until $V_{RRM}$ is reached.
2. **Forward blocking region** (Quadrant I, segment OA) — for $V_{AK} > 0$ with $I_G = 0$, only a small forward leakage $I_{DR}$ flows until the voltage reaches $V_{BO}$.
3. **Forward conduction region** (Quadrant I, after firing) — at $V = V_{BO}$ (or earlier if $I_G$ is applied), the SCR fires; the voltage drops to $\sim 1.5$–$2.5$ V and the current is limited by the load.
- $I_L$ is the anode current at which the device just latches; $I_H$ is the lowest current on the ON-state curve; $V_{BO}$ is the breakover voltage on the OA segment.
- (3 marks)

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern: A or B)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**
> **Q3 (a).** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)**. Draw its static V-I characteristics and mark all critical parameters. **(7 marks)**
> **Q3 (b).** Describe the **two-transistor model of an SCR** and derive the condition for latching. Show that $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ is essential for turn-ON. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question 3 (a) — Model Solution

**Construction:**
The SCR is a four-layer $P_1 - N_1 - P_2 - N_2$ semiconductor device with three junctions $J_1, J_2, J_3$. The anode is connected to the outer $P_1$ layer, the cathode to the outer $N_2$ layer, and the gate to the inner $P_2$ layer. The $N_1$ drift region is thick and lightly doped to provide high voltage blocking capability.

**Working Principle:**
- With $V_{AK} > 0$ and no gate signal, junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are forward-biased while $J_2$ is reverse-biased. Only a small leakage current $I_{DR}$ flows — the SCR is in **forward blocking mode**.
- When $V_{AK}$ reaches $V_{BO}$ (or when a positive gate current $I_G$ is applied), the regenerative action of the internal transistor pair causes the device to switch to the **ON state**, with $V_T \approx 1.5$–$2.5$ V across it.
- The SCR remains ON as long as $I_A \geq I_H$. It can only be turned OFF by reducing the anode current below $I_H$ (or by reversing the anode-cathode voltage) for a duration exceeding the turn-off time $t_q$.

**Static V-I Characteristics:** (See diagram in Section 4.3)
- Reverse blocking (small $I_{RR}$) → up to $V_{RRM}$ → avalanche breakdown.
- Forward blocking (small $I_{DR}$) → up to $V_{BO}$ → fires into ON state.
- Forward conduction: low voltage drop, high current, limited by load.

**Marking Allocation:** Construction 2 marks, Working 3 marks, V-I diagram 2 marks.

---

#### Question 3 (b) — Model Solution

**Two-Transistor Model:**
The four-layer PNPN structure can be split into two complementary bipolar transistors:
- $T_1$: **PNP** formed by $P_1 - N_1 - P_2$
- $T_2$: **NPN** formed by $N_1 - P_2 - N_2$

The internal feedback connection is: base of $T_1$ is tied to collector of $T_2$, and base of $T_2$ is tied to collector of $T_1$. The gate terminal is connected to the base of $T_2$.

**Derivation of Anode Current:**
Using KCL at the anode node:
$$I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2} + I_{CO}$$

Substituting the CB current relations and applying $I_K = I_A + I_G$, we get (full derivation in Section 3.1):
$$I_A = \frac{I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Latching Condition:**
For the device to turn ON without any external gate current ($I_G = 0$):
$$I_A = \frac{I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

As long as $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 < 1$, $I_A$ is finite and small. As $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, the denominator tends to zero and $I_A \to \infty$ (latching). For self-sustaining conduction:
$$\boxed{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The collector current of each transistor provides the base drive of the other. When the loop gain is unity, the feedback is regenerative and the device latches ON.

**Marking Allocation:** Diagram 2 marks, Anode current derivation 3 marks, Latching condition 2 marks.

---

#### Alternative Question 3 — Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q4 (a).** Explain the **different methods of triggering (turn-ON) of an SCR**. Compare them in terms of controllability and reliability. **(7 marks)**
> **Q4 (b).** What is **commutation**? Explain the **Class A (Resonant) and Class B (Complementary) forced commutation** methods with suitable circuits and waveforms. **(7 marks)**

**Q4 (a) Model Answer (Synopsis):**
1. **Gate triggering** — most common; $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ fires the SCR. Reliable and controllable.
2. **Forward voltage triggering** — at $V = V_{BO}$. Uncontrolled; rarely used.
3. **$dV/dt$ triggering** — junction $J_2$ capacitance allows current $i = C \cdot dV/dt$ that fires the device. Uncontrolled; mitigated by snubber.
4. **Light triggering (LTT)** — photo-carriers generated in $J_2$ fire the SCR. Used in HVDC valves.
5. **Temperature triggering** — leakage increases with $T$, eventually firing. Uncontrolled; undesirable.

**Q4 (b) Model Answer (Synopsis):**
Commutation is the process of turning OFF a conducting thyristor. Since the gate cannot turn it off, an external circuit must force $I_A < I_H$ for $t \geq t_q$.

- **Class A (Resonant load commutation):** A resonant $LC$ tank is in series with the SCR. The current becomes naturally zero at a half-cycle of resonance → SCR turns OFF.
- **Class B (Complementary / Class B):** An auxiliary SCR is used to commutate the main SCR via a charged capacitor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — SCRs Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_H$** — they are NOT equal. $I_L > I_H$. Writing "$I_L = I_H$" costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in formulas — always state Amperes for currents, Volts for voltages, °C/W for thermal resistance.
> 3. **Two-transistor model — wrong sign convention**: In the standard model, $I_A = (I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G)/[1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)]$. Writing it as $1 - \alpha_1 \alpha_2$ in the denominator is a common slip — beware.
> 4. **V-I characteristics** — students often forget the *reverse blocking* quadrant; mark the $V_{RRM}$ and $I_{RR}$ clearly.
> 5. **Commutation ≠ triggering** — write clearly that commutation is for *turn-OFF* and triggering is for *turn-ON*. Don't use the words interchangeably.
> 6. **Snubber design formula** $R_s$ and $C_s$ should be computed, not just stated. KTU expects the formula $C_s \geq \frac{V_s \cdot I_T}{V_s^2} \cdot t_{off}$ form.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- SCR = 4-layer (PNPN) 3-terminal **latching** power switch. Belongs to the thyristor family.
- **Latching current $I_L$** = minimum anode current to *establish* conduction. **Holding current $I_H$** = minimum anode current to *maintain* conduction. Always $I_L > I_H$.
- **Two-transistor model:** PNP ($T_1$) + NPN ($T_2$) with cross-coupled bases/collectors. Anode current equation:
$$I_A = \frac{I_{CO} + \alpha_2 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$
- **Latching condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$. Without this, no turn-ON.
- **Static V-I curve** has three regions: reverse blocking (Quadrant III), forward blocking (Quadrant I, OA), forward conduction (after firing). Key points: $V_{BO}$, $V_{RRM}$, $V_{DRM}$, $I_H$, $I_L$.
- **Turn-ON methods:** gate pulse (preferred), high $V_{AK} > V_{BO}$, $dV/dt$, light, temperature.
- **Turn-OFF methods:** natural (line) commutation, forced (Class A resonant, Class B complementary, Class C, Class D, Class E).
- **Dynamic characteristics:** $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$ (typically 1–5 µs), $t_{off} = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$ (typically 10–100 µs).
- **Gate has no turn-OFF control** — this is the SCR's biggest limitation vs. GTO/MOSFET/IGBT.
- **Protection circuits:** over-voltage (selenium / metal-oxide varistor), over-current (fuse, MCB), $dV/dt$ (RC snubber), $di/dt$ (series inductor).
- **Ratings to remember:** $V_{DRM}$, $V_{RRM}$, $I_{T(AV)}$, $I_{TSM}$ (surge), $I^2t$ (fusing), $T_j$ (junction temp).
- **Standard forward drop:** $V_T \approx 1.5$ to 2.5 V (use this as a quick check in numerical problems).
- **Thermal resistance model:** $T_j = T_a + P \cdot (R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)})$.
- **Major applications:** phase-controlled rectifiers, AC voltage controllers, HVDC, cycloconverters, choppers, inverters, crowbar protection, induction heating.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Last-Minute KTU Quick-Fire Recall (10 Points):**
> 1. SCR is a *unidirectional*, *latching*, *current-controlled* device.
> 2. The gate can **only turn it ON**, not OFF.
> 3. Two-transistor model is **PNP + NPN** (not NPN + NPN).
> 4. Breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ is the voltage at which $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ without a gate signal.
> 5. Holding current is always **less** than latching current.
> 6. The forward blocking mode is governed by the **reverse-biased $J_2$** junction.
> 7. Commutation classes: **A, B, C, D, E** — know at least A and B.
> 8. Snubber = $R_s C_s$ in parallel with the SCR — protects against $dV/dt$ and reverse-voltage transients.
> 9. Heat sink selection is based on $R_{th(j-a)}$ budget from $T_j(max)$ and $P_{loss}$.
> 10. In KTU problems, always quote the **latching condition** explicitly — it is a high-weight keyword.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triac
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Triac

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Triac: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **TRIAC** (an acronym for **TRI**ode for **A**lternating **C**urrent) is a five-layer, bidirectional, three-terminal semiconductor power switching device that can conduct current in **both directions** when triggered by a single gate signal. It is functionally equivalent to **two Silicon Controlled Rectifiers (SCRs / thyristors) connected in anti-parallel** within a single monolithic silicon wafer.

The three terminals are:
- **MT1** — Main Terminal 1 (reference terminal)
- **MT2** — Main Terminal 2 (load-carrying terminal)
- **G** — Gate (triggering terminal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The TRIAC belongs to the family of *thyristors with bidirectional conduction*. Unlike an SCR that conducts only in one quadrant of the V-I plane, a TRIAC can block voltage and conduct current in **both quadrants I and III**, making it the natural choice for AC waveform control.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reversible Toll Gate"

Imagine a **two-way toll booth** on a road:
- A **normal SCR** is a one-way toll gate — cars (current) can pass in only **one direction**, and a guard (gate pulse) is required to open it.
- A **TRIAC** is a **two-way toll gate** with a single guard. Whether cars approach from the north or the south, the same guard can open the barrier. Once opened, cars flow in whichever direction they are currently moving, and the gate auto-closes only when the traffic (current) naturally drops to zero (which happens twice per AC cycle).

This is precisely why a TRIAC is the heart of **lamp dimmers, fan speed regulators, and heater controllers** in your home — it cleanly switches AC power using just **one compact trigger circuit**.

## 1.3 Salient Physical & Electrical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Forward Breakover Voltage | $V_{BO}$ | **400 V – 1200 V** |
| RMS On-State Current | $I_{T(RMS)}$ | **4 A – 40 A** |
| Gate Trigger Voltage | $V_{GT}$ | **0.8 V – 1.5 V** |
| Gate Trigger Current | $I_{GT}$ | **5 mA – 50 mA** |
| Holding Current | $I_{H}$ | **10 mA – 75 mA** |
| Operating Temperature Range | $T_{j}$ | **−40 °C to +125 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter for KTU:** During exam problems, you will often be given $V_{GT}$, $I_{GT}$, and $I_H$. Memorize the typical ranges so that you can sanity-check the sanity of numerical values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 1 — Triac: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Construction — Layered Architecture

A TRIAC is fabricated as a **five-layer (P-N-P-N-P)** semiconductor sandwich. Internally, the structure can be decomposed into **two complementary SCRs** sharing a common gate region:

$$\text{TRIAC} \equiv \text{SCR}_1 \;(P_1 N_1 P_2 N_2) \;\parallel\;\text{SCR}_2 \;(P_2 N_1 P_1 N_3)$$

Where:
- $P_1, P_2$ — P-type regions
- $N_1, N_2, N_3$ — N-type regions
- The common junction $N_1 P_2$ is shared between both internal SCRs.

## 2.2 Four Modes of Operation (The Core Concept)

The TRIAC can be triggered in **four distinct quadrants** depending on the **polarity of MT2 with respect to MT1** and the **polarity of the gate signal** with respect to MT1.

| Mode | Polarity of MT2 | Polarity of Gate ($V_G$) | Internal SCR Activated | Sensitivity |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **I (MT2+, G+)** | Positive | Positive | $\text{SCR}_1$ via P-region | **Most Sensitive** |
| **II (MT2+, G−)** | Positive | Negative | $\text{SCR}_2$ via N-region | Low |
| **III (MT2−, G−)** | Negative | Negative | $\text{SCR}_1$ via N-region | Medium |
| **IV (MT2−, G+)** | Negative | Positive | $\text{SCR}_2$ via P-region | **Least Sensitive** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mode I is preferred:** Practical TRIAC triggering circuits almost always use **Mode I (MT2+, G+)** because the lowest gate current is required to fire the device. This is a guaranteed KTU question.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Conduction

1. **Off-State (Blocking Mode):** When no gate signal is present, the TRIAC blocks both positive and negative half-cycles of the AC supply up to $V_{BO}$.
2. **Gate Trigger Applied:** A short gate pulse injects carriers near the $N_1 P_2$ junction.
3. **Latch-Up:** The internal two-transistor regenerative feedback latches the device into a low-impedance on-state.
4. **Conduction Phase:** Current flows between MT1 and MT2 in the direction imposed by the external source.
5. **Natural Commutation:** When the AC source current naturally falls below the **holding current $I_H$** at the end of each half-cycle, the TRIAC turns off — this is *line commutation*.
6. **Cycle Repeats:** In the next half-cycle, a fresh gate pulse retriggers the device in the opposite quadrant.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Unit / Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_{RMS} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}$ | RMS output of single-phase AC controller (Triac) for $\alpha \in [0, \pi]$ | $V_s$ = supply RMS |
| 2 | $V_{ORMS} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}$ | Alternate form of TRIAC output RMS voltage | $\alpha$ = firing angle (rad) |
| 3 | $P_{load} = \dfrac{V_{ORMS}^2}{R}$ | Power delivered to resistive load | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $I_{T(RMS)} = \dfrac{V_{ORMS}}{R}$ | TRIAC RMS current (resistive load) | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $I_{T(avg)} = \dfrac{I_{T(peak)}}{\pi}(\pi - \alpha)$ | Average TRIAC current over half-cycle | Amperes (A) |
| 6 | $\cos\phi = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}$ | Power factor (resistive load) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{GT} \cdot I_{GT} \leq P_{G(max)}$ | Gate power dissipation must not exceed rating | Watts (W) |
| 8 | $V_{DWM} \geq V_{s(peak)} \cdot \text{safety factor}$ | Voltage safety derating rule ($\text{SF} = 1.5$ to $2$) | Volts (V) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule:** Inside markdown tables, the absolute-value / magnitude delimiters are written as $\vert$ and $\pi \cdot V_s$ — **never** as the unescaped pipe character $\mid$ because it breaks table rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

TRIACs are ubiquitous in **residential, commercial, and industrial AC power control**:
- **Domestic lamp dimmers** (phase-angle control)
- **Ceiling fan speed regulators**
- **Heater temperature controllers**
- **Solid-state relays (SSRs)** for PLC interfaces
- **Universal motor speed controls** (drills, mixers, vacuum cleaners)
- **Static VAR compensators** in power factor correction
- **AC solid-state contactors** in industrial automation
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 1 — Triac: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the RMS Output Voltage of a TRIAC-Based AC Voltage Controller

Consider a **single-phase, full-wave AC voltage controller** feeding a resistive load $R$, with a TRIAC as the switching device and a sinusoidal supply $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

### Step 1 — Define the Firing Angle Convention

The TRIAC is triggered at a **firing angle $\alpha$** measured from the zero-crossing of the supply voltage. For each half-cycle:

- From $\omega t = 0$ to $\alpha$: TRIAC is in the **OFF state** (blocking).
- From $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi$: TRIac is in the **ON state** (conducting).
- For the negative half-cycle: same behavior with $\alpha$ measured from the next zero-crossing.

### Step 2 — Set Up the RMS Integral

The RMS value of the output voltage across the load is defined as:

$$V_{ORMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} v_s^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Sinusoidal Supply

Insert $v_s(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ into the integral:

$$V_{ORMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Half-Angle Identity

Use the trigonometric identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$V_{ORMS} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t)}$$

### Step 5 — Split the Integral

$$V_{ORMS} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2\pi}\left[\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} 1 \, d(\omega t) - \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos(2\omega t) \, d(\omega t)\right]}$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the First Integral (Trivial)

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} 1 \, d(\omega t) = \pi - \alpha$$

### Step 7 — Evaluate the Second Integral

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos(2\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \left[\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{\sin(2\pi) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} = -\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}$$

### Step 8 — Combine Results

Substituting back into the RMS expression:

$$V_{ORMS} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) - \left(-\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right)\right]}$$

$$V_{ORMS} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}$$

### Step 9 — Express in Terms of Supply RMS $V_s$

Since $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_s$, we have $V_m^2 = 2 V_s^2$:

$$V_{ORMS} = \sqrt{\frac{2V_s^2}{2\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}$$

$$\boxed{V_{ORMS} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}}$$

### Step 10 — Boundary Condition Check

- When $\alpha = 0$ (fully ON, like a closed switch): $V_{ORMS} = V_s$ (entire waveform passed) — **Correct**.
- When $\alpha = \pi$ (TRIAC never fires): $V_{ORMS} = 0$ (no conduction) — **Correct**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Power Factor

For a resistive load, the apparent power equals the real power. The displacement factor and distortion factor combine as:

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P_{real}}{V_s I_{ORMS}} = \frac{V_{ORMS}}{V_s}$$

Substituting the result from Step 9 above:

$$\boxed{\cos\phi = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}}$$

## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Worked Problem)

> **Problem:** A 230 V, 50 Hz supply feeds a 10 Ω resistive load through a TRIAC. The firing angle is set to $\alpha = 60°$. Calculate (i) RMS output voltage, (ii) load power, and (iii) power factor.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Convert the firing angle to radians: $\alpha = 60° = \pi/3$ rad.

Step 2: Compute $\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(120°) = \sin(60°) = 0.866$.

Step 3: Apply the RMS formula:

$$V_{ORMS} = 230 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \pi/3) + \frac{0.866}{2}\right]}$$

$$V_{ORMS} = 230 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[2.094 + 0.433\right]}$$

$$V_{ORMS} = 230 \sqrt{\frac{2.527}{3.1416}} = 230 \sqrt{0.8045} \approx 230 \times 0.897 \approx 206.3 \text{ V}$$

Step 4: Load power:

$$P_{load} = \frac{V_{ORMS}^2}{R} = \frac{(206.3)^2}{10} = \frac{42{,}560}{10} \approx 4256 \text{ W}$$

Step 5: Power factor:

$$\cos\phi = \frac{V_{ORMS}}{V_s} = \frac{206.3}{230} \approx 0.897 \text{ (lagging)}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 1 — Triac: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic Symbol and Terminal Identification


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 1 — Triac: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a TRIAC. List any four advantages of using a TRIAC over a conventional SCR pair in AC control applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A TRIAC is a **bidirectional thyristor** that can conduct current in both directions when triggered by a single gate signal. It is the equivalent of two SCRs connected in anti-parallel within a single silicon wafer.

Four advantages:
1. **Single gate trigger circuit** is sufficient for both half-cycles, reducing circuit complexity.
2. **Compact, single-package design** saves PCB space and reduces cost.
3. Naturally suited for **AC phase-angle and integral-cycle control**.
4. **No need for two separate SCRs** with isolated gate drives, simplifying triggering logic.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the four triggering modes of a TRIAC. Which mode is the most sensitive and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four triggering modes of a TRIAC are classified based on the polarities of MT2 with respect to MT1 and the gate voltage with respect to MT1:

- **Mode I:** MT2 positive, gate positive
- **Mode II:** MT2 positive, gate negative
- **Mode III:** MT2 negative, gate negative
- **Mode IV:** MT2 negative, gate positive

**Mode I is the most sensitive** because the gate-emitter junction of the internal NPN transistor in the equivalent two-transistor model is forward-biased with the smallest possible resistance, requiring the lowest gate current $I_{GT}$ to initiate regenerative turn-on.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **construction and working of a TRIAC**. Draw its **V-I characteristics** for both polarities. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **single-phase AC voltage controller** uses a TRIAC to feed a **10 Ω resistive load** from a **230 V, 50 Hz** supply. If the TRIAC is fired at an angle of $\alpha = 90°$, determine:
- (i) RMS output voltage
- (ii) RMS load current
- (iii) Power delivered to the load
- (iv) Supply power factor (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (2 marks):**
A TRIAC is a **five-layer, three-terminal** device with layers arranged as **P-N-P-N-P**. It has terminals MT1, MT2, and Gate. Internally, the device can be modeled as **two SCRs (SCR₁ and SCR₂) connected in anti-parallel** sharing a common gate region.

**Working (3 marks):**
When a gate pulse is applied, the device latches into the ON state and conducts current from MT2 to MT1 (positive half-cycle) or MT1 to MT2 (negative half-cycle), depending on the supply polarity. The TRIAC turns off automatically when the current falls below the holding current $I_H$ at the end of each half-cycle — this is **natural or line commutation**.

**V-I Characteristics (2 marks):**
The V-I curve is symmetric about the origin, showing:
- **Forward blocking region** (Quadrant I) — MT2 positive, device OFF up to $V_{BO}$.
- **Reverse blocking region** (Quadrant III) — MT2 negative, device OFF up to $V_{BO}$.
- **On-state regions** in both quadrants with low on-state voltage drop.
- The **breakover voltage decreases** as gate current $I_G$ increases.

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_s = 230$ V, $R = 10$ Ω, $\alpha = 90° = \pi/2$ rad.

**Step 1:** Compute $\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(180°) = 0$.

**Step 2:** Apply the RMS output voltage formula:

$$V_{ORMS} = 230 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \pi/2) + \frac{0}{2}\right]}$$

$$V_{ORMS} = 230 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}} = 230 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 162.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** RMS load current:

$$I_{ORMS} = \frac{V_{ORMS}}{R} = \frac{162.6}{10} = 16.26 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4:** Power delivered:

$$P = \frac{V_{ORMS}^2}{R} = \frac{(162.6)^2}{10} = \frac{26{,}439}{10} \approx 2644 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5:** Power factor:

$$\cos\phi = \frac{V_{ORMS}}{V_s} = \frac{162.6}{230} \approx 0.707 \text{ (lagging)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Alternative Module 1 Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Compare the **TRIAC and SCR** in terms of construction, conduction direction, triggering complexity, and applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with a **phase-angle control circuit diagram** how a TRIAC is used in a **lamp dimmer**. Derive the expression for **average output voltage** in terms of firing angle $\alpha$. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | SCR | TRIAC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conduction** | Unidirectional | Bidirectional |
| **Layers** | 4 layers (P-N-P-N) | 5 layers (P-N-P-N-P) |
| **Terminals** | Anode, Cathode, Gate | MT1, MT2, Gate |
| **Triggering** | One mode only | Four modes (I, II, III, IV) |
| **Equivalent** | Single device | Two anti-parallel SCRs |
| **V-I Plot** | Quadrant I only | Quadrants I and III |
| **Use** | DC, controlled rectifiers | AC phase-angle controllers, dimmers |
| **Complexity** | Simpler triggering | More sensitive, easier AC control |

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Description (3 marks):**
A lamp dimmer circuit consists of a TRIAC in series with the lamp across the AC mains, a diac-triggered RC timing network ($R_{pot}$ and $C$), and a diac (DIAC) providing symmetric breakover pulses to the gate.

**Working:** By adjusting $R_{pot}$, the RC time constant changes, varying the firing angle $\alpha$ of the TRIAC. A smaller $\alpha$ delivers more power (bright lamp); a larger $\alpha$ reduces the RMS voltage (dim lamp).

**Derivation of Average Output Voltage (4 marks):**

The output waveform is a chopped sinusoid from $\alpha$ to $\pi$ in each half-cycle. The average value over one full cycle is:

$$V_{O(avg)} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{O(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

$$V_{O(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\pi) + \cos(\alpha)\right]$$

$$V_{O(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[1 + \cos(\alpha)\right]$$

$$\boxed{V_{O(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing average and RMS values:** The KTU paper often asks both. Mixing $V_{ORMS}$ and $V_{O(avg)}$ formulations costs **2–3 marks** per question.
> 2. **Forgetting the boundary check:** Always verify your formula at $\alpha = 0$ and $\alpha = \pi$ to detect algebraic mistakes.
> 3. **Wrong units on $\alpha$:** Most trig functions in your formulas need $\alpha$ in **radians**, not degrees. State the conversion explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring $I_H$:** Real TRIACs may fail to latch on if the load current is too small — the KTU key often tests this.
> 5. **Drawing the V-I curve in only one quadrant:** You will lose **1 mark** if the negative-quadrant characteristics are missing.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **TRIAC** = **TRI**ode for **AC** = two SCRs in anti-parallel, single gate.
- **Three terminals:** MT1, MT2, Gate.
- **Four firing modes:** I (most sensitive), II, III, IV (least sensitive). Mode I uses positive MT2 and positive gate.
- **Bidirectional conduction**, used for **AC phase-angle control**.
- **Line commutation** turns the device OFF naturally when AC current crosses zero.
- **RMS output voltage** for resistive load: $V_{ORMS} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}$
- **Average output voltage** (full-cycle): $V_{O(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$
- **Power factor** for resistive load: $\cos\phi = \dfrac{V_{ORMS}}{V_s}$
- **Typical applications:** lamp dimmers, fan regulators, heater control, SSRs, universal motor speed controllers.
- **Voltage safety derating rule:** $V_{DWM} \geq 1.5 \times V_{s(peak)}$
- **Gate trigger rating must not be exceeded:** $V_{GT} \cdot I_{GT} \leq P_{G(max)}$
- **Mode I is the practical choice** in almost all commercial triggering circuits for the lowest gate current.
- **Holding current $I_H$** determines the minimum load — below $I_H$, the device will not stay latched.
- **Quadrants I and III** in the V-I plane correspond to positive and negative half-cycles of the AC supply.
- **TRIAC is *not* suitable for high-frequency, high-current DC circuits** — use an SCR or IGBT there.
- **A DIAC** is commonly used in series with the gate to provide symmetric triggering and prevent TRIAC misfires.
- **BT136 and BTA16** are the most popular TRIAC part numbers used in KTU laboratory kits.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GTOs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** GTOs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of GTOs

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gate Turn-Off Thyristor (GTO):** A four-layer (PNPN), three-terminal power semiconductor device belonging to the thyristor family, capable of being **turned ON by a positive gate current** and **turned OFF by a negative gate current pulse**.
