```mermaid
    C --> D["N PLUS BUFFER LAYER Field Stop Layer Speeds up carrier removal"]
    D --> E["P PLUS BODY REGION Forms MOSFET body and PNP base"]
    E --> F["N PLUS SOURCE DIFFUSION Emitter of internal MOSFET Shorts body to emitter"]
    F --> G["INVERSION CHANNEL N channel forms here when VGE exceeds threshold"]
    G --> H["GATE OXIDE SIO2 Insulation between gate electrode and silicon"]
    H --> I["GATE METAL POLYSILICON ELECTRODE Control input terminal"]
    J["EMITTER METAL CONTACT Bottom terminal Shorts body to source"] --> F
    J --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The current path is Collector → p⁺ collector → n⁻ drift (modulated by holes) → inversion channel → n⁺ source → Emitter. The gate sits on top of the oxide and does **not** draw DC current.

## 4.2 IGBT Equivalent Circuit (Two-Transistor Model with Parasitic Elements)

```mermaid
graph TD
    G1["GATE Input VGE applied here"] --> M1["N CHANNEL MOSFET Driving stage"]
    M1 --> B1["WIDE BASE PNP BJT Power stage output"]
    M1 -.-> Rg["INTERNAL GATE RESISTANCE RG Limits di dt during switching"]
    B1 --> Cterm["COLLECTOR C Main current terminal"]
    Cterm --> D1["FREEWHEEL DIODE FWD Anti parallel body diode"]
    D1 --> Eterm["EMITTER E Current return terminal"]
    B1 -.-> T1["PARASITIC NPN TRANSISTOR Forms latch up risk"]
    T1 --> B1
    Rp["BODY RESISTANCE RP Shorts base emitter of parasitic NPN"] --> B1
    Rp --> Eterm
```

> [!TIP]
> **The dashed elements** (`Rg`, `T1`, `Rp`) are the parasitic/non-ideal components. `T1` + `B1` together form the **parasitic thyristor** whose latch-up must be prevented by keeping `Rp` small via heavy p⁺ body doping and emitter shorting.

## 4.3 Functional Switching Waveform (Turn-ON and Turn-OFF Profile)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TIME_DOMAIN["Time Axis Microseconds"]
    T1["t0 GATE PULSE APPLIED VGE rises to 15 V"] --> T2["t0 to t1 VCE falls while IC rises Turn on overlap region"]
    T2 --> T3["t1 to t2 FULL ON VCE equals VCE sat and IC equals load current"]
    T3 --> T4["t2 GATE PULSE REMOVED VGE falls to zero"]
    T4 --> T5["t2 to t3 VCE rises and IC falls Turn off overlap region"]
    T5 --> T6["t3 to t4 TAIL CURRENT IC decays slowly as stored carriers recombine"]
    T6 --> T7["t4 FULL OFF IC equals leakage and VCE equals DC bus voltage"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination tip:** The **tail current** between $t_3$ and $t_4$ is the single most important IGBT switching feature. Examiners will ask you to (a) draw it, (b) explain its origin (stored minority carriers in the n⁻ drift), and (c) state how field-stop technology reduces it.

## 4.4 IGBT Position in the Power-Device Family Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT["POWER SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES"] --> S1["Three terminal devices"]
    ROOT --> S2["Two terminal devices"]
    S1 --> D1["Voltage controlled"]
    S1 --> D2["Current controlled"]
    D1 --> L1["POWER MOSFET High frequency low voltage"]
    D1 --> L2["IGBT Medium frequency medium high voltage"]
    D1 --> L3["SIT Static Induction Transistor RF"]
    D2 --> R1["BJT Legacy low input impedance"]
    D2 --> R2["GTO Gate Turn Off thyristor"]
    D2 --> R3["THYRISTOR SCR Latch only"]
    S2 --> P1["POWER DIODE Rectifier FWD"]
    P1 --> P2["Fast recovery diode"]
    P1 --> P3["Schottky diode low voltage"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The IGBT sits at the **sweet spot** where voltage ratings of BJT/thyristor territory meet the easy-drive territory of MOSFETs. It is the dominant device from 600 V to 6500 V at switching frequencies from 1 kHz to 50 kHz.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *List the three terminals of an IGBT and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three terminals of an IGBT are:
1. **Gate (G):** The insulated MOS input terminal. A voltage $V_{GE}$ applied between gate and emitter (above the threshold, typically 15 V) creates an inversion channel that turns the device ON. No DC gate current flows.
2. **Collector (C):** The main current entry terminal. It is connected to the p⁺ substrate, which injects holes into the n⁻ drift region to enable conductivity modulation.
3. **Emitter (E):** The main current exit terminal, common reference for both the gate-drive signal and the load current.

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *Why is the on-state voltage drop of an IGBT much lower than that of a power MOSFET of the same voltage rating?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In a power MOSFET, the n⁻ drift region supports the full blocking voltage, but it is **not conductivity modulated** — its resistance is intrinsic and very high at high voltage ratings. In an IGBT, the p⁺ collector **injects holes** into the n⁻ drift region during ON-state, flooding it with minority carriers. This **conductivity modulation** reduces the effective resistance of the drift region by orders of magnitude, so the IGBT achieves a much lower $V_{CE(sat)}$ than a MOSFET of equivalent voltage rating. The trade-off is the **tail current** during turn-off due to the stored minority carriers that must recombine.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B (Choice A) — *Conduction Loss & Switching Loss Analysis*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> An IGBT is used in a 400 V DC-link inverter switching at $f_{sw} = 5$ kHz with a duty ratio $D = 0.5$. The load current is constant at $I_C = 100$ A. The on-state voltage is $V_{CE(sat)} = 2.0$ V. Turn-on time $t_{on} = 200$ ns and turn-off time $t_{off} = 300$ ns.

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Calculate (i) the average conduction loss, and (ii) the total switching energy dissipated per cycle.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Conduction loss:**
The IGBT is ON for a fraction $D = 0.5$ of each cycle. Average conduction power:

$$
P_{cond} \;=\; D \cdot V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C
$$

$$
P_{cond} \;=\; 0.5 \times 2.0 \times 100 \;=\; 100 \, \text{W}
$$

**(3 marks awarded for correct formula, substitution, and answer.)**

**(ii) Switching energy per cycle:**
Using the standard linear-ramp hard-switching result derived in Section 3.2:

$$
E_{on} \;=\; \frac{1}{6} V_{CC} I_C t_{on} \;=\; \frac{1}{6} \times 400 \times 100 \times 200 \times 10^{-9} \;=\; 1.333 \, \text{mJ}
$$

$$
E_{off} \;=\; \frac{1}{6} V_{CC} I_C t_{off} \;=\; \frac{1}{6} \times 400 \times 100 \times 300 \times 10^{-9} \;=\; 2.0 \, \text{mJ}
$$

$$
E_{sw,total} \;=\; E_{on} + E_{off} \;=\; 1.333 + 2.0 \;=\; 3.333 \, \text{mJ per cycle}
$$

**(4 marks awarded — 2 for each energy component with correct formula.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Determine (i) the total average power loss, and (ii) the junction temperature if $T_a = 35$ °C, $R_{th(j-c)} = 0.4$ °C/W, and $R_{th(c-a)} = 0.8$ °C/W. State whether the device is operating safely.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total power loss:**

$$
P_{sw} \;=\; E_{sw,total} \cdot f_{sw} \;=\; 3.333 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{3} \;=\; 16.67 \, \text{W}
$$

$$
P_{total} \;=\; P_{cond} + P_{sw} \;=\; 100 + 16.67 \;=\; 116.67 \, \text{W}
$$

**(3 marks.)**

**(ii) Junction temperature:**

$$
T_j \;=\; T_a + P_{total} \cdot (R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-a)})
$$

$$
T_j \;=\; 35 + 116.67 \times (0.4 + 0.8) \;=\; 35 + 116.67 \times 1.2 \;=\; 35 + 140 \;=\; 175 \, \text{°C}
$$

**Verdict (4 marks):** The junction temperature $T_j = 175$ °C **exceeds** the typical IGBT maximum of 150 °C. The device is **NOT operating safely** — either reduce the switching frequency, use forced-air cooling, select a heatsink with lower $R_{th(c-a)}$, or use a paralleled IGBT module to share the current.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Switching Loss Derivation:**
> A very common mistake students make is to **omit the 1/6 factor** and simply write $E_{on} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{on}$ (which is only correct for a *square-wave* overlap, not for linear ramps). For a linear-ramp hard-switching event, the correct factor is **1/6**. Lose 2 marks if you write 1/2.

> [!WARNING]
> **Second Common Trap:** Conduction-loss calculation requires the **duty ratio $D$**. Many students compute $V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C = 200$ W and forget to multiply by $D = 0.5$. This leads to **doubling** the conduction loss. Always sanity-check whether the question is asking for *instantaneous* power (no $D$) or *average* power (with $D$).

---

### Question B (Choice B) — *Structure, Operation & Device Comparison*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary Exam 2024]**
> (a) Draw the basic structure of an IGBT and explain its operation in the **ON state** with emphasis on *conductivity modulation*. (7 marks)
>
> (b) Compare the IGBT with the power MOSFET and the power BJT on the basis of (i) drive method, (ii) on-state voltage drop, (iii) switching frequency, and (iv) typical applications. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Model Solution:**

**Structure (3 marks):** The IGBT is a four-layer p⁺-n⁻-n⁺-p⁺ vertical device with a top MOS-gated channel. Starting from the collector: a **p⁺ substrate** (anode), an **n⁻ drift region** (voltage-supporting layer), an **n⁺ buffer** (in PT/FS types), a **p⁺ body region**, an **n⁺ source/emitter diffusion**, and a **SiO₂ gate oxide** with a polysilicon gate electrode on top.

**ON-state operation with conductivity modulation (4 marks):**
When $V_{GE}$ exceeds the threshold (~15 V), an **n-type inversion channel** forms under the gate, connecting the n⁺ emitter to the n⁻ drift region. Electrons flow from emitter through the channel into the n⁻ drift and across the **forward-biased J₃ junction (n⁻/p⁺ collector)**. The p⁺ collector then **injects holes** into the n⁻ drift region. These holes, together with the electrons, raise the **free-carrier concentration** in the n⁻ drift by 2–3 orders of magnitude. The drift region, normally a high-resistivity layer, becomes a **highly conductive** plasma. This phenomenon is called **conductivity modulation**, and it is the reason the IGBT achieves a very low on-state voltage drop (~2 V) at high blocking voltages — something a power MOSFET cannot do.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Model Solution Table (7 marks — 1.75 each):**

| Parameter | Power BJT | Power MOSFET | **IGBT** |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Drive method | Current-controlled (high base current) | Voltage-controlled (gate draws no DC current) | **Voltage-controlled (MOS gate)** |
| (ii) On-state voltage drop at 600 V, 100 A | ~2.0 V (good) | ~6.0 V (poor — drift resistance dominates) | **~2.0 V (excellent, thanks to conductivity modulation)** |
| (iii) Typical switching frequency | ≤ 5 kHz (limited by stored minority charge) | ≤ 1 MHz (excellent, majority-carrier) | **10 – 50 kHz (limited by tail current)** |
| (iv) Typical applications | Old audio amplifiers, low-power regulators | SMPS, low-voltage DC–DC converters, CPU power | **AC motor drives, traction inverters, HVDC, solar inverters, UPS** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Comparison Answer:**
> A frequent error is to write only *advantages* of the IGBT and ignore its *disadvantages* (notably the **tail current**, **latch-up risk**, and **slightly higher switching loss than MOSFETs**). Examiners award marks for balanced comparison. Mention that IGBTs cannot be paralleled as easily as MOSFETs and that for voltages below 200 V, MOSFETs are still preferred.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU revision checklist — go through this list before entering the exam hall.**

- **IGBT = MOSFET input + BJT output (PNP).** This two-transistor analogy is mandatory to draw for any structural question.
- **Three terminals:** Gate (G), Collector (C), Emitter (E). Note that the *collector* is the high-voltage terminal (not the drain), which is unique to the IGBT.
- **Gate is insulated** — no DC gate current. Drive is a voltage source of typically **+15 V ON / 0 V or −15 V OFF**.
- **Threshold voltage $V_{GE(th)}$** is 4 V – 6 V; always over-drive to 15 V to minimize $R_{DS(on)}$ (here, $V_{CE(sat)}$).
- **Conductivity modulation** is the single most important physical concept. The p⁺ collector injects holes into the n⁻ drift → on-state resistance drops by 2–3 orders of magnitude.
- **Tail current** during turn-off is the IGBT's Achilles' heel. It is the slow decay of stored minority carriers in the n⁻ drift region. Field-stop (FS) and NPT structures reduce this.
- **Latch-up** of the parasitic thyristor is a destructive failure. Modern IGBTs prevent it by emitter-shorting and heavy p⁺ body doping.
- **Standard switching loss formula:** $E_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{6} V_{CC} I_C (t_{on} + t_{off})$ for linear-ramp hard switching. The 1/6 factor is non-negotiable.
- **Conduction loss:** $P_{cond} = D \cdot V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C$.
- **Average switching loss:** $P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$.
- **Junction temperature:** $T_j = T_a + P_{total}(R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-a)})$, must be < 150 °C.
- **Voltage ratings:** 600 V (low-power drives), 1200 V (industrial drives), 1700 V (traction), 3300 V / 6500 V (HVDC).
- **Frequency range:** IGBTs are typically used from **1 kHz to 50 kHz**. Below 1 kHz → use thyristor; above 50 kHz → use MOSFET.
- **Freewheeling diode (FWD):** Every IGBT module includes an anti-parallel diode. Always include it in any circuit diagram.
- **Gate drive requirements:** Use a dedicated gate-driver IC (e.g., HCPL-316J, UCC21750) with isolated power supply. Never drive the gate with a logic pin directly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MCTs - Static and Dynamic Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MCTs - Static and Dynamic Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — MOSFET-Controlled Thyristor (MCT)

The **MOSFET-Controlled Thyristor (MCT)** is a power semiconductor switching device that monolithically integrates a **four-layer PNPN thyristor structure** with one or two **MOSFET control cells** (an "ON-FET" and an "OFF-FET") built directly into each emitter finger of the thyristor. It was pioneered by **General Electric / Harris Semiconductor (1992)** as a hybrid that seeks to combine the **high input impedance and ease of gate drive of a MOSFET** with the **high current density, high blocking voltage, and low forward drop of a thyristor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** MCT is classified as a **"microthyristor"** with voltage-controlled gate. It belongs to the family of **Gate-Commutated Devices** and is the structural ancestor of modern **Gate-Commutated Thyristors (GCTs)** and **IGCTs**.

### 1.2 Device Variants

| Variant | Control MOSFET Channel | Minority Carriers | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P-MCT** | P-channel (turns ON with negative gate) | Minority hole injection | Most common, higher surge current |
| **N-MCT** | N-channel (turns ON with positive gate) | Minority electron injection | Faster turn-off, lower on-state loss |

> [!NOTE]
> For the rest of this module, all references to MCT will default to the **P-MCT** variant, since it is the device covered in the KTU prescribed text (Mohamed Rashid, *Power Electronics*, Pearson).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Station Gate" Model

Think of an MCT as a **booster rocket with a MOSFET ignition switch**:

- The **PNPN thyristor body** is the **massive booster rocket** — capable of delivering enormous thrust (current), but very hard to throttle or shut down once ignited.
- The **MOSFET gate** is the **electric ignition key** — flick it one way, and the rocket fires; flick it the other way, and it is snuffed out (commutated).
- The two MOSFETs (ON-FET and OFF-FET) act like a **single dual-throw switch** at the gate: the ON-FET shunts current *away from* the N-emitter to fire the device, while the OFF-FET *shorts* the emitter-base junction to kill it.

So unlike a **conventional SCR** (which once latched on, behaves like a runaway train until the anode current is forced below holding), the MCT is the **first thyristor you can switch OFF simply by applying a small voltage to its gate** — no commutation circuits required.

### 1.4 Why MCT Matters in Modern Power Electronics

- **Voltage ratings up to 1500 V** and **current ratings up to 300 A** per die.
- **dv/dt** capability orders of magnitude higher than SCR (≥ 20 kV/μs).
- **di/dt** capability exceeding **5 kA/μs** — comparable to the fastest GTOs.
- Used historically in **resonant DC link inverters, traction drives, and FACTS controllers** before being superseded by **IGBT** and **IGCT** in most low/medium power applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants / Metrics (per KTU lab data sheets):**
> - On-state voltage drop: **V_T ≈ 1.0 V to 1.5 V** at rated current.
> - Turn-off time: **t_q ≈ 0.5 μs to 2.0 μs**.
> - Maximum controllable current: **I_TGQ ≈ 100 A/cm²** active cell density.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Structure — Cell-Level Anatomy

A single MCT cell (≈ **30 μm × 30 μm** at 4 μm lithography) is a vertical PNPN stack with **two surface MOSFETs** diffused into the P-base layer. Every die contains **~100,000 to 1,000,000 such cells in parallel**, which is why MCTs exhibit the spectacular current handling that MOSFETs cannot match.

The four functional layers and their roles:

1. **N⁺ emitter (cathode)** — the top layer; the OFF-FET shorts this to the P-base to extract carriers during turn-off.
2. **P-base** — the gate region; the ON-FET channel connects N⁺ emitter to the N-drift (bypassing the cathode emitter), which prevents emitter injection and latches the device OFF.
3. **N-drift (N⁻ blocking layer)** — supports the forward/reverse blocking voltage. Thickness sets the breakdown.
4. **P⁺ anode** — bottom layer, provides the regenerative p-n-p action during conduction.

### 2.2 Two MOSFETs Per Cell — Operational Logic

| Action | ON-FET (P-MOS, in P-MCT) | OFF-FET (N-MOS, in P-MCT) | Physical Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Turn-ON** (V_GK = −5 to −15 V) | ON | OFF | ON-FET shunts N⁺ emitter → injects holes into N-drift → regenerative latch fires |
| **Steady ON** | OFF | OFF | Anode current regenerates itself (thyristor mode) |
| **Turn-OFF** (V_GK = +5 to +15 V) | OFF | ON | OFF-FET shorts N⁺ emitter to P-base → cathode emitter shorted → no more electron injection → device falls out of latch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Point:** The OFF-FET does **not** remove the carriers from the drift region (no gate-sweeping effect like in IGBTs). It simply **denies the regenerative loop a fresh supply of carriers**. Commutation is therefore **fast but not gate-sweep assisted**, which limits the maximum frequency to ≈ **100 kHz – 200 kHz** in practical circuits.

### 2.3 Static Characteristics — Definitions and Curves

Static (DC) characteristics describe the device behavior under **steady-state forward and reverse bias**, with no time variation of the gate signal.

#### 2.3.1 Forward Blocking State

With **V_GK = +5 to +15 V** (P-MCT) and **V_AK > 0**, the device is in **forward blocking mode**. The anode–cathode voltage is supported by the reverse-biased **J2 (P-base / N-drift) junction**. The leakage current is on the order of **μA to mA**.

> **Knee of the curve = forward breakover voltage V_(BO)** — typically 1000 V to 1500 V for medium-power MCTs.

#### 2.3.2 Reverse Blocking State

With **V_AK < 0** and **V_GK = 0** (or +ve), the **J3 (P⁺ anode / N-drift)** junction is reverse biased. MCTs are **symmetric blocking** — i.e., they can block roughly the same magnitude in either polarity (a key difference from asymmetric IGBTs).

#### 2.3.3 On-State (Conduction) Characteristic

Once latched, the I-V curve is **rectifier-like** with a small forward drop. It is well modelled by a piecewise linear model:

$$
V_T = V_{T0} + r_T \cdot I_T
$$

where:
- **V_{T0}** ≈ **0.7 V** (junction turn-on offset)
- **r_T** ≈ **1 to 10 mΩ** (slope resistance, scales inversely with active die area)

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike an IGBT, the MCT on-state drop is **almost temperature-independent** because the regenerative loop produces both electron and hole injection, which tends to self-compensate.

#### 2.3.4 Holding and Latching Currents

- **I_H (Holding current)** — minimum anode current below which the device **falls out of latch** even with gate drive present. For MCT, **I_H ≈ 50 mA to 500 mA**, far higher than SCR.
- **I_L (Latching current)** — minimum anode current at which the device **successfully latches on** with a gate pulse. Typical: **0.5 A to 5 A** for a 100 A MCT.

### 2.4 Dynamic Characteristics — Time-Domain Behaviour

Dynamic characteristics describe the **transient behaviour** during turn-on and turn-off transitions, including switching losses and SOA.

#### 2.4.1 Turn-On Process (P-MCT)

1. **Delay time t_d(on)** ≈ 50 ns — gate voltage rises to threshold of ON-FET.
2. **Rise time t_r** ≈ 200 ns — anode current ramps up; regenerative loop builds.
3. **Spread time t_s** ≈ 500 ns — final cells in the die start conducting (last cells to turn on suffer the highest localized heating).
4. **Total t_on** = t_d(on) + t_r + t_s ≈ **0.5 μs to 1.5 μs**.

#### 2.4.2 Turn-Off Process (P-MCT)

1. **Storage time t_s** ≈ 1 μs — time for the stored minority carriers in the N-drift to fall to the level where the anode current can be supported solely by the residual N-MOS action.
2. **Fall time t_f** ≈ 300 ns — anode current ramps to zero.
3. **Tail time t_t** ≈ 0.5 μs — small residual tail of current due to slow carrier recombination in the wide N-drift.
4. **Total t_off** = t_s + t_f + t_t ≈ **1.5 μs to 3.0 μs**.

> [!WARNING]
> The **tail current** of an MCT is its **biggest enemy** — it represents power dissipation (V_AK × I_tail) that is *not* used to transfer energy to the load, and is a major contributor to **turn-off switching loss P_off**.

#### 2.4.3 Safe Operating Area (SOA)

The MCT SOA is plotted on log-log axes of **V_AK vs I_A**, bounded by:

- **Thermal limit** (steady-state average power)
- **Reverse bias SOA (RBSOA)** — max I_A as a function of reapplied V_AK after turn-off (limited by dv/dt-induced re-trigger).
- **Short-circuit SOA (SCSOA)** — duration the device can survive a shorted load.

> Unlike SCRs, MCTs do **not** have a hard **dv/dt limit** because the OFF-FET keeps the emitter shorted whenever the gate is held positive, suppressing false triggering.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Symbol | Equation | Meaning / Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | On-state drop | $V_T = V_{T0} + r_T \cdot I_T$ | Forward voltage drop (V) | $I_T > I_L$ |
| 2 | Conduction loss | $P_{cond} = V_{T0} \cdot I_{T(avg)} + r_T \cdot I_{T(rms)}^{2}$ | Average dissipation (W) | Continuous duty |
| 3 | Turn-on loss | $P_{on} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{AK} \cdot I_A \cdot t_{on} \cdot f_s$ | Per-cycle turn-on energy × freq | $0 < V_{AK} < V_{BO}$ |
| 4 | Turn-off loss | $P_{off} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{AK} \cdot I_A \cdot t_{off} \cdot f_s$ | Dominant loss in hard-switched MCT | Tail current included |
| 5 | Total switch loss | $P_{sw} = P_{on} + P_{off}$ | Switching portion of P_total | Hard switching |
| 6 | Total dissipation | $P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ | Junction heating source (W) | $P_{total} < \frac{T_{J,max} - T_{C}}{R_{th,JC}}$ |
| 7 | Thermal limit | $P_{max} = \frac{T_{J,max} - T_{C}}{R_{\theta JC}}$ | Max allowed dissipation (W) | $T_J \leq 125^{\circ}\text{C}$ typ. |
| 8 | dv/dt limit | $\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I_{d,displ}}{C_J}$ | Avoidance of parasitic turn-on | $< 20 \text{ kV/}\mu\text{s}$ typical |
| 9 | Turn-on time | $t_{on} = t_{d(on)} + t_r + t_{sp}$ | Total turn-on duration (s) | P-MCT, V_GK = −10 V |
| 10 | Turn-off time | $t_{off} = t_s + t_f + t_{tail}$ | Total turn-off duration (s) | P-MCT, V_GK = +10 V |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

MCTs were deployed (and still appear in legacy textbooks) in:

- **Resonant DC link (RDCL) inverters** — the low tail current allows zero-voltage switching (ZVS).
- **Traction choppers** for electric locomotives — high di/dt and rugged SOA.
- **Static VAR compensators (SVCs)** — symmetric blocking suits AC switch applications.
- **High-frequency induction heating inverters** — switch at 50–100 kHz where IGBT losses become dominant.
- **Snubberless hard-switched inverters** — high dv/dt and SCSOA enable >100 kVA/in³ power density.

> Despite these advantages, **production was discontinued by the late 1990s** by most vendors (Harris → Microsemi → ams) because the **OFF-FET cell density was limited** and the device could not be made as easily scalable as the IGBT. The KTU 2024 syllabus nevertheless retains MCT because the **principle of MOSFET-controlled thyristor action** is foundational to understanding **GCT/IGCT/ETO** devices that succeeded it.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the MCT On-State Drop (Piecewise Linear Model)

**Problem:** Derive the linearized on-state characteristic $V_T = V_{T0} + r_T \cdot I_T$ for a P-MCT carrying anode current $I_T$ at junction temperature $T_J$.

**Starting physics:** The thyristor body, once latched, is equivalent to a **p-i-n diode** in series with two emitter-short resistance terms. The N-drift region of thickness $W$ and electron/hole mobilities $\mu_n$, $\mu_p$ has a conductivity modulation profile that we approximate as ambipolar.

**Step 1: Charge carrier ambipolar diffusion length.**
The drift region of an MCT has a high level of carrier injection. The ambipolar diffusion length is:

$$
L_a = \sqrt{D_a \cdot \tau_{HL}}
$$

where $D_a = \frac{2 D_n D_p}{D_n + D_p}$ is the **ambipolar diffusion coefficient** and $\tau_{HL}$ is the high-level carrier lifetime.

**Step 2: Conductivity modulation.**
The drift-region resistance per unit area is:

$$
R_{drift} = \frac{W}{q \cdot (\mu_n + \mu_p) \cdot \bar{p}}
$$

where $\bar{p}$ is the average injected hole concentration. The junction built-in potential appears as the offset $V_{T0}$.

**Step 3: Linearize around operating point.**
At high forward bias, the diode equation gives:

$$
V_J = \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_T}{I_S}\right) \approx V_{T0} + \frac{kT}{q I_T} \cdot I_T = V_{T0} + r_{dyn} \cdot I_T
$$

Combining with the drift-region drop:

$$
\boxed{\,V_T = V_{T0} + r_T \cdot I_T\,}
$$

with $r_T = r_{drift} + r_{dyn}$, typically **3 to 8 mΩ** for a 100 A / 1200 V MCT die.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Switching Loss in a Hard-Switched MCT Chopper

**Problem:** Compute the total switching loss per switching cycle for an MCT controlling an inductive load of current $I_L$ across a DC link of voltage $V_{DC}$, switching at frequency $f_s$.

**Step 1: Idealized waveforms.**
During turn-on, voltage falls linearly from $V_{DC}$ to 0 in time $t_{on}$ while current rises linearly from 0 to $I_L$. During turn-off, voltage rises linearly from 0 to $V_{DC}$ in time $t_{off}$ while current falls linearly from $I_L$ to 0.

**Step 2: Turn-on energy (single linear overlap).**

$$
E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt
$$

With $v(t) = V_{DC}\!\left(1 - \tfrac{t}{t_{on}}\right)$ and $i(t) = I_L \tfrac{t}{t_{on}}$:

$$
E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} V_{DC}\!\left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{on}}\right) \cdot I_L \frac{t}{t_{on}} \, dt
$$

$$
E_{on} = \frac{V_{DC} I_L}{t_{on}^{2}} \int_{0}^{t_{on}} (t_{on} \, t - t^{2}) \, dt
$$

$$
E_{on} = \frac{V_{DC} I_L}{t_{on}^{2}} \left[ \frac{t_{on}^{3}}{2} - \frac{t_{on}^{3}}{3} \right]
$$

$$
E_{on} = \frac{V_{DC} I_L}{t_{on}^{2}} \cdot \frac{t_{on}^{3}}{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_{on} = \tfrac{1}{6}\, V_{DC} \, I_L \, t_{on}\,}
$$

> This is **half** the standard rectangular assumption because the overlap is triangular, not rectangular.

**Step 3: Turn-off energy.** By symmetry of the linear overlap (and adding the tail term):

$$
E_{off} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, V_{DC} \, I_L \, t_{off} + E_{tail}
$$

For a **first-order approximation neglecting tail** (valid for circuit-level design):

$$
E_{off} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\, V_{DC} \, I_L \, t_{off}
$$

The factor **½** vs **1/6** arises because in turn-off the voltage is rising while current is still near $I_L$ for most of $t_{off}$, giving a near-rectangular overlap.

**Step 4: Total switching loss.**

$$
P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_s
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{sw} = f_s \left[ \tfrac{1}{6}\, V_{DC} \, I_L \, t_{on} + \tfrac{1}{2}\, V_{DC} \, I_L \, t_{off} + E_{tail} \right]\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** Take $V_{DC} = 600 \text{ V}$, $I_L = 100 \text{ A}$, $t_{on} = 1 \mu\text{s}$, $t_{off} = 2 \mu\text{s}$, $f_s = 20 \text{ kHz}$, $E_{tail} = 2 \text{ mJ}$.
> - $E_{on} = (1/6)(600)(100)(1\times10^{-6}) = 10 \text{ mJ}$
> - $E_{off} = (1/2)(600)(100)(2\times10^{-6}) + 2 = 62 \text{ mJ}$
> - $P_{sw} = 72 \text{ mJ} \times 20{,}000 = 1440 \text{ W}$
> - The **turn-off loss dominates** (≈ 86 %), which is the typical reality for MCT/IGBT circuits and the reason soft-switching is pursued.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Junction Temperature Rise

**Problem:** A MCT die dissipates $P_{total} = 80 \text{ W}$ in a TO-247 package with $R_{\theta JC} = 0.5 \text{ K/W}$ and $R_{\theta CS} = 0.1 \text{ K/W}$ (insulating thermal pad). The heatsink is $R_{\theta SA} = 0.4 \text{ K/W}$ and ambient is $T_A = 40^\circ\text{C}$. Compute the junction temperature.

**Step 1: Equivalent thermal network.**
$ T_J - T_A = P_{total} \cdot (R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}) $

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
T_J = 40 + 80 \cdot (0.5 + 0.1 + 0.4)
$$

$$
T_J = 40 + 80 \cdot 1.0 = 40 + 80
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_J = 120^{\circ}\text{C}\,}
$$

**Step 3: Verify against SOA.**
For a silicon MCT, $T_{J,max} = 125^{\circ}\text{C}$. Margin = 5 K — **acceptable but tight**. A designer must either reduce $P_{total}$ (by lowering $f_s$) or upgrade the heatsink.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose MCT over SCR / IGBT

| Criterion | SCR | BJT | **MCT** | IGBT | GTO |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gate drive power | Medium (pulse) | High (current) | **Very low (voltage)** | Very low (voltage) | Low (pulsed current) |
| Turn-off method | External commutation | External | **Gate pulse** | Gate pulse | External capacitor (snubber) |
| Conduction drop | Lowest (~1.2 V) | Medium (~1.5 V) | **Low (~1.0 V)** | Medium (~2.0 V) | Lowest (~1.0 V) |
| Switching freq. | < 400 Hz | < 5 kHz | **< 100 kHz** | < 50 kHz | < 1 kHz |
| Symmetric blocking | Yes (TRIAC) | No | **Yes** | No | Yes |
| dv/dt immunity | Poor (R-C snubber) | Good | **Excellent** | Excellent | Poor |
| Snubber | Large | Medium | **None / very small** | Small | Very large |
| Status in 2024 | Mature | Legacy | **Discontinued (academic)** | Dominant | Niche high-power |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Single MCT Cell Cross-Section (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Top Metal Anode Contact] --> B[N+ Emitter Cathode Layer]
    B --> C[P-Base Control Layer]
    C --> D[N- Drift Blocking Region]
    D --> E[P+ Anode Layer]
    E --> F[Bottom Metal Anode Contact]

    G[ON-FET P-Channel Gate] -.turn-on.-> B
    H[OFF-FET N-Channel Gate] -.turn-off.-> B

    I[Gate Drive -10 V to +10 V] --> G
    I --> H
    I -->|V_AK positive| F

    J[Regenerative Loop: N+ emitter injects electrons to P-base then to N- drift] -.latch.-> B
    K[Emitter Short: OFF-FET shorts N+ to P-base, kills injection] -.commutate.-> B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — MCT Static and Dynamic Characteristic Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Device Under Test]) --> Q1{V_AK polarity?}
    Q1 -->|V_AK greater than 0| Fwd
    Q1 -->|V_AK less than 0| Rev

    Fwd{V_GK state?}
    Fwd -->|V_GK positive| FB[Forward Blocking Mode]
    Fwd -->|V_GK negative| FO[Forward Conduction Mode]
    FB -->|V_AK reaches V_BO| FO

    Rev[Reverse Blocking Mode] -->|V_AK below V_RRM| RB[Blocked]

    FO{Current behavior?}
    FO -->|I_T greater than I_L| LC[Latched ON]
    FO -->|I_T between I_H and I_L| TF[Transitional - weakly latched]
    FO -->|I_T less than I_H| FB

    LC -->|V_GK positive pulse| CO[Commutating - Turn OFF Begins]
    CO --> ST[Storage Time t_s]
    ST --> FT[Fall Time t_f]
    FT --> TT[Tail Time t_t]
    TT --> FB
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — MCT Loss Decomposition Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Total Power Loss P_total] --> B[Conduction Loss P_cond]
    A --> C[Switching Loss P_sw]

    B --> B1[V_T0 times I_avg]
    B --> B2[r_T times I_rms squared]

    C --> C1[Turn-on Loss P_on]
    C --> C2[Turn-off Loss P_off]
    C --> C3[Tail Loss P_tail]

    C1 --> C1a[1/6 V_DC I_L t_on f_s]
    C2 --> C2a[1/2 V_DC I_L t_off f_s]
    C3 --> C3a[Integral of V_AK times I_tail dt times f_s]

    A --> D[Junction Temperature T_J]
    D --> E{T_J less than T_Jmax?}
    E -->|yes| SAFE[Within SOA]
    E -->|no| FAIL[Thermal Failure]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — MCT-Based Resonant DC Link Inverter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    DC[DC Link V_DC] --> INV[MCT H-Bridge Inverter]
    INV --> AC[AC Load]

    CTRL[Gate Drive Controller] -->|V_GK -10 V| ON[Turn-ON Pulse]
    CTRL -->|V_GK +10 V| OFF[Turn-OFF Pulse]
    ON --> INV
    OFF --> INV

    L[RDC Link Inductor L_r] --> INV
    C[RDC Link Capacitor C_r] --> INV
    SN[Zero-Voltage Detection] --> CTRL
```

> **Why this matters:** The MCT's low tail current and high dv/dt make it the *original* preferred switch for **Resonant DC Link (RDCL)** inverters proposed by Deepak Divan (1986) — the same topology is now used with IGBTs but the MCT was the first commercially demonstrated device to do it.
