## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model)]**
*With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the internal structure of a P-channel MOSFET-Controlled Thyristor (P-MCT). What is the role of the two MOSFETs integrated into the cell?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A P-MCT cell is a vertical four-layer (N⁺-P-N⁻-P⁺) thyristor structure. The top N⁺ layer acts as the cathode emitter, the P-base is the control layer, the N⁻ drift supports the blocking voltage, and the P⁺ bottom is the anode. **Two MOSFETs are integrated into the P-base:** the **ON-FET (P-channel)** shunts the N⁺ emitter to the N⁻ drift when the gate is pulsed negative, diverting emitter current and **breaking the regenerative loop** — wait, more precisely, it actually **enhances** the regenerative loop to *initiate* turn-on. The **OFF-FET (N-channel)**, when pulsed positive, **shorts the N⁺ emitter to the P-base**, denying the cathode emitter a path to inject electrons, thereby forcing the thyristor out of latch and commutating the device OFF. (3 marks)

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model)]**
*Define "holding current" and "latching current" as applied to an MCT. How do they differ from those of a conventional SCR?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Latching current $I_L$**: minimum anode current required for the MCT to **successfully enter latched conduction** after a gate trigger pulse is applied.
- **Holding current $I_H$**: minimum anode current required for the MCT to **remain in latched conduction** with the gate drive removed.
- Compared to a conventional SCR, the MCT has **larger** $I_L$ and $I_H$ (typically 0.5–5 A and 50–500 mA respectively) because the additional MOSFET cell structure diverts emitter current, requiring a stronger regenerative loop to latch. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

---

**Question A — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Draw the equivalent circuit and **two-transistor analogue** of an MCT. Using this analogue, derive the condition for latch-up in terms of the common-base current gains $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ of the two transistors. Explain the **turn-on** and **turn-off** mechanism in terms of this analogue.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A P-MCT is used as a switch in a **100 kW hard-switching chopper** with $V_{DC} = 500$ V, switching frequency $f_s = 20$ kHz, and an inductive load of $I_L = 200$ A. The MCT datasheet gives $V_{T0} = 0.9$ V, $r_T = 6 \text{ m}\Omega$, $t_{on} = 1.5 \mu\text{s}$, $t_{off} = 2.0 \mu\text{s}$, and a tail loss $E_{tail} = 8 \text{ mJ}$ per cycle. Compute (i) the conduction loss assuming a duty ratio $D = 0.5$, and (ii) the total switching loss. Comment on the dominant loss component.

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Two-transistor analogue:**
The four-layer PNPN structure of the MCT is split into a p-n-p transistor (top, with emitter at the P-base) and an n-p-n transistor (bottom, with emitter at the N⁺ cathode), with the collector of each feeding the base of the other.

```
Anode P+ ──[p-n-p]── P-base ──[n-p-n]── N+ Cathode
              α₁=β₁/(1+β₁)       α₂=β₂/(1+β₂)
```

**Latch-up condition derivation:**

The collector current of the p-n-p transistor is:
$$
I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CBO1}
$$

The collector current of the n-p-n transistor is:
$$
I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_K + I_{CBO2}
$$

The total anode current is $I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2}$, and the cathode current is $I_K = I_{C1} + I_{C2} = I_A$.

Substituting:
$$
I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_A + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}
$$

$$
I_A (1 - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2) = I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}
$$

$$
I_A = \frac{I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}
$$

As $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, the denominator $\to 0$ and $I_A \to \infty$. This is the **latch-up condition:**

$$
\boxed{\,\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1\,}
$$

**Turn-on:** The ON-FET forces a displacement current into the p-n-p base, increasing $\alpha_1$ until $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ and regenerative action begins. Once started, the gate can be removed and the loop sustains itself.

**Turn-off:** The OFF-FET shorts the N⁺ emitter to the P-base, dropping $\alpha_2$ below the critical value, the loop can no longer sustain itself, and anode current decays to zero. (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Conduction loss:**

$$
P_{cond} = V_{T0} \cdot I_{T(avg)} + r_T \cdot I_{T(rms)}^{2}
$$

For a chopper with $D = 0.5$ and constant load current $I_L$ (ripple-less inductor):
$$
I_{T(avg)} = D \cdot I_L = 0.5 \times 200 = 100 \text{ A}
$$
$$
I_{T(rms)} = \sqrt{D} \cdot I_L = \sqrt{0.5} \times 200 = 141.4 \text{ A}
$$

$$
P_{cond} = 0.9 \times 100 + 0.006 \times (141.4)^{2}
$$

$$
P_{cond} = 90 + 0.006 \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
P_{cond} = 90 + 120 = 210 \text{ W}
$$

**(ii) Switching loss:**

$$
E_{on} = \tfrac{1}{6} V_{DC} I_L t_{on} = \tfrac{1}{6} \times 500 \times 200 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.025 \text{ J} = 25 \text{ mJ}
$$

$$
E_{off} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} I_L t_{off} + E_{tail} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 500 \times 200 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-6} + 0.008
$$

$$
E_{off} = 0.100 + 0.008 = 0.108 \text{ J} = 108 \text{ mJ}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_s = (0.025 + 0.108) \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.133 \times 20{,}000 = 2660 \text{ W}
$$

**Comment on dominant loss:**
The switching loss $P_{sw} = 2660$ W vastly exceeds the conduction loss $P_{cond} = 210$ W (≈ 92.7 % of total). The **turn-off loss dominates** ($E_{off}/E_{sw} = 81\%$) due to the high $V_{DC} \times I_L$ overlap and the **tail current contribution**. This is the classic motivation for **resonant (ZVS) switching** of MCT-class devices. (7 marks)

---

**Question B — Choice 2** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **static I-V characteristics** of a P-MCT, clearly identifying the **forward blocking, forward conduction, reverse blocking, and breakover regions**. Sketch the characteristics and mark the holding current and latching current points.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the **dynamic characteristics** of an MCT during turn-on and turn-off. With the help of waveforms, define $t_d$, $t_r$, $t_s$, $t_f$, and **tail time**. Why is the **tail current** the limiting factor for high-frequency MCT operation, and what is the standard engineering workaround?

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Static I-V characteristics (regions marked on a sketch):**

1. **Forward Blocking Region** ($V_{AK} > 0$, $V_{GK} = +10$ V for P-MCT): A small leakage current (μA to mA) flows. The P-base/N-drift junction J2 is reverse biased. This extends from origin up to the forward breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ (typically 1000–1500 V for medium-power MCTs).

2. **Forward Breakover Point** ($V_{AK} = V_{BO}$): The leakage rises sharply and the device switches into conduction (avalanche-triggered latch).

3. **Forward Conduction Region** ($V_{AK} > 0$, $V_{GK} = -10$ V applied, then can be removed): The device carries anode current $I_T$ with a small forward drop $V_T = V_{T0} + r_T I_T$, typically 1.0–1.5 V at rated current.

4. **Reverse Blocking Region** ($V_{AK} < 0$, $V_{GK} = 0$ or +10 V): A symmetrical blocking capability with reverse leakage in μA. The P⁺/N-drift junction J3 is reverse biased.

**Holding current $I_H$ and latching current $I_L$:**
- On the I-V curve, $I_L$ is the current at which the device **initially reaches** the conduction characteristic after a gate trigger.
- $I_H$ is the current at which the device **leaves** the conduction characteristic and returns to blocking if anode current is reduced.
- For MCT, $I_L > I_H$ always, and both are **higher** than equivalent SCR values. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Turn-on waveform (gate pulse negative, then anode current rises):**
1. **Delay time $t_d$** (≈ 50 ns): time from gate pulse edge to 10% rise of anode current.
2. **Rise time $t_r$** (≈ 200 ns): time for anode current to rise from 10% to 90% of final value.
3. **Spread time $t_{sp}$** (≈ 500 ns): time for **all cells in the die** to turn on uniformly.

**Turn-off waveform (gate pulse positive, then anode current falls):**
1. **Storage time $t_s$** (≈ 1 μs): time for the stored minority carriers in the N-drift to be removed via the OFF-FET action.
2. **Fall time $t_f$** (≈ 300 ns): time for anode current to fall from 90% to 10% of its on-state value.
3. **Tail time $t_{tail}$** (≈ 500 ns): residual slow-decaying current due to carriers deep in the wide N-drift.

**Why tail current limits high-frequency operation:**
During the tail interval, the device is **partially on** (low $V_{AK}$, significant $I_A$), so the **$V_{AK} \times I_A$ product is non-zero**, generating **turn-off switching loss**. This loss scales linearly with $f_s$:

$$
P_{tail} = V_{AK} \cdot I_{tail,avg} \cdot t_{tail} \cdot f_s
$$

Above ≈ 100 kHz, the tail loss becomes comparable to the entire power throughput of the device.

**Standard engineering workaround:**
Use **resonant (zero-voltage or zero-current) switching** topologies such as the **Resonant DC Link Inverter** (Divan, 1986), where the MCT is switched only when either $V_{AK}$ or $I_A$ is naturally zero, eliminating the $V \times I$ overlap and the tail-loss penalty. (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common MCT Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing ON-FET and OFF-FET roles** (most common, costs 1–2 marks). Remember: ON-FET **initiates** latch, OFF-FET **kills** it. They are NOT a complementary pair in the usual push-pull sense.
> 2. **Forgetting the regenerative loop** when describing turn-on. Many students say the MOSFET "drives the current" — wrong. The MOSFET only **primes the p-n-p transistor**; the n-p-n takes over and the pair then sustains conduction without the gate.
> 3. **Using the SCR holding-current formula for MCT** — the MCT's $I_H$ is much larger; the gate shorts the emitter, so the loop is harder to sustain.
> 4. **Mixing up the linear overlap factor** in switching loss: turn-on uses **1/6** (triangular overlap), turn-off uses **1/2** (near-rectangular overlap). Writing both as 1/2 is a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Omitting the tail current** in turn-off energy. Examiners specifically look for this term.
> 6. **Forgetting** that MCT is **symmetrically blocking** (can block both forward and reverse V_AK) — this differentiates it from asymmetric IGBT.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for MCT — Static and Dynamic Characteristics**

**Device Identity**
- ☐ MCT = MOSFET-Controlled Thyristor; combines MOSFET gate with thyristor body.
- ☐ Two MOSFETs per cell: **ON-FET (P-MOS)** and **OFF-FET (N-MOS)** for P-MCT.
- ☐ Voltage-controlled gate drive: −5 to −15 V to turn ON, +5 to +15 V to turn OFF (P-MCT).
- ☐ Symmetrically blocking device (forward and reverse V_AK capability).

**Static Characteristics**
- ☐ **Forward blocking** region: V_AK > 0, V_GK positive; J2 reverse biased.
- ☐ **Forward breakover** at V_BO; **latching current I_L** at first entry to conduction.
- ☐ **On-state drop** $V_T = V_{T0} + r_T I_T$; typical 1.0–1.5 V at rated I_T.
- ☐ **Holding current I_H** at exit from conduction (I_H << I_L).
- ☐ **Reverse blocking**: J3 reverse biased, leakage in μA range.
- ☐ Both I_L and I_H higher than equivalent SCR values.

**Dynamic Characteristics**
- ☐ **Turn-on time** = $t_{d(on)} + t_r + t_{sp}$ ≈ 0.5–1.5 μs.
- ☐ **Turn-off time** = $t_s + t_f + t_{tail}$ ≈ 1.5–3.0 μs.
- ☐ **Tail time** ≈ 0.5 μs is the biggest bottleneck for high-frequency operation.
- ☐ dv/dt immunity: > 20 kV/μs because OFF-FET shorts the emitter.
- ☐ di/dt capability: > 5 kA/μs (limited by spread time).
- ☐ SOA includes thermal, RBSOA, and SCSOA limits.

**Losses (exam-critical formulas)**
- ☐ $P_{cond} = V_{T0} I_{T(avg)} + r_T I_{T(rms)}^{2}$.
- ☐ $E_{on} = \tfrac{1}{6} V_{DC} I_L t_{on}$ (triangular overlap).
- ☐ $E_{off} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} I_L t_{off} + E_{tail}$ (rectangular + tail).
- ☐ $P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) f_s$; turn-off typically dominates.

**Theory Foundations**
- ☐ Two-transistor analogue: p-n-p and n-p-n with collectors cross-coupled to bases.
- ☐ Latch-up condition: $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$.
- ☐ OFF-FET action reduces $\alpha_2$ below critical → device falls out of latch.
- ☐ No gate-sweeping of carriers → limited to ≈ 100 kHz max practical switching.

**Applications & Status**
- ☐ Resonant DC Link inverters, traction choppers, SVCs, induction heating.
- ☐ Discontinued commercially (late 1990s), superseded by IGBT and IGCT.
- ☐ Principle is foundational to understanding modern GCT/IGCT devices.

**Comparison Anchors**
- ☐ MCT vs SCR: MCT has **gate turn-off**, SCR needs commutation circuit.
- ☐ MCT vs IGBT: MCT has **symmetric blocking and lower V_T**, IGBT is asymmetric with higher V_T.
- ☐ MCT vs GTO: MCT has **voltage gate drive**, GTO needs large pulsed current and snubbers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protection Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Protection Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In Power Electronics, **Protection Circuits** refer to the auxiliary electrical networks designed to safeguard semiconductor power devices (such as SCRs, TRIACs, MOSFETs, IGBTs, and GTOs) from abnormal operating conditions. These abnormal conditions arise due to excessive voltage, current, temperature, or rate of change of voltage/current, all of which can permanently destroy the device.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, protection circuits encompass the design of snubber networks, crowbar circuits, overvoltage/overcurrent clamps, thermal management systems (heat sinks), and gate/base drive protections. Their primary objective is to ensure the device operates within its **Safe Operating Area (SOA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safe Operating Area (SOA):** The SOA is a multi-dimensional boundary defined by maximum junction temperature ($T_{j,max}$), peak current ($I_{peak}$), breakdown voltage ($V_{BR}$), and power dissipation ($P_D$) within which a semiconductor device can operate without suffering permanent thermal or electrical damage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 1 of PEICT411 mandates explicit study of protection schemes because power devices have very low thermal mass and small junction area, making them highly susceptible to transient stresses during switching operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a high-performance sports car. The car is the power semiconductor—fast, powerful, and capable of great feats. But without **seatbelts (fuses)**, **airbags (snubbers)**, **speed limiters (current clamps)**, and a **cooling system (heat sinks)**, even a minor accident or overheating could destroy the engine in seconds.

**Real-world analogy: The Household MCB**
Every home has a **Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB)** that trips when current exceeds a safe limit. In power electronics, a protection circuit plays the same role—but on a microsecond timescale and with far greater precision. When you turn off an inductive load (like a motor), the collapsing magnetic field can generate a voltage spike of hundreds of volts. A protection circuit "absorbs" this spike before it reaches the switching transistor.

### Why Protection is Critical

| Threat | Physical Consequence | Typical Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Overvoltage ($V > V_{BR}$) | Avalanche breakdown, device short | Inductive load turn-off |
| Overcurrent ($I > I_{max}$) | Thermal runaway, melting | Short circuit, shoot-through |
| Overheating ($T_j > 150°C$) | Bond wire fusing, die fracture | Excessive conduction loss |
| High $dv/dt$ | False triggering, latch-up | Fast switching transients |
| High $di/dt$ | Localized hot spots, filament formation | Diode reverse recovery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Most silicon power devices are rated for a maximum junction temperature of **$T_{j,max} = 150°C$** (industrial) or **$175°C$** (automotive/military grade). Exceeding this for even milliseconds causes permanent degradation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards

- **Thermal resistance unit:** °C/W (degree Celsius per watt)
- **Junction-to-case thermal resistance ($R_{th(j-c)}$):** typically **0.1 to 2 °C/W** for power modules
- **Junction-to-ambient ($R_{th(j-a)}$):** typically **20 to 60 °C/W** without heat sink
- **Standard creepage distance:** **2.5 mm/kV** for PCB design (per IEC 60664-1)
- **Snubber resistor power rating:** must be derated by **factor of 2** for reliability
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Protection Circuits

Protection circuits in power electronics are systematically classified into **five functional categories**:

1. **Voltage Protection** — Limits voltage across device terminals.
2. **Current Protection** — Limits current through device.
3. **Thermal Protection** — Manages junction temperature.
4. **Gate/Base Drive Protection** — Prevents false triggering and ensures clean switching.
5. **dv/dt and di/dt Protection** — Controls rate of change to avoid dynamic breakdown.

## 2.2 Overvoltage Protection — Detailed Mechanism

When a current-carrying inductor is suddenly interrupted (e.g., a thyristor turning off), the rate of change of current produces an inductive voltage spike:

$$v_L = L \frac{di}{dt}$$

If $di/dt$ is very large (which it is during fast switching), $v_L$ can exceed the device's blocking voltage. Protection is achieved by:

### (a) Metal Oxide Varistor (MOV) Clamping
- A MOV is a non-linear resistor whose resistance drops sharply above a threshold voltage.
- Acts as a **voltage-dependent switch** — high resistance below clamp voltage, low resistance above.
- Used across AC mains or DC bus for transient suppression.

### (b) Selenium Suppressor
- An older technology; offers gradual clamping characteristic.
- Self-healing after absorbing a transient.

### (c) Crowbar Circuit
- A **dedicated short-circuit path** triggered by a sensing circuit when overvoltage is detected.
- Once activated, a thyristor (SCR) latches ON and dumps the excess energy into a resistor, blowing the fuse.
- The name comes from the tool: a crowbar "drops" across the terminals.

### (d) Snubber Circuits (RC, RCD)
- Connected in **parallel with the switching device**.
- Absorbs switching energy and limits $dv/dt$ and $dv/dt$ stresses.

## 2.3 Snubber Circuit — The Most Important Topic in KTU

A **snubber** is a small auxiliary network placed across a semiconductor device to **shape the switching trajectory**, protecting it from simultaneous high voltage and high current (which would exceed SOA).

### Types of Snubbers

| Snubber Type | Symbol | Function | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RC Snubber** | Resistor + Capacitor in series | Limits $dv/dt$ across device | Thyristor commutation |
| **RCD Snubber** | Resistor + Capacitor + Diode | Dissipates turn-off energy, limits turn-on $di/dt$ | Transistor (BJT, IGBT) switching |
| **Discharge Resistor Snubber** | R-D across device | Limits discharge current | High-frequency inverters |
| **Polarized Snubber** | Diode + RC | Asymmetric protection | H-bridge legs |

## 2.4 Overcurrent Protection

Methods to limit excessive current:

1. **Fuses** — One-time sacrificial device; rated in $I^2t$ (let-through energy).
2. **Circuit Breakers** — Resettable, slower response.
3. **Current Limiting Reactors** — Add series inductance.
4. **Electronic Current Sensing** — Hall-effect sensors, shunt resistors feeding back to gate driver.
5. **Pulsed Current Suppression** — Gate drive pulse-width modulation to limit $I_{avg}$.

## 2.5 Thermal Protection — Heat Sink Design

Power dissipation in a semiconductor generates heat that must flow from junction to ambient through a thermal resistance network analogous to an electrical circuit (Ohm's thermal analog).

### Thermal Ohm's Law
$$T_j - T_a = P_D \cdot R_{th(j-a)}$$

where:
- $T_j$ = Junction temperature (°C)
- $T_a$ = Ambient temperature (°C)
- $P_D$ = Power dissipation (W)
- $R_{th(j-a)}$ = Total thermal resistance (°C/W)

The total thermal resistance is the **series sum** of individual resistances:

$$R_{th(j-a)} = R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)}$$

where:
- $R_{th(j-c)}$ = Junction-to-case (device internal)
- $R_{th(c-s)}$ = Case-to-sink (mounting interface, includes thermal compound)
- $R_{th(s-a)}$ = Sink-to-ambient (heat sink fins + convection)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_L = L \frac{di}{dt}$ | Inductive voltage spike | V |
| 2 | $i_C = C \frac{dv}{dt}$ | Capacitor charging current | A |
| 3 | $T_j = T_a + P_D \cdot R_{th(j-a)}$ | Junction temperature | °C |
| 4 | $R_{th(j-a)} = R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)}$ | Total thermal resistance | °C/W |
| 5 | $P_D = \frac{T_{j,max} - T_a}{R_{th(j-a)}}$ | Max allowable dissipation | W |
| 6 | $E_{snubber} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy stored in snubber cap | J |
| 7 | $R_{snubber,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Optimal snubber resistance (critically damped) | Ω |
| 8 | $C_{snubber} = \frac{L \cdot I^2}{V^2}$ | Snubber capacitance | F |
| 9 | $I_{fuse} = 1.6 \cdot I_{load}$ | Fuse rating rule of thumb | A |
| 10 | $f_{sw,max} = \frac{P_{D,max}}{E_{sw}}$ | Max switching frequency | Hz |
| 11 | $\Delta T = P_D \cdot R_{th}$ | Temperature rise | °C |
| 12 | $V_{clamp} = V_{ref} \cdot (1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2})$ | Zener-clamped voltage | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** In high-frequency SMPS and IGBT inverters, **40% of total losses** occur during switching transitions. Snubbers directly reduce these switching losses, improving efficiency by **5-15%** in real systems.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial Motor Drives:** Snubbers protect IGBTs in 3-phase inverters from inductive kickback.
- **HVDC Transmission:** Crowbar circuits protect thyristor valves from lightning-induced surges.
- **EV Powertrains:** Thermal protection via liquid-cooled heat sinks enables 150 kW traction inverters.
- **Solar Inverters:** MOV + DC-side fuses handle PV array reverse-current faults.
- **Welding Inverters:** dv/dt snubbers prevent transformer saturation issues.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Snubber Capacitor Sizing for an Inductive Load

**Problem Setup:** A thyristor switches a load with inductance $L$ and initial current $I_0$. At turn-off, the inductor current must be diverted to a parallel snubber capacitor $C$. Derive the minimum value of $C$ to limit the voltage across the device to a maximum value $V_m$.

### Step 1: Energy Equivalence
The energy stored in the inductor at the moment of turn-off is:

$$E_L = \frac{1}{2} L I_0^2$$

This energy must be transferred to the snubber capacitor (assuming ideal lossless commutation):

$$E_C = \frac{1}{2} C V_m^2$$

### Step 2: Equate Energies
For the voltage not to exceed $V_m$, the capacitor must absorb all the inductive energy:

$$\frac{1}{2} C V_m^2 = \frac{1}{2} L I_0^2$$

### Step 3: Solve for C
Multiply both sides by 2 to cancel the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor:

$$C V_m^2 = L I_0^2$$

Isolate $C$ on the left side by dividing both sides by $V_m^2$:

$$C = \frac{L I_0^2}{V_m^2}$$

### Step 4: Result with Textual Annotation

$$\boxed{C_{snubber} = \frac{L \cdot I_0^2}{V_m^2}}$$

**Annotation:** This is the *minimum* capacitance. In practice, a safety factor of **1.5 to 2** is multiplied to account for non-idealities such as stray inductance, temperature derating, and capacitor tolerance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Snubber Resistor for Critical Damping

**Problem Setup:** For the RC snubber, find the value of $R$ that provides **critical damping** so that voltage overshoot is eliminated.

### Step 1: Define the RLC Loop
When the snubber capacitor charges through the snubber resistor from a source, the equivalent circuit is a series RLC with:
- $L$ = load/stray inductance
- $C$ = snubber capacitance
- $R$ = snubber resistance

### Step 2: Characteristic Equation
The natural response of a series RLC circuit has a characteristic equation:

$$s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC} = 0$$

### Step 3: Condition for Critical Damping
Critical damping occurs when the discriminant of the characteristic equation equals zero:

$$\left(\frac{R}{L}\right)^2 - \frac{4}{LC} = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve Algebraically
Multiply both sides by $L^2$ to clear the denominator:

$$R^2 - \frac{4L}{C} = 0$$

Add $\frac{4L}{C}$ to both sides:

$$R^2 = \frac{4L}{C}$$

Take the square root of both sides (resistance is positive):

$$R = 2 \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

### Step 5: Final Result

$$\boxed{R_{snubber,opt} = 2\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}}$$

**Annotation:** For *underdamped* (faster) response, use $R < 2\sqrt{L/C}$. For *overdamped* (slower, no overshoot) use $R > 2\sqrt{L/C}$. Most practical designs use **0.5 to 0.8 times** this critical value to balance speed and ringing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Sink Sizing for Given Power Loss

**Problem Setup:** A TO-220 MOSFET dissipates $P_D = 5$ W. The device has $R_{th(j-c)} = 1.5$ °C/W. Maximum junction temperature is $150°C$, ambient is $40°C$. Interface thermal resistance $R_{th(c-s)} = 0.5$ °C/W. Find the required heat sink thermal resistance $R_{th(s-a)}$.

### Step 1: Total Thermal Resistance Required
From the thermal Ohm's law, we need:

$$R_{th(j-a),max} = \frac{T_{j,max} - T_a}{P_D}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$R_{th(j-a),max} = \frac{150 - 40}{5} = \frac{110}{5} = 22 \text{ °C/W}$$

### Step 2: Decompose Total Resistance
The total is the sum of three series resistances:

$$R_{th(j-a)} = R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)}$$

### Step 3: Isolate the Unknown $R_{th(s-a)}$
Subtract the known terms on both sides:

$$R_{th(s-a)} = R_{th(j-a),max} - R_{th(j-c)} - R_{th(c-s)}$$

### Step 4: Plug in Numerical Values
$$R_{th(s-a)} = 22 - 1.5 - 0.5 = 20 \text{ °C/W}$$

### Step 5: Final Answer

$$\boxed{R_{th(s-a),required} \le 20 \text{ °C/W}}$$

**Annotation:** A standard extruded aluminum heat sink of dimensions **50 mm × 50 mm × 25 mm** typically has $R_{th(s-a)} \approx 15-20$ °C/W in natural convection, so this design is feasible. If forced-air cooling is used, the effective value drops by a factor of **2 to 3**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Snubber Design Trade-off

| Design Parameter | Increase C | Increase R | Add Diode (RCD) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $dv/dt$ reduction | ↑ Strongly | No effect | ↑↑ Strongly |
| Switching loss reduction | ↑↑ Strongly | Moderate | ↑↑ Strongly |
| Discharge loss in R | No effect | ↑↑ | ↑ (only in R) |
| Component size | ↑ (Cap cost) | ↑ (Heat) | ↑ (Diode) |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Best for | High $V$, low $I$ | Low $V$, high $I$ | High-frequency IGBTs |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Protection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Power Source AC DC Bus] --> nodeB[Overvoltage Stage]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Overcurrent Stage]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Thermal Stage]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Gate Drive Protection]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Rate dv dt and di dt Protection]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[MOV]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[SCR Crowbar]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Zener Clamp]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Fast Fuse]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Current Sensor]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Circuit Breaker]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Heat Sink]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Thermal Fuse]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Liquid Cooling]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Gate Resistor]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Miller Clamp]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[RC Snubber]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[RCD Snubber]
    nodeB1 --> nodeG[Protected Power Device]
    nodeB2 --> nodeG
    nodeB3 --> nodeG
    nodeC1 --> nodeG
    nodeC2 --> nodeG
    nodeC3 --> nodeG
    nodeD1 --> nodeG
    nodeD2 --> nodeG
    nodeD3 --> nodeG
    nodeE1 --> nodeG
    nodeE2 --> nodeG
    nodeF1 --> nodeG
    nodeF2 --> nodeG
```

## 4.2 Circuit Schematic: RCD Snubber Across a Transistor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDC[DC Bus Positive] --> nodeR1[R1 Resistor Snubber]
    nodeR1 --> nodeD1[D1 Diode Anode]
    nodeD1 --> nodeC1[C1 Snubber Capacitor]
    nodeC1 --> nodeN[Neutral]
    nodeSW[Transistor Collector] --> nodeR1
    nodeD1 --> nodeSW
    nodeC1 --> nodeSW
    nodeSW --> nodeEM[Emitter of Transistor]
    nodeEM --> nodeN
    nodeEM --> nodeLoad[Load Inductive]
    nodeLoad --> nodeDC
```

**Functionality:** When the transistor turns off, the inductive load current is forced into $C_1$ through diode $D_1$ (which is forward-biased). The capacitor voltage rises gradually, limiting $dv/dt$. When the transistor turns on again, the capacitor discharges through $R_1$ and the transistor, dissipating the stored energy as heat.

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Crowbar Protection Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[Start Monitoring Bus Voltage] --> nodeQ{Voltage Above Threshold}
    nodeQ -->|No| nodeS
    nodeQ -->|Yes| nodeT[Trigger Crowbar SCR Gate Pulse]
    nodeT --> nodeF[SCR Latches ON Creating Short Across Bus]
    nodeF --> nodeC[Current Diverts to Crowbar Resistor]
    nodeC --> nodeB[Series Fuse Blows Due to High I squared t]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Bus De-energized, Fault Cleared]
    nodeE --> nodeM[Manual Reset Required]
    nodeM --> nodeS
```

## 4.4 Thermal Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Power Dissipation PD] --> nodeRjc[Rth j-c Junction to Case]
    nodeRjc --> nodeRcs[Rth c-s Case to Sink]
    nodeRcs --> nodeRsa[Rth s-a Sink to Ambient]
    nodeRsa --> nodeTA[Ambient Temperature Ta]
```

**Annotation:** This is the exact thermal analog of an electrical circuit. Temperature is analogous to voltage, power dissipation to current, and thermal resistance to electrical resistance. The same Kirchhoff's laws apply.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: What is a snubber circuit? List any two types of snubbers used in power electronic circuits.**

**Model Answer:**
A snubber circuit is an auxiliary network connected across a semiconductor switching device to limit the rate of rise of voltage ($dv/dt$) and current ($di/dt$), thereby protecting the device from switching transients and reducing switching losses.

**Two types of snubbers:**
1. **RC Snubber:** A series combination of a resistor $R$ and capacitor $C$ connected across the device. It absorbs switching energy and limits $dv/dt$.
2. **RCD Snubber:** A polarized snubber with a resistor, capacitor, and a diode. It provides unidirectional energy dissipation and is preferred for transistor (BJT/IGBT) switching circuits.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define Safe Operating Area (SOA) of a power semiconductor device. Mention any two factors that limit the SOA.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** is the defined region of voltage-current coordinates within which a power semiconductor device can be operated without suffering permanent damage, for a specified duration (usually a single pulse or DC).

**Two limiting factors:**
1. **Maximum junction temperature ($T_{j,max}$):** Exceeding the rated junction temperature causes thermal runaway and bond wire failure.
2. **Second breakdown (for BJTs) or latch-up (for thyristors):** Localized hot spots caused by high current density create a regenerative thermal-electric failure mode.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A thyristor circuit switches a load inductance of $L = 50$ mH carrying a steady current of $I_0 = 20$ A. The supply voltage is $V_s = 400$ V DC. The thyristor breakdown voltage is $V_{BR} = 1000$ V. Design an RC snubber to protect the thyristor.**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Calculate the minimum snubber capacitance.

**Step 1: Identify the maximum allowable voltage across the thyristor.**

For safe operation, the voltage across the thyristor must be limited to **80%** of breakdown voltage as a safety margin:

$$V_m = 0.8 \times V_{BR} = 0.8 \times 1000 = 800 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Apply the energy balance equation.**

The energy stored in the inductor at the moment of turn-off:

$$E_L = \frac{1}{2} L I_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times (20)^2$$

$$E_L = 0.5 \times 0.05 \times 400 = 10 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Equate inductor energy to capacitor energy.**

$$\frac{1}{2} C V_m^2 = E_L$$

$$C = \frac{2 E_L}{V_m^2} = \frac{2 \times 10}{(800)^2} = \frac{20}{640000}$$

$$\boxed{C = 31.25 \text{ μF}}$$

**Step 4: Apply safety factor of 1.5.**

$$C_{design} = 1.5 \times 31.25 = 46.875 \text{ μF} \approx 47 \text{ μF}$$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Calculate the snubber resistance and its power rating.

**Step 1: Calculate optimal resistance for critical damping.**

$$R_{opt} = 2\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{47 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$R_{opt} = 2\sqrt{1063.83} = 2 \times 32.6$$

$$\boxed{R_{opt} \approx 65.2 \text{ Ω}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate energy dissipated per switching cycle.**

$$E_{snubber} = \frac{1}{2} C V_m^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 47 \times 10^{-6} \times (800)^2$$

$$E_{snubber} = 15.04 \text{ J per cycle}$$

**Step 3: Calculate average power dissipation at switching frequency $f_{sw} = 1$ kHz.**

$$P_R = E_{snubber} \times f_{sw} = 15.04 \times 1000 = 15.04 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Apply safety factor and derate.**

For reliable operation, the resistor is chosen with rating **2×** the calculated dissipation:

$$P_{R,rating} = 2 \times 15.04 = 30.08 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to apply the **0.8 safety margin** on breakdown voltage when computing $V_m$. This leads to a 20% error in $C$ calculation and full loss of 4 marks in the design problem. Also, students often confuse $R_{opt}$ formula—remember the factor of **2** for critical damping in a series RLC, not $1/(2\sqrt{LC})$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**An IGBT module is used in a 3-phase inverter. The IGBT has the following specifications: $V_{CE(max)} = 1200$ V, $I_C = 100$ A, $R_{th(j-c)} = 0.3$ °C/W. The total power dissipation in the device is $P_D = 80$ W. Maximum junction temperature is $T_{j,max} = 150°C$ and ambient temperature is $T_a = 40°C$. The case-to-sink interface thermal resistance is $R_{th(c-s)} = 0.2$ °C/W.**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Calculate the maximum allowable heat sink thermal resistance.

**Step 1: Total thermal resistance required.**

$$R_{th(j-a),max} = \frac{T_{j,max} - T_a}{P_D} = \frac{150 - 40}{80} = \frac{110}{80}$$

$$R_{th(j-a),max} = 1.375 \text{ °C/W}$$

**Step 2: Subtract internal and interface thermal resistances.**

$$R_{th(s-a),max} = R_{th(j-a),max} - R_{th(j-c)} - R_{th(c-s)}$$

$$R_{th(s-a),max} = 1.375 - 0.3 - 0.2 = 0.875 \text{ °C/W}$$

**Step 3: Final answer.**

$$\boxed{R_{th(s-a),max} = 0.875 \text{ °C/W}}$$

**Step 4: Design Implication.** This very low value (less than 1 °C/W) cannot be achieved with natural convection. **Forced air cooling** with a fan (air velocity 2-4 m/s) is mandatory. Alternatively, a **liquid-cooled cold plate** with $R_{th} \approx 0.05$ °C/W would be a superior choice for high-power industrial applications.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — If the heat sink provides $R_{th(s-a)} = 0.7$ °C/W, calculate the new junction temperature and verify safe operation.

**Step 1: Calculate actual total thermal resistance.**

$$R_{th(j-a),actual} = R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)} = 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.7$$

$$R_{th(j-a),actual} = 1.2 \text{ °C/W}$$

**Step 2: Calculate new junction temperature.**

$$T_j = T_a + P_D \times R_{th(j-a),actual} = 40 + 80 \times 1.2$$

$$T_j = 40 + 96 = 136 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3: Verify against maximum rating.**

Since $T_j = 136°C < T_{j,max} = 150°C$, the device operates within its safe thermal limit.

**Step 4: Calculate thermal margin.**

$$\Delta T_{margin} = T_{j,max} - T_j = 150 - 136 = 14°C$$

The device is operating with a **14°C safety margin**, which is acceptable but tight. For long-term reliability, a junction temperature rise of **20-30°C** below $T_{j,max}$ is recommended (industrial best practice rule of thumb: derate by 80%).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is to forget to convert units correctly—e.g., using $R_{th}$ in W/°C instead of °C/W. The thermal "Ohm's law" formula $T_j = T_a + P_D \cdot R_{th}$ requires the latter. Also, students sometimes add resistances in parallel instead of series, which is a fundamental conceptual error worth **5 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Protection Circuits (Module 1, PEICT411)**

- [ ] **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** is defined by $V_{max}$, $I_{max}$, $P_{D,max}$, and $T_{j,max}$.
- [ ] **Snubbers** are **parallel** to the device; **freewheeling diodes** are also parallel but serve a different purpose (load current continuity).
- [ ] **Snubber capacitor formula:** $C = \dfrac{L I_0^2}{V_m^2}$ (energy balance derivation).
- [ ] **Optimal snubber resistor (critical damping):** $R_{opt} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$.
- [ ] **Crowbar circuit** uses an SCR that latches on during fault, blowing the main fuse.
- [ ] **MOV** (Metal Oxide Varistor) is a non-linear resistor used for transient overvoltage clamping.
- [ ] **Thermal resistances add in series**, just like electrical resistors in series.
- [ ] **Heat sink formula:** $R_{th(s-a),max} = \dfrac{T_{j,max} - T_a}{P_D} - R_{th(j-c)} - R_{th(c-s)}$.
- [ ] **Fuse rating rule of thumb:** $I_{fuse} = 1.6 \times I_{load,normal}$ (allows for inrush).
- [ ] **$dv/dt$ protection** prevents false triggering of thyristors; **$di/dt$ protection** prevents localized hot spots.
- [ ] **RCD snubbers** are polarized—diode must be oriented to conduct during turn-off.
- [ ] **Gate drive protection** includes gate resistor (limits ringing) and Miller clamp (prevents parasitic turn-on).
- [ ] **Standard $T_{j,max}$:** 150°C industrial, 175°C automotive/military grade.
- [ ] **Snubber power rating** must include 2× safety derating for reliability.
- [ ] **Thermal model** is an exact analog of an electrical circuit (Kirchhoff's laws apply).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, questions on snubber design (capacitor sizing using energy balance) and heat sink design (thermal resistance calculation) are **guaranteed to appear** as either Part A (3 marks) or sub-part of Part B (7 marks). Master the two core derivations in Section 3 and you can score full marks in this module.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series and Parallel Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Series and Parallel Connections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In high-power Power Electronic converters, the operating **voltage** and **current** levels frequently exceed the ratings of a single commercially available semiconductor switch (SCR / IGBT / GTO). **Series connection** of devices is employed when the required blocking voltage is greater than the rating of a single device, while **Parallel connection** is employed when the load current demand exceeds the current rating of a single device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Module 1):**
> *Series connection* = N devices stacked end-to-end to share a single **OFF-state voltage** (high-voltage string).
> *Parallel connection* = N devices placed side-by-side to share a single **ON-state current** (high-current stack).

---

## 1.2 The Engineering "Why" — When Are They Needed?

A single SCR may be available only up to ratings of the order of **6.5 kV / 3000 A** in practice. HVDC converters, EHV traction drives, and large industrial drives routinely demand:
- **Blocking voltages** in the order of **±100 kV to ±800 kV** (HVDC).
- **Load currents** in the order of **10 kA – 50 kA** (smelters, DC arc furnaces).

Therefore, multiple SCRs must be *coordinated* either in a string or in a stack, and the **mismatch** in their static and dynamic characteristics is the central problem studied under this topic.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Water Pipe & Dam" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 – Series (The Dam Wall):** Imagine a wall built of individual concrete blocks holding back a reservoir. If one block is slightly weaker, it bears less pressure, so the stronger block bears more — potentially failing it. We add **small leakage pipes (resistors)** across each block to **equalize the water pressure (voltage)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 – Parallel (The Pipe Network):** Imagine three pipes of slightly different diameters connected in parallel carrying the same water pressure. The widest pipe takes the most water (current). We add **restriction valves (series reactors / resistors)** in each branch so that the **flow (current) divides equally**.

The block (SCR) holding back the *highest voltage* is the one most likely to fail — this is the **heart of the series-connection problem**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Device Ratings (Quick Reference)

| Parameter | Typical Single-SCR Range | When to Go Series | When to Go Parallel |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **V<sub>DRM</sub> / V<sub>RRM</sub>** | 600 V – 6500 V | > 6500 V | — |
| **I<sub>T(AV)</sub>** | 100 A – 3000 A | — | > 3000 A |
| **di/dt (A/µs)** | 50 – 1000 | needs snubber | needs reactor |
| **dv/dt (V/µs)** | 50 – 1000 | needs snubber | needs snubber |

**Key Standard Metric** → **String Efficiency (η<sub>s</sub>)** is the most important figure-of-merit for the series string, and **Current Unbalance Factor (K<sub>u</sub>)** is the parallel equivalent.

---

## 1.5 Types of Mismatch in Real Devices

1. **Static (Leakage) Mismatch** – The OFF-state leakage current I<sub>L</sub> of each SCR differs at a given voltage (the dominant problem in steady blocking).
2. **Dynamic (Recovery) Mismatch** – The reverse-recovery charge Q<sub>rr</sub> differs, so voltages during turn-OFF do not divide equally.
3. **ON-state Mismatch** – The ON-state voltage drop V<sub>T</sub> differs, so currents during conduction do not divide equally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** Every series design must equalize *both* static and dynamic voltages. Every parallel design must equalize *both* static currents and dynamic (di/dt) currents.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Series Connection — Analytical Framework

When *n* SCRs are connected in series, the **string** must block a total OFF-state voltage V<sub>s</sub>. Ideally each device would share V<sub>s</sub>/n. In practice, the *i<sup>th</sup>* SCR (with the **lowest leakage current**) blocks a **higher** voltage, while the *j<sup>th</sup>* SCR (with the **highest leakage current**) blocks a **lower** voltage.

### 2.1.1 Need for Series Connection (Bulleted Logic)

- A single device cannot meet the *OFF-state voltage* demand of HVDC / EHV systems.
- A *string* of devices is built to share the *OFF-state* voltage mathematically.
- In the *ON-state*, all devices see the same current (no sharing problem in series).
- **Problems of series connection:**
  1. **Unequal static voltage sharing** → due to differing leakage currents.
  2. **Unequal dynamic voltage sharing** → due to differing Q<sub>rr</sub> at turn-off.
  3. **Unequal turn-on voltage sharing** → during the spread of firing pulse propagation.
  4. **String efficiency drops** → devices are **derated**.

---

### 2.1.2 Static Voltage Equalization (Resistor Network)

A resistor **R** is placed in parallel with each SCR. The current through R is the *equalizing current*. Choosing R so that I<sub>R</sub> ≫ I<sub>L(max)</sub> forces the voltage to divide almost equally.

**Equalizing resistance formula:**

$$R = \dfrac{n \, V_{s(\text{per SCR})} - V_{s(\text{string})}}{I_{L(\text{min})} - I_{L(\text{max})}}$$

- Where I<sub>L(min)</sub> and I<sub>L(max)</sub> are the leakage currents of the *highest-leakage* and *lowest-leakage* SCR at the operating voltage.
- **Smaller R** → better equalization, but **higher power loss** P = V²/R per device.

**Power dissipated in the equalizing network:**

$$P_{R} = \dfrac{\left(V_{s}/n\right)^{2}}{R}$$

### 2.1.3 Dynamic Voltage Equalization (RC Snubber Network)

A capacitor C in parallel with each SCR (with a small damping resistor R<sub>s</sub> in series) equalizes the *transient* voltage during turn-off because the **fastest-turning-off** device (lowest Q<sub>rr</sub>) tries to take all the voltage. The capacitor holds the voltage momentarily.

$$C = \dfrac{\left(n-1\right) \, \Delta Q_{rr}}{n \, \left(\dfrac{V_{s}}{n} - V_{DWM}\right)}$$

Where:
- ΔQ<sub>rr</sub> = difference in reverse-recovery charge between devices.
- V<sub>DWM</sub> = working peak OFF-state voltage of a single device.

### 2.1.4 String Efficiency (η<sub>s</sub>)

$$\eta_{s} = \dfrac{\text{Actual string voltage}}{\text{Ideal string voltage}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{s} = \dfrac{V_{s(\text{actual})}}{n \cdot V_{s(\text{per SCR})}} \times 100\%$$

- A perfectly matched string has **η<sub>s</sub> = 100 %**.
- A practical HVDC string has **η<sub>s</sub> ≈ 85 – 95 %**.

### 2.1.5 Derating Factor (DRF)

$$\text{DRF} = \dfrac{\text{Total device rating} - \text{Usable string rating}}{\text{Total device rating}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{DRF} = \left(1 - \eta_{s}\right) \times 100\%$$

- DRF represents the **percentage of rating that is sacrificed** to achieve safe sharing.

---

## 2.2 Parallel Connection — Analytical Framework

When *m* SCRs are connected in parallel, the string must carry a total current I<sub>T</sub>. In practice, the device with the **lowest ON-state voltage drop** carries the **highest** current. Without intervention, the **current unbalance** is severe and one device will trip thermally.

### 2.2.1 Need for Parallel Connection (Bulleted Logic)

- A single device cannot meet the *ON-state current* demand of high-power drives.
- A *stack* of devices is built to share the *load current*.
- In the *OFF-state*, all devices see the same voltage (no sharing problem in parallel).
- **Problems of parallel connection:**
  1. **Unequal steady-state (static) current sharing** → due to differing V<sub>T</sub>.
  2. **Unequal di/dt sharing** → due to differing turn-on times t<sub>gt</sub>, t<sub>on</sub>.
  3. **Unequal current sharing during reverse-recovery** → due to differing Q<sub>rr</sub>.
  4. **Thermal runaway** → the hottest device draws even more current (positive feedback).

### 2.2.2 Static Current Sharing (Series Reactors)

A small air-core reactor L<sub>s</sub> is placed in series with each SCR. Its impedance *jωL<sub>s</sub>* forces the **current** to divide nearly equally even when V<sub>T</sub> differs. Since ω is small at DC steady-state, reactors are far smaller than resistors (less loss).

For **DC** the reactor offers *no* impedance, so a **small current-sharing resistor** R<sub>p</sub> is also added:

$$R_{p} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{T}}{0.5 \, \Delta I_{T}}$$

Where ΔV<sub>T</sub> is the *spread* in ON-state voltage drop and ΔI<sub>T</sub> is the *allowable* current unbalance.

### 2.2.3 Dynamic Current Sharing (Series Reactor + Snubber)

A reactor in series with each SCR slows the rate of rise of current, forcing the *faster-turning-on* device to wait for the *slower* one. This protects against **di/dt failure**.

The reactor value is chosen by:

$$L_{s} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{T}}{\left(\dfrac{di}{dt}\right)_{\max}}$$

### 2.2.4 Current Unbalance / Sharing Factor (K<sub>u</sub>)

$$K_{u} = \dfrac{I_{T(\max)} - I_{T(\text{avg})}}{I_{T(\text{avg})}} \times 100\%$$

Where I<sub>T(avg)</sub> = I<sub>T</sub>/m.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the symbols and units. KTU question papers often give numerical values in **kV** and **mA (leakage)** — always convert to **V** and **A** before substituting.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Limiting Condition | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Equalizing Resistance (series) | $R = \dfrac{n V_{s/n} - V_{s}}{I_{L(\text{min})} - I_{L(\text{max})}}$ | $I_{R} \gg I_{L(\max)}$ for accuracy | **Ω** |
| 2 | Power lost in R | $P_{R} = \dfrac{(V_{s}/n)^{2}}{R}$ | Heat-sink must dissipate | **W** |
| 3 | Equalizing Capacitance | $C = \dfrac{(n-1) \, \Delta Q_{rr}}{n \left( V_{s}/n - V_{DWM} \right)}$ | $\Delta Q_{rr} = Q_{rr(\max)} - Q_{rr(\min)}$ | **µF** |
| 4 | String Efficiency | $\eta_{s} = \dfrac{V_{s(\text{actual})}}{n \cdot V_{s/n}} \times 100$ | $\eta_{s} \le 100\%$ | **%** |
| 5 | Derating Factor | $\text{DRF} = (1 - \eta_{s}) \times 100$ | $\text{DRF} \ge 0\%$ | **%** |
| 6 | Current-Sharing Resistor | $R_{p} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{T}}{0.5 \, \Delta I_{T}}$ | $R_{p}$ must be small (loss) | **Ω** |
| 7 | Current-Sharing Reactor | $L_{s} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{T}}{(di/dt)_{\max}}$ | Air-core, saturable design | **µH** |
| 8 | Current Unbalance Factor | $K_{u} = \dfrac{I_{\max} - I_{\text{avg}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} \times 100$ | Should be < 10 % ideally | **%** |
| 9 | Snubber Resistor (dynamic) | $R_{s} \ge \sqrt{\dfrac{L_{\text{stray}}}{C}}$ (damping) | Avoids LC oscillation | **Ω** |
| 10 | Number of devices (series) | $n = \dfrac{V_{s}}{\eta_{s} \cdot V_{DWM}}$ | Round **up** to integer | — |
| 11 | Number of devices (parallel) | $m = \dfrac{I_{T}}{\eta_{p} \cdot I_{T(AV)}}$ | Round **up** to integer | — |
| 12 | Heat-sink thermal resistance | $R_{\theta SA} = \dfrac{T_{j(\max)} - T_{A}}{P_{\text{loss}}} - R_{\theta JS}$ | $T_{j} < T_{j(\max)}$ rating | **°C / W** |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVDC Converter Valves** (e.g., the 800 kV Raigad-Delhi line): 75–80 SCRs *in series* per valve-arm, often 2 arms *in parallel* for redundancy.
- **Static VAR Compensators (SVC)** for grid stability.
- **DC Traction Sub-stations** (Indian Railways 25 kV AC → DC loco drive).
- **AC Drives of > 5 MW** for rolling mills, cement kilns, large compressors.
- **Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS)** with IGBT/MOSFET strings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-world Link:** KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly maps this topic to *"design of static and dynamic equalising circuits"* — the resistor-only network is **static**, while the **RC snubber** is **dynamic**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Student Note:** All derivations below are written *line-by-line* with explanatory text rows. They are aligned using the LaTeX `aligned` environment for maximum board-exam readability. No step is omitted.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: String Efficiency of a 2-SCR Series String

**Statement:** Two SCRs, T1 and T2, are connected in series to block a total string voltage V<sub>s</sub>. Their OFF-state leakage currents at voltage V are I<sub>L1</sub>(V) and I<sub>L2</sub>(V), with I<sub>L1</sub> < I<sub>L2</sub> (i.e. T1 has the *higher* characteristic impedance). The reverse characteristics are linearized in the operating region.

**Step 1 — Write the KVL around the string (in OFF state):**

The two series devices are blocking V<sub>s</sub>, but the leakage current I<sub>L</sub> flowing through them is **the same** (since they are in series):

$$I_{L1}(V_{1}) = I_{L2}(V_{2}) = I_{L}$$

**Step 2 — Express the linearized characteristics in the working region:**

From the linearized I-V reverse characteristics of an SCR near the rated voltage, we can write:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{L1} &= a_1 \, V_{1} + b_1 \\
I_{L2} &= a_2 \, V_{2} + b_2
\end{aligned}
$$

For *identical* devices with a small tolerance, we assume the slopes are equal (a<sub>1</sub> = a<sub>2</sub> = a), but the intercepts differ by Δb:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{L1} &= a V_{1} + b \\
I_{L2} &= a V_{2} + (b + \Delta b)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two leakage currents (series constraint):**

$$a V_{1} + b = a V_{2} + b + \Delta b$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$a (V_{1} - V_{2}) = \Delta b \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{1} - V_{2} = \dfrac{\Delta b}{a}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the KVL around the string:**

$$V_{1} + V_{2} = V_{s}$$

**Step 6 — Solve the linear system for V<sub>1</sub> and V<sub>2</sub>:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{1} &= \dfrac{V_{s}}{2} + \dfrac{\Delta b}{2a} \\
V_{2} &= \dfrac{V_{s}}{2} - \dfrac{\Delta b}{2a}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — The device that blocks the higher voltage is T1 (since V<sub>1</sub> > V<sub>2</sub>).** To *survive*, T1 must be rated for V<sub>1</sub> ≥ V<sub>1</sub>:

$$V_{1(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{s}}{2} + \dfrac{\Delta b}{2a}$$

**Step 8 — String Efficiency Definition:**

The string is rated for V<sub>s(usable)</sub> = **2 × V<sub>1(max)</sub>**, *but* the actual voltage that T1 can take is V<sub>1</sub>. So:

$$\eta_{s} = \dfrac{V_{s(\text{usable})}}{2 \, V_{1(\text{max})}} = \dfrac{V_{s}}{2 \, V_{1(\max)}}$$

**Step 9 — Substituting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{s} &= \dfrac{V_{s}}{2 \, \left(\dfrac{V_{s}}{2} + \dfrac{\Delta b}{2a}\right)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{V_{s}}{V_{s} + \dfrac{\Delta b}{a}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\Delta b}{a \, V_{s}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{s} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\Delta b}{a \, V_{s}}}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** The larger the *mismatch* Δb, the **lower** the string efficiency. To raise η<sub>s</sub>, we must (a) buy **closely-matched** SCRs, or (b) add a **parallel equalizing resistor R** so that R ≪ 1/a, which dominates the leakage path and forces V<sub>1</sub> ≈ V<sub>2</sub>.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Series String Design (KTU-style 14-mark)

**Problem:** Three SCRs, each rated at 1000 V, are to be used in series to block 2500 V. The leakage currents of the three SCRs at 800 V are 5 mA, 7 mA, and 9 mA respectively. Design the *static equalizing resistor* for the string and compute (a) string efficiency and (b) derating factor.

**Given Data (Step-wise listing):**

- n = 3
- V<sub>s(string)</sub> = 2500 V
- Per-device rating V<sub>s/n</sub>(rated) = 1000 V
- Leakage currents at 800 V: I<sub>L1</sub> = 5 mA, I<sub>L2</sub> = 7 mA, I<sub>L3</sub> = 9 mA
- (Highest-leakage) I<sub>L(max)</sub> = 9 mA, (Lowest-leakage) I<sub>L(min)</sub> = 5 mA

**Step 1 — Find the equalizing resistor R:**

The formula (per the KTU convention) is:

$$R = \dfrac{n \, V_{s(\text{per SCR, operating})} - V_{s(\text{string})}}{I_{L(\text{min})} - I_{L(\text{max})}}$$

We first compute the *ideal* per-SCR voltage:

$$V_{s/n} = \dfrac{2500}{3} = 833.33 \text{ V}$$

But to make leakage values valid, we use the *rating* voltage of a single device: 1000 V. The KTU-standard convention treats the difference (n × V<sub>rating</sub> − V<sub>actual</sub>) as the *headroom* to be compensated:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n \times V_{\text{rated}} &= 3 \times 1000 = 3000 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
\text{Numerator} &= 3000 - 2500 = 500 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator} &= (5 - 9) \text{ mA} = -4 \text{ mA} = -0.004 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Take magnitudes (resistance must be positive):

$$R = \dfrac{500}{0.004} = 125{,}000 \; \Omega = 125 \; \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Power dissipated in each equalizing resistor:**

$$
P_{R} = \dfrac{(V_{s}/n)^{2}}{R} = \dfrac{(833.33)^{2}}{125{,}000} = \dfrac{694{,}444.4}{125{,}000} \approx 5.55 \text{ W}
$$

Therefore, each R must be rated at **≥ 6 W (with safety factor ≈ 1.5)**.

**Step 3 — String Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{s} = \dfrac{V_{s(\text{actual})}}{n \times V_{\text{rated}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2500}{3000} \times 100 = 83.33 \;\%$$

**Step 4 — Derating Factor:**

$$\text{DRF} = (1 - 0.8333) \times 100 = 16.67 \;\%$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using leakage-spread formula:**

The voltage on the *highest-leakage* (best-equalized) device and the *lowest-leakage* (worst-loaded) device:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{worst}} &= \dfrac{V_{s}}{n} + \dfrac{I_{L(\max)} - I_{L(\min)}}{2} \cdot R \\[4pt]
&= 833.33 + \dfrac{(9-5)\times 10^{-3}}{2} \times 125{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 833.33 + 250 = 1083.33 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **exceeds** the 1000 V rating of a single SCR, so we must add a *larger* string or a *finer* R. (This is exactly why DRF exists in practice.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equalizing Capacitance for Dynamic Sharing

**Statement:** *n* SCRs in series have reverse-recovery charges Q<sub>rr1</sub>, Q<sub>rr2</sub>, … , Q<sub>rrn</sub>. The difference in Q<sub>rr</sub> causes one device to turn off *faster*, forcing it to absorb a disproportionate share of the re-applied forward voltage.

**Step 1 — Worst case charge difference:**

$$\Delta Q_{rr} = Q_{rr(\max)} - Q_{rr(\min)}$$

**Step 2 — The fastest-turning-off device tries to take all the string voltage** V<sub>s</sub> across it. The remaining n − 1 devices each see V<sub>s</sub>/n. The over-voltage on the *fastest* device is:

$$\Delta V = V_{s} - \dfrac{V_{s}}{n} = V_{s} \left( \dfrac{n-1}{n} \right)$$

**Step 3 — The shunt capacitor C absorbs ΔQ<sub>rr</sub> at a voltage rise of ΔV:**

$$C = \dfrac{\Delta Q_{rr}}{\Delta V} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_{rr}}{V_{s} \left( \dfrac{n-1}{n} \right)} = \dfrac{n \, \Delta Q_{rr}}{V_{s}(n-1)}$$

**Step 4 — KTU textbook form (V<sub>DWM</sub> is the desired per-device voltage):**

$$\boxed{\;C = \dfrac{(n-1) \, \Delta Q_{rr}}{n \left( V_{s}/n - V_{DWM} \right)}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical:**

n = 4, V<sub>s</sub> = 2000 V, Q<sub>rr(max)</sub> = 80 µC, Q<sub>rr(min)</sub> = 50 µC, V<sub>DWM</sub> = 500 V.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Q_{rr} &= 30 \; \mu C \\[4pt]
V_{s}/n &= 500 \text{ V (ideally equal to } V_{DWM}\text{)} \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator} &= 4 \times (500 - 500) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{divergent}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** If the design is at the *limit* (V<sub>s</sub>/n = V<sub>DWM</sub>), C must be **infinite**, which is impractical. The student is taught to **add a margin** of (10–20 %) by lowering V<sub>DWM</sub> in the design. This single line is a favourite **KTU follow-up** question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Equal Current-Sharing Resistor (Parallel Connection)

**Statement:** Two SCRs in parallel share a current I<sub>T</sub>. Their ON-state voltage drops at current I are V<sub>T1</sub>(I) and V<sub>T2</sub>(I), with V<sub>T1</sub> < V<sub>T2</sub>. Compute the series resistance R<sub>p</sub> needed to limit the unbalance to a specified value ΔI.

**Step 1 — Branch currents:** Let I<sub>1</sub> and I<sub>2</sub> be the branch currents, I<sub>1</sub> + I<sub>2</sub> = I<sub>T</sub>.

**Step 2 — KVL across the parallel branches (each branch has a series resistor R<sub>p</sub>):**

$$
V_{T1}(I_1) + I_1 R_p = V_{T2}(I_2) + I_2 R_p
$$

**Step 3 — Linearize V<sub>T</sub> around the operating point: V<sub>T</sub> ≈ V<sub>0</sub> + r<sub>d</sub> × I.** Let the difference in ON-state drop be ΔV<sub>T</sub> = V<sub>T2</sub> − V<sub>T1</sub> at the rated current.

**Step 4 — If we allow a current imbalance ΔI = I<sub>1</sub> − I<sub>2</sub>**, the KVL becomes:

$$\Delta V_{T} = (I_1 - I_2) R_p = \Delta I \cdot R_p$$

**Step 5 — Solving for R<sub>p</sub>:**

$$\boxed{\;R_{p} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{T}}{\Delta I}\;}$$

If we limit ΔI = 10 % of I<sub>T</sub>:

$$R_{p} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{T}}{0.10 \cdot I_{T}}$$

**Step 6 — Power lost in R<sub>p</sub> per branch:**

$$P_{R_p} = \left( \dfrac{I_{T}}{2} \right)^{2} \cdot R_{p} = \dfrac{I_{T}^{2} \, \Delta V_{T}}{4 \, \Delta I}$$

This is the **engineering trade-off** the KTU examiner tests: better sharing ⇔ higher loss.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Series vs Parallel)

| Property | Series Connection | Parallel Connection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Quantity being shared** | OFF-state **voltage** | ON-state **current** |
| **Mismatch source** | Leakage current I<sub>L</sub>, Q<sub>rr</sub> | ON-state drop V<sub>T</sub>, t<sub>on</sub> |
| **Static equalizer** | R across each SCR | R or reactor in each branch |
| **Dynamic equalizer** | R-C snubber across each SCR | Series reactor L<sub>s</sub> per branch |
| **Figure-of-merit** | String efficiency η<sub>s</sub> | Current unbalance K<sub>u</sub> |
| **Failure mode if unshared** | Voltage punch-through | Thermal runaway |
| **Loss penalty** | I<sub>R</sub>² × R (in R) | I<sub>T</sub>² × R<sub>p</sub> (in R) |
| **Typical η / K<sub>u</sub>** | 85 – 95 % | 90 – 95 % |
| **Heat-sink concern** | Small (SCRs share current) | **Critical** (hottest one shares more) |
| **Dominated by** | dv/dt, V<sub>RRM</sub> rating | di/dt, I<sub>T(AV)</sub> rating |

## 4.4 Schematic Description (Textual Companion)

For examination purposes, a textual description accompanies the diagrams:

> **Series String:** The positive DC bus is connected to the anode of T1, whose cathode feeds the anode of T2, and so on, terminating at the negative bus. Across each SCR we place:
> 1. A **resistor R** (static equalizer, 10 kΩ – 1 MΩ range depending on voltage).
> 2. A series **R<sub>s</sub>–C** snubber (dynamic equalizer, 0.1 µF – 4 µF range, R<sub>s</sub> 10 – 50 Ω).
>
> **Parallel Stack:** The positive bus feeds *m* parallel branches, each branch containing one SCR, a small air-core reactor L<sub>s</sub> (a few µH), and an optional small resistor R<sub>p</sub> (a few mΩ). The branches reunite at a common DC link feeding the load.
>
> **Heat Sinks:** Each SCR in the parallel stack has its **own heat sink** because thermal runaway will destroy the hottest device. In series, the heat sinks are often *shared* on a single cold plate since the current is common.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*State and explain the concept of "string efficiency" in a series-connected SCR string. What does a string efficiency of 90 % signify in practice?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** String efficiency η<sub>s</sub> is defined as the ratio of the *actual* voltage that a series string of SCRs can block to the *sum of the individual* voltage ratings of the devices, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta_{s} = \dfrac{V_{s(\text{actual string})}}{n \cdot V_{\text{rating of each SCR}}} \times 100\%$$

**Significance (1 mark):** η<sub>s</sub> = 90 % means that out of the *total* voltage rating of the string (n × V<sub>rating</sub>), only **90 %** is safely usable. The remaining 10 % represents the *derating* introduced by mismatch in the leakage currents and reverse-recovery charges of the devices.

**Practical implication (1 mark):** To block 100 kV with a string of η<sub>s</sub> = 90 %, the designer must rate the string for 100/0.9 ≈ 111.1 kV, requiring more devices and increased cost.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Compare the use of "series reactors" versus "series resistors" for current sharing in a parallel-connected SCR stack. Which is preferred for DC applications and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Series Resistor R<sub>p</sub> | Series Reactor L<sub>s</sub> |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| DC operation | Works, but causes **I²R losses** | Has **zero impedance at DC** — does not share |
| AC operation | Works, but loss grows with I | **Preferred** (lossless sharing) |
| di/dt limiting | Limited | **Effective** (limits rate of rise) |
| Cost & size | Cheap, compact | Bulky, costly |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For **pure DC steady-state**, a **small resistor** is mandatory because the reactor cannot equalize DC. For **AC converters and dynamic di/dt protection**, the **reactor** is preferred due to its lossless sharing. In practice, both are used together (reactor for dynamic, resistor for DC).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Series Connection Design**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q-Set A]**

> **(a)** Explain the **need for series connection of SCRs** in high-voltage converters. With the help of a neat schematic, describe the operation of a **static equalizing circuit** for voltage sharing. Derive the expression for the **equalizing resistance R**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A string of **five identical SCRs** is to be used to block a DC voltage of **12 kV**. The OFF-state leakage current of each SCR is **12 mA** at 2.5 kV and **18 mA** at 3 kV. The reverse-recovery charge varies by **ΔQ<sub>rr</sub> = 20 µC** between the fastest and slowest devices. Design the **static equalizing resistance R** and the **dynamic equalizing capacitance C**, and compute the **string efficiency** and **derating factor**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Need (2 marks):**
- Single SCRs are limited to ~ 6.5 kV / 3000 A commercially.
- HVDC, EHV, and large drives demand voltages in the order of **±100 kV to ±800 kV**.
- Therefore, multiple SCRs must be *stacked in series* to share the blocking voltage.

**Schematic (2 marks):** The diagram (Section 4.1) shows *n* SCRs in series with a parallel resistor R across each.

**Operation of static equalizer (2 marks):**
- In the OFF state, the leakage currents of the SCRs differ. The SCR with the *lowest* leakage current would naturally take the *largest* share of the voltage, risking punch-through.
- A resistor R is placed in parallel with each SCR. The current through R (I<sub>R</sub>) is chosen to be **much larger** than the maximum leakage current I<sub>L(max)</sub>, so that the difference in leakage currents becomes a small fraction of the total current. The voltage across each SCR is then forced to divide **almost equally**.

**Derivation of R (1 mark):**

The condition for equal voltage is that the difference in leakage current must be absorbed by the difference in R-current. Setting the voltage across the lowest-leakage SCR (call it V<sub>1</sub>) equal to the voltage across the highest-leakage SCR (V<sub>2</sub>):

$$V_{1} = \dfrac{V_{s}}{n} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{2} = \dfrac{V_{s}}{n}$$

But the actual currents are I<sub>L1</sub> + V<sub>1</sub>/R and I<sub>L2</sub> + V<sub>2</sub>/R, and they must be **equal** (series constraint). Equating:

$$I_{L1} + \dfrac{V_{1}}{R} = I_{L2} + \dfrac{V_{2}}{R}$$

For the boundary case V<sub>1</sub> = V<sub>2</sub> = V<sub>s</sub>/n:

$$I_{L1} = I_{L2}$$

But if I<sub>L1</sub> ≠ I<sub>L2</sub>, the voltage cannot be perfectly equal; the **design formula** is:

$$R = \dfrac{n V_{s/n} - V_{s(\text{string})}}{I_{L(\text{min})} - I_{L(\text{max})}}$$

This is the **KTU textbook formula** that examiners look for.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — List the data:**

- n = 5, V<sub>s</sub> = 12 000 V
- I<sub>L</sub> at 2.5 kV = 12 mA, at 3 kV = 18 mA
- ΔQ<sub>rr</sub> = 20 µC

**Step 2 — Compute string efficiency:**

If we use the *rated* per-device voltage, we take V<sub>per-SCR</sub> = 12 000 / 5 = 2 400 V. The data sheet values are interpolated:

At 2400 V, leakage current I<sub>L</sub> ≈ ?

Use linear interpolation between 2500 V (12 mA) and 3000 V (18 mA):

$$I_{L}(2400) = 12 + \dfrac{(18-12)}{(3000-2500)} \times (2400-2500) = 12 - 1.2 = 10.8 \text{ mA}$$

For the spread, assume the highest-leakage device has I<sub>L(max)</sub> ≈ 11 mA and the lowest I<sub>L(min)</sub> ≈ 10 mA at 2.4 kV (1 mA spread). We treat the *minimum* spread conservatively:

- I<sub>L(min)</sub> = 10 mA, I<sub>L(max)</sub> = 11 mA, spread = 1 mA = 0.001 A.

**Step 3 — Compute R:**

$$R = \dfrac{n V_{s/n} - V_{s}}{I_{L(\text{min})} - I_{L(\text{max})}} = \dfrac{5 \times 2400 - 12{,}000}{0.010 - 0.011} = \dfrac{0}{-0.001}$$

This is a **paradox of zero headroom** — at the design point, R is **infinite**, meaning the design is *on the limit* and any tolerance in leakage will break it. In practice, we **derate** the per-device voltage by 20 %:

$$V_{\text{per-SCR, design}} = 0.8 \times 2400 = 1920 \text{ V}$$

Recompute:

$$R = \dfrac{5 \times 1920 - 12{,}000}{0.010 - 0.011} = \dfrac{9600 - 12{,}000}{-0.001} = \dfrac{-2400}{-0.001} = 2.4 \text{ M}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Power loss per resistor:**

$$P_{R} = \dfrac{(V_{s}/n)^{2}}{R} = \dfrac{(2400)^{2}}{2.4 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{5.76 \times 10^{6}}{2.4 \times 10^{6}} = 2.4 \text{ W}$$

Use a **3 W** resistor with safety factor.

**Step 5 — Compute C (dynamic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \dfrac{(n-1) \, \Delta Q_{rr}}{n \left( V_{s}/n - V_{DWM} \right)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times (2400 - 1920)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{80 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 480} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{80 \times 10^{-6}}{2400} \\[4pt]
&= 33.33 \text{ nF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — String efficiency & DRF:**

$$\eta_{s} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{5 \times 3000} \times 100 = \dfrac{12{,}000}{15{,}000} \times 100 = 80\%$$

$$\text{DRF} = (1 - 0.80) \times 100 = 20\%$$

**Final Answer Box:** R = 2.4 MΩ, C = 33.3 nF, η<sub>s</sub> = 80 %, DRF = 20 %.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Series):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mA → A** when substituting into R formula (silent failure — answer 1000× off).
> 2. **Taking the absolute value** of (I<sub>L(min)</sub> − I<sub>L(max)</sub>) — KTU *does* expect the **sign** to come out positive (it confirms the design is feasible). If the result is **negative**, the design is *unfeasible* without derating.
> 3. **Ignoring derating margin** — a string with η<sub>s</sub> > 95 % looks attractive on paper but fails in 1 year of field operation.
> 4. **Forgetting snubber damping resistor R<sub>s</sub>** — without it, the R-C-L stray circuit rings and creates *over-voltage spikes* that re-fail the string.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Parallel Connection Design**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q-Set B]**

> **(a)** Explain the **need for parallel connection of SCRs** in high-current applications. With neat diagrams, describe **static and dynamic current-sharing methods** in a parallel SCR stack. Derive the expression for the **equal current-sharing reactor L<sub>s</sub>**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Three SCRs are connected in parallel to share a total load current of **1500 A**. Their ON-state voltage drops at the rated current are **1.6 V, 1.8 V, and 2.0 V** respectively. The maximum allowable current imbalance is **±10 %** of the average branch current. Compute (i) the **current in each branch**, (ii) the **series current-sharing resistor R<sub>p</sub>** for the highest-current branch, and (iii) the **current unbalance factor K<sub>u</sub>** if no current-sharing reactor is used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Need (2 marks):**
- Single SCRs are limited to ~ 3000 A commercially.
- High-current applications (HVDC inverters, traction drives, smelters, DC arc furnaces) demand currents of **10 kA – 50 kA**.
- Multiple SCRs must be placed in **parallel** to share the load current.

**Static current sharing (2 marks):**
- A small resistor **R<sub>p</sub>** in series with each SCR forces the current to divide in inverse proportion to the resistances.
- A reactor **L<sub>s</sub>** in series (air-core, saturable) provides a lossless share for AC.
- Schematic (Section 4.2) shows the layout.

**Dynamic current sharing (1 mark):**
- During turn-on, the *fastest* SCR would take a disproportionate di/dt. The series reactor L<sub>s</sub> slows the rate of rise of current in the fastest branch, allowing the slower branches to catch up.
- The reactor value is calculated from:

$$L_{s} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{T}}{(di/dt)_{\max}}$$

**Derivation of L<sub>s</sub> (2 marks):**
- The voltage difference ΔV<sub>T</sub> between the fastest and slowest SCRs drives an extra current di/dt through L<sub>s</sub>:

$$V_{L_s} = L_{s} \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$$

- For the equalization to hold over the entire turn-on transient, L<sub>s</sub> must be large enough to absorb ΔV<sub>T</sub> at the maximum allowable di/dt:

$$L_{s} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{T}}{(di/dt)_{\max}}$$

- This L<sub>s</sub> value ensures that no single SCR exceeds its rated di/dt.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — List the data:**

- m = 3, I<sub>T</sub> = 1500 A
- V<sub>T1</sub> = 1.6 V, V<sub>T2</sub> = 1.8 V, V<sub>T3</sub> = 2.0 V
- Allowable imbalance = ±10 % of average.

**Step 2 — Compute average branch current:**

$$I_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{I_{T}}{m} = \dfrac{1500}{3} = 500 \text{ A}$$

Allowable spread: ±10 % → I<sub>min</sub> = 450 A, I<sub>max</sub> = 550 A.

**Step 3 — Compute branch currents without R<sub>p</sub> (raw case):**

In the *raw* (no R<sub>p</sub>) case, all branches have the same voltage across them (parallel), so the currents are **inversely proportional** to V<sub>T</sub>... but V<sub>T</sub> rises with I. We use a linear model: V<sub>T</sub> = V<sub>0</sub> + r<sub>d</sub>·I.

Without R<sub>p</sub>, the current division depends on the **differential resistance r<sub>d</sub>**. Assume r<sub>d</sub> is identical for all three SCRs (a reasonable assumption for matched devices). Then the *difference* in currents is driven by the *difference* in offset V<sub>0</sub>:

$$\Delta V = V_{T3} - V_{T1} = 0.4 \text{ V}$$

If we treat r<sub>d</sub> ≈ 1 mΩ (typical for a 500 A SCR), then for two branches:

$$\Delta I = \dfrac{\Delta V}{2 \, r_{d}} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.002} = 200 \text{ A}$$

So one branch carries 600 A, the other 500 A, the third 400 A.

**Step 4 — Compute R<sub>p</sub> for the highest-current branch:**

The highest-current branch is T1 (V<sub>T1</sub> = 1.6 V). We want its current to drop from 600 A to 500 A — a ΔI of 100 A. But the KTU convention limits unbalance to ±10 % of the **average**, so we want all branches to be 500 A.

We need to **raise** the effective resistance of T1 and **lower** it for T3. The simplest design is a single R<sub>p</sub> in T1 (worst branch):

$$R_{p} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{T}}{\Delta I} = \dfrac{0.4 \text{ V}}{100 \text{ A}} = 0.004 \; \Omega = 4 \; \text{m}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Power loss in R<sub>p</sub> (per branch):**

$$P_{R_p} = (I_{\text{branch}})^{2} \cdot R_{p} = (500)^{2} \times 0.004 = 1000 \text{ W}$$

This is **significant** — confirming that **R<sub>p</sub> alone is impractical** for high-current (>100 A) parallel stacks. We use **reactors** instead.

**Step 6 — Compute K<sub>u</sub> (no equalizer):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{u} &= \dfrac{I_{\max} - I_{\text{avg}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{600 - 500}{500} \times 100 = 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Box:** I<sub>1</sub> = 600 A, I<sub>2</sub> = 500 A, I<sub>3</sub> = 400 A; R<sub>p</sub> = 4 mΩ (T1 branch only); K<sub>u</sub> = 20 %.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Parallel):**
> 1. **Using R<sub>p</sub> alone for > 1000 A stacks** — the I²R loss is unacceptable; the examiner marks this as an engineering *blunder*.
> 2. **Forgetting thermal runaway** — the hottest branch shares *more* current, so heat sinks must be **independent** (not shared) and **derated** by at least 20 %.
> 3. **Confusing R<sub>p</sub> with the R in the series problem** — they look similar but their physical placement is **opposite** (parallel branch = *series* with SCR; series string = *parallel* with SCR).
> 4. **Not showing the inductor equation** $L_{s} = \Delta V_{T} / (di/dt)_{\max}$ explicitly — KTU gives **2 marks** for this one equation alone.

---

## 5.3 KTU-Style Common Mistakes & Valuation Red Flags

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 10 Reasons Students Lose Marks in this Module (KTU 2024):**
> 1. Confusing *static* with *dynamic* equalizer — static = R, dynamic = C.
> 2. Writing "string efficiency = V<sub>string</sub> / V<sub>device</sub>" — the *correct* form is $V_{\text{string}} / (n \cdot V_{\text{device}})$.
> 3. Forgetting to **derate** the per-device voltage in design.
> 4. Using SI units inconsistently (kV vs V, mA vs A).
> 5. Skipping the **schematic** in a 7-mark sub-question (KTU allots ≥ 2 marks for the figure).
> 6. Not showing the **KVL/KCL** derivation step-by-step.
> 7. Writing the parallel resistor formula in series context or vice versa.
> 8. Ignoring the **Q<sub>rr</sub>** contribution to dynamic sharing.
> 9. Not stating the **boundary conditions** (e.g., I<sub>R</sub> ≫ I<sub>L(max)</sub>).
> 10. Failing to draw the **heat-sink and thermal-path** consideration in high-current design.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-friendly):**

### ✅ Series Connection — Quick Facts
- [x] Used when **V<sub>s</sub> > V<sub>DRM</sub>** of a single SCR.
- [x] All SCRs share the **same current** in ON state.
- [x] Voltage sharing is **unequal** due to leakage mismatch (static) and Q<sub>rr</sub> mismatch (dynamic).
- [x] **Static equalizer = R** in parallel with each SCR. $R = \dfrac{n V_{s/n} - V_{s}}{I_{L(\text{min})} - I_{L(\text{max})}}$.
- [x] **Dynamic equalizer = R-C snubber** in parallel with each SCR.
- [x] **String efficiency** η<sub>s</sub> = (V<sub>actual</sub> / n·V<sub>rating</sub>) × 100. Typical = 80 – 95 %.
- [x] **DRF** = (1 − η<sub>s</sub>) × 100.

### ✅ Parallel Connection — Quick Facts
- [x] Used when **I<sub>T</sub> > I<sub>T(AV)</sub>** of a single SCR.
- [x] All SCRs share the **same voltage** in OFF state.
- [x] Current sharing is **unequal** due to V<sub>T</sub> mismatch (static) and t<sub>on</sub> mismatch (dynamic).
- [x] **Static equalizer = R<sub>p</sub>** in series with each SCR. $R_{p} = \Delta V_{T} / \Delta I$.
- [x] **Dynamic equalizer = L<sub>s</sub>** in series with each SCR. $L_{s} = \Delta V_{T} / (di/dt)_{\max}$.
- [x] **Current unbalance factor** K<sub>u</sub> = (I<sub>max</sub> − I<sub>avg</sub>) / I<sub>avg</sub> × 100. Typical < 10 %.
- [x] Heat sinks are **per-branch** (no sharing) to prevent thermal runaway.

### ✅ Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid
- [x] Always convert mA → A before substituting.
- [x] Round **up** the value of n and m (integer of devices).
- [x] Always include a **safety margin** of 10–20 % in design.
- [x] Heat-sink thermal resistance R<sub>θSA</sub> = (T<sub>j(max)</sub> − T<sub>A</sub>) / P<sub>loss</sub> − R<sub>θJS</sub>.

### ✅ Key Engineering Trade-offs
- [x] Better sharing ⇔ higher losses ⇔ larger heat sinks ⇔ higher cost.
- [x] Reactors > Resistors for AC (lossless).
- [x] Resistors > Reactors for DC (reactors can't share DC).
- [x] Snubbers are mandatory at **every level** of a string (per-device).

### ✅ HVDC Real-World Anchors
- [x] **±800 kV** HVDC line: ~ **80 SCRs** per valve-arm.
- [x] **±100 kV** HVDC: ~ **15 SCRs** per valve-arm.
- [x] **String efficiency** target = **90 %** minimum for HVDC valves.
- [x] **DRF** typical = **10 – 15 %** for commercial valves.
- [x] **η<sub>s</sub> drops** with **temperature** (leakage current rises with T).
- [x] Use of *closely matched* SCRs (same batch, same lot number) is the **first** design rule.

### ✅ KTU Board-Favourite One-Liners to Memorize
- "*Static equalization uses **resistor**, dynamic uses **R-C**.*"
- "*In series connection, **current is the same**, voltage divides.*"
- "*In parallel connection, **voltage is the same**, current divides.*"
- "*String efficiency is the **ratio of usable to rated voltage** of the string.*"
- "*Derating factor is the **complement of string efficiency**.*"
- "*For DC current sharing — **resistor**; for AC — **reactor**.*"
