- "*Thermal runaway makes parallel connection *more critical* than series.*"
- "*The **fastest-turning-off** SCR takes the **most voltage** in a series string.*"
- "*The **lowest-V<sub>T</sub>** SCR takes the **most current** in a parallel stack.*"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turn-On Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Turn-On Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In Power Electronics for Control Engineering, the **Turn-On Characteristics** of a thyristor (Silicon Controlled Rectifier, SCR) describe the **dynamic transient response** of the device as it transitions from the **forward blocking state (OFF state)** to the **forward conduction state (ON state)** when a gate trigger pulse is applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The turn-on process is the sequence of physical events inside the four-layer PNPN structure during which the depletion regions collapse, the device latches, and the anode current rises from a small leakage value to the full load current value, while the anode-cathode voltage simultaneously collapses from the supply voltage to the forward voltage drop (≈ 1 V to 2 V).

Mathematically, the **total turn-on time (t_on)** is defined as the interval between the instant when the gate current reaches **90 % of its steady-state triggering value (I_GT)** and the instant when the anode current reaches **90 % of its final latched value (I_L)**.

The total turn-on time is expressed as:

$$
t_{on} = t_d + t_r
$$

Where:
- **t_d** = Delay Time
- **t_r** = Rise Time

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Floodgate Analogy:**
> Imagine a heavy water gate (the SCR) holding back a large reservoir (the supply voltage). The gate is **locked** in the blocking state. To open it, an operator presses a small **push-button** (the gate trigger pulse). However, the gate does not swing open instantly:
> 1. First, the **mechanical latch disengages** → this is the **Delay Time (t_d)**.
> 2. Then, the gate **swings open and water rushes in, gradually filling the downstream channel** → this is the **Rise Time (t_r)**.
> 
> The total time from pressing the button to the gate being fully open and water flowing at full rate is the **Turn-On Time (t_on)**. The "spread" of water across the channel width is analogous to the **lateral spread velocity** of the conducting region across the silicon wafer.

This analogy makes it clear: turn-on is **not instantaneous** — it involves measurable delays, voltage collapse, current rise, and a spatial spreading of conduction from the gate region to the entire junction area.

## 1.3 Standardized KTU Performance Metrics

The following **bolded metrics** are standard KTU-evaluation parameters for turn-on:

- **I_L (Latching Current):** Minimum anode current required to sustain conduction after the gate signal is removed.
- **I_H (Holding Current):** Minimum anode current to keep the device in the ON state.
- **I_GT (Gate Trigger Current):** Minimum gate current needed to turn ON the SCR.
- **V_GT (Gate Trigger Voltage):** Corresponding gate voltage for I_GT.
- **dv/dt:** Critical rate of rise of forward voltage that causes unwanted (parasitic) turn-on.
- **di/dt:** Critical rate of rise of anode current that the device can safely withstand during turn-on.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must distinguish between **turn-on by gate signal (intentional)** and **turn-on by dv/dt (unintentional / parasitic)**. Both are part of the Module 1 turn-on study.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Distinct Sub-Intervals of Turn-On

When a gate pulse I_G is applied to a forward-biased SCR, the turn-on process unfolds in **four sequential physical sub-stages**:

### Stage 1 — Initiation at the Gate Region (≈ 0 to t_1)
- Gate current I_G begins to flow.
- Holes are injected from the P-layer near the gate into the N-layer.
- A small localized area adjacent to the gate terminal begins to conduct.
- The **anode current i_A is still negligible** (leakage level ≈ mA).
- The **anode voltage v_AK remains essentially at the supply voltage V_S**.

### Stage 2 — Delay Period (t_1 to t_d)
- Holes injected near the gate region spread laterally across the **n-base layer** by diffusion and drift.
- The **conduction area gradually expands** from the gate periphery outward.
- The **anode current i_A rises slowly** from leakage to ≈ 10 % of the latched value.
- The **anode voltage v_AK falls slightly** (≈ 5 % to 10 %).
- This phase ends at t = t_d (Delay Time).

### Stage 3 — Rise Period (t_d to t_d + t_r)
- The **regenerative action** of the PNPN structure takes over (α₁ + α₂ → 1).
- The **anode current i_A rises rapidly** from 10 % to 90 % of its final latched value I_L.
- The **anode voltage v_AK collapses rapidly** from ≈ 90 % of V_S to the forward drop (≈ 1 V to 2 V).
- The **on-state power loss P = v_AK × i_A peaks** during this interval (turn-on loss).

### Stage 4 — Conduction Spread (after t_d + t_r)
- The conducting region continues to spread until the **entire junction area** is in conduction.
- Steady-state ON-state losses dominate.

## 2.2 Definitions According to IEEE / IEC Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Delay Time (t_d):** Time interval from the instant the gate current reaches 50 % of its final value (some texts use 90 %) to the instant the anode current reaches 10 % of its final (latched) value. **KTU convention** uses the 90 %-of-I_GT to 10 %-of-I_L definition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rise Time (t_r):** Time interval during which the anode current rises from 10 % to 90 % of its final latched value I_L. Equivalently, the anode voltage falls from 90 % to 10 % of its initial value V_S.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spread Time (t_s):** Time required for the conduction to spread across the entire junction area (typically 30 µs to 100 µs for conventional SCRs). Often part of the rise time in simplified KTU analysis.

## 2.3 Why Is the Turn-On Time Important?

- **Switching Losses:** P_on = ½ × V_S × I_L × (t_d + t_r) × f, where f is the switching frequency. At high frequencies, turn-on loss dominates the thermal design of the device.
- **Snubber Design:** The di/dt during the rise time determines the **series inductance (L_s)** required in the snubber to limit di/dt below the device's rated critical di/dt.
- **Parallel Operation:** Mismatched turn-on times cause **unequal current sharing** when SCRs are connected in parallel.
- **EMI Generation:** Rapid voltage collapse (dv/dt) generates electromagnetic interference.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total turn-on time | $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$ | 1 – 10 (line-frequency SCR) | µs | High-frequency limit check |
| 2 | Delay time | $t_d$ | 0.5 – 2.0 | µs | Trigger pulse-width design |
| 3 | Rise time | $t_r$ | 0.5 – 5.0 | µs | Snubber inductance sizing |
| 4 | Turn-on loss energy | $E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_S I_L \, t_{on}$ | 0.1 – 50 | mJ | Heatsink design |
| 5 | Turn-on power loss | $P_{on} = E_{on} \times f$ | 0.1 – 500 | W | Thermal calculation |
| 6 | Series snubber inductance | $L_s = \frac{V_S}{(\frac{di}{dt})_{crit}}$ | 1 – 100 | µH | di/dt protection |
| 7 | Critical dv/dt | $(\frac{dv}{dt})_{crit}$ | 50 – 1000 | V/µs | False triggering check |
| 8 | Gate trigger current | $I_{GT}$ | 1 – 200 | mA | Drive circuit design |
| 9 | Latching current | $I_L$ | 10 – 500 | mA | Minimum load check |
| 10 | Holding current | $I_H$ | 1 – 100 | mA | Commutation check |
| 11 | Spread velocity | $v_s \approx 0.1 \times 10^3$ | 0.05 – 0.1 | mm/µs | Spread time estimation |
| 12 | Anode current during rise | $i_A(t) = I_L \, (1 - e^{-t/\tau_{on}})$ | — | A | Transient modeling |
| 13 | Regenerative condition | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \ge 1$ | — | — | Latching criterion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When solving numerical problems, always state the **units explicitly** and the **assumed reference directions** of v_AK and i_A. Marks are frequently lost for omitting units in formula substitution.

## 2.5 Methods of Turn-On (KTU 2024 Module 1 Coverage)

The thyristor can be turned ON by **five distinct methods**, of which gate triggering is the most important for control engineering:

1. **Forward Voltage Triggering** — V_AK exceeds the forward breakover voltage V_BO (rarely used in practice).
2. **Gate Triggering** — Application of I_G > I_GT (the principal control method).
3. **dv/dt Triggering** — Unintentional turn-on when anode voltage rises too rapidly; must be avoided.
4. **Light Triggering (LTT)** — Used in HVDC light-triggered thyristors.
5. **Thermal Triggering (Temperature)** — Junction temperature exceeds the limit; failure mode.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Do not confuse **dv/dt turn-on** (unintentional, parasitic) with the **rate of fall of voltage during intentional turn-on**. They are different physical phenomena measured at different instants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Turn-On Energy Loss (E_on)

We will derive the energy dissipated inside the thyristor during the **rise time interval** (when both v_AK is significant and i_A is significant — the only time substantial loss occurs).

**Step 1 — Identify the time interval of interest.**

The rise time interval is:

$$
t \in [t_d, \; t_d + t_r]
$$

**Step 2 — Approximate the waveforms as linear ramps.**

During turn-on, the anode voltage and current can be approximated as:

$$
v_{AK}(t) \approx V_S \left(1 - \frac{t - t_d}{t_r}\right), \quad t_d \le t \le t_d + t_r
$$

$$
i_A(t) \approx I_L \left(\frac{t - t_d}{t_r}\right), \quad t_d \le t \le t_d + t_r
$$

Here, V_S is the supply voltage and I_L is the final latched current.

**Step 3 — Write the instantaneous power dissipation.**

The instantaneous power dissipated inside the device is the product of voltage and current:

$$
p(t) = v_{AK}(t) \cdot i_A(t)
$$

Substituting the linear approximations:

$$
p(t) = V_S \left(1 - \frac{t - t_d}{t_r}\right) \cdot I_L \left(\frac{t - t_d}{t_r}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute a normalized time variable for simplicity.**

Let

$$
x = \frac{t - t_d}{t_r}, \quad x \in [0, 1], \quad dx = \frac{dt}{t_r}
$$

Then:

$$
p(t) = V_S \, I_L \, (1 - x)(x) = V_S \, I_L \, (x - x^2)
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate power over the rise time to obtain the energy.**

The energy dissipated during turn-on is:

$$
E_{on} = \int_{t_d}^{t_d + t_r} p(t) \, dt
$$

Changing variables using x and dt = t_r \, dx:

$$
E_{on} = \int_{0}^{1} V_S \, I_L \, (x - x^2) \, t_r \, dx
$$

Factoring the constants:

$$
E_{on} = V_S \, I_L \, t_r \int_{0}^{1} (x - x^2) \, dx
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the definite integral.**

$$
\int_{0}^{1} (x - x^2) \, dx = \left[\frac{x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{6}
$$

**Step 7 — Final expression for turn-on energy.**

$$
\boxed{E_{on} = \frac{1}{6} \, V_S \, I_L \, t_r}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The factor **1/6** arises from the symmetric (triangular) overlap of the falling voltage and rising current waveforms. If the rise time t_r is much smaller than t_d (which is typical for modern SCRs), t_d is often neglected, giving E_on ≈ (1/6) V_S I_L t_r.

**Step 8 — Compute the average turn-on power loss for a switching frequency f.**

If the SCR switches at a frequency f, the average power loss is:

$$
P_{on} = E_{on} \times f = \frac{1}{6} \, V_S \, I_L \, t_r \, f
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Snubber Inductance from di/dt Limit

When the SCR turns on, the rate of rise of anode current is limited by the **series inductance L_s** in the anode circuit. We derive the minimum inductance required to keep di/dt below the device rating.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the anode circuit.**

The series circuit consists of supply V_S, series inductance L_s, and the SCR:

$$
V_S = L_s \, \frac{di_A}{dt} + v_{AK}
$$

**Step 2 — During the rise time, v_AK is collapsing from V_S to ≈ 0. Use the worst-case condition.**

The worst case for di/dt occurs at the **instant of turn-on (t = t_d)**, when v_AK ≈ V_S (the device has not yet begun to conduct significantly). At this instant, the entire supply voltage appears across the inductor:

$$
V_S \approx L_s \left.\frac{di_A}{dt}\right|_{t = t_d}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for L_s.**

$$
L_s = \frac{V_S}{\left(\frac{di_A}{dt}\right)_{crit}}
$$

Where (di_A/dt)_crit is the maximum permissible rate of rise of anode current, given in the device datasheet (typically 50 A/µs to 500 A/µs).

**Step 4 — Final expression for snubber inductance.**

$$
\boxed{L_s = \frac{V_S}{(di/dt)_{crit}}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Use:** This inductance is realized either as a **physical air-core inductor** (for high-power SCR inverters) or as the **stray inductance of the bus-bar** (for low-power applications). Modern fast SCRs have very high di/dt ratings, so a small L_s is sufficient.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Exponential Turn-On Current Model (Regenerative Region)

During the regenerative turn-on, the device behaves as a **positive-feedback amplifier**. We can model the anode current as an exponential rise.

**Step 1 — Write the differential equation of the regenerative loop.**

The total current gain of the two-transistor analog is:

$$
\alpha(I_A) = \alpha_1(I_A) + \alpha_2(I_A)
$$

The current rise follows:

$$
\frac{di_A}{dt} = \frac{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}{\tau} \, i_A
$$

Where τ is a time constant related to the carrier transit time.

**Step 2 — Solve the first-order ODE.**

$$
i_A(t) = I_{A0} \, e^{\left(\frac{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}{\tau}\right) t}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the latching condition.**

The device latches when i_A reaches I_L:

$$
I_L = I_{A0} \, e^{\left(\frac{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}{\tau}\right) t_{on}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for turn-on time t_on.**

$$
t_{on} = \tau \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I_L}{I_{A0}}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** As α₁ + α₂ → 1 (just below the regenerative threshold), t_on → ∞. This is why the gate pulse must drive the device **firmly above** the regenerative threshold to obtain a fast, deterministic turn-on.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Turn-On Methods

| Method | Trigger Quantity | Speed | Control? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward voltage | V_AK > V_BO | Slow | No | Over-voltage protection |
| Gate triggering | I_G > I_GT | Fast (1–10 µs) | **Yes (primary)** | Phase-controlled rectifiers, choppers, inverters |
| dv/dt | dv_AK/dt > (dv/dt)_crit | Very fast (sub-µs) | No (parasitic) | Failure mode to avoid |
| Light (LTT) | Photon flux | Medium | Yes (optical isolation) | HVDC valves |
| Thermal | T_junction > T_max | Slow | No (failure) | Not used intentionally |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Turn-On Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forward Biased SCR V_AK less than V_BO] --> B[Gate Pulse I_G Applied]
    B --> C[Stage 1: Initiation near Gate Region]
    C --> D[Stage 2: Delay Time t_d Lateral Spread of Carriers]
    D --> E[Stage 3: Regenerative Action alpha1 plus alpha2 greater than 1]
    E --> F[Stage 4: Rise Time t_r Anode Current Rises to I_L]
    F --> G[SCR Fully ON v_AK collapses to V_T approx 1 to 2 V]
    G --> H[Conduction Spreads Across Full Junction Area]
    H --> I[Steady State ON Loss P_on equals V_T times I_L]
    I --> J[Remove Gate Signal Device stays ON Latched]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Timing Relationships

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Gate as Gate Drive
    participant SCR as SCR Device
    participant Load as Anode Load
    Gate->>SCR: Apply I_G greater than I_GT at t = 0
    Note over SCR: Stage 1 Initiation t approx 0 to t1
    SCR->>SCR: Carrier spread begins
    Note over SCR: Stage 2 Delay Time t_d
    SCR-->>Load: i_A begins to rise reaches 10 percent of I_L
    Note over SCR: Stage 3 Rise Time t_r
    SCR->>Load: i_A rises from 10 percent to 90 percent of I_L
    SCR-->>Gate: v_AK collapses from V_S to V_T
    Note over SCR: Stage 4 Spread Time t_s
    SCR->>Load: Full junction area conducting
    Note over Gate,Load: Total t_on = t_d + t_r complete
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Turn-On Snubber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDC[DC Supply V_S] --> LS[Series Inductance L_s limits di/dt]
    LS --> SCR[SCR Device]
    SCR --> LOAD[Resistive or Inductive Load]
    GD[Gate Drive Circuit] -->|I_GT pulse| SCR
    CS[Parallel RCD Snubber limits dv/dt across SCR] --> SCR
    HEATSINK[Heatsink dissipates P_on] --> SCR
    SCR -.->|Heat E_on| HEATSINK
    SCR -.->|Heat conduction loss| HEATSINK
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Waveform Schematic — v_AK and i_A During Turn-On

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Time Axis: t = 0] --> W2[At t = 0: v_AK = V_S, i_A = I_LK small]
    W2 --> W3[At t = t_d: i_A reaches 10 percent of I_L, v_AK still near V_S]
    W3 --> W4[At t = t_d + t_r: i_A reaches 90 percent of I_L, v_AK collapses to V_T]
    W4 --> W5[For t greater than t_d + t_r: i_A = I_L steady, v_AK = V_T steady]
    note1[Linear approximation used for KTU problems] -.-> W3
    note1 -.-> W4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The waveforms above represent the **idealized linear approximation** used in KTU numerical problems. In reality, the rise of i_A is **exponential** (regenerative), and the fall of v_AK is governed by the **stored charge extraction** in the n-base layer. For derivations, refer to **Section 3.3**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define the terms "delay time" and "rise time" of an SCR during turn-on.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Delay Time (t_d)** [1.5 Marks]: It is the time interval between the instant when the gate current reaches 90 % of its triggering value (I_GT) and the instant when the anode current rises to 10 % of its final latched value (I_L). During this interval, the carriers injected by the gate spread laterally through the n-base region and the anode current is still small.
2. **Rise Time (t_r)** [1.5 Marks]: It is the time interval during which the anode current rises from 10 % to 90 % of its final latched value I_L, and simultaneously the anode-cathode voltage falls from 90 % to 10 % of its initial value V_S. The regenerative action (α₁ + α₂ ≥ 1) dominates this interval, and the turn-on power loss is maximum.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the significance of latching current I_L and holding current I_H in the turn-on and turn-off of an SCR?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Latching Current I_L** [1.5 Marks]: It is the minimum anode current that must be attained during turn-on so that the SCR remains in the ON state even after the gate trigger pulse is removed. If the anode current fails to reach I_L before the gate pulse is removed, the device will turn OFF.
2. **Holding Current I_H** [1.5 Marks]: It is the minimum anode current required to keep the SCR in the ON state under steady-state conditions. If the anode current falls below I_H, the device will turn OFF. Note that **I_H < I_L** for a given device.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the turn-on characteristics of an SCR. Define delay time, rise time, spread time, and turn-on time. Derive an expression for the turn-on power loss of the SCR.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Turn-On Characteristics Sketch and Definitions

**Model Solution:**

1. **Sketch (3 Marks):** Draw the turn-on characteristic showing:
   - **X-axis:** Time (µs), starting from the instant the gate pulse is applied.
   - **Y-axis (left):** Anode current i_A (rising from leakage to I_L).
   - **Y-axis (right):** Anode voltage v_AK (falling from V_S to V_T ≈ 1 to 2 V).
   - Mark the intervals t_d, t_r, and t_s clearly. Show the gate pulse I_G superimposed on the time axis.

2. **Definitions (4 Marks):**
   - **Delay time (t_d):** Time from 90 % of I_GT to 10 % of I_L. During this interval, the conducting region spreads laterally from the gate periphery. Typical value: 0.5 to 2 µs.
   - **Rise time (t_r):** Time for i_A to rise from 10 % to 90 % of I_L. Equivalent to v_AK falling from 90 % to 10 % of V_S. Typical value: 0.5 to 5 µs.
   - **Spread time (t_s):** Time for the conduction to spread across the entire junction area. Typical value: 30 to 100 µs for conventional SCRs.
   - **Turn-on time (t_on):** Sum of delay and rise times: t_on = t_d + t_r. Typical value: 1 to 10 µs for line-frequency SCRs.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Derivation of Turn-On Power Loss

**Model Solution:**

The derivation follows the steps in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key result is:

$$
E_{on} = \frac{1}{6} \, V_S \, I_L \, t_r
$$

And the average power loss at switching frequency f is:

$$
P_{on} = E_{on} \times f = \frac{1}{6} \, V_S \, I_L \, t_r \, f
$$

**Marking distribution for part (b):**
- Linear approximation of v_AK and i_A: **1.5 Marks**
- Expression for instantaneous power: **1.5 Marks**
- Correct integration setup: **2 Marks**
- Evaluation of the integral and final result: **2 Marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: Explain the different methods of turning on an SCR. Discuss the gate triggering method in detail, including the gate characteristics and the design of the gate trigger circuit.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Methods of Turn-On

**Model Solution:**

The five methods of turning on an SCR are:

1. **Forward Voltage Triggering** [1 Mark]: When the forward voltage across the SCR exceeds the forward breakover voltage V_BO, the device turns ON. This is undesirable in normal operation and is used only in over-voltage crowbar protection.
2. **Gate Triggering** [2 Marks]: Application of a gate current I_G greater than the gate trigger current I_GT turns the device ON. This is the **principal method used in control engineering**, as it allows precise phase-angle control of the firing instant. The firing angle α can be varied from 0° to 180° to control the output of a phase-controlled rectifier.
3. **dv/dt Triggering** [1.5 Marks]: A rapid rise of the anode voltage (high dv/dt) causes a displacement current C(dv/dt) through the junction capacitance, which behaves like a gate current and turns the device ON unintentionally. This is a parasitic effect and must be suppressed by a **snubber circuit**.
4. **Light Triggering** [1 Mark]: In Light-Triggered Thyristors (LTTs), incident photons generate carriers in the n-base, turning the device ON. Used in HVDC.
5. **Thermal Triggering** [0.5 Mark]: Excessive junction temperature generates thermal carriers, causing turn-on. This is a **failure mode**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Gate Triggering Method and Gate Characteristics

**Model Solution:**

1. **Gate Characteristics** [3 Marks]: The V_G — I_G characteristic of an SCR gate has three regions:
   - **Non-trigger region:** V_G < V_GD and I_G < I_GD — the device will not be triggered.
   - **Uncertain region (between V_GD and V_GT):** The device may or may not trigger. **Avoid operating in this region.**
   - **Sure-trigger region:** V_G > V_GT and I_G > I_GT — the device will definitely trigger.
   
   All SCRs of the same family must receive gate signals in the **sure-trigger region** to ensure reliable turn-on over the full operating temperature range.

2. **Gate Trigger Circuit Design** [4 Marks]: A typical gate trigger circuit consists of:
   - A **DC supply** (typically 10 V to 20 V).
   - A **series gate resistor R_G** to limit the gate current: R_G = (V_GG − V_GT) / I_GT.
   - A **pulse-shaping element** (often a unijunction transistor UJT or a diac) to produce a sharp, narrow trigger pulse of adequate amplitude.
   - **Isolation** between the control circuit and the power circuit, typically by an **opto-coupler** or a **pulse transformer**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing t_d and t_s:** Many students swap the definitions of delay time and spread time. Recall: t_d is the **initial** interval (0 to 10 % of I_L), while t_s is the **final** spreading of conduction across the wafer.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1/6 factor:** The turn-on energy is **E_on = (1/6) V_S I_L t_r**, not (1/2) V_S I_L t_r. The 1/6 comes from the triangular overlap integral.
> 3. **Mixing up I_L and I_H:** Latching current is for the turn-on process; holding current is for the steady ON state. They are different values, with I_H < I_L.
> 4. **Ignoring units in numerical substitution:** Always carry the units (V, A, µs, mJ) through every step of the calculation. Marks are deducted for missing units in the final answer.
> 5. **Treating dv/dt turn-on as intentional:** dv/dt turn-on is a **parasitic, unintended** phenomenon, not a control method.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Turn-On Characteristics of SCR**

- ☐ **t_on = t_d + t_r** is the fundamental turn-on time equation. t_s (spread time) is a related but separate concept.
- ☐ **Delay time (t_d):** Time from 90 % of I_GT to 10 % of I_L. Carrier spread phase.
- ☐ **Rise time (t_r):** Time for i_A to rise from 10 % to 90 % of I_L. Regenerative phase.
- ☐ **Turn-on energy:** **E_on = (1/6) V_S I_L t_r**. The factor 1/6 comes from the triangular voltage-current overlap.
- ☐ **Turn-on power loss:** P_on = E_on × f. Increases linearly with switching frequency.
- ☐ **Snubber inductance:** **L_s = V_S / (di/dt)_crit** — limits di/dt at turn-on to protect the device.
- ☐ **Five turn-on methods:** Forward voltage, Gate, dv/dt (parasitic), Light, Thermal.
- ☐ **Gate triggering** is the **primary control method** in power electronics. Firing angle α can be varied from 0° to 180°.
- ☐ **Latching current I_L > Holding current I_H** for a given SCR. The gate pulse must be wide enough for i_A to reach I_L.
- ☐ **dv/dt turn-on** is **unintentional** and must be prevented by an RCD snubber across the SCR.
- ☐ **Regenerative condition:** α₁ + α₂ ≥ 1 is the **latching criterion**. The gate pulse must push the device into this regime.
- ☐ **Typical turn-on times** for line-frequency SCRs: 1 to 10 µs. For fast-switching inverter SCRs: < 1 µs.
- ☐ **di/dt protection** is critical during the rise time interval to prevent localized hot-spot failure.
- ☐ **Gate trigger circuit** must operate in the **sure-trigger region** of the V_G — I_G characteristic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turn-Off Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Turn-Off Characteristics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition **Turn-Off Characteristics** of a power semiconductor device (specifically the **Silicon Controlled Rectifier / SCR / Thyristor**) refer to the dynamic voltage and current waveforms observed at the device terminals during the transition from the **ON state (forward conduction mode)** to the **OFF state (forward blocking mode)**. This transient process is governed by the internal recombination of excess minority charge carriers stored in the four-layer (P-N-P-N) structure during conduction. In strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, turn-off is **not a symmetrical process** to turn-on. While turn-on is initiated by a **tiny gate pulse** (latching action), turn-off requires the **anode current to be forced below the Holding Current ($I_H$)** for a finite duration called the **Turn-Off Time ($t_q$)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > "The Turn-Off Time ($t_q$) of an SCR is defined as the minimum time interval between the instant the anode current becomes zero (from a conducting state) and the instant the SCR is capable of blocking a forward voltage without re-triggering." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **heavy, loaded train** (the SCR in conduction) moving along a track: - **Turn-ON** is like the conductor blowing a small whistle → the train starts moving with full momentum. (A small gate pulse triggers a massive anode current.) - **Turn-OFF** is **NOT** like blowing another whistle. Even if you shout, the train will keep moving because of its momentum. - The **only way** to stop the train is to either: 1. Apply the **brakes forcefully** (force the current down to zero externally — called **commutation**), OR 2. Push it up a **steep hill** until it rolls backward (reverse bias the device). - Even after the train stops, the **brakes remain hot** for some time. If you try to push the train forward immediately, the hot brakes may cause it to roll back into motion accidentally. This "hot brake" period is the **Turn-Off Time ($t_q$)**. > [!NOTE] > The "hot brake" represents the **stored charge carriers** in the P-N-P-N junctions. They must be neutralized/removed before the device can safely block forward voltage again. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Metrics (Bolded for Memory) | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude (SCR) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $t_q$ | Turn-Off Time | **30 – 200 $\mu s$** | | $I_H$ | Holding Current | **few mA to few hundred mA** | | $I_L$ | Latching Current | **2$\times$ to 3$\times$ $I_H$** | | $t_s$ | Storage Time | **typically 50% of $t_q$** | | $t_{rr}$ | Reverse Recovery Time | **1 – 10 $\mu s$** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Stage Turn-Off Process — Bulleted Logic The turn-off of an SCR is fundamentally a **charge-removal problem**, not a switch-flipping problem. It proceeds in two distinct stages: ### **Stage 1: Storage Phase (Time $t_s$)** - The external circuit forces the anode current below zero (reverse bias is applied). - During this phase, the **stored minority charge carriers** in the two inner transistor regions (N1 and P2 of the P-N-P-N structure) are still present in high concentration. - The device behaves like a **forward-conducting diode with stored charge**. - The reverse current is limited only by the external circuit impedance. - **End of Stage 1**: Stored charge $Q_{st}$ is fully removed; the device begins to support reverse voltage. ### **Stage 2: Fall Phase (Time $t_f$)** - Once the stored charge is exhausted, the reverse current decays rapidly to zero (with a $di/dt$ rate determined by the snubber). - The depletion regions start rebuilding across the junctions J1 and J3. - The device now begins to block voltage. - **Critical Sub-Condition**: The forward voltage **must not be re-applied** until this phase is complete, or the device will re-trigger (a phenomenon called **commutation failure**). ### **Stage 3: Forward Blocking Recovery (Gate Recovery Time $t_{gr}$)** - Even after the current reaches zero, the junction capacitance must recharge. - The device's **dv/dt capability** is limited during this interval. - Only after $t_s + t_f + t_{gr}$ is the SCR fully ready to block forward voltage. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL LATEX RULE:** All vertical bars use `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. | # | Formula | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $t_q = t_s + t_f$ | Turn-off time (KTU board definition) | seconds | | 2 | $t_q = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$ | Equivalent: reverse recovery + gate recovery | seconds | | 3 | $Q_{st} = \int_{0}^{t_s} i_R \, dt$ | Stored charge removed during storage phase | Coulombs | | 4 | $\dfrac{dv}{dt} \leq \dfrac{V_{BO}}{\Delta t}$ | Critical rate of rise of forward voltage | V/$\mu$s | | 5 | $I_H = \dfrac{V_{BB}}{R_L}\bigg\vert_{I_G=0}$ | Holding current boundary condition | Amperes | | 6 | $P_{loss,\,off} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{RRM} \cdot I_{rr} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f$ | Switching loss during turn-off (per cycle) | Watts | | 7 | $t_{c} \geq t_q$ | Circuit turn-off time must exceed device $t_q$ | seconds | ## 2.3 Why Turn-Off is Harder Than Turn-On — The Engineering "Why" - **Turn-ON** is regenerative: a small gate current triggers an internal positive feedback loop ($I_C = \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_K$ → both alphas exceed unity sum). Once started, it self-sustains. - **Turn-OFF** requires the **deliberate destruction** of this regenerative condition. The anode current must be forced to a value where $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 < 1$. - This is why SCRs are classified as **"semi-controlled"** devices — easy to turn on, hard to turn off. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **AC-DC Rectifiers**: Natural commutation via AC line voltage makes turn-off "free." - **DC-DC Choppers & Inverters**: Requires **forced commutation circuits** (Class A, B, C, D, E) — adds cost and complexity. - **Modern Solution**: This is exactly why **IGBTs, MOSFETs, and GTOs** were invented — to provide easy turn-off capability at the cost of higher conduction loss. - **Application in Drives**: Turn-off time limits the **maximum operating frequency** of an SCR chopper: $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{10 \cdot t_q}$. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Turn-Off Time from Carrier Recombination Physics ### **Setup** During forward conduction, the excess minority carrier concentration at the middle junction (J2) follows a charge continuity equation. When the anode current is suddenly forced to zero, the carrier concentration decays exponentially. ### **Step 1: Charge Continuity Equation** At the moment turn-off is initiated ($t=0$), the stored charge in the N1 and P2 regions begins to decay by recombination: $$ \frac{dQ(t)}{dt} = -\frac{Q(t)}{\tau_p} $$ where $\tau_p$ is the **minority carrier lifetime** in the lightly doped region. ### **Step 2: Solve the Differential Equation** This is a first-order linear ODE. Separating variables: $$ \frac{dQ}{Q} = -\frac{dt}{\tau_p} $$ Integrating both sides from $Q_0$ (initial stored charge) to $Q(t)$: $$ \int_{Q_0}^{Q(t)} \frac{dQ}{Q} = -\frac{1}{\tau_p}\int_{0}^{t} dt $$ $$ \ln\left(\frac{Q(t)}{Q_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau_p} $$ ### **Step 3: Exponential Decay Law** Exponentiating both sides: $$ Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_p} $$ ### **Step 4: Define the Storage Time ($t_s$)** The storage time is defined as the time required for the stored charge to fall to a critical threshold $Q_{min}$ (the minimum charge needed to sustain conduction). Setting $Q(t_s) = Q_{min}$: $$ Q_{min} = Q_0 \cdot e^{-t_s/\tau_p} $$ Solving for $t_s$ by taking natural log of both sides: $$ \ln(Q_{min}) - \ln(Q_0) = -\frac{t_s}{\tau_p} $$ $$ t_s = \tau_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{Q_0}{Q_{min}}\right) $$ ### **Step 5: Relate Stored Charge to Anode Current** The initial stored charge is proportional to the peak forward current $I_F$: $$ Q_0 = K \cdot I_F $$ where $K$ is a device constant with units of **Coulombs per Ampere (s)**. Substituting: $$ \boxed{t_s = \tau_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{K \cdot I_F}{Q_{min}}\right)} $$ ### **Step 6: Engineering Interpretation** This boxed equation tells us three critical design rules: 1. **Higher anode current $I_F$ → longer storage time** (harder to turn off). 2. **Shorter carrier lifetime $\tau_p$ → faster turn-off** (but higher conduction loss). 3. **The turn-off time has a logarithmic dependence** — increasing $I_F$ by 10× only adds about 2.3 $\tau_p$ to the storage time. ### **Step 7: Total Turn-Off Time Expression** Adding the fall time $t_f$ (typically $0.3 \tau_p$ for modern SCRs) and gate recovery time $t_{gr}$: $$ \boxed{t_q = \tau_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{K \cdot I_F}{Q_{min}}\right) + t_f + t_{gr}} $$ ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard) > **Problem:** A thyristor has a storage time of $40 \mu s$ when carrying a forward current of 100 A. If the carrier lifetime is $25 \mu s$, calculate the storage time if the forward current is doubled to 200 A. Assume $Q_{min}$ remains constant. ### **Solution** **Step A:** Use the derived formula for two different currents. $$ t_{s1} = \tau_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{K \cdot I_{F1}}{Q_{min}}\right) $$ $$ t_{s2} = \tau_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{K \cdot I_{F2}}{Q_{min}}\right) $$ **Step B:** Subtract the two equations to eliminate the unknown constant $K/Q_{min}$: $$ t_{s2} - t_{s1} = \tau_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I_{F2}}{I_{F1}}\right) $$ **Step C:** Substitute the numerical values $I_{F1} = 100$ A, $I_{F2} = 200$ A, $\tau_p = 25 \mu s$: $$ t_{s2} - 40 = 25 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{200}{100}\right) $$ $$ t_{s2} - 40 = 25 \cdot \ln(2) $$ **Step D:** Evaluate $\ln(2) \approx 0.6931$: $$ t_{s2} = 40 + 25 \times 0.6931 $$ $$ t_{s2} = 40 + 17.33 $$ $$ \boxed{t_{s2} \approx 57.33 \ \mu s} $$ **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the current increased the storage time by only $\approx 43\%$ — confirming the **logarithmic, not linear**, dependence. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Turn-Off Methods | Method | How It Works | dv/dt Sensitivity | Cost | KTU Frequency | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Natural (Line)** | AC voltage reverses naturally | Low | Free | **High** | | **Class A (Resonant)** | LC tank forces $i_A = 0$ | Very Low | High | Medium | | **Class B (Complementary)** | Auxiliary SCR + LC swaps current | Medium | Medium | **High** | | **Class C (Capacitor coupled)** | Capacitor dumps reverse current | Medium | Low | Medium | | **Class D (Aux. SCR pulse)** | Pulses auxiliary SCR to commutate | High | Medium | Low | | **Class E (External pulse)** | External pulse source forces $i_A=0$ | High | Very High | Low | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Turn-Off Waveform — Voltage and Current vs. Time > Since Mermaid is a flow/render graph engine, we use a **State Transition Flow** to map the temporal evolution of the SCR terminal quantities during turn-off. ```mermaid graph TD A[State 1: Steady ON
iA = IL large
vAK approx 1.5 V] -->|Forward current flowing| B[State 2: Forced Commutation Begins
t = 0
Reverse bias applied externally] B -->|Storage Phase| C[State 3: Storage Time ts
iA reverses to negative
Stored charge removed
vAK still small] C -->|Charge exhausted| D[State 4: Fall Time tf
iA recovers to zero
vAK rises rapidly to VRRM] D -->|Junction capacitance charges| E[State 5: Gate Recovery tgr
vAK reaches forward blocking
dv dt must be limited] E -->|Safe to reapply forward V| F[State 6: Forward Blocking Restored
SCR OFF and ready
Total tq = ts + tf + tgr] ``` ## 4.2 Charge Carrier Decay Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Anode Current IF Flows
during ON state] --> nodeB[Excess Minority Carriers
stored in J1 J2 J3 regions
concentration peak] nodeB --> nodeC[External Circuit forces
reverse bias at t = 0] nodeC --> nodeD[Recombination Process
Q t = Q0 exp to t tau p] nodeD --> nodeE[Q drops below Qmin
at t = ts] nodeE --> nodeF[Junction Depletion
regions rebuild] nodeF --> nodeG[Device blocks forward V
at t = tq] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Class B Commutation Circuit ```mermaid graph TD subgraph MAIN[Main Power Loop] VDC[DC Source VDC] --> T1MAIN[Main SCR T1] T1MAIN --> LOAD[Load R L] LOAD --> VDC end subgraph AUX[Auxiliary Commutation Loop] CAUX[Commutating Capacitor C
pre charged to Vc] --> T2AUX[Auxiliary SCR T2] T2AUX --> LCOMM[Commutating Inductor L] LCOMM --> CAUX end T2AUX -.->|Fired after delay| T1MAIN T1MAIN -->|Reverse biased and
anode current zeroed| OFF[SCR T1 turns OFF] ``` ## 4.4 Triggering Conditions Flow Chart ```mermaid graph TD start[SCR is conducting] --> q1{Is anode current
below IH?} q1 -->|NO| stayon[Remains ON] q1 -->|YES| q2{Has reverse voltage
been applied for tq?} q2 -->|NO| stayon q2 -->|YES| q3{Is dv dt
within limits?} q3 -->|NO| retrig[Re triggers and
fails to turn OFF] q3 -->|YES| success[SCR successfully
turned OFF] style success fill:#90EE90 style retrig fill:#FFB6B6 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define turn-off time of an SCR. Why is it larger than the turn-on time?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **turn-off time ($t_q$)** of an SCR is defined as the time interval between the instant the anode current becomes zero and the instant the device becomes capable of blocking the forward voltage without re-turning on. It is the sum of **storage time ($t_s$)** and **fall time ($t_f$)**, plus the **gate recovery time ($t_{gr}$)**. **Why it is larger (1 mark):** During conduction, a large number of minority charge carriers are stored in the four P-N-P-N layers. The turn-off process must wait for these carriers to recombine (a slow, exponential process governed by carrier lifetime $\tau_p$), whereas turn-on is a regenerative avalanche-like process that completes in 1–2 $\mu$s. ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is meant by commutation of an SCR? Name the two broad classes.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Commutation** is the process of turning off a conducting SCR by reducing the anode current below the holding current $I_H$ and applying a reverse voltage for a duration greater than $t_q$. The two broad classes are: 1. **Natural Commutation** (also called line commutation) – used in AC circuits where the supply voltage naturally reverses. 2. **Forced Commutation** – used in DC circuits using external LC components; subclasses are **Class A, B, C, D, and E**. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED (Standard KTU Pattern)** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat waveform diagram, explain the turn-off characteristics of an SCR. Define storage time, fall time, and turn-off time. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the storage time of an SCR in terms of the carrier lifetime and the anode current. A thyristor has a carrier lifetime of $20 \mu s$ and a storage time of $35 \mu s$ when the forward current is 80 A. Calculate the storage time when the current is 200 A. Assume the minimum stored charge $Q_{min}$ remains unchanged. **(7 marks)** ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the Class B (complementary) commutation method of an SCR with the help of a circuit diagram and relevant waveforms. Why is it called "complementary commutation"? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the various forced commutation methods (Class A, B, C, D, E) for SCRs in a tabular format. State two applications where forced commutation is mandatory. **(7 marks)** --- ### **MODEL SOLUTIONS** #### **Solution to Question A:** ##### **Part (a) — Turn-Off Characteristics Waveform (7 marks)** **Diagram Description (for sketch in exam):** A combined plot of anode current $i_A$ and anode-cathode voltage $v_{AK}$ versus time, showing six distinct regions: - Region 1 (steady ON): $i_A = I_L$ (constant), $v_{AK} \approx 1.5$ V - Region 2 (commutation trigger): $i_A$ starts falling at $t = 0$ - Region 3 (storage time $t_s$): $i_A$ crosses zero and goes negative (reverse recovery current $I_{rr}$) - Region 4 (fall time $t_f$): $i_A$ returns to zero from $-I_{rr}$, $v_{AK}$ rises sharply to $V_{RRM}$ - Region 5 (forward blocking): $v_{AK}$ rises to forward supply, $i_A = 0$ - Region 6 (steady OFF): device holds forward voltage **Definitions (each 1 mark):** - **Storage time ($t_s$):** Time during which the stored charge carriers are removed from the inner two layers. The reverse current flows during this period. - **Fall time ($t_f$):** Time for the reverse current to decay from $I_{rr}$ to zero and for the depletion regions to rebuild. - **Turn-off time ($t_q$):** $t_q = t_s + t_f$. This is the **minimum time** the reverse voltage must be maintained to ensure the SCR does not re-trigger. ##### **Part (b) — Derivation & Numerical (7 marks)** **Derivation (5 marks):** Starting from the charge continuity equation, as derived in Section 3.1: $$ Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_p} $$ Setting $Q(t_s) = Q_{min}$ and noting $Q_0 = K \cdot I_F$: $$ t_s = \tau_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{K \cdot I_F}{Q_{min}}\right) $$ **Numerical Calculation (2 marks):** Using the ratio method from Section 3.2: $$ t_{s2} - t_{s1} = \tau_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I_{F2}}{I_{F1}}\right) $$ $$ t_{s2} - 35 = 20 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{200}{80}\right) $$ $$ t_{s2} = 35 + 20 \cdot \ln(2.5) $$ $$ t_{s2} = 35 + 20 \times 0.9163 = 35 + 18.33 $$ $$ \boxed{t_{s2} \approx 53.33 \ \mu s} $$ --- #### **Solution to Question B:** ##### **Part (a) — Class B Commutation (7 marks)** **Circuit Description:** A **Class B commutation circuit** has: - Main SCR ($T_1$) carrying the load current - Auxiliary SCR ($T_2$) connected in series with a **commutating capacitor $C$** - A diode $D$ and inductor $L$ may be used for resonant charging **Operating Sequence (4 steps for 4 marks):** 1. **Initial:** $T_1$ conducts load current; $C$ is pre-charged to a voltage $V_C$ with polarity that reverse-biases $T_1$ when $T_2$ is fired. 2. **Commutation Trigger:** $T_2$ is fired. $C$ discharges through $T_1$ in the reverse direction. 3. **Current Commutation:** The capacitor current $i_C$ opposes the load current through $T_1$. Net $i_{T1} = I_L - i_C$. When $i_C > I_L$, the SCR current reverses and goes below $I_H$. 4. **Turn-off Complete:** $T_1$ turns off. $C$ charges to opposite polarity through the load, ready for the next cycle. **Why "Complementary" (1 mark):** It is called complementary commutation because the auxiliary SCR's firing is **complementary** to the main SCR's firing — they are timed to alternate. The commutation capacitor's stored energy is used to commutate the main SCR. **Waveforms to Draw:** $i_{T1}$, $i_{T2}$, $v_{T1}$, $v_C$ versus time — showing the current crossover and the reverse voltage pulse across $T_1$. ##### **Part (b) — Comparison & Applications (7 marks)** **Comparison Table (5 marks):** Refer to the table in Section 3.3 for the comparison matrix. **Two Applications Where Forced Commutation is Mandatory (2 marks):** 1. **DC Choppers** (DC-DC converters) — the input is pure DC with no natural zero crossing. 2. **Voltage Source Inverters** (VSI) — converting DC to AC requires the SCR to be force-commutated since the input DC voltage never reverses naturally. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write $t_q = t_{on}$ in formulas → **ZERO mark**. Turn-off time is always **larger** than turn-on time. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to include $t_{gr}$ (gate recovery time) when the question specifically asks for "total time before forward voltage can be reapplied." > - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **latching current $I_L$** with **holding current $I_H$** in derivation. $I_L > I_H$, and they apply to turn-on and turn-off respectively. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Drawing the turn-off waveform with the voltage rising *before* the current reaches zero — this is physically wrong. The voltage rises **only after** the storage phase is complete. > - **Common Mistake #5:** Using natural log without converting units of $\tau_p$ to match the time unit in the answer. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Turn-off time $t_q = t_s + t_f$** (or equivalently $t_{rr} + t_{gr}$). Memorize this equation. - [ ] Turn-off requires **anode current to fall below $I_H$** — gate signal alone is **NOT enough**. - [ ] The **storage time $t_s$** is the dominant component of $t_q$ (often 50–80%). - [ ] Storage time depends on **carrier lifetime $\tau_p$** and **anode current $I_F$** logarithmically. - [ ] **Boxed formula:** $t_s = \tau_p \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{K \cdot I_F}{Q_{min}}\right)$ — derive this if asked. - [ ] Forced commutation classes: **A** (resonant load), **B** (resonant LC + aux SCR), **C** (complementary), **D** & **E** (aux pulse). - [ ] **Natural commutation** works only in **AC circuits** (line commutation). - [ ] **DC circuits** (choppers, inverters) **always** need forced commutation. - [ ] Failure to keep reverse voltage for $t_q$ duration → **commutation failure** → catastrophic short circuit. - [ ] Turn-off is **asymmetric** to turn-on: turn-on is regenerative (fast), turn-off is recombination-limited (slow). - [ ] dv/dt across the SCR during the gate recovery period must be **limited** by a **snubber circuit** to prevent retriggering. - [ ] Modern replacement: **IGBT, MOSFET, GTO** devices were invented specifically to overcome the SCR's difficult turn-off. - [ ] The ratio $\dfrac{I_F}{I_H}$ is called the **latching ratio** — a high ratio means harder commutation. - [ ] For KTU numericals, the **logarithmic ratio method** (taking differences of two equations) is the cleanest approach — do NOT solve for $K$ and $Q_{min}$ separately.

topics.title: AC-DC Converters (Rectifiers): Operation of Half-Wave and Full-Wave Rectifiers, Bridge Rectifiers and Performance Parameters (Ripple, Efficiency)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AC-DC Converters (Rectifiers): Operation of Half-Wave and Full-Wave Rectifiers, Bridge Rectifiers and Performance Parameters (Ripple, Efficiency)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **AC-DC Converter (Rectifier):** An electrical power converter that converts a bi-directional alternating current (AC) supply into a unidirectional direct current (DC) output by employing one or more semiconductor diodes arranged in a controlled unidirectional conduction topology.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (aligned with **NEP 2020** and **Outcome-Based Education**), a rectifier is formally classified as a **single-phase, uncontrolled power electronic converter** because its switching action is governed entirely by the natural line commutation of the AC source. The output is inherently **pulsating DC** (not pure DC), and the deviation from ideal DC is quantified using performance parameters such as **ripple factor** and **rectification efficiency**.

The three principal configurations studied in **Module 2** are:

1. **Half-Wave Rectifier (HWR)** – single diode, conducts only one half-cycle.
2. **Full-Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier (FW-CT)** – two diodes with a center-tapped transformer secondary.
3. **Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier (FW-BR)** – four diodes in a Graetz bridge configuration; no center-tap required.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The One-Way Water Wheel**
> Imagine a river flowing back and forth (AC) turning a paddle wheel. A **half-wave rectifier** is like a one-way ratchet that lets the wheel turn only when the water pushes **left-to-right**; the **left-to-right flow is allowed**, but the right-to-left motion is completely blocked. The wheel still spins, but only half the time. A **full-wave rectifier** is smarter: it uses a **gear-and-flipper** mechanism that *redirects* the reverse flow so that the wheel always rotates in the same direction, doubling the useful output. The **bridge rectifier** is the most elegant of the three — it uses four ratchets (diodes) arranged in a diamond so that the wheel always sees forward torque, without needing a fancy center-tapped gear. The "leftover wobble" (ripple) in the wheel's rotation is what we later smooth out with a capacitor or LC filter.

### 1.3 Universal Symbols and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $V_s$ | Instantaneous source voltage | Volt (V) |
| $V_m$ | Peak (maximum) source voltage | Volt (V) |
| $V_{rms}$ | Root-mean-square source voltage | Volt (V) |
| $V_{DC}$ | Average (DC) output voltage | Volt (V) |
| $I_{DC}$ | Average (DC) load current | Ampere (A) |
| $I_{rms}$ | RMS load current | Ampere (A) |
| $P_{DC}$ | DC output power | Watt (W) |
| $P_{AC}$ | AC input power | Watt (W) |
| $\gamma$ | Ripple factor | Dimensionless |
| $\eta$ | Rectification efficiency | Percentage (%) |
| $R_L$ | Load resistance | Ohm ($\Omega$) |
| $f$ | Supply frequency | Hertz (**Hz**) |
| $\omega$ | Angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The course outcome mapped to this topic (**CO2 — Analyse uncontrolled rectifier circuits**) is tested primarily in the 3-mark short-answer slot and the 14-mark analytical slot. Mastery of the *derivation* of ripple factor and efficiency is mandatory, as it appears in nearly every End-Semester Examination (ESE) under the 2024 Scheme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Three Topologies

#### 2.1.1 Half-Wave Rectifier (HWR)

* The circuit consists of a single diode **D** in series with the load $R_L$ across an AC source $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
* During the **positive half-cycle** ($0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi$), diode D is forward-biased and conducts. The output voltage equals the input.
* During the **negative half-cycle** ($\pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$), diode D is reverse-biased and blocks; the output is zero.
* **Result:** The load sees only the positive lobes of the sine wave.

#### 2.1.2 Full-Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier (FW-CT)

* The transformer secondary has a **center-tap**, giving two equal voltages of $V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and $-V_m \sin(\omega t)$ relative to the tap.
* Diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ are connected to the two ends of the secondary; their cathodes join at the load.
* During the **positive half-cycle**, $D_1$ conducts and the load sees $+V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
* During the **negative half-cycle**, $D_2$ conducts and the load sees $+V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (because the polarity is naturally inverted by the tap).
* **Result:** Both half-cycles deliver unipolar output — the fundamental frequency is **doubled to $2f$**.

#### 2.1.3 Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier (FW-BR)

* Four diodes $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$ are arranged in a **Graetz bridge**; the AC source is fed across one diagonal and the load across the other.
* During **positive half-cycle**, the diagonal pair $(D_1, D_3)$ conducts; during **negative half-cycle**, $(D_2, D_4)$ conducts.
* The load current is always in the same direction.
* **Result:** Same output waveform as FW-CT, but only **one secondary winding is needed** and the **PIV per diode is halved** ($V_m$ instead of $2V_m$).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Performance Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important revision resource** for the ESE on this topic. Memorize every row. **For all KTU problems involving a resistive load $R_L$, the relationship $V_{rms} / V_m$ and $V_{DC} / V_m$ is constant for a given topology**, regardless of $V_m$ or $R_L$.

**Important notation rule (per KTU 2024 typography):** the absolute value of $x$ is written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$, never using the bare pipe character.

| # | Parameter | Half-Wave (HWR) | Full-Wave CT (FW-CT) | Bridge (FW-BR) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | DC output voltage $V_{DC}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ |
| 2 | RMS output voltage $V_{rms}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ |
| 3 | DC load current $I_{DC}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi R_L}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi R_L}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi R_L}$ |
| 4 | RMS load current $I_{rms}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2 R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2} R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2} R_L}$ |
| 5 | Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) | $V_m$ | $2 V_m$ | $V_m$ |
| 6 | Ripple factor $\gamma$ | $1.21$ | $0.482$ | $0.482$ |
| 7 | Rectification efficiency $\eta$ | $40.6 \, \%$ | $81.2 \, \%$ | $81.2 \, \%$ |
| 8 | Transformer Utilisation Factor (TUF) | $0.2865$ | $0.6928$ | $0.8106$ |
| 9 | Output fundamental frequency | $f$ | $2f$ | $2f$ |
| 10 | Number of conducting diodes per half-cycle | $1$ | $1$ | $2$ |

### 2.3 Physical Meaning of the Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are these numbers important in real engineering?** In control engineering applications (KTU's PEICT411 deals with drives and control), the rectifier is the **front-end** of every variable-frequency drive (VFD), UPS, and DC motor armature supply. The ripple factor determines how much **harmonic distortion** propagates into the downstream inverter, which in turn affects motor torque pulsations (cogging). The rectification efficiency dictates **thermal losses** in the diode stack and therefore the size of the heatsink. The TUF governs the **physical size and cost** of the input transformer — a higher TUF means a smaller, cheaper transformer for the same delivered DC power.

### 2.4 The Two Cardinal Definitions (used in *every* KTU derivation)

The **average (DC) value** of any periodic waveform $v(t)$ with period $T$:

$$V_{DC} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t) \, dt$$

The **root-mean-square (RMS) value** of the same waveform:

$$V_{rms} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t) \, dt}$$

The **ripple factor** is the standard deviation of the output normalised to its mean:

$$\gamma \;=\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{DC}}\right)^{2} - 1}$$

The **rectification efficiency** is the ratio of DC power delivered to the load to the total AC power drawn:

$$\eta \;=\; \frac{P_{DC}}{P_{AC}} \;\times\; 100 \, \% \;=\; \frac{V_{DC}^{2} / R_L}{V_{rms}^{2} / R_L} \;\times\; 100 \, \% \;=\; \left(\frac{V_{DC}}{V_{rms}}\right)^{2} \times 100 \, \%$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** All derivations below assume an **ideal diode** (zero forward drop, infinite reverse resistance) and a **purely resistive load** $R_L$. These are the same assumptions used in the standard ESE problems for PEICT411.

### 3.1 Half-Wave Rectifier — Full Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the output waveform.**
The diode conducts only during $0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi$. Therefore, the load voltage is:

$$v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t), & 0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi \\ 0, & \pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Derive $V_{DC}$.**
The period of the output waveform is $2\pi$ (one full cycle of the AC supply). Applying the mean-value formula and using the change of variable $x = \omega t$:

$$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

Evaluating the integral $\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{0}^{\pi} = (-\cos\pi) - (-\cos 0) = 1 - (-1) = 2$:

$$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot V_m \cdot (2) = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.3183 \, V_m$$

**Step 3 — Derive $V_{rms}$.**
Square the output waveform and integrate over the full period:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

Use the half-angle identity $\sin^{2}(\omega t) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^{2}}{4\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right] d(\omega t)$$

The integral of $\cos(2\omega t)$ over $[0, \pi]$ is $\left[\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{0}^{\pi} = 0$, hence:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{4\pi} \cdot \pi = \frac{V_m^{2}}{4} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Derive Ripple Factor $\gamma$.**

$$\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{V_m / 2}{V_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{4} - 1} = \sqrt{2.4674 - 1} = \sqrt{1.4674} \approx 1.211$$

**Step 5 — Derive Rectification Efficiency $\eta$.**

$$\eta = \left(\frac{V_{DC}}{V_{rms}}\right)^{2} \times 100 \, \% = \left(\frac{2}{\pi}\right)^{2} \times 100 \, \% = \frac{4}{\pi^{2}} \times 100 \, \%$$

$$\eta = 0.4053 \times 100 \, \% \approx 40.53 \, \%$$

**Step 6 — Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV).**
During the negative half-cycle, the diode is reverse-biased. The full peak voltage $V_m$ appears across it:

$$PIV_{HWR} = V_m$$

---

### 3.2 Full-Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier — Full Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the output waveform.**
Both half-cycles deliver a positive lobe to the load, so the output period is $\pi$ (fundamental frequency $2f$). The amplitude per half-winding is $V_m$, so:

$$v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t), & 0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi \\ V_m \sin(\omega t - \pi), & \pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi \end{cases}$$

In both cases, $v_o(\omega t) = \vert V_m \sin(\omega t) \vert$. Averaging over the rectified period $\pi$ (note: denominator is $\pi$, not $2\pi$):

$$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.6366 \, V_m$$

**Step 2 — Derive $V_{rms}$.**

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2}$$

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \, V_m$$

**Step 3 — Derive Ripple Factor $\gamma$.**

$$\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{V_m / \sqrt{2}}{2 V_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{8} - 1} = \sqrt{1.2337 - 1} = \sqrt{0.2337} \approx 0.482$$

**Step 4 — Derive Rectification Efficiency $\eta$.**

$$\eta = \left(\frac{V_{DC}}{V_{rms}}\right)^{2} \times 100 \, \% = \left(\frac{2 V_m / \pi}{V_m / \sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} \times 100 \, \% = \left(\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\right)^{2} \times 100 \, \% = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \times 100 \, \%$$

$$\eta = 0.8106 \times 100 \, \% \approx 81.06 \, \%$$

**Step 5 — Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV).**
When one diode (say $D_1$) conducts, the voltage drop across it is ≈ 0 V. The non-conducting diode $D_2$ sees the sum of the entire secondary voltage (which is $2V_m$ peak-to-peak across the full winding) minus the conducting diode drop. Therefore:

$$PIV_{FW-CT} = 2 V_m$$

---

### 3.3 Bridge Rectifier — Analytical Summary

The bridge rectifier produces an *identical* output waveform to the FW-CT, so:

$$V_{DC} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}, \qquad V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}, \qquad \gamma = 0.482, \qquad \eta = 81.2 \, \%$$

**The key difference is the PIV.** In a bridge, when $D_1$ and $D_3$ conduct, the non-conducting pair $D_2$ and $D_4$ are reverse-biased across the secondary which is only $V_m$ peak. Hence:

$$PIV_{Bridge} = V_m$$

This makes the bridge the **preferred topology for high-voltage applications** (e.g., 230 V mains rectification in laboratory power supplies), because lower-rated, cheaper diodes can be used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** The bridge uses **two diodes in series** in the conduction path during each half-cycle, so the total forward drop is $2 V_D$ (≈ 1.4 V for silicon) instead of $V_D$. This causes slightly higher conduction loss in low-voltage applications, but in mains-frequency rectifier circuits the loss is negligible.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Performance Criterion | HWR | FW-CT | Bridge | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $V_{DC} / V_m$ | $0.318$ | $0.637$ | $0.637$ | Higher DC output per volt of input |
| DC ripple amplitude | High | Medium | Medium | Lower ripple ⇒ smaller filter |
| Transformer secondary utilisation | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Bridge ⇒ smallest transformer |
| Diode voltage stress (PIV) | $V_m$ | $2V_m$ | $V_m$ | Bridge uses lower-rated diodes |
| Number of diodes | $1$ | $2$ | $4$ | Trade-off between cost and PIV |
| Conduction drop per cycle | $V_D$ | $V_D$ | $2V_D$ | Bridge less efficient at low voltage |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Circuit-drawing is a 1-mark reward in 14-mark analytical questions. Use proper **loop-labelling** (Mesh-1, Mesh-2) and **node labelling** (A, B, C, D) in your exam sheets to claim this mark.

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Three Topologies

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the power flow and switching logic of the three rectifier configurations, which is the typical "Block Diagram" question asked in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACsrc["AC SOURCE v_s Vm sin omega t"] --> Trf["STEP DOWN TRANSFORMER"]
    Trf --> HWRblock["HALF WAVE STAGE One Diode in Series"]
    Trf --> FWCTblock["FULL WAVE CT STAGE Two Diodes with Center Tap"]
    Trf --> BRGblock["BRIDGE STAGE Four Diode Graetz Bridge"]
    HWRblock --> HWRout["HWR OUTPUT Pulsating DC Period T Frequency f"]
    FWCTblock --> FWRout["FW OUTPUT Pulsating DC Period T by 2 Frequency 2f"]
    BRGblock --> BRGout["BRG OUTPUT Pulsating DC Period T by 2 Frequency 2f"]
    HWRout --> FilterHWR["LC FILTER Capacitor Smoothing"]
    FWRout --> FilterFW["LC FILTER Capacitor Smoothing"]
    BRGout --> FilterBRG["LC FILTER Capacitor Smoothing"]
    FilterHWR --> LoadHWR["LOAD R_L Pure DC for Control Application"]
    FilterFW --> LoadFW["LOAD R_L Pure DC for Control Application"]
    FilterBRG --> LoadBRG["LOAD R_L Pure DC for Control Application"]
    classDef source fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#d48806,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#1677ff,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f6ffed,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
    class ACsrc source
    class HWRblock,FWCTblock,BRGblock stage
    class HWRout,FWRout,BRGout output
```

### 4.2 Half-Wave Rectifier — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph loopA["Mesh 1 Conduction Path"]
        A1["AC Source v_s"] -->|positive half cycle| A2["Diode D Forward Biased"]
        A2 --> A3["Load R_L"]
        A3 --> A1
    end
    subgraph loopB["Reverse Biased State"]
        B1["AC Source v_s"] -.->|negative half cycle| B2["Diode D Reverse Biased"]
        B2 -.-> B3["Load R_L Receives Zero Volts"]
    end
    A1 --- B1
    A3 --- B3
    classDef active fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#1677ff,color:#000
    classDef inactive fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#bfbfbf,color:#595959
    class A1,A2,A3 active
    class B1,B2,B3 inactive
```

### 4.3 Full-Wave Bridge — Conduction Sequence (4-Quadrant Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph s1["Positive Half Cycle D1 and D3 ON"]
        s1a["Source Top End Positive"] -->|current| s1b["Diode D1"]
        s1b --> s1c["Load R_L Top to Bottom"]
        s1c --> s1d["Diode D3"]
        s1d -->|return| s1e["Source Bottom End Negative"]
    end
    subgraph s2["Negative Half Cycle D2 and D4 ON"]
        s2a["Source Bottom End Positive"] -->|current| s2b["Diode D2"]
        s2b --> s2c["Load R_L Top to Bottom"]
        s2c --> s2d["Diode D4"]
        s2d -->|return| s2e["Source Top End Negative"]
    end
    classDef onpath fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#1677ff,color:#000
    class s1a,s1b,s1c,s1d,s1e onpath
    classDef onpath2 fill:#f6ffed,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
    class s2a,s2b,s2c,s2d,s2e onpath2
```

### 4.4 Input-Output Waveform Logic (Time-Domain Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1["Input vs is Sinusoidal Bi directional"] --> W2["Rectifier Switching Matrix Diodes"]
    W2 --> W3["Output vo Unidirectional but Pulsating"]
    W3 --> W4["Filter LC Network"]
    W4 --> W5["Smooth DC Output for Load"]
    classDef wfin fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#d48806,color:#000
    classDef wfmid fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#1677ff,color:#000
    classDef wfout fill:#f6ffed,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
    class W1 wfin
    class W2,W3,W4 wfmid
    class W5 wfout
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to draw waveforms in the ESE, always draw *three* traces on the same time axis: (1) input $v_s$, (2) output voltage $v_o$, and (3) diode current $i_D$. The HWR shows a single lobe per cycle; the FW-CT and Bridge show a "full-wave rectified" shape with two identical lobes per cycle, and the diode current is half-wave sinusoids phase-shifted by $\pi$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Mandate (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module 2 contributes **two 14-mark questions** in the End-Semester Examination (ESE), with internal choice. Part-A carries 3 marks. The expected answers below strictly follow the **2024 Scheme answer-key pattern**, where every formula is preceded by a *one-line reason* and every numerical value is boxed or underlined.

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** Define the term **ripple factor** for a rectifier. What is its value for an ideal DC supply?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Ripple Factor ($\gamma$):** It is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of the AC component (ripple) present in the rectified output to the average (DC) value of that output.
> $$\gamma = \frac{V_{ac,rms}}{V_{DC}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{DC}}\right)^{2} - 1}$$
> For a **pure, ideal DC supply** (no AC component), $V_{ac,rms} = 0$, therefore $\boxed{\gamma = 0}$. **(3 marks)** — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for ideal value.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** Compare the **Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV)** requirement of diodes used in a half-wave rectifier and a bridge rectifier of the same secondary voltage $V_m$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Topology | PIV per Diode | Reason |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Half-Wave Rectifier | $V_m$ | The non-conducting diode sees the full secondary peak. |
| Bridge Rectifier | $V_m$ | When $D_1, D_3$ conduct, $D_2, D_4$ see the secondary peak across them. |

> **Conclusion:** Both topologies have **identical PIV per diode = $V_m$**, but in the bridge, the secondary is stressed for the full cycle in one winding (no center tap required). **(3 marks)** — 1 mark each for value and 1 mark for the comparative comment.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Analytical Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**
> *Standard KTU Pattern: Part (a) = 7 marks, Part (b) = 7 marks.*

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **operation of a single-phase full-wave bridge rectifier** with a purely resistive load. Draw the input and output voltage waveforms. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A single-phase full-wave bridge rectifier consists of four diodes $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$ connected in a closed-loop bridge configuration. The AC supply $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is connected across one diagonal of the bridge (between nodes A and C), and the load resistance $R_L$ is connected across the other diagonal (between nodes B and D).

**Operation during Positive Half-Cycle ($0 \to \pi$) (2 marks):**
During this interval, terminal A is positive and terminal C is negative. Diodes $D_1$ and $D_3$ are forward-biased and conduct, while $D_2$ and $D_4$ are reverse-biased. The current path is: $A \to D_1 \to B \to R_L \to D \to D_3 \to C$. The load voltage $v_o = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and the load current $i_o = V_m \sin(\omega t) / R_L$.

**Operation during Negative Half-Cycle ($\pi \to 2\pi$) (2 marks):**
Now terminal C is positive and A is negative. Diodes $D_2$ and $D_4$ conduct, while $D_1$ and $D_3$ are reverse-biased. The current path is: $C \to D_2 \to B \to R_L \to D \to D_4 \to A$. The load voltage is again $v_o = v_s$ in magnitude, but in the same polarity as before, because the current has been *re-routed* through the bridge.

**Waveform (1 mark):** The output $v_o$ is a series of unidirectional sinusoidal half-loops with fundamental frequency $2f$ and peak $V_m$.

**(b)** Derive expressions for the **average output voltage $V_{DC}$**, **RMS output voltage $V_{rms}$**, and **rectification efficiency $\eta$** for the same bridge rectifier. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Average Output Voltage (2 marks):**
The output waveform has period $T/2$ (or angular period $\pi$). Integrating over one rectified pulse:

$$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{0}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2$$

$$\boxed{V_{DC} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.6366 \, V_m}$$

**RMS Output Voltage (2 marks):**

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right] d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \, V_m}$$

**Rectification Efficiency (3 marks):**
DC power: $P_{DC} = V_{DC}^{2} / R_L = (2 V_m / \pi)^{2} / R_L = 4 V_m^{2} / (\pi^{2} R_L)$.  
AC power: $P_{AC} = V_{rms}^{2} / R_L = V_m^{2} / (2 R_L)$.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{DC}}{P_{AC}} \times 100 \, \% = \frac{4 V_m^{2} / (\pi^{2} R_L)}{V_m^{2} / (2 R_L)} \times 100 \, \% = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \times 100 \, \%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 81.06 \, \%}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks)  *— Alternative choice*

**(a)** Explain the **working of a single-phase half-wave rectifier** with a resistive load. Derive the **DC output voltage $V_{DC}$** and the **RMS output voltage $V_{rms}$**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
A single diode $D$ is connected in series between the AC source $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and the load $R_L$. During the positive half-cycle, $D$ is forward-biased, conducts, and the load voltage $v_o = v_s$. During the negative half-cycle, $D$ is reverse-biased, blocks, and $v_o = 0$. The output is therefore a series of positive half-sinusoids separated by zero intervals of equal duration.

**Derivation of $V_{DC}$ (2.5 marks):**
The period of the output is $2\pi$. Averaging over the full cycle:

$$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{0}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2$$

$$\boxed{V_{DC} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.3183 \, V_m}$$

**Derivation of $V_{rms}$ (2.5 marks):**

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^{2}}{4\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right] d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^{2}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2} = 0.5 \, V_m}$$

**(b)** For the half-wave rectifier, compute the **ripple factor $\gamma$** and the **rectification efficiency $\eta$**. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Ripple Factor (3 marks):**
Using the standard definition with $V_{DC} = V_m / \pi$ and $V_{rms} = V_m / 2$:

$$\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{V_m / 2}{V_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.5708$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{4} - 1} = \sqrt{2.4674 - 1} = \sqrt{1.4674}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma \approx 1.211}$$

**Rectification Efficiency (3 marks):**

$$\eta = \left(\frac{V_{DC}}{V_{rms}}\right)^{2} \times 100 \, \% = \left(\frac{2}{\pi}\right)^{2} \times 100 \, \% = \frac{4}{\pi^{2}} \times 100 \, \%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 40.53 \, \%}$$

**Commentary (1 mark):**
The half-wave rectifier has a **high ripple factor (1.21)** indicating a large AC component, and a **poor efficiency (~40.5 %)** meaning more than half the input power is wasted as harmonic content. It is therefore restricted to **low-power, non-critical applications** such as battery chargers, signal demodulators, and small DC bias supplies. For control-engineering applications (KTU's PEICT411 focus), the bridge rectifier is preferred.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Before Writing!**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse period in the integral.** In the HWR the integral is over $2\pi$ (one AC cycle), but in FW-CT/Bridge the integral is over $\pi$ (one rectified pulse). This is the single most common 3-mark deduction.
> 2. **PIV for FW-CT is $2V_m$, not $V_m$.** Students who write $V_m$ lose 1 mark. The non-conducting diode sees the *sum* of both half-winding voltages.
> 3. **For FW-Bridge, PIV is $V_m$, not $2V_m$.** This is the *opposite* of FW-CT, and is a classic KTU "compare and contrast" trap.
> 4. **Always write the value of $\eta$ in %**, e.g., $81.06 \, \%$ and not just $0.81$.
> 5. **Mention the assumption of an ideal diode and resistive load** at the start of every derivation. This is worth 0.5 mark and signals maturity to the examiner.
> 6. **TUF is rarely asked in ESE**, but if asked, remember: Bridge = $0.8106$, CT = $0.6928$, HWR = $0.2865$. The bridge is the most transformer-efficient.

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 2-minute Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Section):**
>
> ✅ **Three Topologies:** Half-Wave, Full-Wave Center-Tapped, Full-Wave Bridge — know them cold.
> ✅ **$V_{DC}$ Values:** $V_m/\pi$ (HWR), $2V_m/\pi$ (FW-CT & Bridge).
> ✅ **$V_{rms}$ Values:** $V_m/2$ (HWR), $V_m/\sqrt{2}$ (FW-CT & Bridge).
> ✅ **Ripple Factor:** $\gamma = \sqrt{(V_{rms}/V_{DC})^2 - 1}$ → $1.21$ (HWR), $0.482$ (FW-CT & Bridge).
> ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = (V_{DC}/V_{rms})^2 \times 100 \, \%$ → $40.6 \, \%$ (HWR), $81.2 \, \%$ (FW-CT & Bridge).
> ✅ **PIV:** $V_m$ for HWR, $2V_m$ for FW-CT, $V_m$ for Bridge.
> ✅ **Fundamental Output Frequency:** $f$ for HWR, $2f$ for FW-CT and Bridge.
> ✅ **TUF:** $0.2865$ (HWR) < $0.6928$ (FW-CT) < $0.8106$ (Bridge) — Bridge is the best for transformer utilisation.
> ✅ **Always use ideal diode + resistive load assumptions.**
> ✅ **Integral denominators: $2\pi$ for HWR, $\pi$ for FW-CT and Bridge.**
> ✅ **Half-angle identity:** $\sin^{2}(\omega t) = (1 - \cos(2\omega t))/2$ — needed in every $V_{rms}$ derivation.
> ✅ **The peak of the rectified output is $V_m$ in all three topologies; only the average and RMS ratios differ.**
> ✅ **In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, always box the final answer and show units (V, A, Hz, %).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC-DC Converters: Types of DC-DC Converters (Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost, Cuk), Mode of Operation, Duty Cycle Calculations, and Efficiency Analysis. Design and Application Examples in Power Supplies and Renewable Energy Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DC-DC Converters: Types of DC-DC Converters (Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost, Cuk), Mode of Operation, Duty Cycle Calculations, and Efficiency Analysis. Design and Application Examples in Power Supplies and Renewable Energy Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a DC-DC Converter?

A **DC-DC Converter** (also called a *chopper circuit*) is a static power electronic switching circuit that converts a **fixed DC input voltage** into another **DC output voltage** whose magnitude can be varied by controlling the duty cycle of the semiconductor switch. The conversion is achieved through high-frequency switching (typically 20 kHz to 500 kHz) of power semiconductor devices such as **MOSFETs, IGBTs, or BJTs**, in conjunction with energy storage elements like **inductors (L) and capacitors (C)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
