> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** A chopper is a power electronic device that converts fixed DC input to a variable DC output voltage directly. When a chopper is interfaced between a fixed DC source and a DC load, it is referred to as a **DC-DC converter**.

The classification of DC-DC converters under the KTU 2024 scheme for **PEICT411** is based on two major criteria:
1. **Transformer isolation** — *Non-isolated converters* (Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost, Cuk, SEPIC, Zeta) vs. *Isolated converters* (Flyback, Forward, Push-Pull, Half-Bridge, Full-Bridge).
2. **Quadrant operation** — *Single-quadrant* (Step-down, Step-up), *Two-quadrant*, and *Four-quadrant* choppers.

This module focuses on the **four fundamental non-isolated topologies** explicitly required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a pressurized water tank (your **DC source $V_s$**) feeding a garden through a pipe. The water pressure at the output tap is what your load receives.

- **Buck Converter (Step-Down) → Pressure Reducer Valve:** The valve restricts flow, so output pressure is *less* than input pressure. You "chop" the pressure.
- **Boost Converter (Step-Up) → Manual Pump / Water Ram:** A one-way valve plus a pump chamber. Water is first stored in an accumulator (inductor), then forcibly ejected at a *higher* pressure than the source.
- **Buck-Boost Converter → Pressure Reverser:** Like a water-hammer pump that can produce *negative* pressure (suction) or *positive* pressure (boost), depending on how long you let water accumulate.
- **Cuk Converter → Capacitive Water Tower:** Uses a *transfer capacitor* (the "water tower on wheels") to shuttle energy from input to output in smooth, continuous packets, producing very low ripple.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Magic Components in Every DC-DC Converter:**
> 1. **Switch (S)** — MOSFET/IGBT acting as a high-speed on/off valve.
> 2. **Inductor (L)** — Stores energy in a magnetic field (the "flywheel" of the circuit).
> 3. **Capacitor (C)** — Stores energy in an electric field, smooths voltage ripple.
> 4. **Diode (D)** — Provides a freewheeling path for inductor current when the switch opens.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Physical Constants

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|------|--------|------------|------|
| Input (Source) Voltage | $V_s$ | Unregulated DC supply | **V (Volts)** |
| Output Voltage | $V_o$ | Regulated DC delivered to load | **V (Volts)** |
| Switching Period | $T_s$ | Time for one complete switching cycle | **s (seconds)** |
| Switching Frequency | $f_s$ | Number of switching cycles per second | **Hz** |
| Duty Cycle | $D$ | Fraction of time the switch is ON | **dimensionless (0 to 1)** |
| Inductor Current | $i_L$ | Current flowing through the energy-storage inductor | **A (Amperes)** |
| Inductance | $L$ | Property resisting change in current | **H (Henry)** |
| Capacitance | $C$ | Property storing electrical charge | **F (Farad)** |
| Load Resistance | $R$ | Resistance seen at the converter output | **Ω (Ohm)** |
| Ripple | $\Delta x$ | Peak-to-peak variation in a waveform | **unit of $x$** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duty Cycle (D) — The Heart of DC-DC Control:**
> 
> $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s} = T_{on} \cdot f_s$$
> 
> where $T_{on}$ is the time the switch is closed (conducting). Since $0 \le T_{on} \le T_s$, the duty cycle is bounded as $0 \le D \le 1$.

## 1.4 Modes of Conduction — A Critical KTU Concept

DC-DC converters operate in one of **two conduction modes**, and this directly affects the design equations, efficiency, and stability:

1. **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM):** The inductor current $i_L(t)$ *never falls to zero* during the switching cycle. This is the *normal* operating mode assumed in most KTU problems.
2. **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM):** The inductor current $i_L(t)$ *reaches zero* and stays at zero for a finite interval within each switching cycle. Occurs at light loads or when L is too small.

The boundary between CCM and DCM is defined by a **critical value of inductance $L_{crit}$** (or equivalently, a critical load resistance $R_{crit}$), which we will derive in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Switch-Cycle Paradigm

Every DC-DC converter operates on a **two-state cycle** that repeats at frequency $f_s$:

**State 1 (Switch ON, $0 \le t \le D \cdot T_s$):** The controlled switch (typically a MOSFET) is closed. The diode is reverse-biased (off). The inductor is *charged* by the source — current $i_L$ ramps up linearly. Energy is stored in the magnetic field: $E_L = \frac{1}{2} L i_L^2$.

**State 2 (Switch OFF, $D \cdot T_s \le t \le T_s$):** The switch is opened. The diode becomes forward-biased (on), providing a freewheeling path. The inductor *discharges* into the load and/or capacitor — current $i_L$ ramps down linearly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Volt-Second Balance (VSB) — The Single Most Important KTU Theorem:**
> In **steady-state CCM**, the average voltage across an inductor over one complete switching cycle must be zero (otherwise the inductor current would grow unboundedly).
> 
> $$\int_0^{T_s} v_L(t)\, dt = 0$$
> 
> This is the **Volt-Second Balance Principle**, and it is the primary tool used to derive every output-voltage equation in DC-DC converters.

## 2.2 Topology-by-Topology Operation Logic

### 2.2.1 Buck Converter (Step-Down Chopper)

**Why it steps down:** The load is connected *in parallel* with the source via the switch. When the switch is ON, full source voltage appears across the load. When OFF, the load is disconnected. An LC filter averages the chopped waveform to produce a *lower* average voltage.

**Logic Steps:**
1. **Switch ON:** Diode reverse-biased. Inductor connected to $V_s$ → $v_L = V_s - V_o > 0$ → $i_L$ ramps up.
2. **Switch OFF:** Diode conducts (freewheeling). Inductor connected to load only → $v_L = -V_o < 0$ → $i_L$ ramps down.
3. Apply VSB → $D(V_s - V_o) + (1-D)(-V_o) = 0$ → output equation.
4. Apply **Charge Balance on Capacitor C** → ripple analysis.

### 2.2.2 Boost Converter (Step-Up Chopper)

**Why it steps up:** The inductor is in *series* with the source. When the switch is ON, the inductor is shorted across the source, building up a large current. When the switch opens, the inductor's collapsing field *adds* to the source voltage, pushing current into the load at a *higher* voltage.

**Logic Steps:**
1. **Switch ON:** Diode OFF. Inductor charges from $V_s$ → $v_L = V_s$ → $i_L$ ramps up. Load is supplied by capacitor C alone.
2. **Switch OFF:** Diode ON. Inductor in series with $V_s$ supplies load → $v_L = V_s - V_o < 0$ (since $V_o > V_s$) → $i_L$ ramps down.
3. Apply VSB → $D \cdot V_s + (1-D)(V_s - V_o) = 0$ → output equation.

### 2.2.3 Buck-Boost Converter

**Why it inverts and can step up/down:** The inductor is in parallel with the source during ON-state (storing energy), and in series with the load during OFF-state (delivering energy). The output is **inverted** (negative polarity) relative to the input.

**Logic Steps:**
1. **Switch ON:** Inductor charges from $V_s$ → $v_L = V_s$ → $i_L$ ramps up. Diode OFF, load supplied by capacitor.
2. **Switch OFF:** Inductor discharges into load (with reversed polarity) → $v_L = V_o$ → $i_L$ ramps down.
3. Apply VSB → $D \cdot V_s + (1-D) \cdot V_o = 0$ → output equation (note $V_o$ is negative).

### 2.2.4 Cuk Converter

**Why it uses capacitive energy transfer:** Unlike the other three, the Cuk converter uses a **coupling capacitor $C_1$** to transfer energy *from input to output in every switching cycle*. This results in **continuous input and output currents** with very low ripple — a major advantage in sensitive applications.

**Logic Steps:**
1. **Switch ON:** Inductor $L_1$ charges from $V_s$. Capacitor $C_1$ discharges through $L_2$ into load and $C_2$. Diode OFF.
2. **Switch OFF:** Inductor $L_1$ charges capacitor $C_1$ from $V_s$. Inductor $L_2$ freewheels through diode into load. $C_2$ smooths output.
3. Apply VSB on both inductors → output equation (same form as Buck-Boost, but with **non-pulsating currents**).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for instant marks in KTU ESE. All quantities are in steady-state CCM.**

| Topology | Output Voltage $V_o$ | Voltage Gain $M(D)$ | Inductor Voltage (ON) | Inductor Voltage (OFF) | Inductor Current Avg $I_L$ | Critical Inductance $L_{crit}$ | Output Voltage Polarity |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Buck** | $D \cdot V_s$ | $D$ | $V_s - V_o$ | $-V_o$ | $V_o / R$ | $\frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}$ | Positive |
| **Boost** | $\frac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $\frac{1}{1-D}$ | $V_s$ | $V_s - V_o$ | $\frac{V_o}{R(1-D)}$ | $\frac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | Positive |
| **Buck-Boost** | $-\frac{D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$ | $-\frac{D}{1-D}$ | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $\frac{V_o}{R(1-D)}$ | $\frac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | Inverted (Negative) |
| **Cuk** | $-\frac{D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$ | $-\frac{D}{1-D}$ | $V_s$ | $V_o - V_{C_1}$ | $\frac{V_o}{R(1-D)}$ | $\frac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | Inverted (Negative) |

### 2.3.1 Common Design Equations (All Topologies)

**Peak-to-Peak Inductor Current Ripple $\Delta i_L$:**

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{V_L,on \cdot D \cdot T_s}{L} = \frac{V_L,off \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s}{L}$$

**Peak-to-Peak Output Voltage Ripple $\Delta V_o$:**

For **Buck** and **Boost** (capacitor supplies load during $T_{on}$ and $(1-D)T_s$ respectively):

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta i_L}{8 f_s C}$$

For **Cuk** (continuous output current, smaller ripple):

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{D \cdot T_s \cdot V_o}{R \cdot C_2}$$

**Boundary Between CCM and DCM** — The converter operates at the boundary when:

$$L = L_{crit} = \frac{R \cdot (1-D)}{2 f_s} \quad \text{(for Buck)}$$

- If $L > L_{crit}$ → **CCM**
- If $L < L_{crit}$ → **DCM**

**Efficiency $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_o \cdot I_o}{V_s \cdot I_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** The Cuk converter has the **same voltage transfer ratio** as the Buck-Boost converter, but with **non-pulsating input and output currents**, making it superior for EMI-sensitive applications such as **solar PV MPPT systems** and **battery chargers**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Converter | Primary Application Domains |
|-----------|-----------------------------|
| **Buck** | Laptop chargers, LED drivers, USB-PD supplies, CPU/GPU VRM (Voltage Regulator Modules) in computers, point-of-load (POL) converters. |
| **Boost** | Power factor correction (PFC) front-ends, hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) battery boosters, low-voltage PV panel step-up for grid-tie inverters, camera flash circuits, OLED display drivers. |
| **Buck-Boost** | Battery-powered devices with wide input variation (e.g., 1S Li-ion to 3.3V), SEPIC predecessor, automotive electronics with load-dump transients. |
| **Cuk** | Solar PV MPPT charge controllers, electric vehicle (EV) chargers, UPS systems, audio amplifiers (low ripple), high-precision instrumentation supplies. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Renewable Energy Context (KTU 2024 Application Focus):** In a **standalone solar PV system**, a PV panel output ($V_{pv} \approx 17$–$40$ V) must be boosted to charge a 48 V battery bank → **Boost Converter** with MPPT. In a **grid-tie PV inverter front-end**, a **Boost** or **Cuk** converter first elevates voltage, then a DC-AC inverter converts to AC. In **wind energy systems**, a **Buck** converter regulates the rectified DC link voltage feeding battery storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Buck Converter — Complete Steady-State Derivation

**Circuit Assumptions (KTU Standard):**
- Switch and diode are ideal (zero voltage drop when ON, infinite resistance when OFF).
- Inductor and capacitor are ideal (no parasitic resistance).
- Load is purely resistive $R$.
- Converter operates in **steady-state CCM** with switching period $T_s$ and duty cycle $D$.

### 3.1.1 Output Voltage Gain $M(D) = V_o / V_s$

**Mode 1: Switch ON (Interval: $0 \le t \le D T_s$)**

When the switch is closed, the diode is reverse-biased (off). The source $V_s$ is directly connected to the $LC$ filter and load. The voltage across the inductor is:

$$v_L = V_s - V_o$$

Since $V_s > V_o$ (step-down operation), we have $v_L > 0$, so the inductor current $i_L$ *increases linearly*:

$$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{v_L}{L} = \frac{V_s - V_o}{L} > 0$$

The change in inductor current during this interval is:

$$\Delta i_{L,on} = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T_s}{L}$$

**Mode 2: Switch OFF (Interval: $D T_s \le t \le T_s$)**

When the switch opens, the inductor forces current to continue flowing (it cannot change instantaneously). The diode becomes forward-biased and provides a freewheeling path. The source is now disconnected from the load. The voltage across the inductor is:

$$v_L = -V_o$$

Since $V_o > 0$, we have $v_L < 0$, so the inductor current $i_L$ *decreases linearly*:

$$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{v_L}{L} = \frac{-V_o}{L} < 0$$

The change in inductor current during this interval is:

$$\Delta i_{L,off} = \frac{(-V_o) \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s}{L}$$

**Applying Volt-Second Balance (VSB):**

In steady state, the net change in inductor current over one complete cycle must be zero (otherwise the inductor would saturate or de-energize):

$$\Delta i_{L,on} + \Delta i_{L,off} = 0$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T_s}{L} + \frac{(-V_o) \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s}{L} = 0$$

Multiplying both sides by $L / T_s$ (both are positive constants, so equality is preserved):

$$(V_s - V_o) \cdot D + (-V_o) \cdot (1-D) = 0$$

Expanding the product:

$$D \cdot V_s - D \cdot V_o - V_o + D \cdot V_o = 0$$

The $-D \cdot V_o$ and $+D \cdot V_o$ terms cancel:

$$D \cdot V_s - V_o = 0$$

Solving for $V_o$:

$$\boxed{V_o = D \cdot V_s}$$

**Voltage Gain:**

$$M(D) = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = D$$

Since $0 \le D \le 1$, we have $V_o \le V_s$ → **step-down** behavior confirmed. ✓

### 3.1.2 Inductor Current Ripple $\Delta i_L$

The peak-to-peak ripple equals the absolute change during either interval (they are equal in magnitude at steady state):

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T_s}{L}$$

Substituting $V_o = D \cdot V_s$, so $V_s - V_o = V_s(1-D)$:

$$\boxed{\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s \cdot (1-D) \cdot D \cdot T_s}{L} = \frac{V_o \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s}{L}}$$

### 3.1.3 Output Voltage Ripple $\Delta V_o$

In CCM, the inductor current $i_L$ is approximately DC plus a small triangular ripple of amplitude $\Delta i_L / 2$. The capacitor supplies the *AC* portion of the inductor current (the DC goes to the load). The peak-to-peak voltage ripple across the capacitor is:

$$\boxed{\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta i_L}{8 f_s C} = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{8 L C f_s^2}}$$

### 3.1.4 Critical Inductance $L_{crit}$ (CCM/DCM Boundary)

The boundary condition is reached when the minimum inductor current $I_{L,min}$ just touches zero. Since $I_{L,avg} = V_o / R$ and the ripple is $\Delta i_L$ peak-to-peak:

$$I_{L,min} = I_{L,avg} - \frac{\Delta i_L}{2} = 0$$

Setting this to zero:

$$\frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{\Delta i_L}{2} = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{2 L_{crit} f_s}$$

Solving for $L_{crit}$:

$$\boxed{L_{crit} = \frac{(1-D) \cdot R}{2 f_s}}$$

- $L > L_{crit}$ → CCM operation
- $L < L_{crit}$ → DCM operation

## 3.2 Boost Converter — Complete Steady-State Derivation

### 3.2.1 Output Voltage Gain $M(D) = V_o / V_s$

**Mode 1: Switch ON (Interval: $0 \le t \le D T_s$)**

When the switch is closed, the diode is reverse-biased (because $V_o > V_s$, the diode sees reverse voltage). The inductor is directly connected across the source:

$$v_L = V_s$$

Inductor current ramps up. The load is supplied *entirely* by capacitor $C$ during this interval.

**Mode 2: Switch OFF (Interval: $D T_s \le t \le T_s$)**

When the switch opens, the inductor current must continue, so it forward-biases the diode. Now the inductor is in series with the source, both feeding the load:

$$v_L = V_s - V_o$$

Since $V_o > V_s$ in a boost converter, this voltage is **negative**, so the inductor current ramps down.

**Applying Volt-Second Balance:**

$$D \cdot V_s + (1-D) \cdot (V_s - V_o) = 0$$

Expanding:

$$D \cdot V_s + V_s - V_o - D \cdot V_s + D \cdot V_o = 0$$

The $D \cdot V_s$ terms cancel:

$$V_s - V_o + D \cdot V_o = 0$$

$$V_s = V_o (1 - D)$$

Solving for $V_o$:

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - D}}$$

**Voltage Gain:**

$$M(D) = \frac{1}{1 - D}$$

Since $0 \le D < 1$, we have $V_o > V_s$ → **step-up** behavior confirmed. As $D \to 1$, $V_o \to \infty$ (ideal case; in practice, parasitic resistances limit the gain). ✓

### 3.2.2 Inductor Current Ripple and Critical Inductance

**Inductor ripple:**

$$\boxed{\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s \cdot D \cdot T_s}{L} = \frac{V_o (1-D) D \cdot T_s}{L}}$$

**Average inductor current** (input current, since the inductor is in series with the source):

$$I_L = I_s = \frac{V_o \cdot I_o}{V_s} = \frac{I_o}{1-D} = \frac{V_o}{R(1-D)}$$

**Critical inductance** (when $I_{L,min} = 0$):

$$\frac{V_o}{R(1-D)} = \frac{V_s \cdot D \cdot T_s}{2 L_{crit}}$$

Substituting $V_s = V_o (1-D)$:

$$\frac{V_o}{R(1-D)} = \frac{V_o (1-D) D \cdot T_s}{2 L_{crit}}$$

Solving for $L_{crit}$:

$$\boxed{L_{crit} = \frac{D (1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}}$$

## 3.3 Buck-Boost Converter — Complete Steady-State Derivation

### 3.3.1 Output Voltage Gain (Inverted Polarity)

**Mode 1: Switch ON (Interval: $0 \le t \le D T_s$)**

The switch shorts the inductor across the source. The diode is reverse-biased. Inductor charges from $V_s$:

$$v_L = V_s$$

**Mode 2: Switch OFF (Interval: $D T_s \le t \le T_s$)**

The switch opens. The inductor current forward-biases the diode, which now connects the inductor across the load (with the polarity flipped). The inductor discharges into the load. The voltage across the inductor (using passive sign convention aligned with discharge current direction) is $V_o$:

$$v_L = V_o$$

**Applying Volt-Second Balance:**

$$D \cdot V_s + (1-D) \cdot V_o = 0$$

Solving for $V_o$:

$$\boxed{V_o = -\frac{D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}}$$

The negative sign indicates **inverted output polarity** (anode of the load is at ground potential). The magnitude of voltage gain is:

$$\vert M(D) \vert = \frac{D}{1 - D}$$

- When $D < 0.5$ → step-down (buck) behavior
- When $D > 0.5$ → step-up (boost) behavior
- When $D = 0.5$ → $\vert V_o \vert = V_s$ (unity magnitude, inverted polarity)

### 3.3.2 Critical Inductance

Using $\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s \cdot D \cdot T_s}{L}$ and $I_L = \frac{\vert V_o \vert}{R(1-D)}$:

$$I_{L,min} = 0 \implies \frac{\vert V_o \vert}{R(1-D)} = \frac{V_s D}{2 L_{crit} f_s}$$

Substituting $V_s = \vert V_o \vert (1-D)/D$:

$$\frac{\vert V_o \vert}{R(1-D)} = \frac{\vert V_o \vert (1-D) D}{2 L_{crit} f_s \cdot D} = \frac{\vert V_o \vert (1-D)}{2 L_{crit} f_s}$$

Solving for $L_{crit}$:

$$\boxed{L_{crit} = \frac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}}$$

## 3.4 Cuk Converter — Complete Steady-State Derivation

### 3.4.1 Circuit Operation

The Cuk converter uses **two inductors ($L_1, L_2$)**, **two capacitors ($C_1$ coupling capacitor, $C_2$ output filter)**, a switch, and a diode. The coupling capacitor $C_1$ is the **primary energy transfer element**.

**Mode 1: Switch ON (Interval: $0 \le t \le D T_s$)**

- Inductor $L_1$ charges from $V_s$ through the switch: $v_{L_1} = V_s$
- Diode is reverse-biased (off)
- Capacitor $C_1$ (charged to voltage $V_{C_1} > V_s$ in steady state) discharges through the switch and $L_2$, transferring energy to the output stage
- Inductor $L_2$ sees voltage $V_{C_1} - V_o$ (where $V_o$ is the magnitude of the inverted output): $v_{L_2} = V_{C_1} - V_o$

**Mode 2: Switch OFF (Interval: $D T_s \le t \le T_s$)**

- Inductor $L_1$ current freewheels through the diode, charging $C_1$ from $V_s$: $v_{L_1} = V_s - V_{C_1}$
- Diode is forward-biased (on), providing freewheeling path for $L_2$
- Inductor $L_2$ freewheels through the diode into the load: $v_{L_2} = -V_o$

### 3.4.2 Applying Volt-Second Balance on $L_1$:

$$D \cdot V_s + (1-D) \cdot (V_s - V_{C_1}) = 0$$

$$V_s = (1-D) \cdot V_{C_1}$$

$$V_{C_1} = \frac{V_s}{1 - D}$$

### 3.4.3 Applying Volt-Second Balance on $L_2$:

$$D \cdot (V_{C_1} - V_o) + (1-D) \cdot (-V_o) = 0$$

$$D \cdot V_{C_1} - D \cdot V_o - V_o + D \cdot V_o = 0$$

$$D \cdot V_{C_1} = V_o$$

$$V_o = D \cdot V_{C_1} = D \cdot \frac{V_s}{1 - D}$$

$$\boxed{V_o = -\frac{D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}}$$

(The negative sign appears when we trace the actual polarity; magnitude is the same as Buck-Boost.)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The Cuk converter has the **same DC voltage transfer ratio** as the Buck-Boost, but its key advantage is that **both $i_{L_1}$ (input) and $i_{L_2}$ (output) currents are continuous and non-pulsating**, drastically reducing conducted and radiated EMI.

## 3.5 Efficiency Analysis Framework

For a practical (non-ideal) DC-DC converter, the efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}}$$

**Sources of Power Loss:**

| Loss Component | Symbol | Cause | Typical Magnitude (at 100 W) |
|----------------|--------|-------|------------------------------|
| **Conduction loss (switch)** | $P_{cond,S}$ | $I^2 R_{DS(on)}$ in MOSFET | 1–5 W |
| **Switching loss (switch)** | $P_{sw,S}$ | $0.5 V_s I_o (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_s$ | 1–10 W |
| **Diode conduction loss** | $P_{cond,D}$ | $V_F \cdot I_F \cdot (1-D)$ (Buck) | 1–3 W |
| **Diode reverse recovery** | $P_{rr}$ | $0.5 V_R Q_{rr} f_s$ | 0.5–2 W |
| **Inductor copper loss** | $P_{Cu}$ | $I_L^2 R_{L,ESR}$ | 0.5–2 W |
| **Inductor core loss** | $P_{core}$ | Hysteresis + eddy currents in ferrite | 0.5–2 W |
| **Capacitor ESR loss** | $P_{ESR}$ | $I_{C,rms}^2 R_{C,ESR}$ | 0.1–0.5 W |

**Typical Achievable Efficiencies (Modern Power Electronics):**

| Converter Topology | Efficiency (CCM, 48 V → 12 V, 100 W) |
|--------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Buck (with Schottky diode) | **92–95%** |
| Buck (synchronous, MOSFET replaces diode) | **96–98%** |
| Boost | **90–94%** |
| Buck-Boost | **88–92%** |
| Cuk | **90–94%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronous Rectification:** Replacing the freewheeling diode with a low-$R_{DS(on)}$ MOSFET can boost efficiency by 3–6% in low-voltage high-current applications (e.g., laptop VRMs). This is standard practice in KTU 2024 design problems.

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A buck converter has $V_s = 24$ V, $L = 100$ μH, $C = 47$ μF, $R = 10$ Ω, and $f_s = 50$ kHz. Determine: (a) duty cycle for $V_o = 12$ V, (b) inductor current ripple, (c) output voltage ripple, (d) critical inductance, and verify CCM.

**Solution:**

(a) $D = V_o / V_s = 12 / 24 = 0.5$ → **D = 0.5**

(b) $\Delta i_L = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{L f_s} = \frac{12 \times 0.5}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^3} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2$ A → **$\Delta i_L = 1.2$ A**

(c) $\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta i_L}{8 f_s C} = \frac{1.2}{8 \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 47 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.2}{18.8} \approx 0.0638$ V → **$\Delta V_o \approx 64$ mV**

(d) $L_{crit} = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s} = \frac{0.5 \times 10}{2 \times 50 \times 10^3} = \frac{5}{10^5} = 50$ μH

Since $L = 100$ μH $> L_{crit} = 50$ μH → **Converter operates in CCM.** ✓

```mermaid
    B --> D[Diode cathode to output]
    D --> E[Output Capacitor C and Load R with inverted polarity]
    F[Gate Driver PWM] --> C

    stepA[State 1 Switch ON diode OFF L charges from Vs load supplied by C]:::active
    stepB[State 2 Switch OFF diode ON L discharges into load with REVERSED polarity Vo negative]:::active

```

```mermaid
graph TD

    F --> G[Output Inductor L2]
    G --> H[Output Capacitor C2 and Load R with inverted polarity]
    I[Gate Driver PWM] --> D

    stepA[State 1 Switch ON C1 discharges through L2 into load L1 charges from Vs]:::active
    stepB[State 2 Switch OFF L1 charges C1 L2 freewheels through diode into load]:::active

```

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Inductor Current iL t] --> B{Is minimum above zero?}
    B -- Yes --> C[CCM Continuous Conduction Mode]

    stepA[L greater than Lcrit → CCM triangular ripple above zero]:::mode
    stepB[L less than Lcrit → DCM ripple hits zero stays at zero for interval]:::mode
    stepC[L equals Lcrit → Boundary iL min equals 0]:::mode
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define duty cycle in a DC-DC converter. How does varying the duty cycle affect the output voltage of a buck and a boost converter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **duty cycle $D$** is defined as the ratio of the switch ON-time to the total switching period:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s} = T_{on} \cdot f_s$$

For a **buck converter**: $V_o = D \cdot V_s$, so the output voltage varies **linearly** from 0 to $V_s$ as $D$ varies from 0 to 1.
For a **boost converter**: $V_o = V_s / (1-D)$, so the output voltage varies **non-linearly (hyperbolically)** from $V_s$ (at $D = 0$) to infinity (as $D \to 1$). Thus, the boost converter can produce an output much larger than the input. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) and Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)? State the boundary condition between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **CCM:** The inductor current $i_L$ flows continuously and never falls to zero during the switching cycle. (1 mark)
- **DCM:** The inductor current $i_L$ falls to zero and remains at zero for a finite portion of the switching cycle. (1 mark)
- **Boundary condition:** The boundary is reached when the minimum inductor current just touches zero, i.e., $I_{L,min} = 0$. This occurs at a **critical inductance $L_{crit}$** value. For a buck converter, $L_{crit} = (1-D) R / (2 f_s)$. The converter is in CCM if $L > L_{crit}$ and in DCM if $L < L_{crit}$. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice pair. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) Derive the expression for the output voltage of a buck converter in CCM. A buck converter operates with $V_s = 48$ V, $L = 200$ μH, $C = 100$ μF, $R = 20$ Ω, $f_s = 25$ kHz, and $D = 0.4$. Calculate the output voltage, inductor current ripple, and output voltage ripple. Verify whether the converter is operating in CCM.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** See Section 3.1.1 of these notes. Key result:

$$V_o = D \cdot V_s$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

$V_o = D \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 48 = 19.2$ V ✓

$\Delta i_L = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{L f_s} = \frac{19.2 \times 0.6}{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^3} = \frac{11.52}{5} = 2.304$ A

$\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta i_L}{8 f_s C} = \frac{2.304}{8 \times 25 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.304}{20} = 0.1152$ V

$L_{crit} = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s} = \frac{0.6 \times 20}{2 \times 25 \times 10^3} = \frac{12}{50{,}000} = 240$ μH

Since $L = 200$ μH $< L_{crit} = 240$ μH → **Converter operates in DCM!** (Note: this is a trick — the student must verify CCM, not just assume it.)

---

**(b) With the aid of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a boost converter in CCM. Derive the expression for the output voltage. State two advantages of the boost converter over the buck converter.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit operation (3 marks):** See Section 2.2.2. The boost converter consists of an input inductor $L$ in series with the source, a switch (MOSFET) from the switch node to ground, a diode from the switch node to the output, and an output capacitor $C$ in parallel with the load. During switch ON, the inductor charges from $V_s$ (diode reverse-biased, load supplied by $C$). During switch OFF, the inductor discharges in series with $V_s$ through the diode into the load (diode forward-biased).

**Derivation (3 marks):** See Section 3.2.1. Key result:

$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - D}$$

**Two advantages over buck converter (1 mark):**
1. The boost converter can produce an output voltage **higher** than the input — useful for battery and PV applications.
2. The input current is **continuous and non-pulsating**, reducing input-side EMI and input capacitor stress.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) Derive the voltage transfer ratio of a buck-boost converter. Why is the output polarity inverted? An SMPS uses a buck-boost converter with $V_s = 15$ V, $D = 0.6$, and $R = 30$ Ω. Compute $V_o$ and the average inductor current.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3 marks):** See Section 3.3.1. Key result:

$$V_o = -\frac{D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}$$

**Why output is inverted (2 marks):** During the switch ON-state, the inductor is connected across the source (charging). When the switch opens, the inductor discharges through the diode into the load. The diode is oriented such that the inductor's *positive* terminal (during discharge) connects to the *ground* side of the load, while its *negative* terminal connects to the *output* side. This polarity reversal results in a negative output voltage relative to the input ground.

**Calculations (2 marks):**

$V_o = -\frac{0.6 \times 15}{1 - 0.6} = -\frac{9}{0.4} = -22.5$ V (magnitude = 22.5 V, inverted polarity)

$I_L = \frac{\vert V_o \vert}{R(1-D)} = \frac{22.5}{30 \times 0.4} = \frac{22.5}{12} = 1.875$ A

---

**(b) Compare the Cuk converter with the buck-boost converter in terms of (i) voltage transfer ratio, (ii) input/output current ripple, (iii) EMI performance, and (iv) complexity. Mention two applications of the Cuk converter in renewable energy systems.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Buck-Boost | Cuk |
|-----------|-----------|-----|
| (i) Voltage transfer ratio | $V_o = -DV_s/(1-D)$ | $V_o = -DV_s/(1-D)$ (same) |
| (ii) Input current ripple | Pulsating (discontinuous) | **Non-pulsating (continuous)** ✓ |
| (iii) Output current ripple | Pulsating (discontinuous) | **Non-pulsating (continuous)** ✓ |
| (iv) EMI performance | Higher EMI due to pulsed currents | **Lower EMI** ✓ |
| (v) Component count | 1 L, 1 C, 1 D, 1 S | 2 L, 2 C, 1 D, 1 S (more complex) |

**Two renewable energy applications of Cuk converter (2 marks):**
1. **Solar PV MPPT charge controller** — the continuous input current is ideal for tracking the maximum power point of a PV panel without injecting switching noise back into the panel (which would perturb the MPPT algorithm).
2. **Battery-powered grid-tie inverter front-end** — the continuous output current produces low output voltage ripple, reducing stress on downstream battery cells in lithium-ion battery banks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 
> 1. **Assuming CCM without checking:** Always compute $L_{crit}$ and compare with actual $L$. If the problem states CCM, you may skip this; if not, you MUST verify. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in Buck-Boost and Cuk:** The output is **inverted** (anode of load at ground). Writing $V_o = +D V_s / (1-D)$ instead of the negative form is a 1-mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Sign error in Volt-Second Balance:** During switch ON, the inductor voltage is NOT always $V_s$. In the buck converter, it's $V_s - V_o$ (a common error). Re-draw the equivalent circuit carefully for each mode.
> 
> 4. **Wrong units in ripple calculations:** Convert μH to H, μF to F, and kHz to Hz before substituting. A factor-of-1000 error is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 
> 5. **Misapplying efficiency formula:** $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in}$ — not the inverse. And do not confuse $V_s$ (input) with $V_o$ (output) in the denominator.
> 
> 6. **Cuk vs. Buck-Boost confusion:** The voltage transfer ratio is the SAME, but the Cuk has continuous currents. If a question asks "compare," explicitly state *both* similarities and differences.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the circuit diagram:** In 7-mark questions, an unlabeled or missing circuit diagram costs 1–2 marks. Always draw the switch, inductor, capacitor, diode, and load clearly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These 15 Points Before Walking Into the KTU Exam Hall:**

1. **Duty cycle** $D = T_{on}/T_s$ is the *only* control variable in a DC-DC converter (in CCM).

2. **Buck:** $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ → step-down → output polarity matches input.

3. **Boost:** $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$ → step-up → output polarity matches input.

4. **Buck-Boost:** $V_o = -D \cdot V_s/(1-D)$ → step-up or step-down → **inverted** output.

5. **Cuk:** $V_o = -D \cdot V_s/(1-D)$ → same gain as Buck-Boost → **continuous input and output currents**.

6. **Volt-Second Balance** is the master theorem: average inductor voltage over a cycle is zero in steady state.

7. **Inductor ripple formula (universal):** $\Delta i_L = V_{L,on} \cdot D \cdot T_s / L = V_{L,off} \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s / L$.

8. **Output ripple (Buck/Boost):** $\Delta V_o = \Delta i_L / (8 f_s C)$.

9. **Critical inductance (Buck):** $L_{crit} = (1-D) R / (2 f_s)$; converter is in CCM if $L > L_{crit}$.

10. **Synchronous rectification** (replacing diode with MOSFET) can boost efficiency by 3–6% in low-voltage high-current designs.

11. **Cuk converter** uses a coupling capacitor $C_1$ as the primary energy transfer element — energy flows input → $L_1$ → $C_1$ → $L_2$ → output in *every* switching cycle.

12. **Boost converter** cannot operate at $D = 1$ (infinite gain in theory, voltage collapse in practice due to parasitic resistances). Always $D < 1$.

13. **Buck-Boost at $D = 0.5$** gives $\vert V_o \vert = V_s$ — this is the "unity-gain crossover" point between buck-like ($D < 0.5$) and boost-like ($D > 0.5$) behavior.

14. **Renewable energy applications:** Boost → PV MPPT, HEV battery boosters; Buck → battery chargers, LED drivers; Buck-Boost → wide-input-range portable electronics; Cuk → low-ripple PV/battery systems.

15. **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = V_o I_o / (V_s I_s)$ — always less than 100% due to switching losses, conduction losses, and core losses.

> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When solving a numerical, **always show the units in every line** of your calculation. The KTU evaluator scans for unit consistency as a partial-marking safeguard. A numerical answer without units is a ½-mark deduction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC-AC Converters (Inverters): Basic Principles of Inverters and Types (Square Wave, Sine Wave, PWM Inverters), Performance Parameters and Harmonic Analysis. Applications in UPS, Motor Drives, and Grid-Tied Renewable Energy Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ DC-AC CONVERTERS (INVERTERS) — FOUNDATIONS

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DC-AC Converters (Inverters): Basic Principles of Inverters and Types (Square Wave, Sine Wave, PWM Inverters), Performance Parameters and Harmonic Analysis. Applications in UPS, Motor Drives, and Grid-Tied Renewable Energy Systems

---

## 1.1 What is an Inverter? — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> An **inverter** is a power electronic DC-to-AC converter that converts a **DC input voltage** (from a battery, fuel cell, solar PV, or rectified AC bus) into a **controlled AC output voltage of desired frequency and magnitude** suitable for feeding AC loads, AC motors, UPS systems, or the utility grid.

The conversion is achieved by **forced (hard) commutation** of semiconductor switches — typically **IGBT, MOSFET, GTO, or IGCT** — arranged in a bridge topology. The output is synthesized, not generated by rotation, hence it is also called a *static inverter*.

### Salient Input / Output Quantities (highlighted for KTU Board exams)

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range | Bolded Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC Input Voltage | $V_{dc}$ or $V_s$ | **12 V – 1000 V** | Most common KTU numerical value |
| Output RMS Voltage | $V_{o,rms}$ | **0 to $V_{dc}$** | Controlled by modulation index |
| Output Frequency | $f_o$ | **50 Hz / 60 Hz** (fixed) or **0 – 400 Hz** (variable) | Motor drives go up to **400 Hz** |
| Switching Frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **2 – 20 kHz** | Above audible band |
| Apparent Power Rating | $S$ | **VA / kVA / MVA** | Standard rating metric |

---

## 1.2 The Big Picture — Inverters in the Power Electronics Family

A power electronic converter is classified by its input/output nature:

$$\text{Converter Family} = \{\text{DC-DC (Chopper)},\ \text{AC-DC (Rectifier)},\ \text{DC-AC (Inverter)},\ \text{AC-AC (Cycloconverter)}\}$$

The **inverter** performs the **DC $\rightarrow$ AC** transformation, which is the *reverse* of the conventional line-frequency diode/thyristor rectifier.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water-Tap Translator" 🚰

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (for first-time learners):**
> Imagine you have a **steady water tank** (the DC bus — pressure is constant, flow is constant) and you want to feed a **washing machine that demands water sloshing back and forth at 50 cycles per minute** (the AC load). You cannot connect the tank directly because the tank pushes water in only *one direction*. So you install an **automatic 4-valve switching manifold** (the H-bridge) that, in a synchronized rhythm, swaps the inlet and outlet of the washing machine 50 times per second. That manifold is the **inverter**.
> - When all 4 valves are equally opened/closed in pairs → you get a *crude* square-wave flow.
> - When you *modulate* the valve opening times within each half-cycle using a **smart controller** → you get a *smooth sinusoidal* flow. This is PWM.

The valves = **transistors (IGBT/MOSFET)**, the controller = **modulation logic**, the tank = **DC source**, and the washing machine = **AC load**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Inverters (As Per KTU 2024 Module 2)

```
INVERTERS
├── 1. By Output Waveform
│   ├── Square-Wave Inverter
│   ├── Quasi-Square-Wave (Modified Sine Wave)
│   └── Sine-Wave PWM (SPWM) Inverter
│
├── 2. By Number of Output Voltage Levels
│   ├── 2-Level Inverter
│   ├── 3-Level (Neutral Point Clamped) Inverter
│   └── Multilevel (Cascaded H-Bridge, Flying Capacitor, MMC)
│
├── 3. By Number of Phases
│   ├── Single-Phase (Half-Bridge, Full-Bridge)
│   └── Three-Phase (180° Mode, 120° Mode)
│
├── 4. By Commutation Method
│   ├── Line / Load Commutated
│   ├── Forced Commutated (Class A, B, C, D, E)
│
└── 5. By Power Flow Direction
    ├── Unidirectional (DC → AC)
    └── Bidirectional (DC ↔ AC)  ← Active Front-End / Grid-Tie
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The syllabus explicitly names **Square Wave, Sine Wave, and PWM Inverters** — these are the three waveform families you MUST be able to draw, derive, and compare in any ESE question on this module.

---

## 1.5 Why Are Inverters Everywhere? — Engineering Motivation

| Application Sector | Why Inverter? | KTU Exam Favourite? |
|---|---|---|
| **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** | Produces clean 230 V / 50 Hz sine from battery during mains failure | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **AC Motor Drives (VFD)** | Vary voltage AND frequency to control speed $\left(N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}\right)$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Solar PV Grid-Tie Inverters** | Convert PV DC panel output to grid-synchronous AC | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Wind Turbine Converters** | Doubly-Fed Induction Generator (DFIG) stator/rotor side | ⭐⭐ |
| **HVDC Light (VSC-HVDC)** | Long-distance subsea/underground DC transmission | ⭐ |
| **Induction Heating** | High-frequency (10–100 kHz) inverters | ⭐ |
| **Electric Vehicle Traction** | Battery DC → 3-phase AC traction motor | ⭐⭐ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Single-Phase Full-Bridge Inverter — The Workhorse

The **single-phase voltage source inverter (VSI)** full-bridge consists of **4 controlled switches** ($T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$) with **4 anti-parallel diodes** ($D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$) connected across a DC source $V_{dc}$.

The load is connected between the **mid-point A** (junction of $T_1$–$T_2$) and the **mid-point B** (junction of $T_3$–$T_4$).

### 2.1.1 Switch Pairing Logic (Step-by-Step)

| Time Interval | Switches ON | Load Voltage $v_o$ | Current Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0 \rightarrow T/2$ | $T_1, T_4$ | $+V_{dc}$ | $V_{dc} \rightarrow T_1 \rightarrow \text{Load} \rightarrow T_4 \rightarrow V_{dc}^-$ |
| $T/2 \rightarrow T$ | $T_2, T_3$ | $-V_{dc}$ | $V_{dc} \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow \text{Load} \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow V_{dc}^-$ |
| During Reverse Current | $D_2, D_3$ (or $D_1, D_4$) | $\pm V_{dc}$ (feedback) | Reactive load current freewheels through diodes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need anti-parallel diodes?**
> Inductive loads (motors, transformers) store energy in their magnetic field. When the switch turns off, the load current *cannot* change instantaneously. The diodes provide a **freewheeling path** and **return the trapped inductive energy back to the DC source**, protecting the transistors from destructive over-voltage.

### 2.1.2 The Pure Square-Wave Output

If $T_1$–$T_4$ are switched at fundamental frequency $f_o = 1/T$ with **50% duty cycle** and no modulation:

$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} +V_{dc}, & 0 \leq t < T/2 \\ -V_{dc}, & T/2 \leq t < T \end{cases}$$

This is the **square-wave inverter** waveform. It is the simplest, cheapest, most efficient, but has the **worst harmonic content**.

### 2.1.3 The Quasi-Square-Wave (Notched) Output

By introducing a notch of width $2\mu$ in each half-cycle, the fundamental amplitude can be **reduced without reducing the DC link voltage**:

$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \leq t < \mu \\ +V_{dc}, & \mu \leq t < \pi/2 \\ 0, & \pi/2 \leq t < \pi/2+\mu \\ -V_{dc}, & \pi/2+\mu \leq t < 3\pi/2-\mu \\ \cdots & \end{cases}$$

This is called **single-pulse-width modulation (single-PWM)**. The notch width $\mu$ becomes the control variable.

---

## 2.2 Three-Phase VSI — 180° Conduction Mode (The Industry Standard)

The **three-phase VSI** uses **6 switches** ($T_1$ to $T_6$) and **6 anti-parallel diodes** in a bridge. In the **180° conduction mode**, each switch conducts for **180°** of the electrical cycle. Conduction sequence is $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_4 \rightarrow T_5 \rightarrow T_6 \rightarrow T_1$.

| Mode Interval (60°) | ON Switches | Conducting Phases | Line-to-Line Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0°–60° | $T_1, T_2, T_3$ | A, B, C | $V_{AB}=V_{dc},\ V_{BC}=0,\ V_{CA}=-V_{dc}$ |
| 60°–120° | $T_2, T_3, T_4$ | C, B, A | $V_{AB}=0,\ V_{BC}=V_{dc},\ V_{CA}=-V_{dc}$ |
| 120°–180° | $T_3, T_4, T_5$ | C, A, B | … |
| … | … | … | … |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** The phase voltages (with respect to the DC bus midpoint, if a fictitious neutral exists) and line voltages in a 180°-mode 3-phase VSI take values from $\left\{+V_{dc}/3,\ -V_{dc}/3,\ 2V_{dc}/3,\ -2V_{dc}/3\right\}$ and $\left\{+V_{dc},\ 0,\ -V_{dc}\right\}$ respectively.

---

## 2.3 Sine-Triangle PWM (SPWM) — The Pulse-Width Modulation Principle

In SPWM, a **low-frequency sinusoidal reference wave** $v_r = V_r \sin(\omega t)$ is compared with a **high-frequency triangular carrier wave** $v_c$ of amplitude $V_c$ and frequency $f_c$.

- When $v_r > v_c$ → upper switch is ON, $v_o = +V_{dc}/2$ (or $+V_{dc}$ for full-bridge)
- When $v_r < v_c$ → lower switch is ON, $v_o = -V_{dc}/2$

### Two Critical Control Parameters

| Parameter | Definition | Range | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Modulation Index** $m$ | $m = \dfrac{V_r}{V_c}$ | $0 \leq m \leq 1$ (linear), up to **1.154** (over-modulation) | Controls **output amplitude** |
| **Frequency Modulation Ratio** $m_f$ | $m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_r}$ | $m_f \geq 9$ (integer) for **synchronous**, otherwise **asynchronous** | Controls **harmonic spectrum** |

$$V_{o,1,peak} = m \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \quad \text{(single-phase half-bridge)}$$

$$V_{o,1,peak} = m \cdot V_{dc} \quad \text{(single-phase full-bridge, peak fundamental)}$$

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are direct KTU 2024 Module-2 high-yield material.** Memorise the table, then attempt Section 3 derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Square-wave RMS output | $V_{o,rms} = V_{dc}$ | Square wave between $\pm V_{dc}$ | Volts |
| 2 | Square-wave fundamental peak | $V_{o1,pk} = \dfrac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi}$ | Fourier of square wave | Volts |
| 3 | Square-wave fundamental RMS | $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = 0.9 V_{dc}$ | — | Volts |
| 4 | Quasi-square-wave fundamental | $V_{o1,pk} = \dfrac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi} \cos(\mu)$ | Notch half-width $\mu$ | Volts |
| 5 | SPWM fundamental (1-φ HB) | $V_{o1,pk} = m \cdot \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}$ | Linear region | Volts |
| 6 | SPWM fundamental (1-φ FB) | $V_{o1,pk} = m \cdot V_{dc}$ | Linear region | Volts |
| 7 | 3-φ VSI line-voltage peak | $V_{L1,pk} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{dc}$ | Square wave, 180° mode | Volts |
| 8 | 3-φ VSI line-voltage RMS | $V_{L,rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} V_{dc}$ | Square wave, 180° mode | Volts |
| 9 | 3-φ VSI phase-voltage RMS | $V_{ph,rms} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{6}}$ | Square wave, 180° mode | Volts |
| 10 | THD definition | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$ | Universal | % |
| 11 | THD of square wave | $THD = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 - 1} \approx 48.3\%$ | Pure square | % |
| 12 | Harmonic order in 3-φ | $n = 6k \pm 1$ | 180° mode, $k=1,2,3,\ldots$ | — |
| 13 | Harmonic order in 1-φ FB | $n = 4k \pm 1$ | Square wave | — |
| 14 | HF factor (single-PWM) | $HF_n = \dfrac{1}{n}\left[\dfrac{\sin(n\mu)}{n\mu}\right]$ | Notch $\mu$ | — |
| 15 | Crest Factor | $CF = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$ | Quality metric | — |
| 16 | Distortion Factor (DF) | $DF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (THD)^2}}$ | — | — |
| 17 | Form Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$ | — | — |
| 18 | RMS of quasi-square | $V_{o,rms} = V_{dc}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4\mu}{\pi}}$ | Notch half-width $\mu$ | Volts |
| 19 | Switching Loss (per switch) | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{dc} I_o (t_{on}+t_{off}) f_{sw}$ | Linear approximation | Watts |
| 20 | Inverter efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{ac}}{P_{dc}} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{dc}}$ | — | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical PITFALL:** The values $V_{dc}/\sqrt{6}$ and $\sqrt{2/3} V_{dc}$ for 3-φ VSI are **square-wave, 180°-mode, line-to-line** values. Examiners often trap students who apply PWM formulas to a square-wave scenario. **Read the question carefully!**

---

## 2.5 Harmonic Analysis — Why It Matters

Real inverters (square wave, quasi-square, SPWM) all produce **non-sinusoidal** outputs. These outputs are decomposed using **Fourier Series**:

$$v_o(t) = \underbrace{\frac{a_0}{2}}_{\text{DC (zero in symmetric)}} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega t) + b_n \sin(n\omega t)\right]$$

For an **odd-symmetric square wave**, only **odd sine harmonics** survive, with amplitudes $V_n = \dfrac{4 V_{dc}}{n\pi}$, where $n = 1, 3, 5, 7, \ldots$

Harmonics cause:
- **Torque pulsations** in motors (cogging, cogging torque)
- **Iron losses** (hysteresis + eddy) in transformers
- **Resonance** with line inductances/capacitors
- **Acoustic noise** in motors (especially 3rd, 5th, 7th)
- **Interference** with communication/control circuits
- **Increased copper losses** ($I^2R$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental goal of PWM is to SHIFT harmonics away from the low-order region (which are hard to filter and cause heavy losses) to high-order frequencies (which are easy to filter and produce small loss).** This single idea has powered every UPS, drive, and grid-tie inverter for 50+ years.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> **Standard Conventions Used:** All derivations assume odd-symmetric, half-wave-symmetric (or quarter-wave) waveforms to exploit symmetry and kill redundant Fourier terms. Time origin is set where the waveform has a "rise" so that **only sine terms** survive.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fourier Series of a Pure Square Wave

### Step 1: Define the waveform

$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} +V_{dc}, & 0 \leq t \leq T/2 \\ -V_{dc}, & T/2 \leq t \leq T \end{cases}$$

### Step 2: Identify symmetries

- **Odd function:** $v_o(-t) = -v_o(t)$ → **all $a_n = 0$**, only sine terms.
- **Half-wave symmetric:** $v_o(t + T/2) = -v_o(t)$ → **all even harmonics $n = 2, 4, 6, \ldots$ vanish**.

So only **odd $n$** contributes.

### Step 3: Compute $b_n$

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v_o(t) \sin(n\omega t)\, dt$$

Because the function is odd, we can write $b_n = \dfrac{4}{T}\int_0^{T/2} v_o(t) \sin(n\omega t)\, dt$.

Since $v_o = +V_{dc}$ on $[0, T/2]$:

$$b_n = \frac{4}{T} \int_0^{T/2} V_{dc} \sin(n\omega t)\, dt$$

### Step 4: Integrate

$$b_n = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{T} \left[-\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n\omega}\right]_0^{T/2}$$

Substitute limits: at $t = T/2$, $n\omega t = n\pi$. $\cos(n\pi) = (-1)^n$. At $t = 0$, $\cos(0) = 1$.

$$b_n = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{n\omega}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right]$$

Since $\omega = 2\pi/T$, we have $T \cdot n\omega = 2\pi n$:

$$b_n = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{2\pi n}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right] = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi n}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right]$$

### Step 5: Apply parity

For **odd $n$**: $(-1)^n = -1 \Rightarrow b_n = \dfrac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi n}[1-(-1)] = \dfrac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi n}$.

For **even $n$**: $(-1)^n = +1 \Rightarrow b_n = 0$. ✓ (Consistent with our symmetry argument.)

### Step 6: Final Fourier Series

$$\boxed{v_o(t) = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t)}$$

Expanding the first few terms:

$$v_o(t) = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}\left[\sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t) + \cdots\right]$$

### Step 7: Extract fundamental peak and THD

- **Fundamental peak:** $V_{o1,pk} = \dfrac{4V_{dc}}{\pi} = 1.273 V_{dc}$ ✓
- **Fundamental RMS:** $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{V_{o1,pk}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{4V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.9003 V_{dc}$ ✓
- **Total RMS:** $V_{o,rms} = V_{dc}$ (because square wave spends full time at $\pm V_{dc}$)

### Step 8: Compute THD

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_{o,rms}^2 - V_{o1,rms}^2}}{V_{o1,rms}}$$

$$= \frac{\sqrt{V_{dc}^2 - \left(\frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}}{\frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}}}$$

$$= \frac{\sqrt{V_{dc}^2 - \frac{8 V_{dc}^2}{\pi^2}}}{\frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}}}$$

$$= \frac{V_{dc}\sqrt{1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}}}{\frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}}}$$

$$= \frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{4}\sqrt{1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}}$$

$$= \frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 - 8}{\pi^2}}$$

$$= \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4}\sqrt{\pi^2 - 8}$$

### Step 9: Numerical evaluation

$$\pi^2 \approx 9.8696, \quad \pi^2 - 8 \approx 1.8696$$

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4}\sqrt{1.8696} = \frac{1.4142}{4} \cdot 1.3674 = 0.4834$$

$$\boxed{THD_{\text{square}} \approx 48.34\%}$$

This is a **huge distortion** — only 51.66% of the output power is at the fundamental frequency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fourier Series of a Quasi-Square Wave (Notched Output)

### Step 1: Define waveform (one period)

Let $\mu$ be the half-notch width in **radians**.

$$v_o(\theta) = \begin{cases} +V_{dc}, & \mu \leq \theta \leq \pi - \mu \\ -V_{dc}, & \pi+\mu \leq \theta \leq 2\pi - \mu \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\theta = \omega t$.

### Step 2: Use quarter-wave symmetry

Because $v_o(\theta)$ is **odd** and **half-wave symmetric**, only odd $n$ sine terms exist.

$$b_n = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2} v_o(\theta)\sin(n\theta)\, d\theta$$

The first pulse $v_o = +V_{dc}$ occurs from $\mu$ to $\pi/2$ (since by symmetry, the second half-cycle mirrors the first).

$$b_n = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi}\int_{\mu}^{\pi/2}\sin(n\theta)\, d\theta$$

### Step 3: Integrate

$$b_n = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi} \left[-\frac{\cos(n\theta)}{n}\right]_{\mu}^{\pi/2} = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{n\pi}\left[\cos(n\mu) - \cos\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right)\right]$$

### Step 4: Note that $\cos(n\pi/2) = 0$ for all odd $n$

For odd $n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$, $\cos(n\pi/2) = 0$. So:

$$b_n = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{n\pi}\cos(n\mu)$$

### Step 5: Final quasi-square wave Fourier series

$$\boxed{v_o(\theta) = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{\cos(n\mu)}{n}\sin(n\theta)}$$

### Step 6: Special case — pure square wave

When $\mu = 0$: $\cos(0) = 1 \Rightarrow v_o(\theta) = \dfrac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}\sum \frac{1}{n}\sin(n\theta)$ ✓ (matches Section 3.1)

### Step 7: RMS value of quasi-square wave

$$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\mu}^{\pi-\mu} V_{dc}^2\, d\theta} = V_{dc}\sqrt{\frac{\pi - 2\mu}{\pi}} = V_{dc}\sqrt{1 - \frac{2\mu}{\pi}}$$

> *(For a full period normalised to $2\pi$, the equation becomes $V_{o,rms} = V_{dc}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4\mu}{\pi}}$ — same result, just confirming consistency.)*

### Step 8: THD expression

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_{o,rms}^2 - V_{o1,rms}^2}}{V_{o1,rms}}$$

With $V_{o1,pk} = \dfrac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi}\cos(\mu)$:

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{\left(1 - \frac{2\mu}{\pi}\right) - \frac{8}{\pi^2}\cos^2(\mu)}}{\frac{4}{\pi\sqrt{2}}\cos(\mu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** As $\mu$ increases from $0 \rightarrow \pi/2$, the fundamental amplitude $\cos(\mu)$ decreases (good for amplitude control), but the harmonic content first **decreases** (THD improves), then worsens after an optimum. The optimum is near $\mu = 30°$ where the waveform is closest to a sine.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Three-Phase 180° VSI Line Voltage

### Step 1: Line voltage from phase voltages

In a balanced 3-phase inverter with no neutral connection, the **line voltages** are:

$$V_{AB} = V_{AN} - V_{BN}, \quad V_{BC} = V_{BN} - V_{CN}, \quad V_{CA} = V_{CN} - V_{AN}$$

### Step 2: Phase voltage in 180° mode

For a single phase (say A), the phase voltage (with respect to DC-bus negative rail) is a square wave of amplitude $V_{dc}$ for 180° and 0 for the next 180°. Its Fourier series is:

$$V_{AN}(\theta) = \frac{V_{dc}}{2} + \frac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi}\left[\sin(\theta) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\theta) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\theta) + \cdots\right]$$

(Decomposed: 180° pulse train shifted to start at 0).

Wait — for a 180°-conducting switch in a 3-φ bridge, the phase voltage is $V_{dc}$ for half the period, but referenced to the DC bus.

### Step 3: Phase-to-neutral equivalent (with star point)

With a star-connected load and the star point floating (no neutral), the **phase-to-star-point voltage** $V_{AN'} = \dfrac{2}{3}V_{AN} - \dfrac{1}{3}V_{BN} - \dfrac{1}{3}V_{CN}$ takes the staircase shape $\pm V_{dc}/3$ and $\pm 2V_{dc}/3$.

### Step 4: Fourier analysis of $V_{AN'}$

$$V_{AN'}(\theta) = \frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi}\left[\sin(\theta) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\theta) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\theta) + \frac{1}{11}\sin(11\theta) + \frac{1}{13}\sin(13\theta) + \cdots\right]$$

Notice the **triplen harmonics ($n = 3, 9, 15, \ldots$) vanish** in the line voltage! This is a major advantage of 3-phase inverters over single-phase.

### Step 5: Line voltage

$$V_{AB}(\theta) = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{AN'}(\theta) \angle +30°$$

Peak fundamental line voltage:

$$\boxed{V_{L1,pk} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}\, V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 1.1027\, V_{dc}}$$

RMS line voltage:

$$V_{L,rms} = \frac{V_{L1,pk}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}V_{dc} \approx 0.7797 V_{dc}$$

Alternative form: $V_{L,rms} = \sqrt{2/3}\, V_{dc} = 0.8165 V_{dc}$ (for full square wave, used in some textbooks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Two textbook conventions exist.** Some texts compute $V_{L,rms}$ from the time-domain step waveform (giving $\sqrt{2/3}\, V_{dc}$), others from the Fourier fundamental (giving $\sqrt{6}/\pi\, V_{dc}$). For a 180° square-wave 3-φ VSI feeding a balanced star load, the **fundamental RMS** line voltage is $\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}V_{dc}$ and the **total RMS** (including all harmonics) is $\sqrt{2/3}\,V_{dc}$. Both are correct under their respective definitions. KTU favours the fundamental form unless stated otherwise.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — RMS Value and Power Delivered to a Resistive Load

For a single-phase square-wave inverter feeding a resistor $R$:

$$P = \frac{V_{o,rms}^2}{R} = \frac{V_{dc}^2}{R}$$

The fundamental power:

$$P_1 = \frac{V_{o1,rms}^2}{R} = \frac{1}{R}\left(\frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{8 V_{dc}^2}{\pi^2 R}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{P_1}{P} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \approx 0.8106$$

So **81.06%** of the total power is in the fundamental, the remaining 18.94% in harmonics.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Output Voltage of SPWM (Single-Phase Full-Bridge)

### Step 1: Modulation index

$$m = \frac{V_r}{V_c} \quad \text{(amplitude ratio)}$$

### Step 2: Area-equivalence principle

Assuming the carrier frequency $f_c$ is much greater than the reference frequency $f_r$ (i.e., $m_f >> 1$), the output pulse within one carrier cycle approximates the reference. Equivalently, the fundamental of the SPWM output equals the average over a carrier cycle:

$$V_{o,1,avg}(t) = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}\cdot \text{(duty cycle)} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}\cdot \frac{1 + v_r(t)/V_c}{1} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}\left[1 + m\sin(\omega t)\right]$$

The DC component ($\dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}$) is blocked by the transformer / load in a full-bridge bipolar SPWM (or is cancelled in the differential output). The AC component gives:

$$V_{o,1,pk} = m \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$

For a **full-bridge** with bipolar SPWM (output between the two mid-points = $V_A - V_B$), the differential output swing is doubled:

$$\boxed{V_{o,1,pk} = m \cdot V_{dc}}$$

For a **half-bridge** (output between midpoint and DC bus negative rail):

$$V_{o,1,pk} = m \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$

### Step 3: RMS value of SPWM output

The SPWM output is a train of $\pm V_{dc}$ pulses. The duty cycle varies sinusoidally. The RMS value of the fundamental is:

$$V_{o1,rms} = \frac{m V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

The **total RMS** (including all harmonics) is:

$$V_{o,rms} = V_{dc}\sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T [s_P(t)]^2 dt}$$

where $s_P(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ is the switching function. By the average duty cycle argument:

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = V_{dc}^2 \cdot \text{Mean}[s_P^2] = V_{dc}^2 \cdot 1 = V_{dc}^2$$

(since $s_P^2 = 1$ always — every pulse is $\pm V_{dc}$ in magnitude). Hence:

$$V_{o,rms} = V_{dc} \quad \text{(always, for bipolar SPWM)}$$

This is **independent** of $m$! Only the fundamental amplitude changes with $m$. The "extra" RMS appears as harmonic content.

### Step 4: THD of SPWM (approximate)

For $m < 1$, dominant harmonic cluster is around $f_c$ and $2f_c$. The fundamental dominates strongly:

$$THD_{SPWM} \approx \frac{1}{m}\sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 - 1} \approx \frac{0.483}{m}$$

For $m = 0.8$, $THD \approx 60.4\%$ — wait, this seems high. Let me clarify.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refined Insight:** The above approximation is for the *complete* spectrum including high-frequency carrier-sidebands, whose RMS sum actually grows as $m$ decreases. However, **practically these high-frequency components are easily filtered** by a small LC filter at the output. The effective THD *after filtering* is typically **< 5%** for SPWM, vastly better than the 48% of square wave.

This is why **SPWM dominates industrial applications** — the harmonics are *pushable to high frequencies* where filter cost is minimal.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Harmonic Elimination via Selective Notch (Single-PWM)

For a quasi-square wave with $p$ notches per half-cycle, the $n$-th harmonic amplitude is:

$$V_n = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{n\pi}\left[1 - 2\sum_{k=1}^{p}(-1)^k \cos(n\mu_k)\right]$$

By choosing $p$ notch angles $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_p$ appropriately, we can **set the lowest $p$ non-fundamental harmonics to zero**.

For example, with $p = 2$ (eliminate 3rd and 5th):

$$\cos(3\mu_1) = \cos(5\mu_2) = 0, \quad \mu_1 = 30°, \quad \mu_2 = 54° + \epsilon$$

Solving simultaneously yields transcendental equations. This is **Selective Harmonic Elimination (SHE-PWM)** — used in high-power GTO/IGCT inverters.

---

## 3.7 📊 Analytical Master Matrix (Quick Revision Table)

| Inverter Type | Fundamental $V_{o1}$ | Total $V_{o,rms}$ | THD | Best Use Case | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Square Wave** | $\dfrac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi} = 1.273 V_{dc}$ (peak) | $V_{dc}$ | **48.3%** | High-power fixed-freq, low cost | ⭐⭐ |
| **Quasi-Square** | $\dfrac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi}\cos(\mu)$ | $V_{dc}\sqrt{1-2\mu/\pi}$ | **Varies 0–48%** | Variable voltage, low-power | ⭐⭐ |
| **Single-PWM** | Controlled via $\mu$ | $V_{dc}\sqrt{1-2\mu/\pi}$ | **25–48%** | Older industrial drives | ⭐⭐ |
| **Multi-PWM (SPWM)** | $m \cdot V_{dc}$ (FB) | $V_{dc}$ | **<5% (post-filter)** | UPS, drives, grid-tie | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Sine-Sigma PWM** | $m \cdot V_{dc}/\sqrt{2}$ | $V_{dc}/\sqrt{2}$ | **<3% (post-filter)** | Audio, precision | ⭐ |
| **3-φ Square (180°)** | $\dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi}V_{dc}$ line | $\sqrt{2/3}V_{dc}$ | **31% (line)** | 3-φ motor drives, low cost | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **3-φ SPWM** | $m \cdot \sqrt{3} V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$ | $\sqrt{3} V_{dc}$ | **<5% (post-filter)** | Industrial 3-φ drives | ⭐⭐⭐ |
