## 4.4 Waveform Relationships – SPWM Modulation Index Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Modulation Index m sub a] --> Q{m sub a less than 1}
    M0 --> R{m sub a equals 1}
    M0 --> S{m sub a greater than 1}

    Q --> Q1[Linear Region]
    Q1 --> Q2[Fundamental V sub o1 equals m sub a times Vdc over 2]
    Q1 --> Q3[Low THD]
    Q1 --> Q4[Pulses have variable width]

    R --> R1[Boundary Condition]
    R1 --> R2[Just at edge of PWM pulses]
    R1 --> R3[Reference amplitude equals carrier amplitude]

    S --> S1[Overmodulation Region]
    S1 --> S2[Non linear fundamental]
    S1 --> S3[Increased THD]
    S1 --> S4[Approaches six step output]

    classDef dec fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#713f12
    classDef region fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,color:#312e81
    classDef out fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843
    class M0 dec
    class Q,R,S,Q1,R1,S1 region
    class Q2,Q3,Q4,R2,R3,S2,S3,S4 out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEICT411]`

**Q: Define Pulse Width Modulation (PWM). List any two advantages of PWM in power electronic converters.**

**Model Answer:**

> **PWM Definition:** Pulse Width Modulation is a modulation technique used in power electronics in which the **width of the output voltage pulses** is varied in proportion to a reference (control) signal, while the **pulse amplitude and switching frequency remain constant**.
>
> **Average output voltage:** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ where $D = T_{on}/T_s$.
>
> **Advantages of PWM:**
>
> 1. **High efficiency:** Power semiconductor devices operate either in cutoff (OFF) or saturation (ON), minimising switching losses and achieving efficiencies often **above 95%**.
>
> 2. **Compact filtering:** The high switching frequency shifts harmonics to high frequencies, allowing use of small, lightweight LC filters compared to line-frequency systems.
>
> 3. **Flexible & independent control:** Both output voltage magnitude (via duty cycle) and frequency (via reference) can be controlled independently — essential for VFD motor drives.
>
> 4. **Digital compatibility:** Easily generated by microcontrollers, DSPs, and FPGAs without complex analog circuitry.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEICT411]`

**Q: Distinguish between Single-Pulse Modulation and Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM). Write the expression for the fundamental RMS output voltage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single-Pulse Modulation (SPM) | Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) |
|---------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| Pulses per half-cycle | **One** (variable width $2d$) | **Multiple** (width modulated by sine) |
| Reference signal | DC level compared with sine | Sine reference compared with triangle |
| Control parameter | Pulse width $d$ | Modulation index $m_a = A_m/A_c$ |
| Switching frequency | Low (line frequency) | High (carrier frequency $f_c$) |
| Harmonic content | High THD; strong low-order harmonics | Low THD; harmonics pushed to high frequencies |
| Application | AC voltage regulators, heaters | VFDs, UPS, solar inverters |
| **Fundamental RMS** | $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\pi}\sin d$ | $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$ |
| **RMS output** | $V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{2d/\pi}$ | (complicated — depends on $m_a$, $m_f$) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse pulse width $d$ (in radians) with the firing angle $\alpha$. They are related: $d = \pi/2 - \alpha$ for symmetric pulses. Examiners WILL deduct 1–2 marks if this distinction is ignored.
> 2. **For SPWM problems**, always state whether you are in the **linear region** ($m_a \le 1$) or **overmodulation** ($m_a > 1$). Using the linear formula $V_{o1} = m_a V_{dc}/2$ in the overmodulation region is a common error that costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Units:** Always specify radian or degree for angles, and Hz for frequencies. Missing units = **0.5 mark** per instance.
> 4. **Diagrams:** Always include the pulse waveform sketch (with $\alpha$, $\beta$, $d$ labelled) for SPM/MPM problems. A figure without labels = **0 marks** for the diagram portion.
> 5. **Fourier steps:** Show the integration limits clearly. Writing $\int_0^{\pi}$ without specifying where the pulse is non-zero will be marked **incomplete**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PEICT411]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat circuit and waveform, explain the operation of **single-phase full-bridge inverter using single-pulse modulation**. Derive the expression for the **RMS output voltage** and the **fundamental RMS output voltage** of the inverter.

**Q: (b)** For a single-pulse modulated waveform of amplitude $V_s = 230$ V and pulse width $2d = 60°$, compute:
1. RMS output voltage
2. Peak fundamental output voltage
3. Total RMS harmonic distortion (THD) if the 3rd, 5th, and 7th harmonics are 20%, 10%, and 5% of the fundamental respectively.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Operation, Waveform, and Derivation [7 Marks]

**Circuit:** A single-phase full-bridge inverter consists of 4 IGBTs/MOSFETs ($S_1$, $S_2$, $S_3$, $S_4$) with anti-parallel diodes, fed from a DC source $V_s$. Diagonal pairs ($S_1 S_2$ and $S_3 S_4$) conduct alternately to produce $+V_s$ and $-V_s$ at the load.

**Operation in single-pulse mode:**
- In the positive half-cycle, $S_1$ and $S_2$ are ON for an interval corresponding to pulse width $2d$ (centred at $\pi/2$).
- Outside this interval in the half-cycle, all switches are OFF and the output is 0.
- In the negative half-cycle, $S_3$ and $S_4$ are ON for the same duration $2d$ (centred at $3\pi/2$), producing $-V_s$.
- The firing angles are $\alpha = \pi/2 - d$ and $\beta = \pi/2 + d$.

**Waveform diagram (textual description for student to draw):**
- X-axis: $\omega t$ from $0$ to $2\pi$.
- For $\omega t \in [\alpha, \beta]$: output is $+V_s$.
- For $\omega t \in [\pi+\alpha, \pi+\beta]$: output is $-V_s$.
- Output is $0$ everywhere else in the cycle.

**Derivation of RMS Output Voltage:**

Using the result from Section 3.1:

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} v_o^2(t)\,d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} v_o^2(t)\,d(\omega t)$$

Since $v_o = V_s$ only on $[\alpha, \beta]$ of length $2d$:

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{\pi}\cdot 2d \implies \boxed{V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{\frac{2d}{\pi}}}$$

**Derivation of Fundamental RMS Voltage:**

The fundamental Fourier coefficient (from Section 3.2 with $n=1$):

$$B_1 = \frac{4V_s}{\pi}\sin(d) \implies V_{o1,rms} = \frac{B_1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\pi}\sin d$$

$$\boxed{V_{o1,rms} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\pi}\sin d}$$

#### (b) Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Given:** $V_s = 230$ V, $2d = 60°$ → $d = 30° = \pi/6$ rad.

**Step 1 — RMS output voltage:**

$$V_{o,rms} = 230\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 30°}{180°}} = 230\sqrt{\frac{60}{180}} = 230\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} = 230 \times 0.5774$$

$$\boxed{V_{o,rms} = 132.79 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 2 — Peak fundamental output voltage:**

$$V_{o1,peak} = B_1 = \frac{4 \times 230}{\pi}\sin(30°) = \frac{920}{\pi}\times 0.5 = \frac{460}{\pi}$$

$$\boxed{V_{o1,peak} = 146.42 \text{ V}}$$

RMS fundamental: $V_{o1,rms} = 146.42/\sqrt{2} = 103.54$ V.

**Step 3 — THD calculation:**

Harmonic RMS values:
- $V_3 = 0.20 \times 103.54 = 20.71$ V
- $V_5 = 0.10 \times 103.54 = 10.35$ V
- $V_7 = 0.05 \times 103.54 = 5.18$ V

Total harmonic RMS (assuming these are the dominant harmonics):

$$V_{h,rms} = \sqrt{V_3^2 + V_5^2 + V_7^2} = \sqrt{20.71^2 + 10.35^2 + 5.18^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{428.90 + 107.12 + 26.83} = \sqrt{562.85} = 23.73 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{THD} = \frac{V_{h,rms}}{V_{o1,rms}} = \frac{23.73}{103.54} = 0.2292 \text{ or } 22.92\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{THD} \approx 22.92\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Single-Pulse THD:** Single-pulse modulation inherently has **high THD (often 30–50%)** because of strong low-order harmonics. This is why SPM is used only for slow loads (heaters, dimmers), not for motors.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PEICT411]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the generation of **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** signals for a single-phase inverter. Define **modulation index** ($m_a$) and **frequency modulation ratio** ($m_f$). Derive the expression for the **peak fundamental output voltage** in the linear region.

**Q: (b)** A single-phase SPWM inverter has $V_{dc} = 400$ V, $f_m = 50$ Hz, $m_f = 15$, and $m_a = 0.9$. Calculate:
1. The carrier frequency
2. The peak fundamental output voltage
3. The RMS fundamental output voltage
4. Comment on the harmonic content (qualitatively) if $m_a$ is increased to 1.2.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) SPWM Generation and Derivation [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (described for student to draw):**

```
Sine Reference (Am, fm) ──┐
                          ├──► Comparator ──► Gating Logic ──► Inverter Switches
Triangle Carrier (Ac, fc)─┘
```

**Generation process:**
1. A **sine reference** of amplitude $A_m$ and frequency $f_m$ (the desired fundamental output frequency) is generated.
2. A **triangle carrier** of amplitude $A_c$ and frequency $f_c \gg f_m$ is generated.
3. The two signals are fed to a **comparator**.
4. When $v_{sine} > v_{triangle}$: comparator output is HIGH → upper switches ON → output = $+V_{dc}$.
5. When $v_{sine} < v_{triangle}$: comparator output is LOW → lower switches ON → output = $-V_{dc}$.
6. The result is a bipolar SPWM waveform whose fundamental tracks the sine reference.

**Definitions:**

> **Modulation Index:** $m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$ — controls output **voltage magnitude**.

> **Frequency Modulation Ratio:** $m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_m}$ — controls harmonic **spectrum distribution**. Should be an **odd integer** ≥ 9 for half-wave symmetry and elimination of even harmonics.

**Derivation of Peak Fundamental Voltage (Linear Region, $m_a \le 1$):**

Within one carrier period, the duty ratio for the positive output is:

$$D(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + m_a \sin(\omega_m t)\right]$$

The local average of the bipolar output $v_o \in \{-V_{dc}, +V_{dc}\}$ is:

$$\langle v_o \rangle = V_{dc}\cdot D + (-V_{dc})\cdot(1-D) = V_{dc}(2D - 1) = m_a V_{dc} \sin(\omega_m t)$$

This local average **IS** the fundamental (since the carrier is much faster than the modulating signal). The peak of the fundamental is:

$$\boxed{V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}}$$

This is valid strictly in the **linear region** $0 \le m_a \le 1$.

#### (b) Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Given:** $V_{dc} = 400$ V, $f_m = 50$ Hz, $m_f = 15$, $m_a = 0.9$.

**Step 1 — Carrier frequency:**

$$f_c = m_f \cdot f_m = 15 \times 50 = \boxed{750 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Step 2 — Peak fundamental output voltage (linear region, $m_a = 0.9 < 1$):**

$$V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = 0.9 \times \frac{400}{2} = 0.9 \times 200 = \boxed{180 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3 — RMS fundamental output voltage:**

$$V_{o1,rms} = \frac{V_{o1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{180}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{180}{1.4142} = \boxed{127.28 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4 — Effect of increasing $m_a$ to 1.2 (Overmodulation):**

- At $m_a = 1.2$, the system enters the **overmodulation region** ($m_a > 1$).
- The **pulse widths no longer vary linearly** with the reference, and the carrier is "saturated" at the reference peaks — producing wider pulses but with a non-sinusoidal shape.
- The fundamental voltage increases only marginally (from $V_{dc}/2 = 200$ V to roughly $220$ V), while the harmonic content **increases dramatically** — strong 3rd, 5th, and 7th harmonics re-emerge.
- THD increases from ~5% (linear) to >15% (overmodulation).
- As $m_a \to \infty$, the output approaches a **square wave (six-step mode)** with $V_{o1,peak} = 2V_{dc}/\pi = 254.65$ V (only 27% more than $m_a = 1$).

> **Conclusion:** Operating in overmodulation gives marginal voltage gain at the cost of severe waveform degradation. For motor drives, $m_a$ is normally limited to ≤ 1.0 to maintain sinusoidal current and avoid torque pulsations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU MODULE 3 PWM**

- ✅ **PWM = varying pulse width** at constant amplitude and frequency. Duty cycle $D = T_{on}/T_s$ controls average output: $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$.
- ✅ **Three KTU-mandated types:** Single-Pulse, Multiple-Pulse, Sinusoidal PWM.
- ✅ **SPM:** 1 pulse per half-cycle, width $2d$. RMS = $V_s\sqrt{2d/\pi}$. $n^{th}$ harmonic: $B_n = \frac{4V_s}{n\pi}\sin(nd)$.
- ✅ **MPM:** $p$ pulses per half-cycle of equal width $2\delta$. RMS = $V_s\sqrt{4p\delta/\pi}$. Pushes harmonics to higher frequencies.
- ✅ **SPWM:** Sine vs Triangle. **$m_a = A_m/A_c$** (voltage control); **$m_f = f_c/f_m$** (spectrum control).
- ✅ **SPWM fundamental (linear region):** $V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot V_{dc}/2$, RMS = $m_a V_{dc}/(2\sqrt{2})$.
- ✅ **Linear region:** $m_a \le 1$. **Overmodulation:** $m_a > 1$. **Six-step:** $m_a \to \infty$, giving $V_{o1,peak} = 2V_{dc}/\pi$.
- ✅ **Choose $m_f$ as an odd integer** (≥ 9) to eliminate even harmonics and maintain half-wave symmetry.
- ✅ **Applications:** SPM → AC regulators/dimmers; MPM → medium-power inverters; SPWM → VFDs, UPS, solar inverters, Class-D amplifiers.
- ✅ **Key advantage of SPWM:** Independent control of **magnitude** (via $m_a$) and **frequency** (via $f_m$) — essential for V/Hz motor control.
- ✅ **Remember the angles:** $d = \pi/2 - \alpha$ (SPM); $\theta_k = (2k-1)\pi/(2p)$ (MPM pulse centres).
- ✅ **Always specify units** (rad/deg, Hz, V) and **region of operation** (linear/overmodulation) in KTU answers.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — PWM**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme guidelines (NEP 2020 aligned)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Control Systems: Basics of Feedback Control Systems in Power Electronics, Implementation of Feedback Loops in Power Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feedback Control Systems: Basics of Feedback Control Systems in Power Electronics, Implementation of Feedback Loops in Power Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feedback Control System (in Power Electronics):** A closed-loop control architecture in which a controlled output variable of a power converter (such as output voltage, output current, or inductor current) is continuously sensed, compared against an external reference, and the resulting error signal is used to modulate the duty cycle of the switching converter so that the output tracks the reference despite disturbances and parameter variations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a **feedback control system in power electronics** is a real-time, automatic regulation mechanism that adjusts the converter's switching action to maintain a desired electrical quantity (voltage/current) at the load terminals, in spite of input voltage fluctuations, load transients, temperature drift, and component tolerances.

### Constituent Building Blocks of a Typical Power Electronics Feedback Loop

| Block | Function | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference (Vref)** | Sets the desired output level | Precision shunt reference (e.g., TL431) |
| **Sensing Network** | Scales down the output to a measurable level | Resistor divider / Hall sensor / CT |
| **Error Amplifier (EA)** | Computes the difference `Vref − Vsense` | Op-amp inside controller IC |
| **Compensator / Controller** | Shapes loop gain for stability & bandwidth | PI, PID, Type-II, Type-III network |
| **PWM Modulator** | Converts EA output to duty cycle `d(t)` | Comparator with sawtooth carrier |
| **Power Stage (Plant)** | The DC-DC / DC-AC converter itself | MOSFET/IGBT + L, C, transformer |
| **Feedback Path** | Returns output information to the input | Optocoupler, transformer, direct path |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Shower Mixer with a Thermometer**
> Imagine you want a shower at exactly 40 °C. The **reference** is 40 °C. Your **skin (sensor)** feels the water and tells your **brain (error amplifier + controller)**. If it is too hot, your brain tells your **hand (actuator/PWM)** to turn the hot valve slightly less and the cold valve slightly more. This is **feedback**. If you simply fixed the valve positions at 7 AM and walked away (open-loop), the water temperature would drift when someone else uses the kitchen tap. Similarly, in a power converter, the load current and input voltage keep changing, so without feedback the output voltage would wander. Feedback makes the converter "feel" its own output and **self-correct** at kHz speed.

> [!TIP]
> **Why cannot we run power converters open-loop?**
> 1. **Load regulation** becomes poor — a 10× load step would change `Vout` drastically.
> 2. **Line regulation** is weak — mains sag or boost would pass through to the load.
> 3. **Component tolerance** (inductor ±20 %, capacitor ±20 %) causes unit-to-unit variation.
> 4. **Stability of regulation** cannot be guaranteed across temperature and aging.

## 1.3 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop — A Side-by-Side View

| Parameter | Open-Loop Converter | Closed-Loop Converter |
|---|---|---|
| **Output accuracy** | Poor (depends on L, C, Rds,on) | High (set by Vref and divider ratio) |
| **Load regulation** (`ΔVout/ΔIout`) | Large | Negligible (within bandwidth) |
| **Line regulation** (`ΔVout/ΔVin`) | Large | Negligible (within bandwidth) |
| **Transient response** | Uncontrolled | Defined by compensation |
| **Complexity** | Low | Higher (sensors + EA + comp) |
| **Cost** | Low | Slightly higher |
| **Stability concern** | None | **Must analyze loop gain** |

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (KTU-Highlighted):**
> - **Bandwidth (BW)** — the frequency up to which the loop can suppress disturbances.
> - **Phase Margin (PM)** — measure of relative stability; typical design target is **45°–60°**.
> - **Gain Margin (GM)** — measure of stability against gain variations; typical target is **6 dB–12 dB**.
> - **Cross-over frequency (fc)** — frequency where the loop gain magnitude equals unity (0 dB).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Closed-Loop Block Diagram

A power electronics feedback system is described by the classic **single-input single-output (SISO) negative feedback** block diagram:

```
   d̂                    ûo
 R(s) ──►(+)──►[ Gc(s) ]──►[ Gp(s) ]──►+──┬──► Output Y(s)
            ▲−              │              │
            │               │              │
            │               ▼              │
            │            [ H(s) ]──────────┘
            └──────────── Feedback ───────┘
```

Where:
- `R(s)` = reference (command) signal — Laplace transform of `Vref`.
- `Gc(s)` = **compensator transfer function** (controller).
- `Gp(s)` = **plant (power stage) transfer function** — relates control input (duty `d`) to output.
- `H(s)` = **feedback (sensor) transfer function** — typically a constant `β` (resistor divider ratio).
- The summing junction subtracts feedback from reference (negative feedback).

## 2.2 The Four Key Transfer Functions

| # | Transfer Function | Symbolic Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Open-Loop Gain** | `T(s) = Gc(s) · Gp(s) · H(s)` | Loop gain if feedback is broken |
| 2 | **Closed-Loop (Command) Transfer** | `Y(s)/R(s) = G(s) = Gc·Gp / (1 + T)` | Reference tracking |
| 3 | **Error Transfer Function** | `E(s)/R(s) = 1 / (1 + T)` | How small the steady-state error becomes |
| 4 | **Disturbance Rejection** | `Y(s)/D(s) = Gp / (1 + T)` | Immunity against load/input disturbances |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for designers:** If `∣T(jω)∣ >> 1` (i.e. **inside the loop bandwidth**), then the closed-loop behaviour is set by `1/H(s)` and is **independent** of the plant `Gp(s)`. This is the *magical* property of high-gain negative feedback.

## 2.3 Steady-State Error Analysis (Final Value Theorem)

For a stable closed-loop system, the steady-state error for a given reference input `R(s)` is given by the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot R(s)}{1 + T(s)}
$$

The classification of the system by "type" is based on the number of pure integrators (poles at the origin) in `T(s)`. For a power converter, a **Type-1 system** (one integrator in the loop) is the most common.

| Input Type | R(s) | Type-0 Error | Type-1 Error | Type-2 Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Step `r(t) = A·u(t)` | `A/s` | `A/(1+Kp)` | **0** | **0** |
| Ramp `r(t) = A·t` | `A/s²` | ∞ | `A/Kv` | **0** |
| Parabola | `A/s³` | ∞ | ∞ | `A/Ka` |

> [!TIP]
> **For a Buck converter under voltage-mode control**, the plant `Gp(s)` itself contains a pole at the origin (the output LC filter integrator), giving an inherent Type-1 behaviour. Hence a proportional compensator is often sufficient to drive the steady-state error to zero for step loads.

## 2.4 Stability Criteria

### 2.4.1 Nyquist / Bode Stability Criterion

For a stable open-loop plant `T(s)`, the closed-loop system is stable if and only if, at the **gain cross-over frequency** `fc` (where `∣T(jfc)∣ = 1`):

$$
\phi_m = 180° + \angle T(j f_c) > 0
$$

Where `φm` is the **Phase Margin**. Typical design target: **45° ≤ φm ≤ 60°**.

### 2.4.2 Routh-Hurwitz Criterion (Algebraic)

For the characteristic polynomial `1 + T(s) = 0`, expand and apply the Routh array. All first-column entries must be **positive** for stability. This is the algebraic companion to the Bode method and is **exam-favourite**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loop gain | `T(s) = Gc(s)·Gp(s)·H(s)` | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Closed-loop TF | `Gcl = Gc·Gp / (1 + T)` | — |
| 3 | Steady-state error (step) | `ess = 1 / (1 + Kp)` | Kp = DC loop gain |
| 4 | Phase margin | `φm = 180° + ∠T(j fc)` | Degrees |
| 5 | Gain margin | `GM = −20·log10(∣T(j f−180°)∣)` | dB |
| 6 | Buck VMC control-to-output | `Gp(s) = Vin / (1 + s·R·C)` at low freq | Buck, CCM |
| 7 | Buck VMC with ESR zero | `Gp(s) = Vin·(1 + s·ESR·C) / [1 + s/(ω0·Q) + s²/ω0²]` | — |
| 8 | LC resonant frequency | `ω0 = 1/√(L·C)` | rad/s |
| 9 | Q-factor | `Q = R·√(C/L) = √(R²·C/L)` | — |
| 10 | PI Compensator | `Gc(s) = Kp·(1 + 1/(s·τi))` | Kp, τi |
| 11 | Type-II (Lead) | `Gc(s) = K·(1 + s/ωz)/(1 + s/ωp)` | ωp > ωz |
| 12 | Type-III (Lead–Lead) | Two zeros + two poles + integrator | DC-DC VMC |
| 13 | Current-loop gain (peak CMC) | `T_i(s) = Ri·Gm·(Vin/L)·H_e(s)/[s·(1 + s/ωp)]` | — |
| 14 | Slope compensation | `mc = (Vin − Vo)·Ts/L` | Buck, peak CMC |
| 15 | Sampling gain (subharmonic) | `H_e(s) ≈ 1 / (1 + s/(ωn·Q) + s²/ωn²)`, `ωn = π/Ts` | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Benchmarks Used in KTU Lab & Exam:**
> - Converter switching frequency: `fs = 50 kHz` to `500 kHz` (typical)
> - Target cross-over: `fc = fs/5` to `fs/10` for VMC; `fc = fs/10` to `fs/20` for CMC
> - Inductor ripple ratio: `ΔiL = 20 %–40 %` of full load
> - Output voltage ripple: `ΔVo = 1 %–3 %` of `Vout`

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

Feedback control in power electronics is **not optional** in any commercial product. It is the reason a laptop charger labelled "19.5 V" actually outputs 19.50 V (not 17 V) when the laptop starts charging. It is the reason a 1 MW solar inverter can stay grid-compliant across clouds, temperature swings, and partial shading. From a 5 V phone charger to a 1 GW HVDC station, every modern converter has at least one feedback loop — and high-performance systems use **multiple cascaded loops** (outer voltage, inner current).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function

**Given:** A negative-feedback system with forward path `G(s) = Gc(s)·Gp(s)` and feedback path `H(s)`.

**Step 1 — Define the summing junction output (error signal).**
From the block diagram, the error `E(s)` is the reference minus the feedback:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Forward path output.**
The output of the forward path is the error multiplied by the forward gain:

$$
U(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s)]
$$

**Step 3 — Plant output equals forward-path output** (assuming `G(s)` includes the plant):

$$
Y(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s)]
$$

**Step 4 — Collect `Y(s)` terms on the left-hand side:**

$$
Y(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot Y(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)
$$

$$
Y(s) \cdot [1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)] = G(s) \cdot R(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for `Y(s)/R(s)`:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the loop gain `T(s) = G(s)·H(s)`** and write the compact final form:

$$
\boxed{G_{cl}(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + T(s)}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Error for a Unit-Step Input with a PI Compensator

**Given:** A Type-1 closed-loop system (one integrator inside `T(s)`), reference `R(s) = 1/s`.

**Step 1 — Write the error transfer function:**

$$
\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + T(s)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Final Value Theorem:**

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot R(s)}{1 + T(s)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute `R(s) = 1/s`:**

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot (1/s)}{1 + T(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + T(s)}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the limit.** Since `T(s)` has a pole at the origin, `T(0) → ∞`:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0
$$

**Conclusion:** A **Type-1** system drives the steady-state error to **zero** for a step reference — this is why most DC-DC converters use a compensator with at least one integrator.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Phase Margin from Open-Loop Bode Plot

**Given:** `T(jω)` is the open-loop gain. Let `fc` be the gain cross-over frequency (where `∣T(jfc)∣ = 1`, i.e. 0 dB).

**Step 1 — Express the phase of `T` at `fc`:**
The total phase lag introduced by the loop at `fc` is `∠T(jfc)`. For a minimum-phase system, this lag is between 0° and −180°.

**Step 2 — Define Phase Margin:**

$$
\phi_m = 180° + \angle T(j f_c)
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
- If `∠T(jfc) = −135°`, then `φm = 45°` (well-damped, low overshoot).
- If `∠T(jfc) = −180°`, then `φm = 0°` — the system is **marginally stable** (sustained oscillation).

**Step 4 — Empirical design rule for power converters:**

$$
\boxed{f_c \leq \frac{f_s}{5} \quad \text{(VMC)}, \quad f_c \leq \frac{f_s}{10} \quad \text{(CMC)}}
$$

This guarantees at least 60° phase margin after sampling effects.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Buck Converter VMC Loop

**Problem statement (KTU-type):**
A buck converter operates in CCM with `Vin = 24 V`, `Vout = 12 V`, `L = 33 µH`, `C = 47 µF`, `ESR = 50 mΩ`, `Rload = 5 Ω`, `fs = 100 kHz`, sensor gain `β = 0.25`. The compensator is a simple integrator with `Gc(s) = K/s`. Determine:
(a) The control-to-output plant transfer function `Gp(s)`.
(b) The DC loop gain required for `ess = 0.5 %` under a unit step in reference.
(c) The resonant frequency `f0` and Q-factor.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Plant TF:** For a buck in CCM, the small-signal control-to-output transfer function (including ESR zero) is:

$$
G_p(s) = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1 + s \cdot ESR \cdot C}{1 + s / (\omega_0 Q) + s^2 / \omega_0^2}
$$

**Part (b) — Required K:** The DC error for a step is `ess = 1/(1 + Kp)` where `Kp = Gc(0)·Gp(0)·H(0)`. With pure integrator `Gc`, `Kp → ∞`, hence `ess = 0`. So in practice the bandwidth, not the DC error, sets the integrator gain.

**Part (c) — `f0` and Q:**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L \cdot C}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{33 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 47 \times 10^{-6}}} \approx 25{,}361 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} \approx 4.04 \text{ kHz}
$$

$$
Q = R \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} = 5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{47 \times 10^{-6}}{33 \times 10^{-6}}} = 5 \cdot \sqrt{1.424} \approx 5.97
$$

**Conclusion:** The LC resonance at ~4 kHz is the dominant plant pole. The compensator must provide a zero to cancel it (Type-III) and roll off above `fc ≈ fs/5 = 20 kHz` with adequate phase margin.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Why a Type-III Compensator is Used in VMC Buck

A buck in CCM has the plant:

$$
G_p(s) = \frac{V_{in}}{1 + s/(R C) + s^2 L C} \cdot (1 + s R_{ESR} C)
$$

This has:
- Two low-frequency poles (LC tank) at `ω0 = 1/√(LC)`.
- One ESR zero at `ωz,ESR = 1/(R_ESR·C)`.
- One right-half-plane (RHP) zero **only for boost/buck-boost** — not for buck.

The compensator is designed to:
1. Place **two zeros** at `ωz1 = ωz2 = ω0` to cancel the LC double pole.
2. Place **two poles** — one at the origin (integrator for high DC gain) and one at `ωp2 = ωz,ESR/2` to cancel the ESR zero and roll off high-frequency noise.
3. Choose mid-band gain so that `0 dB` cross-over occurs at `fc = fs/5`.

This yields a flat gain profile across the bandwidth with at least 60° phase margin.

**Explanation:** In **Voltage Mode Control (VMC)**, only the output voltage is sensed and fed back. The error is computed, compensated, and compared against a fixed-amplitude sawtooth waveform to generate the PWM duty cycle. VMC has a single loop and is the simplest implementation, but its input-voltage disturbance rejection is only as fast as the voltage loop itself.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Peak Current Mode Control (PCMC) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vrefC["Vref"]:::src
    errC["Outer Voltage Error Amp"]:::amp
    compC["Voltage Compensator"]:::ctrl
    innerC["Inner Current Loop"]:::inner
    csfC["Current Sense Resistor"]:::csf
    slopeC["Slope Compensation Ramp"]:::slp
    pwmC["PWM Latch SR flip flop"]:::pwm
    clkC["Clock fs"]:::clk
    drvC["Gate Driver"]:::drv
    pwrC["Buck Power Stage"]:::pwr
    outC["Output Vo"]:::out

    vrefC --> errC
    outC --> errC
    errC --> compC
    compC --> innerC
    csfC --> innerC
    slopeC --> innerC
    clkC --> pwmC
    innerC --> pwmC
    pwmC --> drvC
    drvC --> pwrC
    pwrC --> outC
    pwrC --> csfC

    classDef src fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#c8a02a,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#d1e7ff,stroke:#2a5fc8,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2a8a4a,color:#000
    classDef inner fill:#f0d4ff,stroke:#7a2ac8,color:#000
    classDef csf fill:#ffd1d1,stroke:#a83232,color:#000
    classDef slp fill:#ffe1c2,stroke:#c8622a,color:#000
    classDef pwm fill:#e2d4ff,stroke:#5a2ac8,color:#000
    classDef clk fill:#e2d4ff,stroke:#5a2ac8,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#ffd1d1,stroke:#a83232,color:#000
    classDef pwr fill:#ffe1c2,stroke:#c8622a,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#e2e2e2,stroke:#444,color:#000
```

**Explanation:** In **Peak Current Mode Control (PCMC)**, the system uses **two cascaded loops**:
1. **Outer (slow) voltage loop** — compares `Vout` with `Vref` and produces a current reference `Iref`.
2. **Inner (fast) current loop** — the switch current (sensed by `Rcsf`) is compared with `Iref` plus a slope-compensation ramp; the latch resets when the current peak equals the reference.

**Advantages of PCMC over VMC:**
- Cycle-by-cycle current limiting (built-in over-current protection).
- Input-voltage feed-forward (line disturbance rejected in one cycle).
- Inductor becomes a current source — outer voltage loop becomes single-pole (easier to compensate).

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires slope compensation for `D > 0.5` to prevent sub-harmonic oscillation.
- Needs current sensing element (lossy or expensive).

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a feedback control system as applied to a switched-mode power converter. List the four essential functional blocks of any such system.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **feedback control system** in a power converter is a closed-loop arrangement in which an output variable (voltage or current) is continuously sensed, compared with a reference, and the difference (error) is used to modulate the switching duty cycle so that the output tracks the reference under varying load, line, and temperature conditions.

**Four essential functional blocks:**

1. **Reference Generator** — produces a stable set-point (`Vref`).
2. **Sensing & Feedback Network** (`H(s)`) — scales the output to a measurable level.
3. **Error Amplifier with Compensator** (`Gc(s)`) — computes the error and shapes the loop dynamics.
4. **PWM Modulator + Power Stage** (`Gp(s)`) — converts the compensated error into a duty-cycle command and delivers power to the load.

> [!TIP]
> For full marks (3/3), the KTU examiner expects you to **explicitly mention** the closed-loop nature and name *all four* blocks with their functions. A vague "feedback loop" alone gets at most 1 mark.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Voltage Mode Control (VMC) and Current Mode Control (CMC) in DC-DC converters. State two merits and one demerit of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | VMC | CMC |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensed variable** | Output voltage only | Output voltage + inductor/switch current |
| **Number of loops** | Single (voltage) | Two cascaded (outer V, inner I) |
| **Compensator design** | Complex (Type-II/III) | Simple (single-pole plant) |
| **Line disturbance rejection** | Slow (limited by voltage loop BW) | Fast (cycle-by-cycle) |
| **Over-current protection** | External | Built-in |
| **Slope compensation** | Not needed | Required for `D > 0.5` |

**Merits of VMC:** simple, noise-immune, single compensation network. **Demerit:** poor line transient response, complex compensation.

**Merits of CMC:** inherent OC protection, input feed-forward, simpler outer-loop comp. **Demerit:** needs current sensor and slope-comp ramp.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — VMC Design-Oriented

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Adapted]`

**(a)** Draw the functional block diagram of a closed-loop voltage-regulated buck converter operating in CCM under voltage mode control. Clearly label the reference, error amplifier, compensator, PWM modulator, power stage, and feedback network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a buck converter with `Vin = 24 V`, `Vout = 12 V`, switching frequency `fs = 100 kHz`, inductor `L = 50 µH`, capacitor `C = 100 µF` with `ESR = 30 mΩ`, and load resistance `R = 6 Ω`:
1. Derive the small-signal control-to-output transfer function `Gp(s)`. **(4 marks)**
2. Compute the LC resonant frequency `f0` and the Q-factor. **(2 marks)**
3. State the cross-over-frequency design rule for VMC. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram (7 marks):**

A buck converter under VMC consists of the following cascaded blocks (drawn in any clean style):

```
Vref ──►(Σ)──► Error Amp ──► Compensator ──► PWM Comparator ──► Gate Driver
        ▲−                          ▲                          │
        │                           │                          ▼
        │                       Sawtooth fs                Power Stage
        │                           │                  (MOSFET + L, C)
        │                           │                          │
        └─────── Sensor β ◄───── Output Vo ◄────────────────────┘
```

**Key labelling expected for full credit:**
- **Summing junction** with negative sign on the feedback input.
- **PWM modulator** explicitly shown with **sawtooth carrier** at frequency `fs`.
- **Feedback path** marked as a resistor divider with ratio `β = R2/(R1+R2)`.
- **Compensator block** labelled `Gc(s)` and **power stage** labelled `Gp(s)`.

**(b) Plant transfer function, resonant frequency, Q-factor and crossover rule:**

**(b.1) Derivation of `Gp(s)` (4 marks):**

For a buck converter in CCM, applying state-space averaging around the operating point, the small-signal control-to-output transfer function is:

$$
G_p(s) = \frac{\hat{v}_o(s)}{\hat{d}(s)} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1 + s \cdot R_{ESR} \cdot C}{1 + s \cdot R \cdot C + s^2 \cdot L \cdot C}
$$

Or equivalently, in canonical second-order form:

$$
G_p(s) = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1 + s/\omega_{z,ESR}}{1 + s/(\omega_0 Q) + s^2/\omega_0^2}
$$

Where the resonant frequency and Q are:

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}, \quad Q = R \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}, \quad \omega_{z,ESR} = \frac{1}{R_{ESR} C}
$$

The DC gain is `Gp(0) = Vin = 24 V` (because at DC, `Vout = d·Vin` and `d/Vout` control sensitivity = `Vin`).

**(b.2) Numerical computation of `f0` and `Q` (2 marks):**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5 \times 10^{-9}}} = \frac{1}{7.07 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 14{,}142 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2 \pi} \approx 2.25 \text{ kHz}
$$

$$
Q = 6 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{50 \times 10^{-6}}} = 6 \cdot \sqrt{2} \approx 8.485
$$

The high Q indicates an under-damped LC resonance that must be compensated to avoid loop instability.

**(b.3) Cross-over-frequency design rule (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{f_c \leq \frac{f_s}{5} \text{ to } \frac{f_s}{10} \approx 10 \text{ kHz to } 20 \text{ kHz}}
$$

Choosing `fc = 10 kHz` leaves at least one decade of frequency headroom for the compensator to roll off the gain at −20 dB/decade before the switching frequency is reached, preserving phase margin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — CMC Design-Oriented

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain **Peak Current Mode Control (PCMC)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **outer voltage loop** and the **inner current loop**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a buck converter under PCMC with `Vin = 20 V`, `Vout = 5 V`, `L = 20 µH`, `fs = 200 kHz`, `Rcsf = 50 mΩ`, and current-sense gain `Ri = 10`:
1. Derive the expression for the **up-slope of the inductor current** `m1` and the **down-slope** `m2`. **(3 marks)**
2. State the condition for **slope compensation** and compute the **minimum compensating slope** required for stable operation at `D = 0.5`. **(3 marks)**
3. Explain **sub-harmonic oscillation** and how slope compensation eliminates it. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) PCMC block diagram and explanation (7 marks):**

Peak Current Mode Control is a **two-loop control technique** in which:
- The **outer (slow) voltage loop** senses `Vout`, compares it with `Vref`, and produces a current command `Iref` (output of the voltage compensator).
- The **inner (fast) current loop** senses the **switch/inductor current** `iL` via a sense resistor `Rcsf` (or CT), adds a slope-compensation ramp, and compares the sum with `Iref` using a PWM latch. The latch resets at the next clock pulse and sets when `iL·Rcsf + Vramp = Iref`.

**Block diagram description (textual — examiner accepts a Mermaid-like or hand-drawn equivalent):**

```
Vref ──►(Σ)──► Voltage EA ──► Vcomp ──┐
        ▲−                              ▼
        │                          (Σ)──► PWM Latch ──► Driver ──► Buck Stage ──► Vo
        │                              ▲
        │                              │
        └─── β·Vo ◄────── Vo ◄──── iL · Rcsf
                                       +
                                  Slope Comp m_c
```

**Role of outer loop:** regulates average `Vout`; sets the bandwidth (typically `fc,V = fs/5` or slower).
**Role of inner loop:** forces the peak inductor current to follow `Iref` cycle-by-cycle; provides inherent over-current protection and input-voltage feed-forward.

**(b.1) Up-slope `m1` and down-slope `m2` of inductor current (3 marks):**

For a buck converter, during the **on-time** the inductor sees `Vin − Vout` across it, so the current rises linearly:

$$
m_1 = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{L}
$$

During the **off-time** the inductor sees `−Vout`, so the current falls:

$$
m_2 = \frac{V_{out}}{L}
$$

**Numerical values:**

$$
m_1 = \frac{20 - 5}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{15}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.75 \times 10^{6} \text{ A/s} = 0.75 \text{ A/µs}
$$

$$
m_2 = \frac{5}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ A/s} = 0.25 \text{ A/µs}
$$

**(b.2) Slope compensation condition and minimum compensating slope (3 marks):**

For stable operation in PCMC, the **compensating slope `mc`** must satisfy the classic Ridley / Middlebrook condition:

$$
m_c \geq \frac{m_2}{2} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{D} - 1 \right) \cdot 2 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad m_c \geq \frac{m_2}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - 2D}{D}
$$

But the simpler and more commonly-quoted KTU rule is:

$$
\boxed{m_c \geq \frac{m_2}{2} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{D} - 1 \right)}
$$

A more conservative and **exam-safe** statement often used in KTU answers is:

$$
m_c \geq \frac{m_2}{2} \quad \text{(for D ≤ 0.5)}, \quad m_c \geq m_2 \quad \text{(for all D)}
$$

**At `D = 0.5`:** minimum `mc = m2/2 = 0.125 A/µs`. Designers usually take `mc = 0.5 × m2 = 0.125 A/µs` or **half the down-slope** as a safe starting value.

**Expressing `mc` as a voltage ramp on the sense resistor** (with `Ri = 10`):

$$
V_{mc} = m_c \cdot R_{csf} \cdot R_i = 0.125 \times 10^6 \times 0.05 \times 10 = 62.5 \text{ V/s equivalent} = 0.3125 \text{ V per switching cycle}
$$

Or per time: `Vmc = 0.125 A/µs × 50 mΩ × 10 = 0.0625 V/µs` (i.e. the compensation ramp rises 62.5 mV every microsecond, equivalent to 312.5 mV over the 5 µs switching period).

**(b.3) Sub-harmonic oscillation (1 mark):**

When `D > 0.5`, in PCMC an inductor-current perturbation does not decay from cycle to cycle; instead, it grows, producing oscillation at `fs/2` (and lower harmonics). This is called **sub-harmonic oscillation**. Adding a slope-compensation ramp of sufficient magnitude (`mc > m2/2`) damps the perturbation, restoring cycle-by-cycle stability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign at the summing junction** in part-(a) diagram → 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up `m1` and `m2`** in part-(b) — students often write `m1 = Vout/L` (incorrect; this is the down-slope) → 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Stating the slope-comp condition as `mc = m2`** without explaining the **≥** inequality → lose the stability-justification mark.
> 4. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners are strict; write `A/µs`, `rad/s`, `Hz` explicitly.
> 5. **Not mentioning the negative feedback** in the definition → 1-mark deduction in Q1.
> 6. **Confusing Q-factor formula** `Q = R·√(C/L)` with `Q = (1/R)·√(L/C)` (which applies to **series** RLC, not parallel) → full-mark loss for the question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Pointers for KTU 2024 ESE & Internal Assessments:**

- [x] A **closed-loop power converter** uses the output (voltage/current) to drive a **summing junction** that computes the error with respect to a **reference**.
- [x] The four canonical transfer functions are: `T(s) = Gc·Gp·H`, `Gcl = G/(1+T)`, `E/R = 1/(1+T)`, `Y/D = Gp/(1+T)`.
- [x] **Steady-state error** → zero for step inputs in a **Type-1** system (which all CCM buck-derived converters are).
- [x] **Phase margin target:** 45°–60° (typically 60° in power electronics).
- [x] **Cross-over frequency target:** `fc ≤ fs/5` for VMC, `fc ≤ fs/10` for CMC.
- [x] **Voltage Mode Control (VMC):** single loop, output voltage only; compensator usually **Type-II or Type-III**.
- [x] **Current Mode Control (CMC):** two loops; outer voltage, inner current; compensator usually **Type-II** on the voltage side.
- [x] **Peak CMC** needs **slope compensation** for `D > 0.5` to prevent sub-harmonic oscillation.
- [x] **Compensator rule of placement:** zeros of `Gc` at the LC double pole `ω0 = 1/√(LC)`; high-frequency pole at the ESR zero `ωz,ESR = 1/(R_ESR·C)`.
- [x] **Stability criteria:** Bode (PM/GM), Nyquist, and **Routh-Hurwitz** — all three are KTU-exam relevant.
- [x] **Practical ICs:** `UC3842` (CMC), `SG3525` (VMC), `TL494` (both), `TPS5430` (VMC integrated).
- [x] **Current sensing methods:** sense resistor, current transformer (CT), Hall-effect sensor, RDS(on) sensing.
- [x] **Optocoupler** is used in **isolated** topologies (flyback, forward) to send the error signal across the isolation barrier.
- [x] **Right-Half-Plane (RHP) zero** appears in **boost and buck-boost** VMC plants — it limits achievable bandwidth to roughly `~fs/3`.
- [x] **Higher loop bandwidth → better transient response**, but lower phase margin if compensator is poorly designed.
- [x] **Bandwidth too close to fs** → switching noise aliasing and **sampling-effect** sub-harmonic issues.
- [x] **Cascaded control** (voltage → current) is the foundation of **PFC, motor drives, and grid-tied inverters**.
- [x] **Feed-forward** (input-voltage sensing) is a *complement* to feedback — it improves line transient response in **one switching cycle**.
- [x] **KTU design workflow:** (i) derive `Gp(s)` → (ii) plot Bode → (iii) choose `fc` → (iv) design `Gc(s)` for PM ≥ 60° → (v) verify with Routh → (vi) build, test, measure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Applications and Practical Considerations: Power Electronics in Renewable Energy - Role of Power Electronics in Solar PV and Wind Energy Systems, Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) Techniques, Grid Integration and Storage Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT411 - POWER ELECTRONICS FOR CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial Applications and Practical Considerations: Power Electronics in Renewable Energy - Role of Power Electronics in Solar PV and Wind Energy Systems, Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) Techniques, Grid Integration and Storage Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Power Electronics for Renewable Energy Systems (RES)** is the specialized domain of electrical engineering that deals with the design, control, and deployment of semiconductor switching devices (IGBTs, MOSFETs, thyristors, SiC/GaN devices) to efficiently condition, convert, and manage the electrical energy harvested from naturally replenishing sources such as solar photovoltaic (PV) arrays and wind turbine generators (WTGs). The discipline ensures that the variable, low-frequency, and often unregulated output of renewable sources is transformed into a stable, grid-compliant, and load-usable form through controlled power electronic converters (rectifiers, DC-DC choppers, voltage source inverters, and matrix converters).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "The application of solid-state power semiconductor devices and associated control circuitry to condition, convert, and regulate the electrical output from renewable energy sources to match load requirements or grid specifications."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator at a United Nations Summit"

Imagine renewable energy sources as foreign delegates speaking entirely different languages:
- A **solar PV module** is a Japanese delegate (speaks only in DC voltage, but the level keeps changing with sunlight).
- A **wind turbine** is a Brazilian delegate (speaks in wildly fluctuating AC frequency, depending on wind speed).
- The **electrical grid** is a strict French chairperson demanding exactly **50 Hz, 230 V (or 415 V) sinusoidal** language, with perfect power factor and low harmonics.

**Power Electronics acts as the simultaneous interpreter (translator)** — converting, cleaning, and synchronizing each delegate's "language" into the precise form demanded by the grid. The **MPPT controller** is the interpreter's ear, constantly listening and adjusting so the delegate speaks at the most efficient volume (Maximum Power Point).

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industrial Standards)

The performance of power electronic interfaces in RES is quantified using these standard industry metrics:

- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**: Must be **< 5%** as per IEEE 519-2014.
- **Power Factor (PF)**: Should be **> 0.95** (leading or lagging) at the point of common coupling (PCC).
- **Conversion Efficiency (η)**: Modern grid-tie inverters achieve **η = 96% to 99%**.
- **MPPT Efficiency (η_MPPT)**: Typically **> 99%** for advanced algorithms.
- **European Efficiency (η_EU)**: Weighted average efficiency — **> 95%** for premium inverters.
- **CEC Efficiency**: California Energy Commission weighted efficiency — **> 96%** benchmark.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Power Electronics is the Backbone of Modern RES:**
> 1. **DC-AC Conversion**: PV produces DC, wind produces variable-frequency AC — both need inversion to **50/60 Hz grid AC**.
> 2. **DC-DC Boost**: PV module voltage (typically 25-40 V per module) must be stepped up to **400-800 V DC bus** for grid-tie inverters.
> 3. **MPPT Implementation**: Achieves **20-30% additional energy harvest** compared to direct coupling.
> 4. **Grid Compliance**: Ensures anti-islanding, low-voltage ride-through (LVRT), and reactive power support.

### 1.4 The Three Industrial Power Electronics Subsystems in RES

| Subsystem | Primary Function | Key Power Electronic Converter |
|---|---|---|
| **PV Side (DC-DC Stage)** | MPPT + voltage boosting | Boost, Buck-Boost, Cuk, SEPIC |
| **DC-AC Stage (Inversion)** | DC to grid-quality AC | VSI (Voltage Source Inverter) with PWM/SVPWM |
| **Wind Side (AC-DC-AC)** | Variable freq. AC → Fixed freq. AC | Rectifier + DC-Link + Inverter, or DFIG back-to-back converter |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Role of Power Electronics in Solar PV Systems

#### 2.1.1 The PV Cell — Foundation

A photovoltaic cell is essentially a large-area semiconductor PN-junction diode that generates a photocurrent $I_{ph}$ when illuminated. The single-diode equivalent circuit is the standard model used in industry:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_t}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Where:**
- $I$ = Cell output current (A)
- $V$ = Cell terminal voltage (V)
- $I_{ph}$ = Photocurrent (proportional to irradiance $G$)
- $I_0$ = Diode reverse saturation current
- $R_s$ = Series resistance (ideally 0)
- $R_{sh}$ = Shunt resistance (ideally ∞)
- $n$ = Ideality factor (1 to 2)
- $V_t = \frac{kT}{q}$ = Thermal voltage ≈ **25.85 mV at 300 K**

#### 2.1.2 The Non-Linear I-V and P-V Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PV curve is **non-linear** and has a unique **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**. This MPP shifts with **irradiance (G)** and **temperature (T)**.

**Three Critical Points on the I-V Curve:**
1. **Short-Circuit Current ($I_{sc}$)**: Current at $V = 0$. Proportional to irradiance.
2. **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$)**: Voltage at $I = 0$. Depends logarithmically on irradiance and linearly decreases with temperature.
3. **Maximum Power Point ($P_{max} = V_{mpp} \cdot I_{mpp}$)**: The unique operating point where power extraction is maximized.

**Temperature and Irradiance Effects (Industrial Truths):**
- ↑ Irradiance $G$ → ↑ $I_{sc}$ (linearly), ↑ $V_{oc}$ (logarithmically)
- ↑ Temperature $T$ → ↑ $I_{sc}$ (slightly), ↓↓ $V_{oc}$ (significantly, **−0.3 to −0.4 %/°C**)
- **Practical Result**: Cold sunny days yield the highest PV power output.

### 2.2 The DC-DC Boost Converter for PV MPPT

The boost converter is the **most commonly deployed** converter in residential and commercial PV systems for MPPT. It steps up the low PV module voltage to a higher DC-link voltage.

**Operating Principle (Continuous Conduction Mode — CCM):**

**Switch ON (duration $D \cdot T_s$):**
- Inductor stores energy: $V_L = V_{pv}$
- Diode is reverse-biased
- Load is supplied by capacitor

**Switch OFF (duration $(1-D) \cdot T_s$):**
- Inductor releases energy: $V_L = V_{pv} - V_{dc}$
- Diode conducts, capacitor charges

**Volt-Second Balance on Inductor:**

$$V_{pv} \cdot D T_s + (V_{pv} - V_{dc})(1-D) T_s = 0$$

Solving for the voltage gain:

$$\frac{V_{dc}}{V_{pv}} = \frac{1}{1 - D}$$

**Where:**
- $D$ = Duty cycle (0 to 1)
- $V_{pv}$ = PV input voltage
- $V_{dc}$ = DC-link output voltage

**Inductor Design Equation:**

$$L = \frac{V_{pv} \cdot D}{\Delta I_L \cdot f_s}$$

**Capacitor Design Equation:**

$$C = \frac{I_{dc} \cdot D}{\Delta V_{dc} \cdot f_s}$$

### 2.3 MPPT Techniques — The Heart of PV Efficiency

#### 2.3.1 Why MPPT is Essential

Without MPPT, a PV module operates at a fixed voltage determined by the load. This fixed operating point is rarely at the MPP, resulting in **20–30% energy loss**. MPPT dynamically adjusts the converter duty cycle $D$ to force the PV array to operate at $V_{mpp}$.

#### 2.3.2 Perturb & Observe (P&O) Algorithm

**Logic:** Periodically perturb (increase or decrease) the PV voltage by a small step $\Delta V$, then observe the resulting change in power $\Delta P$.

- If $\Delta P > 0$: Continue perturbing in the **same direction** (we are moving toward MPP).
- If $\Delta P < 0$: Reverse the perturbation direction (we have passed the MPP).

**Mathematical Condition:**

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = \frac{d(VI)}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV}$$

At MPP, this derivative equals **zero**.

#### 2.3.3 Incremental Conductance (IncCond) Algorithm

**The most analytically rigorous MPPT method.** It directly uses the condition $dP/dV = 0$:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

**Rearranging:**

$$\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I}{V}$$

**Decision Logic:**
- If $\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I}{V}$ → At MPP → Hold duty cycle
- If $\frac{dI}{dV} > -\frac{I}{V}$ → Left of MPP → Decrease V (increase D for boost)
- If $\frac{dI}{dV} < -\frac{I}{V}$ → Right of MPP → Increase V (decrease D for boost)

**Advantage over P&O:** Faster tracking, **no oscillation** around MPP under steady-state conditions.

### 2.4 Role of Power Electronics in Wind Energy Systems

#### 2.4.1 Wind Turbine Power Extraction

The mechanical power available from wind is governed by **Betz's Law**:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p(\lambda, \beta)$$

**Where:**
- $\rho$ = Air density (≈ **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level)
- $A$ = Swept area of blades ($= \pi R^2$)
- $v$ = Wind velocity (m/s)
- $C_p$ = Power coefficient (max theoretical = **0.593** = **Betz Limit**)
- $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v}$ = Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)
- $\beta$ = Blade pitch angle (degrees)

**Maximum $C_p$**: Achieved at optimal $\lambda_{opt} \approx 7$–8 for a typical 3-blade turbine.

#### 2.4.2 Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) Topologies

| Topology | Generator | Power Electronic Interface |
|---|---|---|
| **Type A (Fixed Speed)** | Squirrel Cage IG | Soft-starter only; no converter |
| **Type B (Limited Variable Speed)** | Wound Rotor IG + Rotor Resistor | Rotor resistance control |
| **Type C (DFIG — Doubly Fed)** | Wound Rotor IG | **Back-to-back PWM converter** on rotor (≈30% of rated power) |
| **Type D (Full Converter)** | PMSG or SCIG | **Full-rated back-to-back converter** (100% of rated power) |

**Industry Trend:** Type C (DFIG) dominates the **5–10 MW** segment; Type D (PMSG + full converter) dominates **offshore wind** (reliability, no gearbox).

### 2.5 Grid Integration — The Critical Interface

A grid-tie inverter must satisfy **IEEE 929-2000 / IEEE 1547-2018** standards:

1. **Anti-Islanding Protection**: Inverter must disconnect within **2 seconds** of grid loss.
2. **Synchronization**: Phase, frequency, and voltage must match grid.
3. **Power Quality**: THD < 5%, DC injection < 0.5% of rated output.
4. **Low-Voltage Ride-Through (LVRT)**: Must remain connected during grid faults (typically 0.15–0.85 p.u. voltage for 150–625 ms).
5. **Reactive Power Support**: Modern inverters can provide **Q-control** to support grid voltage.

### 2.6 Energy Storage Systems (ESS)

Storage smooths the inherent intermittency of renewables. The bidirectional DC-DC converter is the key power electronic interface:

**Battery Charging (Buck Mode):** $V_{bat} = D \cdot V_{dc}$

**Battery Discharging (Boost Mode):** $V_{dc} = \frac{V_{bat}}{1 - D}$

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V+IR_s}{nV_t}\right)-1\right] - \frac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | PV cell I-V equation | Amperes |
| 2 | $V_t = \frac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV at 300 K}$ | Thermal voltage | Volts |
| 3 | $FF = \frac{V_{mpp} I_{mpp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor (typically 0.7–0.85) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\frac{V_{dc}}{V_{pv}} = \frac{1}{1-D}$ | Boost converter voltage gain | $D \in [0,1)$ |
| 5 | $\frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{-D}{1-D}$ | Buck-Boost converter gain | Inverting output |
| 6 | $L_{crit} = \frac{(1-D)^2 D R}{2 f_s}$ | Critical inductance for boost CCM | Henry |
| 7 | $\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V\frac{dI}{dV} = 0$ | MPPT optimality condition | At MPP |
| 8 | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 C_p(\lambda,\beta)$ | Wind power equation | Watts |
| 9 | $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | Betz Limit | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v}$ | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $V_{LL,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}$ | Inverter line-to-line RMS voltage (SPWM) | Volts |
| 12 | $\text{THD}\% = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1}\times 100$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | % |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: PV Cell Open-Circuit Voltage Dependence on Irradiance

**Given:** The PV cell I-V equation with $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$ (ideal cell):

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{nV_t}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 1:** At open-circuit condition, $I = 0$:

$$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_t}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:

$$I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_t}\right) - 1\right] = I_{ph}$$

**Step 3:** Since $I_{ph} \gg I_0$ (typically by 6-8 orders of magnitude), the $-1$ term is negligible:

$$I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_t}\right) \approx I_{ph}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $V_{oc}$ by taking the natural logarithm:

$$\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_t}\right) = \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}$$

**Step 5:** Apply $\ln$ to both sides:

$$\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_t} = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Final closed-form expression:

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = n V_t \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This logarithmic dependence explains why a 50% reduction in irradiance (e.g., cloudy day) only reduces $V_{oc}$ by approximately **8-10%**, while $I_{sc}$ drops by nearly 50%. The cell voltage is therefore relatively robust, but the current is highly irradiance-sensitive.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Boost Converter Voltage Gain (CCM)

**Given:** Inductor volt-second balance for steady-state operation.

**Step 1:** During the ON state ($0 < t < D T_s$): The switch is closed, the diode is reverse-biased, and the full input voltage appears across the inductor.

$$V_{L,on} = V_{pv}$$

**Step 2:** During the OFF state ($D T_s < t < T_s$): The switch is open, the diode conducts, and the inductor voltage equals the input minus the output.

$$V_{L,off} = V_{pv} - V_{dc}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the volt-second balance principle (average inductor voltage over one switching period must be zero in steady state):

$$\int_0^{T_s} V_L \, dt = 0$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the piecewise voltage expression:

$$V_{pv} \cdot (D T_s) + (V_{pv} - V_{dc}) \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $T_s$ (non-zero):

$$V_{pv} \cdot D + (V_{pv} - V_{dc}) \cdot (1-D) = 0$$

**Step 6:** Expand the second term:

$$V_{pv} D + V_{pv} - V_{pv} D - V_{dc}(1-D) = 0$$

**Step 7:** The $V_{pv} D$ terms cancel:

$$V_{pv} - V_{dc}(1-D) = 0$$

**Step 8:** Solve for the voltage gain $M(D) = \frac{V_{dc}}{V_{pv}}$:

$$V_{dc}(1-D) = V_{pv}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{dc}}{V_{pv}} = \frac{1}{1-D} = M(D)}$$

**Engineering Insight:** As $D \to 1$, the gain $M \to \infty$ theoretically. However, practical limitations (switch losses, inductor resistance, diode forward drop) limit the maximum useful gain to approximately **4-5x** in CCM operation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: MPPT Optimality Condition from First Principles

**Given:** PV output power $P = V \cdot I$ must be maximized with respect to voltage $V$ at the MPP.

**Step 1:** Define the objective function:

$$P(V) = V \cdot I(V)$$

**Step 2:** The necessary condition for a maximum is that the first derivative equals zero:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = 0 \quad \text{at MPP}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the product rule of differentiation:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = \frac{d(V \cdot I)}{dV} = I \cdot \frac{dV}{dV} + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify $\frac{dV}{dV} = 1$:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV}$$

**Step 5:** Set the derivative to zero at the MPP:

$$I_{mpp} + V_{mpp} \cdot \left.\frac{dI}{dV}\right|_{mpp} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the incremental conductance $\frac{dI}{dV}$ at MPP:

$$\left.\frac{dI}{dV}\right|_{mpp} = -\frac{I_{mpp}}{V_{mpp}}$$

**Step 7:** Define the instantaneous conductance $G = \frac{I}{V}$ and the incremental conductance $G_{inc} = \frac{dI}{dV}$:

$$\boxed{G_{inc} + G = 0 \quad \text{at MPP}}$$

**Generalized Decision Rule for IncCond Algorithm:**

| Condition | Operating Region | Corrective Action (Boost Converter) |
|---|---|---|
| $G_{inc} > -G$ | Left of MPP (low V) | Decrease D (increases $V_{pv}$) |
| $G_{inc} = -G$ | At MPP | Hold D constant |
| $G_{inc} < -G$ | Right of MPP (high V) | Increase D (decreases $V_{pv}$) |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: SPWM Inverter Line Voltage (Fundamental Component)

**Given:** Single-phase full-bridge or three-phase VSI with sinusoidal PWM.

**Step 1:** The Fourier series of a unipolar SPWM waveform (phase voltage) has a fundamental amplitude:

$$V_{phase,1} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2}$$

**Step 2:** For a three-phase inverter, the line-to-line voltage is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltage:

$$V_{LL,1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{phase,1}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the phase voltage expression:

$$V_{LL,1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Convert peak value to RMS:

$$V_{LL,1,peak} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}$$

$$V_{LL,1,rms} = \frac{V_{LL,1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Final compact form:

$$\boxed{V_{LL,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc} \approx 0.612 \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}}$$

**Where:**
- $m_a = \frac{V_{control,peak}}{V_{tri,peak}}$ = Modulation index (0 to 1 for linear region)
- $V_{dc}$ = DC-link voltage

**Engineering Insight:** This equation is the foundation for inverter sizing in grid-tie applications. For a 415 V (line-to-line, RMS) grid, with $V_{dc} = 700$ V, we need $m_a = \frac{415}{0.612 \times 700} \approx 0.968$ — operating in **overmodulation**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Wind Turbine Power Coefficient (Betz's Law)

**Given:** Air mass flow through the turbine swept area.

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate of air:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$$

**Step 2:** Kinetic energy flux of undisturbed wind (upstream):

$$P_{upstream} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

**Step 3:** After passing through the rotor, wind speed reduces from $v$ to $v_2$. The extracted power is the difference in kinetic energy:

$$P_{extracted} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (v^2 - v_2^2)$$

**Step 4:** The mass flow through the rotor disk uses the **average** velocity $v_{avg} = \frac{v + v_2}{2}$:

$$\dot{m}_{rotor} = \rho A \frac{v + v_2}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the power equation:

$$P_{extracted} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \left(\frac{v + v_2}{2}\right) (v^2 - v_2^2) = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v + v_2)(v^2 - v_2^2)$$

**Step 6:** Factor the difference of squares:

$$P_{extracted} = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v + v_2)^2 (v - v_2)$$

**Step 7:** Define the power coefficient $C_p$ and the induction factor $a = \frac{v - v_2}{2v}$ (so that $v - v_2 = 2av$ and $v + v_2 = v(2-a)$):

$$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{\frac{1}{4} \rho A v^3 (2-a)^2 \cdot 2a}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3}$$

**Step 8:** Simplify:

$$C_p(a) = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 9:** Maximize $C_p$ by setting $\frac{dC_p}{da} = 0$:

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)] = 4(1-a)[(1-a) - 2a] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

**Step 10:** Non-trivial solution: $1 - 3a = 0 \Rightarrow a = \frac{1}{3}$.

**Step 11:** Substitute back:

$$C_{p,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{(Betz Limit)}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** No wind turbine, no matter how well designed, can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in wind. Real turbines achieve $C_p$ between **0.35 and 0.48** due to aerodynamic, mechanical, and electrical losses.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MPPT Algorithms

| Parameter | P&O | Incremental Conductance | Fractional $V_{oc}$ | Fuzzy Logic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tracking Speed** | Moderate | Fast | Slow | Fast |
| **Steady-State Oscillation** | Yes (always) | No (theoretically) | Yes | Minimal |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | Very Low | High |
| **Sensors Required** | V, I | V, I | V only | V, I + DSP |
| **Efficiency** | 90-95% | 95-99% | 80-90% | 98-99% |
| **Partial Shading Handling** | Poor | Poor | Poor | Good |
| **Industrial Use** | Most common | Premium | Low-cost | R&D |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Power Electronics Architecture for a Grid-Tied PV System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[PV Array<br>Multiple Modules<br>in Series Parallel] --> node2[DC DC Boost<br>Converter with<br>MPPT Controller]
    node2 --> node3[DC Link<br>Capacitor Bank<br>400 to 800 V]
    node3 --> node4[Three Phase<br>Voltage Source<br>Inverter VSI]
    node4 --> node5[LC LCL<br>Filter Stage]
    node5 --> node6[Step Up<br>Transformer<br>415 V Grid]
    node6 --> node7a[Utility Grid<br>50 Hz 415 V]
    node6 --> node7b[Local AC<br>Loads]
    node8[MPPT<br>Algorithm<br>P and O] -.-> node2
    node9[PLL<br>Synchronization<br>and PQ Control] -.-> node4
    node10[Battery Bank<br>with Bidirectional<br>DC DC Converter] -.-> node3
    node11[Supervisory<br>SCADA and<br>Monitoring] -.-> node8
    node11 -.-> node9
```

### 4.2 DFIG Wind Energy Conversion System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Wind<br>Kinetic<br>Energy] --> nodeB[Aerodynamic<br>Rotor and<br>Hub]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Gearbox<br>Speed<br>Multiplier]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Doubly Fed<br>Induction<br>Generator DFIG]
    nodeD -->|Stator| nodeE[Step Up<br>Transformer]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Utility Grid<br>50 Hz 11 kV]
    nodeD -->|Rotor| nodeG[Rotor Side<br>Converter RSC<br>AC to DC]
    nodeG --> nodeH[DC Link<br>Capacitor]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Grid Side<br>Converter GSC<br>DC to AC]
    nodeI --> nodeF
    nodeJ[MPPT<br>Controller<br>TSR Optimal] -.-> nodeG
    nodeK[PLL and<br>Grid Sync<br>Control] -.-> nodeI
    nodeJ -.-> nodeK
```

### 4.3 P&O MPPT Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start]) --> init[Read V pv and I pv<br>Compute P pv]
    init --> meas[Perturb V pv<br>by delta V]
    meas --> wait[Wait for<br>Transient<br>Settling]
    wait --> read2[Read New V pv<br>and I pv<br>Compute P new]
    read2 --> dec{Is P new<br>greater than<br>P previous?}
    dec -->|Yes| dir{Is V new<br>greater than<br>V previous?}
    dir -->|Yes| inc1[Keep Increasing<br>Duty Cycle D]
    dir -->|No| dec1[Keep Decreasing<br>Duty Cycle D]
    dec -->|No| dir2{Is V new<br>greater than<br>V previous?}
    dir2 -->|Yes| dec2[Reverse Direction<br>Decrease D]
    dir2 -->|No| inc2[Reverse Direction<br>Increase D]
    inc1 --> meas
    dec1 --> meas
    dec2 --> meas
    inc2 --> meas
```

### 4.4 IncCond MPPT Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> readA[Read V k and I k<br>Compute P k]
    readA --> diff[Compute dV and dI<br>delta I over delta V]
    diff --> chk{dV<br>equals<br>zero?}
    chk -->|Yes| cur{Is I k<br>greater than<br>zero?}
    cur -->|Yes| decV[Decrease V ref<br>Increase D]
    cur -->|No| incV[Increase V ref<br>Decrease D]
    chk -->|No| calc[Compute Ratio<br>dI over dV plus<br>I over V]
    calc --> cmp{Is Ratio<br>equal to<br>zero?}
    cmp -->|Yes| hold[At MPP<br>Hold D constant]
    cmp -->|No| sign{Is Ratio<br>greater than<br>zero?}
    sign -->|Yes| decV2[Left of MPP<br>Decrease V ref<br>Increase D]
    sign -->|No| incV2[Right of MPP<br>Increase V ref<br>Decrease D]
    decV --> readA
    incV --> readA
    hold --> readA
    decV2 --> readA
    incV2 --> readA
```

flowchart LR
    nodeP[DC Link<br>Bus 400 V] --> nodeQ[Half Bridge<br>Bi directional<br>DC DC]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[LC Filter<br>Stage]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Battery<br>Bank 48 V<br>to 200 V]
    nodeT[Mode 1<br>Buck<br>Charging] -.-> nodeQ
    nodeU[Mode 2<br>Boost<br>Discharging] -.-> nodeQ
